| Plant capacity | $C$ = 5 tonnes/day | 5,000 kg/day |
| Block mass per can | $m_b$ | 50 kg |
| Number of cans needed per day | $n = C / m_b$ | 100 cans/day |
| Freezing time per can (Plank) | $t_f$ | 10 h (actual) |
| Harvest + refill time per can | $t_h$ | 0.5 h |
| Total cycle time | $T_{cycle} = t_f + t_h$ | 10.5 h |
| Total cans in plant (with 24 h cycle) | $N_{total} = n \cdot (T_{cycle}/24)$ | $100 \times (10.5/24) = 44$ cans |
| Daily refrigeration load | $Q_R = n \cdot Q_{total}$ | $100 \times 23{,}504 = 2.35 \times 10^6$ kJ/day |
| Compressor tonnage | $TR = Q_R / (3.517 \times 24 \times 3600)$ | $\approx 7.7$ TR |
| Brine tank volume | $V_{brine} = N_{total} \times V_{can} \times 1.3$ | $44 \times 0.0625 \times 1.3 \approx 3.6$ m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **V_can** for 50 kg block ≈ 0.05 m³ (since ρ_ice ≈ 917 kg/m³ → 50/917 ≈ 0.0545 m³). The factor **1.3** accounts for void volume and brine circulation space.

## 3.4 Complete Sizing Example (KTU-style Numerical)

**Problem:** Design a 10 tonne/day block ice plant using 50 kg ice cans with brine at −15 °C. Water enters at 25 °C; final ice temperature is −5 °C. Determine: (i) cans frozen per day, (ii) total refrigeration load in kJ/day, (iii) compressor TR required, and (iv) total number of cans in plant if cycle time = 11 hours.

### Solution:

**(i) Cans per day**:
$$n = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}{50 \text{ kg/can}} = 200 \; \text{cans/day}$$

**(ii) Total refrigeration load**:
$$Q_s = 50 \times 4.187 \times 25 = 5233.75 \; \text{kJ}$$

$$Q_L = 50 \times 334 = 16{,}700 \; \text{kJ}$$

$$Q_{sub} = 50 \times 2.093 \times 5 = 523.25 \; \text{kJ}$$

$$Q_{total} = 22{,}457 \; \text{kJ/can}$$

$$Q_{daily} = 200 \times 22{,}457 = 4.49 \times 10^6 \; \text{kJ/day}$$

**(iii) Compressor TR**:
$$TR = \frac{4.49 \times 10^6}{3.517 \times 24 \times 3600} = \frac{4.49 \times 10^6}{303{,}964.8}$$

$$\boxed{TR \approx 14.77 \approx 15 \; \text{TR}}$$

**(iv) Total cans in plant**:
$$N = 200 \times \frac{11}{24} = 91.67 \approx 92 \; \text{cans}$$

Add 10% spare cans:
$$N_{total} = 92 \times 1.1 \approx 102 \; \text{cans}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constructional Anatomy of an Ice Can

The following block diagram illustrates the **structural anatomy** and **material specification** of a typical galvanized-iron ice can used in Indian block ice plants:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOP OPENING - Pouring Spout] --> B[VERTICAL BODY - GI Sheet 1.6 mm]
    B --> C[REINFORCED RIM - 25 mm Flange]
    B --> D[OUTER FLAT SURFACE - Heat Transfer Zone]
    B --> E[CORNER STITCH WELD - Leak Proof Seam]
    B --> F[BOTTOM CLOSURE - Insulated Base]
    F --> G[STEEL SUPPORT SKID - For Forklift Handling]
    
    H[AUXILIARY FEATURES] --> I[SLIGHTLY TAPERED BODY - Easy Block Release]
    H --> J[LIFTING HOOKS - Two Point Suspension]
    H --> K[NUMBER PLATE - Capacity Tag]
    
    style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    style D fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    style E fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000
    style F fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000
    style G fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The can body is **slightly tapered** (typically 2–3° outward from top to bottom) so that the frozen ice block releases easily when the can is inverted — no mechanical thawing needed.

## 4.2 Block Ice Plant — Functional Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Treated Water Tank] -->|Filling| IC[Ice Can Bank]
    IC -->|Immersion in Brine| BT[Brine Tank at -15 C]
    BT -->|Refrigerant Cooling| EV[Evaporator Coils]
    EV -->|Suction| COMP[Compressor]
    COMP -->|Discharge| COND[Condenser]
    COND -->|Liquid| EV
    IC -->|Thaw Cycle| EX[Ice Block Extraction]
    EX -->|Storage| IS[Ice Storage Room]
    IS -->|Distribution| MKT[Fisheries and Markets]
    
    style W fill:#dbeafe,color:#000
    style IC fill:#fef3c7,color:#000
    style BT fill:#dcfce7,color:#000
    style EV fill:#fee2e2,color:#000
    style COMP fill:#ede9fe,color:#000
    style COND fill:#fce7f3,color:#000
    style EX fill:#e0f2fe,color:#000
    style IS fill:#fef3c7,color:#000
    style MKT fill:#dcfce7,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Freezing Front Progression Inside the Can

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TIME ZERO - Water at 25 C throughout] -->|Sensible cooling| B[TIME 30 min - Water at 4 C, supercooled]
    B -->|Latent begins| C[TIME 1 h - Thin ice shell on walls]
    C -->|Front moves inward| D[TIME 4 h - Ice layer 25 percent of width]
    D -->|Front accelerates| E[TIME 8 h - Ice layer 60 percent]
    E -->|Plank governed| F[TIME 10 h - Block 95 percent solid]
    F -->|Subcooling| G[TIME 10.5 h - Ice at -5 C, ready to harvest]
    
    style A fill:#fee2e2,color:#000
    style B fill:#fed7aa,color:#000
    style C fill:#fef3c7,color:#000
    style D fill:#d9f99d,color:#000
    style E fill:#86efac,color:#000
    style F fill:#4ade80,color:#000
    style G fill:#22c55e,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** The freezing front velocity is **not constant** — it starts fast (large surface area, small ice layer) and slows down as the front approaches the center. This is exactly what Plank's equation predicts via the $\frac{ab}{2}$ term dominating at the start.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define an ice can. List any two standard sizes of ice cans used in the Indian food industry with their typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**
An ice can is a thin-walled, rectangular, galvanized-iron container used to produce solid block ice by immersion in a brine tank. Standard sizes include:

- **50 kg block** (500 × 250 × 500 mm) — most widely used for fisheries and milk chilling.
- **100 kg block** (700 × 300 × 700 mm) — used in harbour wholesale markets and large cold storages.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State Plank's equation for freezing time of an ice block. What physical parameters does it relate?**

**Model Answer:**
$$t = \frac{\rho \cdot L_f}{k_{ice} \cdot (T_f - T_{brine})} \left[ \frac{ab}{2} + (a+b) \cdot \frac{\delta}{2} + \frac{\delta^2}{4} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{3600}$$

It relates freezing time to: **(i)** the thermophysical properties of water/ice ($\rho$, $L_f$, $k_{ice}$), **(ii)** the brine temperature depression ($T_f - T_{brine}$), and **(iii)** the geometric dimensions of the can ($a$, $b$, $\delta$).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the total refrigeration load required to freeze a **50 kg block of ice** in a standard ice can, given water enters at 27 °C and the final ice temperature is −5 °C. Clearly show sensible, latent, and sub-cooling contributions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Plank's equation, estimate the freezing time for a 50 kg block ice can with dimensions 500 × 250 × 500 mm, brine temperature of −12 °C, and can wall thickness of 2 mm. Comment on why the calculated time differs from the actual plant operation time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Using $c_w = 4.187$ kJ/kg·K, $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg, $c_{ice} = 2.093$ kJ/kg·K:

- Sensible: $Q_s = 50 \times 4.187 \times 27 = 5652.45$ kJ
- Latent: $Q_L = 50 \times 334 = 16{,}700$ kJ
- Sub-cooling: $Q_{sub} = 50 \times 2.093 \times 5 = 523.25$ kJ
- **Total: $Q_{total} = 22{,}875.7$ kJ per can**

**Model Solution (b):**
With $a = b = 0.125$ m, $\delta = 0.002$ m, $\Delta T = 12$ K, $k_{ice} = 2.22$ W/m·K, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $L_f = 334{,}000$ J/kg:

- Geometric term = $\frac{(0.125)^2}{2} + (0.25) \cdot \frac{0.002}{2} + \frac{(0.002)^2}{4}$
- Geometric term = $0.0078125 + 0.00025 + 0.000001 = 0.0080635$ m²
- $t = \dfrac{1000 \times 334{,}000}{2.22 \times 12} \times 0.0080635 / 3600$
- $t = \dfrac{3.34 \times 10^8}{26.64} \times 0.0080635 / 3600$
- $t = 1.254 \times 10^7 \times 0.0080635 / 3600 = 101{,}116 / 3600$
- **$t \approx 28.1$ hours (Plank estimate)**

**Comment:** Actual plant time is 8–10 hours because Plank's equation (i) neglects natural convection currents inside the water, (ii) assumes no brine agitation, and (iii) ignores surface heat transfer enhancement at the can–brine interface.

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Design a **5 tonne/day block ice plant** using 50 kg ice cans. Determine the number of cans to be frozen per day, total cans in the plant (assuming a 10-hour cycle), and the daily refrigeration load in kJ. Water at 30 °C is frozen to −5 °C. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat functional flow diagram the operation of a brine-immersion block ice plant. Discuss the role of the thaw cycle and the typical materials used for can construction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- Cans/day: $n = 5000 / 50 = 100$ cans/day
- Cycle time: 10 h → Total cans in plant: $N = 100 \times (10/24) = 41.67 \approx 42$ cans (+10% spare = 47 cans)
- Sensible: $Q_s = 50 \times 4.187 \times 30 = 6280.5$ kJ
- Latent: $Q_L = 50 \times 334 = 16{,}700$ kJ
- Sub-cooling: $Q_{sub} = 50 \times 2.093 \times 5 = 523.25$ kJ
- Per-can total: $Q_{total} = 23{,}503.75$ kJ
- **Daily load: $Q_{daily} = 100 \times 23{,}504 = 2.35 \times 10^6$ kJ/day**

**Model Solution (b):**
Refer to the **functional process flow** in Section 4.2. The operation sequence is:
1. Fill cans with treated water in the filling station.
2. Lower cans into the brine tank at −12 to −15 °C.
3. Freeze for ~10 hours.
4. Lift cans to the **thaw station** — a small auxiliary tank with warm brine (~+10 °C) for 30–60 seconds to release the surface ice film.
5. Invert cans on the dumping table to release the solid block.
6. Transfer blocks via conveyor to the ice storage room.

**Can material:** Galvanized iron (1.6–2.0 mm) is standard. Aluminium cans are used in premium plants for higher thermal conductivity (~3× faster freezing) but at 4× the cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sub-cooling term** in Part (a) costs you **2 marks** — every KTU numerical must account for sensible + latent + sub-cooling.
> 2. **Mixing up $a$ and $b$ in Plank's equation** — remember: $a$ is the **thickness** (smaller dimension / 2) and $b$ is the **width** (larger dimension / 2). Using the full dimension instead of half-thickness is the **most common error**, and it inflates the freezing time by **a factor of 4**.
> 3. **Not converting seconds to hours** in the final step — KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** if your final answer is in seconds.
> 4. **Forgetting the 10% spare can allowance** in plant sizing — this is a **standard KTU convention** for design numericals.
> 5. **Neglecting Plank's equation limitations** — any answer that does not discuss the assumption vs. reality gap will be marked down for lack of engineering judgment.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Ice can** = thin-walled GI container for block ice production; standard size = **50 kg** in India.
- ⭐ Material: **Galvanized Iron (GI) sheet**, 1.6–2.0 mm thick; aluminium is faster but costlier.
- ⭐ Process: **Sensible cooling → Latent freezing → Sub-cooling** (Three explicit phases — never skip any).
- ⭐ Dominant load: **Latent heat** (~80% of total); $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg.
- ⭐ **Plank's equation** governs freezing time — memorize the bracketed geometric term exactly.
- ⭐ Brine temperature: **−10 °C to −15 °C** is standard; lower brine = faster freezing but higher compressor work.
- ⭐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** = 12,660 kJ/h — memorize for all refrigeration calculations.
- ⭐ Specific energy benchmark: **80–90 kWh per tonne of ice** for well-designed plants.
- ⭐ **Tapering** of can body (2–3°) enables block release without mechanical thawing.
- ⭐ Plank's equation is an **upper-bound estimate** — actual plant time is 25–35% of Plank's prediction.
- ⭐ **Cycle time = Freezing time + Harvest time** (typically 10–11 h total per batch).
- ⭐ Spare can allowance = **10%** of working cans (KTU design convention).
- ⭐ Block ice = highest bulk density ice form; ideal for **fisheries, milk chilling, and long-distance transport**.
- ⭐ **IS 4594** governs dimensional and material standards for ice cans in India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Agitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Agitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Agitation** is a mechanical/physical technique used in secondary refrigerant systems (such as ice bank tanks, brine chillers, and cold storage units) in which **compressed air is bubbled through the liquid refrigerant** (water, brine, or glycol solutions) to induce forced convection currents, prevent thermal stratification, and enhance the overall heat transfer rate between the refrigerant and the cooling coil/evaporator.

In food industry refrigeration, air agitation is most commonly employed in:
- **Ice Bank / Ice Building Coolers (IBT / IBC)**
- **Cold Storage brine circulation tanks**
- **Milk cooling tanks** and dairy chilling plants
- **Hydro-cooling systems** for fresh produce

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like stirring your morning coffee with a spoon — but without the spoon!**

Imagine a large tank of water that needs to be cooled uniformly. Without intervention, cold water (being denser) sinks to the bottom while warmer water stays at the top. This is called **thermal stratification** — the system has "layers" of different temperatures.

Now imagine blowing air from the bottom of the tank, like a bubble-maker in a fish aquarium. The rising bubbles drag the colder bottom water upward, while pushing the warmer surface water down. This churning effect:
1. **Mixes** the fluid thoroughly (no more temperature layers),
2. **Increases contact** between the fluid and cooling surfaces, and
3. **Boosts heat transfer** significantly without using any mechanical impeller.

That, in essence, is **air agitation** — using **bubbles as a free internal stirrer**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Parameters

The following standard metrics govern air-agitated refrigeration systems:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Quantity Required** | **0.004 to 0.008 m³/min per 1000 kcal/h of refrigeration** | Empirical industry value (IS standards) |
| **Air Pressure** | **0.5 to 1.5 bar (gauge)** | Low-pressure blower air |
| **Orifice / Nozzle Size** | **2 mm to 5 mm diameter** | Distributor holes on submerged pipe |
| **Air Compressor Power** | Typically **0.5 to 2 kW** for industrial ice banks | Auxiliary load |
| **Tube Pitch of Agitator Pipe** | **100 mm to 150 mm** | Distribution uniformity |

> [!NOTE]
> The **air quantity ratio (0.004–0.008 m³/min per 1000 kcal/h)** is a critical empirical rule-of-thumb frequently tested in KTU university examinations. Memorize it!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The operational mechanism of air agitation can be broken down into the following sequential steps:

1. **Compressed Air Generation**: A low-pressure air compressor (or blower) supplies a continuous stream of dry, oil-free compressed air at controlled pressure (typically **0.5–1.5 bar gauge**).

2. **Distribution via Perforated Pipes**: This air is routed through **perforated PVC or copper distributor pipes** located near the **bottom** of the refrigerant tank, just below or beside the evaporator coil.

3. **Bubble Formation**: The compressed air escapes through small orifices (**2–5 mm diameter**), forming a curtain of fine bubbles that rise vertically through the liquid.

4. **Buoyancy-Driven Fluid Motion**: As the bubbles rise, they entrain surrounding liquid and create an **upward drag force**, pulling cold bottom liquid toward the surface. This is governed by the **buoyancy principle (Archimedes' Law)**.

5. **Density Equalization**: The rising (lighter) bubbles plus cold liquid mix with the warm surface liquid, creating a **turbulent flow regime** that destroys thermal stratification.

6. **Enhanced Convective Heat Transfer**: The fluid in motion at higher velocity increases the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, which directly boosts the **convective heat transfer coefficient (h)** on the evaporator coil surface.

7. **Prevention of Localized Freezing**: Continuous agitation ensures that no liquid layer remains stationary against the evaporator surface long enough to freeze, preventing **ice build-up** and coil damage.

8. **Air Release & Recirculation**: The air escapes from the liquid surface, is vented (or de-humidified and recirculated), and the cycle repeats.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **one-stop exam reference** for all formulas, units, and boundary conditions for "Air Agitation" in KTU examinations.

| S.No. | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | KTU Significance |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $Q_a = (0.004 \text{ to } 0.008) \times \frac{Q_R}{1000}$ | Empirical air quantity rule | $m^3/\min$ | **Most frequently asked** |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number for flow | dimensionless | Determines flow regime |
| 3 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter equation (heating fluid) | dimensionless | Heat transfer inside tubes |
| 4 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | $W/m^2 \cdot K$ | Boosted by agitation |
| 5 | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Newton's Law of Cooling | $W$ | Rate of heat removal |
| 6 | $F_{buoy} = (\rho_l - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot V_{bubble}$ | Buoyancy force on bubble | $N$ | Drives mixing |
| 7 | $P_{comp} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_a \cdot p_1 \cdot \ln(p_2/p_1)}{\eta_{isentropic}}$ | Air compressor power | $kW$ | Auxiliary load estimation |
| 8 | $STR = \dfrac{T_{top} - T_{bottom}}{T_{inlet} - T_{outlet}}$ | Stratification Number (lower = better mixing) | dimensionless | Agitation effectiveness |
| 9 | $T_{mean} = \dfrac{\int T \, dV}{V_{total}}$ | Mean fluid temperature | $K$ or $°C$ | Uniform cooling indicator |
| 10 | $v_{bubble} = \dfrac{g \cdot d^2 (\rho_l - \rho_a)}{18 \mu_l}$ | Stokes' Law bubble rise velocity | $m/s$ | Determines mixing intensity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key**: $Q_a$ = air quantity required, $Q_R$ = total refrigeration load, $\rho_l$ = liquid density, $\rho_a$ = air density, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $D$ = tube diameter, $\Delta T$ = temperature difference, $STR$ = Stratification number.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Air agitation is extensively used in:
- **Dairy Industry**: Milk chilling tanks require rapid and uniform cooling to **inhibit bacterial growth** (psychrotrophic spoilage organisms thrive above 4°C).
- **Cold Storage Facilities**: Brine tanks for indirect refrigeration use air agitation to **maintain uniform brine temperature** for slab cooling.
- **Ice Bank Tank (IBT) Coolers**: Critical for **milk cooling at village collection centers** (AMCU – Automatic Milk Collection Units).
- **Fishing Industry**: Brine immersion freezing of fish requires uniform $-5°C$ to $-10°C$ brine — achieved via air agitation.
- **Hydro-Cooling**: Pre-cooling of fruits and vegetables (e.g., mangoes, sweet corn) to **remove field heat** before refrigerated transport.

### 2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages Matrix

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Simple, low-cost installation | Adds **auxiliary compressor load** (energy penalty) |
| No moving mechanical parts in tank | Air bubbles can **entrain moisture** in refrigerant |
| Excellent for low-viscosity liquids | Noise from bubbling (85–95 dB range) |
| Prevents ice build-up on coils | Requires oil-free, dry air supply |
| Maintains uniform temperature distribution | Not suitable for **viscous fluids** (oils, glycerol) |
| Easy maintenance & scaling | Air must be vented, not trapped |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Transfer Enhancement by Air Agitation

**Problem Setup:** A refrigeration coil immersed in a tank of water has a surface area $A$ and a surface temperature $T_s$. Without agitation, the water is at rest, and heat transfer is by **free (natural) convection**. With air agitation, the water moves with an effective velocity $v$, and heat transfer is by **forced convection**.

We need to derive the **enhancement factor** — the ratio of heat transfer with agitation to that without.

---

**Step 1: Free Convection Baseline (No Agitation)**

For free convection on a horizontal surface in a fluid, the **Nusselt Number correlation** is given by the **McAdams correlation**:

$$Nu_{nat} = 0.53 \cdot (Gr \cdot Pr)^{0.25}$$

where $Gr$ is the Grashof number and $Pr$ is the Prandtl number. Therefore, the natural convection heat transfer coefficient is:

$$h_{nat} = \frac{Nu_{nat} \cdot k}{D} = \frac{0.53 \cdot k}{D} \cdot (Gr \cdot Pr)^{0.25}$$

The heat transfer rate without agitation is:

$$Q_{nat} = h_{nat} \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_{fluid})$$

---

**Step 2: Forced Convection with Agitation**

When air agitation drives the fluid into motion, the velocity of fluid near the coil surface increases. We approximate the agitation-induced flow as having an **effective bulk velocity** $v_{eff}$.

The flow is now characterized by a **Reynolds Number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho_l \cdot v_{eff} \cdot D}{\mu_l}$$

For turbulent flow ($Re > 10{,}000$), we apply the **Dittus-Boelter correlation** (heating of fluid):

$$Nu_{forced} = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$$

The forced convection heat transfer coefficient becomes:

$$h_{forced} = \frac{Nu_{forced} \cdot k}{D} = \frac{0.023 \cdot k}{D} \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$$

The heat transfer rate with agitation is:

$$Q_{forced} = h_{forced} \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_{fluid})$$

---

**Step 3: Enhancement Factor Derivation**

We define the **Enhancement Factor** $E_f$ as the ratio:

$$E_f = \frac{Q_{forced}}{Q_{nat}} = \frac{h_{forced}}{h_{nat}}$$

Substituting both expressions:

$$E_f = \frac{0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}}{0.53 \cdot (Gr \cdot Pr)^{0.25}}$$

Using $Pr$ to its respective power and combining:

$$E_f = \frac{0.023}{0.53} \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4 - 0.25} \cdot Gr^{-0.25}$$

$$E_f = 0.0434 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.15} \cdot Gr^{-0.25}$$

Rewriting using $\frac{Gr}{Re^2} = \frac{1}{Fr^2}$ (Froude Number squared) simplification:

$$E_f = 0.0434 \cdot Re^{0.8 - 0.5} \cdot Pr^{0.15}$$

$$\boxed{E_f \approx 0.0434 \cdot Re^{0.3} \cdot Pr^{0.15}}$$

---

**Step 4: Physical Interpretation**

- The enhancement factor is **proportional to $Re^{0.3}$** — a small increase in Reynolds number (i.e., better agitation) gives a noticeable boost in heat transfer.
- For typical air-agitated systems, $E_f$ ranges between **1.5 and 4.0**, meaning heat transfer is **enhanced by 50% to 300%**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- An ice bank tank has a refrigeration load of $Q_R = 20{,}000$ kcal/h.
- Empirical air quantity coefficient: $0.006$ m³/min per 1000 kcal/h.
- Air is supplied at gauge pressure $p_g = 0.5$ bar, atmospheric pressure $p_{atm} = 1.013$ bar.
- Air compressor isentropic efficiency $\eta_{isen} = 0.75$.

**Find:**
(a) Volume of air required per minute.
(b) Mass flow rate of air.
(c) Theoretical compressor power required.

---

**Solution (a):** Air volume rate:

$$\dot{V}_a = 0.006 \times \frac{Q_R}{1000}$$

$$\dot{V}_a = 0.006 \times \frac{20{,}000}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_a = 0.12 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 7.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

---

**Solution (b):** Mass flow rate of air.

Absolute pressure at compressor inlet:

$$p_1 = p_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar} = 101{,}300 \text{ Pa}$$

Absolute pressure at compressor outlet:

$$p_2 = p_{atm} + p_g = 1.013 + 0.5 = 1.513 \text{ bar} = 151{,}300 \text{ Pa}$$

Using ideal gas equation $pV = mRT$ for air (R = 287 J/kg·K, T = 300 K):

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{p_1 \cdot \dot{V}_a}{R \cdot T_1}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{101{,}300 \times (0.12/60)}{287 \times 300}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{101{,}300 \times 0.002}{86{,}100}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_a = 0.00235 \text{ kg/s} = 8.47 \text{ kg/h}}$$

---

**Solution (c):** Theoretical compressor power.

Pressure ratio:

$$r_p = \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{1.513}{1.013} = 1.4935$$

For isentropic compression of air ($\gamma = 1.4$):

$$W_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ \left( \frac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

$$W_{isen} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 287 \times 300 \times \left[ (1.4935)^{0.2857} - 1 \right]$$

$$W_{isen} = 3.5 \times 287 \times 300 \times [1.123 - 1]$$

$$W_{isen} = 3.5 \times 86{,}100 \times 0.123$$

$$W_{isen} = 37{,}066 \text{ J/kg}$$

Actual compressor power:

$$P_{actual} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot W_{isen}}{\eta_{isen}}$$

$$P_{actual} = \frac{0.00235 \times 37{,}066}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 116.1 \text{ W} \approx 0.116 \text{ kW}}$$

This is a small auxiliary load, typically absorbed in the total plant power consumption of the ice bank cooler.

### 3.3 Air Quantity Estimator — Quick Reference Table

| Refrigeration Load (kcal/h) | Air Quantity (m³/min) using 0.006 coefficient |
|---:|---:|
| 5,000 | 0.030 |
| 10,000 | 0.060 |
| 20,000 | 0.120 |
| 50,000 | 0.300 |
| 1,00,000 | 0.600 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Air Agitation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Power Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Air Compressor Unit]
    B --> C[Pressure Regulator 0.5 to 1.5 bar]
    C --> D[Air Filter and Drier Unit]
    D --> E[Distribution Manifold Header]
    E --> F1[Perforated Pipe Section 1]
    E --> F2[Perforated Pipe Section 2]
    E --> F3[Perforated Pipe Section 3]
    F1 --> G1[Bubble Plume Zone 1]
    F2 --> G2[Bubble Plume Zone 2]
    F3 --> G3[Bubble Plume Zone 3]
    G1 --> H[Liquid Refrigerant Tank Brine or Water]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Evaporator Coil Heat Exchanger]
    I --> J[Refrigeration Circuit Primary Loop]
    J --> K[Compressor Condenser Expansion Valve]
    K --> I
    H --> L[Vent and Air Release Port]
    L --> M[Atmosphere or Recirculation]
    H --> N[Food Product Inlet Chilled Liquid Contact]
    N --> O[Cold Storage or Process Output]
```

### 4.2 System Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUPPLY[Air Supply Side]
        A1[Compressor] --> A2[Filter] --> A3[Drier] --> A4[Pressure Gauge]
    end
    SUPPLY --> DIST[Distribution Network]
    subgraph TANK[Liquid Tank with Evaporator Coil]
        DIST --> T1[Bottom Distributor Pipes]
        T1 --> T2[Rising Bubble Column]
        T2 --> T3[Turbulent Mixing Zone]
        T3 --> T4[Surface Agitation]
        EVAP[Evaporator Coil Immersed] -.-> T3
    end
    TANK --> OUTLET[Chilled Liquid Outlet to Food Process]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Air Agitation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[System Start] --> P1[Compressor ON]
    P1 --> P2{Air Pressure OK?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Air Routed to Filter and Drier]
    P2 -- No --> P4[Pressure Alarm]
    P4 --> P1
    P3 --> P5[Air Distributed via Perforated Pipes]
    P5 --> P6[Bubbles Rise Through Liquid]
    P6 --> P7[Buoyancy Driven Mixing]
    P7 --> P8[Temperature Uniformity Achieved]
    P8 --> P9[Heat Transfer Enhanced]
    P9 --> P10[Liquid Temperature Reaches Setpoint]
    P10 --> P11[Refrigeration Load Met]
    P11 --> END[Continuous Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - **Block 1 (Air Supply Side)**: Houses the compressor, filter, drier, and pressure gauge. The filter removes particulates, while the drier removes moisture to prevent frost formation in the refrigerant.
> - **Block 2 (Liquid Tank)**: The perforated distributor pipes are placed at the **bottom** to leverage buoyancy. The evaporator coil is immersed in the liquid.
> - **Block 3 (Output)**: Chilled liquid circulates to the food processing line, with continuous agitation maintaining a uniform temperature throughout the tank.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define air agitation in a refrigeration system. State the empirical rule for air quantity required per 1000 kcal/h of refrigeration load.*

**Model Answer:**
Air agitation is the process of bubbling compressed air through a liquid secondary refrigerant (like brine, water, or glycol) in a refrigeration tank to maintain uniform temperature distribution, prevent thermal stratification, and enhance the convective heat transfer coefficient at the evaporator coil surface.

> **Empirical Rule:** The air quantity required is **0.004 to 0.008 m³/min per 1000 kcal/h of refrigeration load**, with a typical design value of **0.006 m³/min per 1000 kcal/h**.

*(Self-Assessment: Mention both the range AND the typical design value to score full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model Question]**
*List any three advantages and two disadvantages of air agitation in ice bank tanks used for milk cooling.*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Simple, low-cost installation with no moving mechanical parts inside the tank.
2. Prevents ice build-up on evaporator coil surfaces, ensuring long equipment life.
3. Provides uniform temperature throughout the tank, ensuring consistent milk quality.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Adds auxiliary compressor load to the plant power consumption (energy penalty).
2. Requires clean, oil-free, dry air supply — increasing system complexity.

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write only "0.006 m³/min" without specifying **"per 1000 kcal/h"** — this loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert the gauge pressure to **absolute pressure** when applying the ideal gas equation — this loses 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not mentioning the **typical pressure range (0.5–1.5 bar gauge)** in descriptive answers — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing air agitation with **mechanical/stirrer agitation** — these are fundamentally different. Stick to the definition involving **compressed air bubbling**.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not stating the **units clearly** in numerical problems (mixing kcal/h with kW) — costs 1–2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the working principle of air agitation in an ice bank tank cooler used in the dairy industry. Illustrate the role of buoyancy in fluid mixing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A milk chilling plant has a refrigeration load of 30,000 kcal/h. Calculate:
   (i) The volume of air required per minute, using a design coefficient of 0.006 m³/min per 1000 kcal/h.
   (ii) The mass flow rate of air at standard conditions (1.013 bar, 300 K).
   (iii) The theoretical isentropic compressor power, assuming air is compressed to 1.6 bar absolute with isentropic efficiency of 0.7. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

Air agitation in an ice bank tank cooler operates on the principle of **buoyancy-driven fluid mixing**. The system consists of the following components working in sequence:

1. **Air Compressor**: A low-pressure oil-free compressor supplies dry air at 0.5–1.5 bar gauge pressure.

2. **Distribution Network**: The air is routed through perforated copper or PVC pipes placed near the bottom of the tank. Each orifice (2–5 mm) acts as a bubble release point.

3. **Bubble Plume Formation**: Compressed air escapes as discrete bubbles that rise vertically through the liquid. As each bubble rises, it exerts an **upward drag force** on the surrounding liquid.

4. **Role of Buoyancy**: According to **Archimedes' Principle**, the bubble experiences a net upward force equal to the weight of liquid displaced:

$$F_{buoy} = (\rho_l - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot V_{bubble}$$

Since $\rho_l \gg \rho_a$ (water ≈ 1000 kg/m³, air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³), the buoyancy force is large and positive, driving rapid bubble ascent. This rising motion **entrains** cold bottom liquid upward, while warmer surface liquid flows down to replace it, creating a **bulk recirculation pattern**.

5. **Turbulent Mixing**: The bubble plume generates a turbulent mixing zone that destroys thermal stratification. The fluid velocity near the evaporator coil increases, raising the Reynolds number and thus the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$.

6. **Result**: The dairy plant achieves **uniform milk cooling** at 4°C within a short time, inhibiting psychrotrophic bacterial growth and extending shelf life.

---

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**(i) Air Volume Rate:**

$$\dot{V}_a = 0.006 \times \frac{Q_R}{1000}$$

$$\dot{V}_a = 0.006 \times \frac{30{,}000}{1000} = 0.18 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_a = 0.18 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 10.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

---

**(ii) Mass Flow Rate of Air:**

Using ideal gas equation: $pV = mRT$, hence $m = \frac{pV}{RT}$

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{p_1 \cdot \dot{V}_a}{R \cdot T_1}$$

Convert volume to seconds: $\dot{V}_a = 0.18/60 = 0.003$ m³/s

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{101{,}300 \times 0.003}{287 \times 300}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{303.9}{86{,}100}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_a = 0.00353 \text{ kg/s} = 12.71 \text{ kg/h}}$$

---

**(iii) Isentropic Compressor Power:**

Pressure ratio:

$$r_p = \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{1.6}{1.013} = 1.580$$

For air ($\gamma = 1.4$):

$$W_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

$$W_{isen} = 3.5 \times 287 \times 300 \times \left[ (1.580)^{0.2857} - 1 \right]$$

$$W_{isen} = 3.5 \times 86{,}100 \times [1.1434 - 1]$$

$$W_{isen} = 3.5 \times 86{,}100 \times 0.1434$$

$$W_{isen} = 43{,}222 \text{ J/kg}$$

Actual power:

$$P_{actual} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot W_{isen}}{\eta_{isen}}$$

$$P_{actual} = \frac{0.00353 \times 43{,}222}{0.7}$$

$$\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 217.9 \text{ W} \approx 0.218 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of air agitation in **ice bank coolers (IBCs)** used in rural milk collection centers. Compare air agitation with **mechanical agitation** in terms of cost, maintenance, and heat transfer efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ice bank cooler has a coil surface area of $A = 4 \text{ m}^2$, surface temperature $T_s = -5°C$, and a mean fluid temperature of $4°C$. The natural convection coefficient is $h_{nat} = 200 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot K$, and the forced convection coefficient with agitation is $h_{forced} = 600 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot K$. Calculate:
   (i) Heat transfer rate without agitation.
   (ii) Heat transfer rate with agitation.
   (iii) The percentage enhancement in heat transfer. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

In rural milk collection centers (especially in India's Operation Flood network), **Ice Bank Coolers (IBCs)** are used to rapidly chill raw milk from 35°C to 4°C within the critical **golden 3-hour window** post-milking.

**Role of Air Agitation in IBCs:**
- Maintains **uniform 4°C** throughout the ice-cold water bath surrounding the milk cans.
- Prevents **thermal stratification** that would otherwise leave pockets of warm water at the top.
- Enhances the **rate of ice formation** on the evaporator coil, allowing larger ice reserves for daytime cooling.
- Eliminates need for moving mechanical parts in the tank — ideal for **remote, low-maintenance rural deployment**.

**Comparison Table — Air Agitation vs. Mechanical Agitation:**

| Parameter | Air Agitation | Mechanical Agitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Cost** | **Low** (compressor + piping) | **High** (motor, gearbox, impeller) |
| **Maintenance** | Minimal (no submerged moving parts) | High (bearings, seals, impeller wear) |
| **Heat Transfer Efficiency** | Moderate (relies on bubble plumes) | High (direct impeller-driven flow) |
| **Energy Consumption** | 0.1–0.3 kW auxiliary | 0.5–2 kW continuous motor load |
| **Hygiene** | Excellent (no crevices for bacteria) | Risk of biofilm on impeller |
| **Noise** | Moderate (bubble + compressor noise) | Low (modern variable-speed drives) |
| **Suitability for Viscous Fluids** | Poor (bubbles can't drag viscous fluid) | Good (impeller can shear thick fluids) |
| **Application in Food Industry** | **Preferred** for milk, brine, water | Used in sauces, syrups, dough |

**Conclusion**: For dairy and cold storage applications in the food industry, **air agitation is preferred** due to its low cost, hygiene, and ease of maintenance.

---

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- $A = 4 \text{ m}^2$
- $T_s = -5°C$
- $T_{fluid} = 4°C$
- $\Delta T = T_s - T_{fluid} = -5 - 4 = -9°C$ (magnitude = 9 K)
- $h_{nat} = 200 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot K$
- $h_{forced} = 600 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot K$

---

**(i) Heat Transfer Rate Without Agitation:**

$$Q_{nat} = h_{nat} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{nat} = 200 \times 4 \times 9$$

$$\boxed{Q_{nat} = 7200 \text{ W} = 7.2 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**(ii) Heat Transfer Rate With Agitation:**

$$Q_{forced} = h_{forced} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{forced} = 600 \times 4 \times 9$$

$$\boxed{Q_{forced} = 21{,}600 \text{ W} = 21.6 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**(iii) Percentage Enhancement:**

$$\% \text{Enhancement} = \frac{Q_{forced} - Q_{nat}}{Q_{nat}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Enhancement} = \frac{21{,}600 - 7{,}200}{7{,}200} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Enhancement} = \frac{14{,}400}{7{,}200} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{Enhancement} = 200\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Air agitation **triples** the heat transfer rate in this scenario, reducing the milk cooling time by approximately **2/3** — a critical advantage in dairy processing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Agitation (KTU Module 2)**

- ✅ **Definition**: Air agitation = bubbling compressed air through liquid refrigerant to mix it and enhance heat transfer.
- ✅ **Empirical Air Quantity**: **0.004 to 0.008 m³/min per 1000 kcal/h** (typical design: **0.006**).
- ✅ **Air Pressure Range**: **0.5 to 1.5 bar gauge**.
- ✅ **Orifice Size**: **2–5 mm diameter** in distributor pipes.
- ✅ **Distributor Pipe Location**: **At the bottom** of the tank, just below or beside the evaporator coil.
- ✅ **Working Principle**: Buoyancy-driven bubble plumes cause bulk fluid recirculation, eliminating thermal stratification.
- ✅ **Key Advantage**: **Prevents ice build-up** on evaporator coil surfaces.
- ✅ **Major Application**: **Ice Bank Coolers (IBCs)** in dairy industry for rapid milk chilling.
- ✅ **Enhancement Factor**: $E_f \approx 0.0434 \cdot Re^{0.3} \cdot Pr^{0.15}$ (ranges 1.5–4.0).
- ✅ **Auxiliary Power**: Typically **0.1–0.3 kW** for industrial ice bank tanks.
- ✅ **Air Quality Requirement**: Must be **oil-free and dry** (use filter + desiccant drier).
- ✅ **Buoyancy Force Formula**: $F_{buoy} = (\rho_l - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot V_{bubble}$.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law for Bubble Velocity**: $v_{bubble} = \dfrac{g \cdot d^2 (\rho_l - \rho_a)}{18 \mu_l}$.
- ✅ **Compressor Power Formula**: $P = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma RT_1}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] / \eta$.
- ✅ **Gas Constant for Air**: R = 287 J/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$.
- ✅ **Conversion to Absolute Pressure**: Always add atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar) to gauge pressure readings.
- ✅ **Disadvantage**: **Energy penalty** due to auxiliary air compressor load.
- ✅ **Not Suitable For**: **Viscous fluids** (e.g., oils, syrups) — mechanical agitation preferred there.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality of Ice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality of Ice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — QUALITY OF ICE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Refrigeration and Air Conditioning in the Food Industry**, the **Quality of Ice** refers to the sum total of the physical, thermal, chemical, and microbiological attributes that determine the suitability of ice for a specific food preservation, processing, or transportation application. It is a **multi-parameter index** governed by:

> **Water Purity (TDS, Salinity, Hardness)**,
> **Crystal Structure (Nucleation Rate, Grain Size, Homogeneity)**,
> **Thermal State (Subcooling, Enthalpy, Latent Heat Content)**,
> **Geometric Form (Block, Flake, Tube, Plate, Slurry)**,
> and **Hygienic Load (Microbial Count, Odour, Colour)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Quality of Ice is the quantitative and qualitative measure of purity, crystal uniformity, density, temperature, thermal conductivity, and hygienic safety of frozen water used as a primary or secondary refrigerant in food cold-chain operations.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why does ice vary in quality?"

Imagine you are filling a tray with water and putting it in your home freezer versus an industrial plate freezer in a seafood plant. Both produce ice, but they are **fundamentally different materials**.

* The **home-freezer cube** is cloudy, milky-white, has trapped air bubbles, irregular crystals, and melts unevenly — **Low Quality Ice**.
* The **industrial plate ice** is crystal-clear, dense, uniformly subcooled to **–5 °C**, melts slowly, and carries **zero microbial load** — **High Quality Ice**.

Why the difference? Three reasons:
1. **Freezing Rate:** Slow freezing (home) → large, irregular crystals with trapped air. Fast freezing (industrial) → small, uniform crystals with no air.
2. **Water Source:** Tap water (chlorinated, mineralised) vs. demineralised/UV-treated water.
3. **Subsequent Handling:** Open-air contact vs. sealed hygienic production lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Quality Equation" in plain English:**
> **Good Ice = Pure Water + Controlled Fast Freezing + Clean Handling + Correct Geometry for the Food Application.**

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of pure ice at 0 °C | **917** | kg/m³ |
| Latent Heat of Fusion (L_f) | **333.55** | kJ/kg |
| Specific Heat of Ice (c_ice) | **2.10** | kJ/kg·K |
| Thermal Conductivity of Ice (k) | **2.22** | W/m·K |
| Specific Heat of Water (c_w) | **4.186** | kJ/kg·K |
| Melting Point of Pure Ice | **0** | °C (273.15 K) |
| Freezing Point Depression (ΔT per mol/kg solute) | **1.86** | K·kg/mol |

These constants are **indispensable** for any KTU numerical on ice-cooling load.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Ice Quality

KTU examinations frequently test the **six governing parameters** that define ice quality. Each is explained below in structured, exam-ready language.

---

#### **Pillar 1 — Purity (Chemical Quality)**

* **Definition:** Concentration of dissolved salts, minerals, chlorides, and organic matter in the ice.
* **Why it matters:** Salts lower the freezing point (colligative property) → ice melts prematurely, bacteria survive longer, and food surfaces get contaminated.
* **Acceptable Limit:** **TDS < 10 ppm** for food-grade ice; potable water standard.
* **Measurement:** Electrical conductivity (μS/cm); lower conductivity = higher purity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Freezing Point Depression (Colligative Relation):**
> ΔT_f = K_f · m
> where K_f = 1.86 K·kg/mol for water, m = molality of solute.

---

#### **Pillar 2 — Crystal Structure (Physical Quality)**

* **Slow Freezing** (rate < 1 cm/hr): Large, dendritic crystals → cloudy, brittle ice, poor cooling efficiency.
* **Fast Freezing** (rate > 5 cm/hr): Small, equiaxed crystals → transparent, dense, mechanically strong ice.
* **Quality Indicator:** **Crystal size < 50 μm** is the industrial benchmark for high-grade ice.

---

#### **Pillar 3 — Thermal State (Enthalpic Quality)**

* Ice is rarely at exactly 0 °C in practice. It is **subcooled** (typically between –2 °C and –10 °C).
* Subcooled ice stores **extra sensible heat capacity**, meaning it can absorb more heat from the food before melting.
* **Sensible heat absorbed per kg of subcooled ice:**
> Q_s = m · c_ice · (0 – T_ice) = m · 2.10 · |T_ice|

* **Total cooling capacity per kg of ice:**
> Q_total = m · [L_f + c_ice · (0 – T_ice)]

---

#### **Pillar 4 — Geometric Form (Application-Driven Quality)**

| Ice Type | Geometry | Typical Food Application | Key Quality Attribute |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Block Ice** | Large 12–150 kg blocks | Long-distance fish transport | High latent heat, slow melt |
| **Flake Ice** | Irregular 1–3 mm flakes | Fish display, vegetable hydrocooling | High surface area, conformable |
| **Tube Ice** | Hollow cylinders 29–50 mm OD | Beverage cooling, fish icing | Clear, hygienic, free-flowing |
| **Plate Ice** | Flat 6–12 mm plates | Bakery, meat processing | Uniform thickness, easy to dose |
| **Nugget Ice** | Compressed flakes 6–10 mm | Retail display | Dry, non-sticking |
| **Slurry Ice (Binary Ice)** | Ice crystals in pumpable fluid | Fish chilling at sea | Pumpable, 30–50% ice fraction |

---

#### **Pillar 5 — Hygienic / Microbiological Quality**

* Food-grade ice must meet **WHO drinking-water microbial standards**:
    * Total Plate Count < 100 CFU/mL
    * Zero *E. coli* and *Coliforms*
    * Zero *Salmonella* and *Listeria*
* Achieved via **UV sterilization, ozonation, or chlorination** of feed water and **closed-loop hygienic ice makers**.

---

#### **Pillar 6 — Mechanical / Physical Integrity**

* Hardness (resistance to fracture) and **friability** (tendency to crumble) determine how the ice survives handling, transport, and stacking.
* Measured by **Mohs hardness (ice ≈ 1.5)** and by standardised **drop tests**.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the spine of every KTU 14-mark numerical on ice quality.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Q = m · L_f** | Latent heat absorbed as ice melts | kJ |
| 2 | **Q = m · c_ice · ΔT** | Sensible heat absorbed by subcooled ice | kJ |
| 3 | **Q_total = m · (L_f + c_ice · ΔT)** | Total cooling capacity of subcooled ice | kJ |
| 4 | **ΔT_f = K_f · m_mol** | Freezing point depression due to impurities | K |
| 5 | **ṁ = h · A · (T_amb – T_melt) / L_f** | Melting rate of ice exposed to ambient | kg/s |
| 6 | **Q = U · A · LMTD** | Heat transfer through ice layer (Fourier) | W |
| 7 | **ρ_ice = 917 kg/m³** | Reference density of pure ice at 0 °C | kg/m³ |
| 8 | **Icing Ratio (R) = m_ice / m_fish** | Standard fish preservation ratio (R = 1 for fresh fish) | — |
| 9 | **Storage Life (days) ≈ (T_inside – T_amb) · R / k** | Empirical shelf-life via insulation | days |

> **Note on LMTD in Ice Cooling:**
> LMTD = (ΔT₁ – ΔT₂) / ln(ΔT₁/ΔT₂), used when fish/ambient temperatures differ at start vs. end of cooling.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Fish Cold Chain:** Calculating the **icing ratio (typically 1:1 by mass)** for sea-fresh fish to maintain 0 °C during 8–12 day transport.
* **Dairy Industry:** Slurry ice used to rapidly cool milk from 32 °C to 4 °C within 90 seconds.
* **Bakery:** Plate ice dosed into dough mixers for instant cooling without dilution.
* **Horticulture:** Flake ice for hydrocooling of freshly harvested peas, sweet corn, and broccoli.
* **Meat Processing:** Tube ice for carcass chilling to avoid surface freezing while removing metabolic heat.

> [!TIP]
> KTU favours **ice-cooling problems** where the student must balance the **latent load (melting)** and the **sensible load (subcooling & food-temperature drop)**, and then determine the **total mass of ice required** for a given batch of food.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Cooling Capacity of Subcooled Ice

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU 14-mark stem):**
A batch of freshly caught fish at **T_f = 25 °C** must be cooled to **0 °C** and maintained at that temperature using ice at **T_i = –5 °C**. The mass of fish is **M_f = 100 kg** with average specific heat **c_f = 3.5 kJ/kg·K**. Determine the **total mass of ice required** to bring the fish from 25 °C to 0 °C, assuming the ice is fully used (i.e., warmed from –5 °C to 0 °C and then melted).

---

**Step 1: Heat that must be removed from the fish (sensible cooling).**

$$Q_{fish} = M_f \cdot c_f \cdot (T_f - T_{final})$$

**Convert logic:** We multiply the mass of fish by its specific heat capacity and the temperature drop.

$$Q_{fish} = 100 \cdot 3.5 \cdot (25 - 0)$$

$$Q_{fish} = 100 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 25$$

$$Q_{fish} = 8750 \text{ kJ}$$

---

**Step 2: Heat that one kilogram of ice can absorb.**

**Stage A — Sensible heating of ice from –5 °C to 0 °C:**

$$q_{sensible} = 1 \cdot c_{ice} \cdot (0 - (-5)) = 1 \cdot 2.10 \cdot 5 = 10.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Stage B — Latent heat of melting at 0 °C:**

$$q_{latent} = L_f = 333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Total cooling per kg of ice:**

$$q_{ice} = q_{sensible} + q_{latent} = 10.5 + 333.55$$

$$q_{ice} = 344.05 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

---

**Step 3: Equate heat supplied by ice to heat absorbed by fish.**

$$M_{ice} \cdot q_{ice} = Q_{fish}$$

$$M_{ice} = \frac{Q_{fish}}{q_{ice}} = \frac{8750}{344.05}$$

$$M_{ice} \approx 25.43 \text{ kg}$$

---

**Step 4: Icing Ratio Verification.**

$$R = \frac{M_{ice}}{M_{fish}} = \frac{25.43}{100} \approx 0.254$$

Since this is **initial pull-down cooling** only (not multi-day storage), R < 1 is acceptable. For multi-day storage, R should be **1.0–1.5**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Melting Rate of Ice in a Cold Store (Fourier + Newton)

**Setup:** A block of ice of surface area **A = 0.5 m²** is stored in a room at **T_∞ = 20 °C** with a convective heat transfer coefficient **h = 10 W/m²·K**. The ice surface is at **0 °C**. Find the **time to melt 10 kg of ice**.

---

**Step 1: Heat transfer rate by convection (melting rate equation).**

$$\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_\infty - T_{surface})$$

**Convert logic:** Newton's law of cooling: heat flows from warm air to cold ice surface.

$$\dot{Q} = 10 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (20 - 0) = 100 \text{ W} = 0.1 \text{ kJ/s}$$

---

**Step 2: Mass melting rate.**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{L_f} = \frac{0.1 \text{ kJ/s}}{333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$\dot{m} \approx 2.998 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

---

**Step 3: Time to melt 10 kg.**

$$t = \frac{m}{\dot{m}} = \frac{10}{2.998 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t \approx 33355 \text{ s} \approx 9.27 \text{ hours}$$

---

**Step 4: Verify via alternative conduction route (ice-on-floor storage).**

If instead the ice sits on a cold plate at **T_p = –5 °C**, conduction through the ice layer of thickness **δ = 5 cm = 0.05 m** gives:

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_p - T_m)}{\delta} = \frac{2.22 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (-5 - 0)}{0.05}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = -111 \text{ W}$$

The negative sign confirms heat flows **upward** (from ice surface at 0 °C toward colder plate at –5 °C). The magnitude (111 W) is comparable to convection, validating the model.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting Ice Type for Food Application

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asks "Which ice type is best suited for X food application?"** — Use this matrix in the exam.

| Food Application | Recommended Ice Type | Justification | Quality Criterion Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Whole fish at sea (multi-day)** | Slurry Ice | Pumpable, full surface contact, no mechanical damage | Pillar 4 (Geometry) + Pillar 5 (Hygiene) |
| **Fish retail display** | Flake Ice | Moulds to fish shape, attractive display | Pillar 4 (Geometry) |
| **Meat carcasses (large mass)** | Block Ice or Tube Ice | Slow melt, sustained cooling, easy to load | Pillar 4 (Geometry) + Pillar 3 (Thermal State) |
| **Bakery dough cooling** | Plate Ice | Flat, clean, fast to dose in mixers | Pillar 5 (Hygiene) + Pillar 4 |
| **Vegetable hydrocooling** | Flake Ice + Chilled Water | Mixes into slurry, rapid uniform cooling | Pillar 1 (Purity — no chemical residue) |
| **Beverage industry** | Tube Ice | Clear, dry, free-flowing, no splinters | Pillar 1 (Purity) + Pillar 6 (Integrity) |
| **Dairy bulk chilling** | Slurry Ice | Pumpable, 90 s chill, no foaming | Pillar 3 (Thermal State) |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Icing Ratio for Multi-Day Fish Storage

**Problem (KTU 14-mark standard):**
5000 kg of freshly caught tuna must be transported from the harbour to an inland market over 4 days. The fish must be held at **0 °C** throughout. The ice available is at **–8 °C** with L_f = 333.55 kJ/kg and c_ice = 2.10 kJ/kg·K.
Ambient heat ingress rate into the insulated fish hold is **Q_leak = 50 kJ/kg_fish·day**.
Specific heat of fish, **c_f = 3.2 kJ/kg·K**; initial temperature **T_i = 28 °C**.

**Find:** (a) Initial pull-down ice mass. (b) Ice mass for 4-day storage. (c) Total ice mass & icing ratio.

---

**Step 1: Initial pull-down (sensible cooling of fish).**

$$Q_{pull-down} = M_f \cdot c_f \cdot (T_i - 0) = 5000 \cdot 3.2 \cdot 28 = 448{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

$$q_{ice,total} = c_{ice} \cdot 8 + L_f = 2.10 \cdot 8 + 333.55 = 16.8 + 333.55 = 350.35 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$M_{ice,1} = \frac{448{,}000}{350.35} \approx 1278.5 \text{ kg}$$

---

**Step 2: Storage load over 4 days (latent + ambient).**

$$Q_{storage} = Q_{leak} \cdot \text{days} \cdot M_f = 50 \cdot 4 \cdot 5000 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

(Note: latent cooling for fish is zero once at 0 °C; all heat load is ambient ingress.)

$$M_{ice,2} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{L_f} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{333.55} \approx 2997.9 \text{ kg}$$

(Using L_f since subcooling advantage is already spent on Stage 1.)

---

**Step 3: Total ice mass and icing ratio.**

$$M_{ice,total} = M_{ice,1} + M_{ice,2} = 1278.5 + 2997.9 \approx 4276.4 \text{ kg}$$

$$R = \frac{4276.4}{5000} \approx 0.855$$

**Interpretation:** For 4-day transport with a well-insulated hold, R ≈ 0.86 is realistic. For poorly insulated holds, R can rise to **1.0–1.5**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ice Quality Determinants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Source] --> B[Pre-Treatment]
    B --> B1[Filtration]
    B --> B2[UV Sterilization]
    B --> B3[Demineralization]
    B1 --> C[Ice Generator]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Freezing Process Parameters]
    D --> D1[Freezing Rate]
    D --> D2[Surface Temperature]
    D --> D3[Residence Time]
    D1 --> E[Ice Quality Output]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F1[Purity - TDS, Salinity]
    E --> F2[Crystal Structure - Size, Uniformity]
    E --> F3[Thermal State - Subcooling, Enthalpy]
    E --> F4[Geometry - Block, Flake, Tube, Plate, Slurry]
    E --> F5[Hygiene - Microbial Count]
    E --> F6[Mechanical Integrity - Hardness, Friability]
    F1 --> G[Food Application Suitability]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    G --> H1[Sea Fish - Slurry Ice]
    G --> H2[Meat - Block or Tube]
    G --> H3[Vegetable - Flake Slurry]
    G --> H4[Bakery - Plate Ice]
    G --> H5[Beverages - Tube Ice]
    G --> H6[Dairy - Slurry Ice]
```

**Caption:** The block diagram maps how upstream parameters (water, treatment, freezing process) cascade into six quality pillars (F1–F6) and ultimately determine which food industry application the ice is suitable for (H1–H6). A student can reference this in an exam to show a complete systems-level understanding.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow — Ice-Making & Quality Verification Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Water Intake] --> P2[Pre-Treatment Module]
    P2 --> P3[Refrigeration Cycle Evaporator]
    P3 --> P4[Ice Formation on Surface]
    P4 --> P5[Harvesting]
    P5 --> P6[Quality Inspection Station]
    P6 --> P6A[Purity Test - TDS Meter]
    P6 --> P6B[Thermal Test - Subcooling Probe]
    P6 --> P6C[Hygiene Test - Microbial Swab]
    P6 --> P6D[Geometry Check - Sieve Analysis]
    P6A --> P7{Quality Acceptable?}
    P6B --> P7
    P6C --> P7
    P6D --> P7
    P7 -->|Yes| P8[Storage Silo at -5 C to -10 C]
    P7 -->|No| P9[Reject - Recycle to Pre-Treatment]
    P8 --> P10[Dispatch to Food Industry]
```

**Caption:** Quality of ice is **not a downstream afterthought** — it is built into a **closed-loop verification system** where every batch is tested for the six pillars before dispatch.

---

### 4.3 Comparative Bar (Conceptual) — Latent + Sensible Heat vs. Ice Temperature

The relationship between **subcooled ice temperature** and **total cooling capacity** is a **linear function**:

$$q_{ice}(T_{ice}) = L_f + c_{ice} \cdot \vert T_{ice} \vert$$

| T_ice (°C) | q_ice (kJ/kg) | % Increase vs. 0 °C |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 333.55 | 0 % |
| –5 | 344.05 | 3.15 % |
| –10 | 354.55 | 6.30 % |
| –15 | 365.05 | 9.45 % |
| –20 | 375.55 | 12.6 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Dropping ice temperature from 0 °C to –10 °C increases cooling capacity by **6.3 %** — a significant margin in long-haul fish transport, but achieved at the cost of extra refrigeration energy at the ice plant.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define the term 'Quality of Ice' as used in food refrigeration. List any four parameters that determine it."**

**Model Answer:**
The *Quality of Ice* is the combined measure of the physical, chemical, thermal, and microbiological attributes of ice used as a refrigerant in food cold-chain applications.

Four determining parameters:
1. **Purity** — low TDS, low salinity.
2. **Crystal structure** — small, uniform grains.
3. **Thermal state** — subcooling and enthalpy content.
4. **Geometric form** — suitability of shape (block, flake, tube, plate, slurry) for the food application.

*(Additional valid parameters: hygienic/microbial count, mechanical integrity/hardness.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Why is slurry ice preferred over block ice for chilling freshly caught fish on board a fishing vessel?"**

**Model Answer:**
Slurry ice is preferred because:
1. It is **pumpable** — can be circulated through pipes and sprayed over the catch, ensuring uniform contact.
2. It has a **higher surface-area-to-volume ratio**, giving **faster heat transfer** and rapid cooling.
3. It **conforms to fish geometry**, avoiding mechanical bruising.
4. It maintains the fish at **0 °C without localised freezing damage**, preserving texture and freshness.
5. It can be made directly from **sea water** (no fresh-water requirement on long voyages).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six parameters that determine the quality of ice** used in the food industry. For each parameter, state its significance and the standard/specification benchmark. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fishery plant processes **2000 kg of fresh pomfret** arriving at **25 °C** daily. The fish must be cooled to **0 °C** using **flake ice at –6 °C**. The specific heat of fish is **c_f = 3.4 kJ/kg·K**. The latent heat of ice is **L_f = 334 kJ/kg** and the specific heat of ice is **c_ice = 2.1 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate:
  (i) The total cooling load (kJ) on the fish.
  (ii) The total mass of ice required.
  (iii) The icing ratio. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Six Parameters of Ice Quality — 7 Marks**

| # | Parameter | Significance | Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Purity** | Determines microbial and chemical safety; salts depress freezing point | TDS < 10 ppm |
| 2 | **Crystal Structure** | Small uniform crystals give transparent, dense, mechanically strong ice | Crystal size < 50 μm |
| 3 | **Thermal State** | Subcooled ice has higher enthalpy; gives more cooling per kg | T_ice between –5 and –10 °C |
| 4 | **Geometric Form** | Match between ice shape and food geometry maximises contact | Block / Flake / Tube / Plate / Slurry |
| 5 | **Hygienic Quality** | Zero pathogens; food-grade standards | TPC < 100 CFU/mL; zero coliforms |
| 6 | **Mechanical Integrity** | Resistance to fracture during handling and stacking | Drop test ≥ 95 % survival |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
M_f = 2000 kg, T_f = 25 °C, T_ice = –6 °C, c_f = 3.4 kJ/kg·K, L_f = 334 kJ/kg, c_ice = 2.1 kJ/kg·K.

**(i) Total cooling load on fish:**

$$Q_{fish} = M_f \cdot c_f \cdot (T_f - 0)$$

$$Q_{fish} = 2000 \cdot 3.4 \cdot 25 = 170{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Total mass of ice required:**

Cooling per kg of subcooled ice:

$$q_{ice} = c_{ice} \cdot \vert T_{ice} \vert + L_f = 2.1 \cdot 6 + 334 = 12.6 + 334 = 346.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Equating heat removed to heat absorbed:

$$M_{ice} = \frac{Q_{fish}}{q_{ice}} = \frac{170{,}000}{346.6} \approx 490.5 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Icing ratio:**

$$R = \frac{M_{ice}}{M_f} = \frac{490.5}{2000} \approx 0.245$$

Since this is the *initial pull-down only* (no multi-day storage), R ≈ 0.25 is acceptable. For long-term transport, additional ice for storage load would push R to 1.0–1.5.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare **block ice, flake ice, tube ice, plate ice, and slurry ice** with respect to their geometry, manufacturing method, and suitability for **fish, meat, vegetable, bakery, and dairy** applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **5-tonne batch of meat** enters a cold room at **T_i = 35 °C** and must be cooled to **T_f = 4 °C** within **6 hours**. The cold room uses **block ice at 0 °C** for emergency cooling. Calculate:
  (i) Total heat to be removed (kJ). Use c_meat = 3.1 kJ/kg·K.
  (ii) Mass of block ice required.
  (iii) Whether the cooling can be completed in 6 hours if the maximum melting rate of the available block ice is **0.04 kg/s**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Comparative Ice-Type Analysis — 7 Marks**

| Ice Type | Geometry | Mfg. Method | Best Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Block Ice** | 12–150 kg rectangular blocks | Slow freezing in cans, 12–48 hr | Meat (carcasses), long-haul fish |
| **Flake Ice** | 1–3 mm thin flakes | Scraped from refrigerated drum | Fish display, vegetable hydrocooling |
| **Tube Ice** | Hollow cylinders 29–50 mm OD | Frozen on inside of vertical tubes | Beverages, retail fish icing |
| **Plate Ice** | Flat 6–12 mm plates | Frozen on refrigerated plate evaporator | Bakery, meat portioning |
| **Slurry Ice** | Ice crystals in propylene-glycol/water | Ice crystals grown in fluid, pumpable | Sea-fish chilling, dairy bulk milk cooling |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given:** M_meat = 5000 kg, T_i = 35 °C, T_f = 4 °C, c_meat = 3.1 kJ/kg·K, T_ice = 0 °C, L_f = 334 kJ/kg.

**(i) Total heat to be removed:**

$$Q_{meat} = M \cdot c_{meat} \cdot (T_i - T_f) = 5000 \cdot 3.1 \cdot (35 - 4)$$

$$Q_{meat} = 5000 \cdot 3.1 \cdot 31 = 480{,}500 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Mass of block ice required (at 0 °C, no subcooling):**

$$M_{ice} = \frac{Q_{meat}}{L_f} = \frac{480{,}500}{334} \approx 1438.6 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Time feasibility check:**

At melting rate ṁ = 0.04 kg/s:

$$t = \frac{M_{ice}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{1438.6}{0.04} = 35{,}965 \text{ s} \approx 9.99 \text{ hours}$$

**Conclusion:** Since 9.99 hours > 6 hours target, **block ice alone is insufficient**. Either increase melting rate (e.g., use flake ice with higher surface area) or supplement with mechanical refrigeration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Traps**
> 1. **Forgetting subcooling term (c_ice · |T_ice|):** Many students use only L_f = 334 kJ/kg, losing 1–2 marks when ice is at –5 °C or –10 °C.
> 2. **Mixing units:** Ensure mass in kg (not tonnes) and time in seconds (not minutes) when computing ṁ.
> 3. **Confusing icing ratio with refrigeration load:** R is a *dimensionless ratio*, not a heat quantity.
> 4. **Ignoring the latent-heat-in-sensible-cooling trick:** The 6th term c_ice·ΔT is the "hidden" cooling capacity that the food industry relies on.
> 5. **Single-ice-type answers:** KTU expects *application-justified* ice selection. Saying "use ice" is incomplete — you must say "use *slurry* ice *because* it is pumpable and conforms to fish geometry."
> 6. **Neglecting freezing-point depression (ΔT_f = K_f · m):** Salty or impure ice melts at temperatures below 0 °C, which is critical for marine applications.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Rapid-Revision Checklist — Quality of Ice**

- **Definition:** Quality of Ice = purity + crystal structure + thermal state + geometry + hygiene + mechanical integrity.
- **Six pillars (P1–P6):** Purity, Crystal, Thermal, Geometry, Hygiene, Integrity.
- **Key constants to memorise:** ρ_ice = **917 kg/m³**, L_f = **333.55 kJ/kg**, c_ice = **2.10 kJ/kg·K**, k_ice = **2.22 W/m·K**.
- **Master equation:** $q_{ice} = L_f + c_{ice} \cdot \vert T_{ice} \vert$ (cooling capacity of subcooled ice).
- **Master equation:** $M_{ice} = \dfrac{Q_{load}}{q_{ice}}$ (mass of ice required).
- **Melting rate:** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L_f}$.
- **Icing Ratio (R):** R = 1 for fresh fish multi-day storage; R < 0.5 for pull-down only.
- **Ice-type → Application map (memorise):**
    * **Slurry →** sea-fish, dairy.
    * **Flake →** retail fish, vegetables.
    * **Block →** meat, long transport.
    * **Plate →** bakery.
    * **Tube →** beverages, retail fish.
- **Freezing-point depression:** ΔT_f = 1.86 × molality (K·kg/mol) — apply for salty or impure ice.
- **Hygiene standard:** TPC < 100 CFU/mL, zero coliforms (food-grade).
- **Fast vs. slow freezing:** > 5 cm/hr gives transparent, high-quality ice; < 1 cm/hr gives cloudy, low-quality ice.
- **Decision rule for examiner:** Always state *which* ice, *why* (justified by a quality pillar), and *how much* (with numerical proof if 14-mark).
- **Common trap:** Don't confuse "quality of ice" (in food industry sense — purity & suitability) with "quality" in pure thermodynamics (vapour/liquid mass fraction) — they are different concepts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cold Storage Design for Different Categories of Food Resource (Size and Shape)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cold Storage Design for Different Categories of Food Resource (Size and Shape)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Cold Storage Design** is the systematic engineering process of determining the optimal physical dimensions, thermal capacity, refrigeration load, airflow architecture, and stacking geometry required to preserve a specific category of food resource at a controlled low temperature. The design is intrinsically governed by the **intrinsic thermo-physical properties** of the food — most critically its **size**, **shape**, **density**, **specific heat**, **moisture content**, and **thermal conductivity**.

For the KTU 2024 scheme, "categories of food resource" refers to the classification of foods into families based on their biological origin, perishability kinetics, respiration behavior (for fresh horticultural produce), and post-harvest physiology. These categories include:

| Category Code | Food Family | Typical Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **F-I** | Fresh Fruits & Vegetables (Horticulture) | Apple, Mango, Tomato, Spinach |
| **F-II** | Frozen Foods | Peas, Meat blocks, Ice cream |
| **F-III** | Dairy & Eggs | Milk, Cheese, Butter, Eggs |
| **F-IV** | Meat, Poultry & Fisheries | Chicken, Mutton, Fish, Shrimp |
| **F-V** | Dry Goods & Grains | Wheat, Rice, Pulses, Spices |
| **F-VI** | Processed / Packaged Foods | Canned goods, Ready-to-eat meals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: The size and shape of the food product directly govern the **surface-area-to-volume ratio (A/V)**, which in turn dictates the rate of sensible heat removal, the boundary-layer airflow requirement, and the optimal stacking voidage fraction inside the cold room.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are trying to cool a cup of hot tea. Now imagine cooling **200 tiny marbles** of the same total volume of hot tea, spread out on a plate.

- The **single cup** has a small surface touching the air → cools slowly.
- The **200 marbles** have a massive combined surface touching the air → cools almost instantly.

This is exactly what happens in cold storage. A single large frozen meat carcass cools far more slowly than the same mass cut into small steaks. The **shape (geometry)** and **size (characteristic dimension)** of the food dictate how quickly the refrigeration system can pull heat out of the product. Designers must therefore match the **air throw pattern, duct placement, evaporator capacity, and stack height** to the geometry of the product family being stored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why size & shape matter:**
> 1. **Heat Transfer Rate** ∝ Surface Area (A) — smaller pieces release heat faster.
> 2. **Airflow Resistance** ∝ Shape (Drag coefficient) — angular shapes create turbulence.
> 3. **Storage Density** (kg/m³) ∝ Shape (Packing factor) — spheres pack loosely, cuboids pack tightly.
> 4. **Respiration Load** (for fresh produce) ∝ Mass — applies only to living tissues.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat of water**: **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Latent heat of fusion of water**: **333.4 kJ/kg**
- **Standard cold storage temperature for frozen foods**: **−18 °C to −25 °C**
- **Standard chilled storage temperature**: **0 °C to 4 °C**
- **Recommended relative humidity (RH)**: **85–95 %** for fresh produce, **50–60 %** for frozen goods
- **Recommended air velocity inside cold room**: **0.1 to 0.5 m/s**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The design of a cold storage facility for a heterogeneous food resource follows a structured decision hierarchy. Each step depends on the **physical geometry** of the food:

#### Step 1 — Food Categorization & Property Lookup
The first step is to identify the food category and pull its thermo-physical database values:
- Moisture content (**W**, decimal fraction, typically 0.60–0.90)
- Initial temperature (**T₁**)
- Final storage temperature (**T₂**)
- Freezing point (**T_f**, typically −0.5 °C to −2.5 °C)
- Specific heat above freezing (**c_u**)
- Specific heat below freezing (**c_f**)

#### Step 2 — Geometric Characterization
Each food category is approximated as a canonical geometric primitive:
- **Spherical** → eggs, fruits (apple, orange)
- **Cylindrical** → carrots, cucumbers, fish, sausages
- **Cuboidal / Rectangular** → meat carcasses, cheese blocks, packaged cartons
- **Irregular / Amorphous** → leafy greens, whole poultry, fish

The **equivalent spherical diameter (D_eq)** is computed for irregular shapes:

$$ D_{eq} = \left( \frac{6 \cdot V_p}{\pi} \right)^{1/3} $$

where **V_p** is the volume of a single product piece.

#### Step 3 — Surface Area to Volume (A/V) Ratio Determination
The A/V ratio is the **single most critical geometric parameter** for cold storage design:

$$ \left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{sphere} = \frac{6}{D} $$

$$ \left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{cube} = \frac{6}{a} $$

$$ \left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{cylinder} = \frac{2}{r} \left( 1 + \frac{r}{L} \right) $$

A **higher A/V ratio** → faster pre-cooling, higher instantaneous refrigeration load, and smaller individual package dimensions.

#### Step 4 — Storage Density & Stacking Geometry
The **bulk storage density (ρ_b)** depends on the packing factor (φ), which is itself a function of shape:

$$ \rho_b = \phi \cdot \rho_s $$

| Food Shape | Packing Factor (φ) | Typical ρ_b (kg/m³) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Spheres (random close packing) | 0.60 – 0.64 | 400 – 600 |
| Cuboids (aligned) | 0.85 – 0.95 | 700 – 900 |
| Cylinders (random) | 0.65 – 0.75 | 500 – 700 |
| Irregular leafy produce | 0.20 – 0.35 | 80 – 200 |

#### Step 5 — Airflow Architecture
Once size and shape are known, the duct/evaporator layout is chosen:
- **Small items** (berries, peas, diced fruit) → **high-velocity cross-flow** (0.3–0.5 m/s)
- **Large items** (carcasses, cheese wheels) → **low-velocity displacement flow** (0.1–0.2 m/s)

#### Step 6 — Cooling Load Calculation
The refrigeration load is calculated segment-wise using the food's moisture content and geometry-driven mass.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Applicable Food Category |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $c_u = 1.673 + 0.025 W$ | Specific heat above freezing | kJ/(kg·K) | All moist foods |
| 2 | $c_f = 1.549 + 0.008 W$ | Specific heat below freezing | kJ/(kg·K) | All moist foods |
| 3 | $Q_s = m \cdot c_u \cdot (T_1 - T_f)$ | Sensible heat (above freezing) | kJ | All categories |
| 4 | $Q_s' = m \cdot c_f \cdot (T_f - T_2)$ | Sensible heat (below freezing) | kJ | Frozen foods |
| 5 | $Q_L = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat of freezing | kJ | Frozen foods |
| 6 | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_{CO_2} \cdot h_{resp}$ | Respiration heat load | kW | Fresh produce only |
| 7 | $D_{eq} = (6 V_p / \pi)^{1/3}$ | Equivalent diameter | m | All irregular items |
| 8 | $(A/V) = 6 / D_{eq}$ | Surface area to volume ratio | m⁻¹ | All items |
| 9 | $\rho_b = \phi \cdot \rho_s$ | Bulk density | kg/m³ | All categories |
| 10 | $Nu = C \cdot Re^n \cdot Pr^{1/3}$ | Forced convection correlation | dimensionless | All |
| 11 | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Heat transfer rate | W | All categories |
| 12 | $q_{sensible} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ | Air-side load | W | All categories |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **W** in formulas (1) and (2) is the **decimal moisture content** (e.g., 0.85 for apple, NOT 85 %). Many KTU students lose marks by plugging in 85 instead of 0.85.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This design methodology is the foundation for:
- **IQF (Individually Quick Frozen) tunnels** — exploit high A/V of small items
- **Blast freezers** for fish and shrimp — use forced air at high velocity
- **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) cold rooms** — for apples, kiwifruit
- **Hydro-coolers and vacuum coolers** — for leafy vegetables
- **Plate freezers** — for irregular meat blocks

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Refrigeration Load for a Mixed Food Cold Room

**Problem Statement:** A cold storage is to be designed for **10,000 kg of fish** (W = 0.80, ρ_s = 1050 kg/m³) entering at **+10 °C** and to be stored at **−20 °C**. Fish is approximately cylindrical (L = 0.40 m, r = 0.025 m). Calculate the total cooling load per batch.

**Given:**
- Mass m = 10,000 kg
- W = 0.80
- T₁ = 10 °C
- T_f ≈ −1 °C (freezing point of fish)
- T₂ = −20 °C
- ρ_s = 1050 kg/m³
- L_f = 333.4 kJ/kg (approximated to water)
- c_u (above freezing) = 1.673 + 0.025(0.80) = **1.693 kJ/(kg·K)**
- c_f (below freezing) = 1.549 + 0.008(0.80) = **1.5554 kJ/(kg·K)**

**Step 1 — Sensible heat from T₁ to T_f (above freezing zone):**

$$ Q_{s1} = m \cdot c_u \cdot (T_1 - T_f) $$

$$ Q_{s1} = 10000 \cdot 1.693 \cdot (10 - (-1)) $$

$$ Q_{s1} = 10000 \cdot 1.693 \cdot 11 $$

$$ Q_{s1} = 186{,}230 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of freezing (phase change at T_f):**

$$ Q_L = m \cdot L_f $$

$$ Q_L = 10000 \cdot 333.4 $$

$$ Q_L = 3{,}334{,}000 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat from T_f to T₂ (below freezing zone):**

$$ Q_{s2} = m \cdot c_f \cdot (T_f - T_2) $$

$$ Q_{s2} = 10000 \cdot 1.5554 \cdot (-1 - (-20)) $$

$$ Q_{s2} = 10000 \cdot 1.5554 \cdot 19 $$

$$ Q_{s2} = 295{,}526 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 4 — Total refrigeration load:**

$$ Q_{total} = Q_{s1} + Q_L + Q_{s2} $$

$$ Q_{total} = 186{,}230 + 3{,}334{,}000 + 295{,}526 $$

$$ Q_{total} = 3{,}815{,}756 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 5 — Convert to refrigeration tonnes (1 TR = 13,860 kJ/day or 3.517 kW):**

$$ \dot{Q} = \frac{Q_{total}}{t \cdot 3600} $$

Assuming a pull-down time **t = 8 hours = 28,800 s**:

$$ \dot{Q} = \frac{3{,}815{,}756}{28800} $$

$$ \dot{Q} = 132.49 \text{ kW} $$

Converting to tonnes of refrigeration:

$$ \dot{Q}_{TR} = \frac{132.49}{3.517} = 37.67 \text{ TR} $$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Area to Volume Ratio for an Apple

**Given:** Apple approximated as sphere with diameter D = 0.075 m.

**Step 1 — Surface area of sphere:**

$$ A = \pi D^2 = \pi \cdot (0.075)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Volume of sphere:**

$$ V = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.075)^3}{6} = 2.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — A/V ratio:**

$$ \frac{A}{V} = \frac{0.01767}{2.21 \times 10^{-4}} = 79.96 \text{ m}^{-1} $$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with formula:**

$$ \frac{A}{V} = \frac{6}{D} = \frac{6}{0.075} = 80 \text{ m}^{-1} \quad (\text{Matches!}) $$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bulk Volume Required

**Problem:** Determine the cold room volume required to store 10,000 kg of fish using cylindrical geometry.

**Step 1 — Single fish volume:**

$$ V_p = \pi r^2 L = \pi \cdot (0.025)^2 \cdot 0.40 = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Number of fish:**

$$ N = \frac{m}{\rho_s \cdot V_p} = \frac{10000}{1050 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-4}} = 12{,}123 \text{ fish} $$

**Step 3 — Total solid volume:**

$$ V_{solid} = N \cdot V_p = 12123 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-4} = 9.52 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 4 — Bulk volume with packing factor φ = 0.65 (random cylindrical packing):**

$$ V_{bulk} = \frac{V_{solid}}{\phi} = \frac{9.52}{0.65} = 14.65 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 5 — Add circulation & aisle allowance (factor 1.6 for working cold rooms):**

$$ V_{room} = 1.6 \cdot V_{bulk} = 1.6 \cdot 14.65 = 23.44 \text{ m}^3 $$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Shape Effect on Design

| Parameter | Spherical (Eggs) | Cylindrical (Fish) | Cuboidal (Meat Blocks) | Irregular (Leafy Greens) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A/V ratio (1/m) | **80** | **108** | **24** | **150+** |
| Packing factor φ | 0.62 | 0.65 | 0.90 | 0.25 |
| Storage density ρ_b | 620 | 682 | 945 | 150 |
| Recommended air velocity (m/s) | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.15 | 0.40 |
| Cooling time relative | 1.0 × | 0.85 × | 1.8 × | 0.5 × |
| Stacking method | Trayed | Tiered racks | Block pallet | Crate/loose |

> [!IMPORTANT]
