# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*"List any four commonly used materials for the interior finish of a refrigerated truck and state one specific application where each is preferred."*

**Model Answer (Structured for 3 Marks):**

| Material | Preferred Application |
|---|---|
| **SS 304 stainless steel** | Fish, meat, dairy transport (chloride resistance and hygiene) |
| **Galvanized steel (GS)** | General-purpose frozen vegetable reefer trucks (cost-effective) |
| **Aluminum 5052** | Lightweight marine reefer containers (weight-critical) |
| **GRP (fibreglass)** | Curved marine reefer ship holds (seamless molded panels) |

*(Each material + application = 0.75 marks. Tabular presentation fetches full marks.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*"What is the function of a vapor barrier in a refrigerated enclosure wall? Why is it placed on both sides of the insulation?"*

**Model Answer:**

A **vapor barrier** is a layer of **impermeable material (aluminum foil 50 µm or polyethylene film 0.2 mm)** that prevents water vapor from migrating into the insulation core.

**Why both sides:**
- **Outer-side vapor barrier:** Stops ambient humid air (35 °C, 60 % RH) from migrating *inward* during off-cycle when the wall is warming.
- **Inner-side vapor barrier:** Stops moisture from any wash-down water or condensation inside the cold room from reaching the insulation from the cold side.

If moisture enters PUF insulation, its thermal conductivity rises from **0.022 W/m·K** to ~**0.045 W/m·K**, doubling the heat load and causing compressor failure within months.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "outer purpose", 1 mark for "inner purpose".)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — 14 Marks Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Every Part B question in Engineering Science / Professional Core courses carries **14 marks** with internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **layered construction of a refrigerated truck wall** from inside to outside. State the function of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage wall consists of the following layers (from inside to outside):

| Layer | Material | Thickness | k (W/m·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SS 304 inner liner | 1.0 mm | 16.2 |
| 2 | PUF insulation | 120 mm | 0.024 |
| 3 | GRP outer skin | 2.5 mm | 0.25 |

Inside air: **–18 °C**, Outside air: **+38 °C**. Internal surface resistance = **0.13 m²·K/W**, External surface resistance = **0.04 m²·K/W**. Calculate:
- (i) Total thermal resistance
- (ii) U-value
- (iii) Heat flux per m² of wall area
- (iv) Inner surface temperature

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Layered Wall Construction (7 Marks)**

> The wall sandwich (from inside to outside) consists of **five distinct layers**:

**Layer 1 — Interior Finish (SS 304 / Galvanized / Aluminum, 0.8–1.5 mm)**
Function: Provides **hygienic, food-grade, easy-to-clean** inner surface; resists corrosion; protects cargo from insulation exposure.

**Layer 2 — Inner Vapor Barrier (Aluminum foil 50 µm)**
Function: Prevents **moisture migration from inside the cold room** (during wash-down or defrost) into the PUF insulation.

**Layer 3 — Insulation Core (PUF, 100–150 mm)**
Function: Provides **>95 %** of total thermal resistance. Density 35–50 kg/m³, λ ≈ 0.022–0.024 W/m·K.

**Layer 4 — Outer Vapor Barrier (Aluminum foil 50 µm or PE film 0.2 mm)**
Function: Prevents **ambient humid air** from migrating inward into the insulation when the wall is in off-cycle (warming).

**Layer 5 — Outer Skin (GRP / Aluminum / Galvanized, 1.5–2.5 mm)**
Function: Provides **mechanical protection, weather resistance, structural rigidity**, and acts as the vapor barrier support.

*(Each layer with diagram = 1.4 marks. Total = 7 marks. Examiner expects a labelled cross-section drawing showing all 5 layers.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — List resistances (1 mark):**

$$R_{\text{si}} = 0.130 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{SS}} = \frac{0.001}{16.2} = 6.17 \times 10^{-5} \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{PUF}} = \frac{0.120}{0.024} = 5.000 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{GRP}} = \frac{0.0025}{0.25} = 0.010 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{se}} = 0.040 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance (1 mark):**

$$R_{\text{total}} = 0.130 + 6.17 \times 10^{-5} + 5.000 + 0.010 + 0.040 = 5.180 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

**Step 3 — U-value (1 mark):**

$$U = \frac{1}{5.180} = 0.1931 \ W/(m^{2}\cdot K)$$

**Step 4 — Heat flux (2 marks):**

$$\Delta T = 38 - (-18) = 56 \ K$$

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.1931 \times 56 = 10.81 \ W/m^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Inner surface temperature (2 marks):**

$$T_{\text{inner surface}} = T_{\text{inside}} + q \cdot R_{\text{si}}$$

$$T_{\text{inner surface}} = -18 + (10.81 \times 0.130) = -18 + 1.405 = -16.59 \ °C$$

**Final Answer Box:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $R_{\text{total}}$ | **5.180 m²·K/W** |
| $U$ | **0.1931 W/(m²·K)** |
| $q$ | **10.81 W/m²** |
| $T_{\text{inner surface}}$ | **–16.59 °C** |

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **floor construction of a refrigerated cold storage room**. Discuss the importance of floor slope, drain trap, and material selection for hygiene. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A marine reefer container has interior dimensions **5.9 m × 2.3 m × 2.5 m** (length × width × height). The wall, floor, and ceiling each consist of PUF insulation of thickness **80 mm** sandwiched between SS 304 liners (1.0 mm thick, k = 16.2 W/m·K) and GRP outer skin (2.0 mm, k = 0.25). Inside temperature is **–25 °C**, outside is **+30 °C**, surface resistances $R_{\text{si}} = 0.13$ and $R_{\text{se}} = 0.04 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$. Calculate:
- (i) The U-value of the wall
- (ii) Total heat load through walls + floor + ceiling
- (iii) Required refrigeration capacity in **TR (Tons of Refrigeration)** if safety factor = 1.15.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Cold Storage Floor Construction (7 Marks)**

The cold storage floor is a **multi-layered sandwich** designed for load-bearing, insulation, and hygiene:

**Layer 1 — Sub-base concrete** (150 mm, M20 grade) provides structural foundation.

**Layer 2 — Vapor barrier** (PE film 0.2 mm) prevents ground moisture from rising.

**Layer 3 — Insulation** (PUF or XPS, 100–150 mm) provides thermal cut-off from ground.

**Layer 4 — Screed layer** (50 mm cement-sand) creates uniform surface.

**Layer 5 — Floor finish** (SS chequer plate 1.5 mm or food-grade epoxy) provides durable, washable surface.

**Critical Design Requirements:**

| Requirement | Specification | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Floor slope | **1 in 50 toward drain** | Ensures complete water drainage |
| Drain trap | **DN 100 with water seal** | Prevents sewer gases + vermin entry |
| Trap seal type | **Heated or glycol-filled** | Prevents freezing of water seal |
| Surface finish | **SS chequer / food-grade epoxy** | Anti-slip + cleanable |
| Compressive strength | **≥ 3000 kg/m²** | Withstands pallet loads |
| Load capacity | **Forklift wheel 5000 N** | Dynamic load |

*(4 marks for layered construction, 3 marks for critical design requirements.)*

---

**(b) Marine Reefer Container Heat Load Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute U-value (2 marks):**

$$R_{\text{si}} = 0.130 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{SS}} = \frac{0.001}{16.2} = 6.17 \times 10^{-5} \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{PUF}} = \frac{0.080}{0.022} = 3.636 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{GRP}} = \frac{0.002}{0.25} = 0.008 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{se}} = 0.040 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = 0.130 + 6.17 \times 10^{-5} + 3.636 + 0.008 + 0.040 = 3.814 \ m^{2}\cdot K/W$$

$$U = \frac{1}{3.814} = 0.262 \ W/(m^{2}\cdot K)$$

**Step 2 — Compute total surface area (1 mark):**

Walls: $2 \times (5.9 \times 2.5) + 2 \times (2.3 \times 2.5) = 29.5 + 11.5 = 41.0 \ m^{2}$

Floor + Ceiling: $2 \times (5.9 \times 2.3) = 27.14 \ m^{2}$

$$A_{\text{total}} = 41.0 + 27.14 = 68.14 \ m^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total heat load (2 marks):**

$$\Delta T = 30 - (-25) = 55 \ K$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.262 \times 68.14 \times 55$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 982.0 \ W$$

**Step 4 — Apply safety factor and convert to TR (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{design}} = 982.0 \times 1.15 = 1129.3 \ W$$

Recall that **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3517 W**:

$$\text{Refrigeration Capacity} = \frac{1129.3}{3517} = 0.321 \ TR$$

$$\boxed{\text{Required Capacity} = 0.32 \ TR}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing interior finish with insulation** — These are **two different layers**. Interior finish is the *innermost visible* layer. Insulation is *sandwiched in between*. Writing "PUF is the interior finish" loses **2 marks immediately**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to add air film resistances ($R_{\text{si}}$ and $R_{\text{se}}$)** — Examiners specifically check for these. Skipping them loses **1 mark** but more critically shows incomplete understanding.
>
> 3. **Unit errors in heat load problems** — $q$ is in **W/m²**, $Q$ is in **W**, $TR$ is dimensionless but **1 TR = 3.517 kW**. Mixing these is the **#1 numerical error**.
>
> 4. **Missing vapor barrier explanation** — The vapor barrier is **NOT** the same as insulation. Many students skip mentioning it. Examiner allocates **1–2 marks** explicitly for "explain the role of vapor barrier".
>
> 5. **Not drawing the cross-section** — KTU Part B (a) carries 7 marks; **3 marks** are reserved for the **neat labelled diagram**. A solution without the wall sandwich sketch loses substantial marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting floor slope and drain** — These are **HACCP compliance items** and the examiner will specifically look for "1 in 50 slope" and "DN 100 trap with water seal".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

### Interior Finish Materials
- ☐ **SS 304** → fish, meat, dairy (chloride-safe, hygienic)
- ☐ **Galvanized steel** → dry goods, frozen vegetables (cost-effective)
- ☐ **Aluminum 5052** → marine, lightweight (corrosion-resistant)
- ☐ **GRP** → curved surfaces, ship holds (seamless)
- ☐ **Food-grade PVC** → modular cold rooms (cheap, clean)

### Wall Sandwich (5 Layers)
- ☐ **Inside:** SS / GS liner
- ☐ **Vapor barrier 1** (Al foil 50 µm)
- ☐ **PUF insulation** (100–150 mm, λ ≈ 0.022)
- ☐ **Vapor barrier 2** (Al foil or PE)
- ☐ **Outside:** GRP / Al outer skin

### Floor Construction
- ☐ **Slope 1 in 50** toward drain
- ☐ **Drain DN 100 minimum** with water seal trap
- ☐ **Compressive load ≥ 3000 kg/m²**
- ☐ **SS chequer plate** finish for hygiene zones
- ☐ **PUF insulation 100–150 mm** under floor

### Door Assembly
- ☐ **PUF core 80–100 mm**
- ☐ **Heater strip** around gasket (prevents ice)
- ☐ **Pressure relief vent** (large rooms)
- ☐ **Triple-lip silicone gasket**
- ☐ **Heater wattage 25 W/m** at 12 V or 24 V

### Air Distribution
- ☐ **Ceiling ducts** (T-bar or full-width chute)
- ☐ **Floor return grilles**
- ☐ **Air velocity 1.5–3.0 m/s**
- ☐ **Throw pattern** must reach all corners

### Critical Formulas to Memorize

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{R_{\text{si}} + \sum L_i/k_i + R_{\text{se}}}$$

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \quad [W]$$

$$T_{\text{inner surface}} = T_{\text{inside}} + q \cdot R_{\text{si}}$$

$$1 \ TR = 3.517 \ kW = 3517 \ W$$

### KTU Buzzwords to Use in Answers
- ☐ "Vapor barrier integrity"
- ☐ "Thermal bridge prevention"
- ☐ "HACCP compliance"
- ☐ "Food-grade SS 304 finish"
- ☐ "1 in 50 floor slope"
- ☐ "Hygienic drainage with water-seal trap"
- ☐ "Heater strip to prevent gasket freeze-up"
- ☐ "FSSAI / EU 853/2004 compliance"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Topic Notes — Interior Finish and Fitting.**
> This topic is **heavily diagram-dependent** in KTU exams. Practice drawing the **5-layer wall sandwich**, the **7-layer floor construction**, and the **door assembly** at least 5 times before the exam. Numerical problems will always involve the **U-value calculation** with a specific cargo or container scenario. Carry forward your understanding to **Module 3 (Refrigeration Cycles)** and **Module 4 (System Components)** where the heat loads calculated here become inputs to compressor selection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychrometry Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Psychrometry Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Psychrometry** (from Greek *psychros* = cold, *metron* = measure) is the branch of engineering thermodynamics that deals with the study of **thermodynamic properties of moist air** (a binary mixture of **dry air** and **water vapour**) treated as a single homogeneous gas. It establishes the quantitative relationship between the **dry-bulb temperature**, **moisture content**, **relative humidity**, **enthalpy**, and **specific volume** of air–vapour mixtures, especially in the temperature range relevant to food processing (**0 °C to 60 °C**).

In the context of food refrigeration and air-conditioning engineering, psychrometry provides the analytical framework for designing **controlled atmosphere storage, food dehydration systems, cold storage humidification, ripening chambers, and clean-room processing environments** where precise moisture control is mandatory to inhibit microbial growth, enzymatic browning, and lipid oxidation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim-aligned):**
> "Psychrometry deals with the measurement and behaviour of air–water vapour mixtures. It is the foundation for evaluating heating, cooling, humidification, dehumidification, and mixing processes encountered in food industry HVAC and cold chain engineering."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a sponge: a *dry* sponge can soak up a lot of water before it is "full." The **atmosphere is like a giant invisible sponge for water vapour** — it has a *holding capacity* that depends on its temperature. Hotter air is a *bigger* sponge (it can hold more water), and cooler air is a *smaller* sponge.

- The **water it is already holding** = humidity ratio, *W* (kg water / kg dry air).
- The **total water it can hold** at that temperature = *Ws* (saturation humidity ratio).
- The ratio *W/Ws* = **relative humidity** (how "full" the air is).
- If we keep cooling the sponge-air, eventually it overflows (condensation) — that temperature is the **dew point**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in food industry:** Microbial growth on bakery products, dried fruits, and meat is governed by *water activity (aw)*, which is directly related to the **equilibrium relative humidity (ERH)** of the surrounding air. Controlling RH via psychrometric processes is therefore a *food safety imperative*, not just a comfort parameter.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Memorability)

- **Specific gas constant for dry air**: *Ra* = **0.287 kJ/kg·K (287 J/kg·K)**
- **Specific gas constant for water vapour**: *Rv* = **0.4615 kJ/kg·K**
- **Molecular weight of dry air**: *Ma* = **28.97 kg/kg-mol**
- **Molecular weight of water vapour**: *Mv* = **18.02 kg/kg-mol**
- **Ratio of molecular weights** (Mv/Ma): **0.622** ← *(the most recurring constant in psychrometry)*
- **Specific heat of dry air (cp,a)**: **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- **Specific heat of water vapour (cp,v)**: **1.88 kJ/kg·K**
- **Latent heat of vaporisation at 0 °C**: **2500 kJ/kg**
- **Universal gas constant**: *Ru* = **8.314 kJ/kg-mol·K**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure**: *P* = **101.325 kPa** (≈ 1 atm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Industry Insight — Water Activity (aw):**
> Water activity *aw* ≈ ERH/100. Most spoilage bacteria require *aw* > 0.91, yeasts > 0.88, and moulds > 0.70. A psychrometric calculation of storage-room RH is the *first step* in HACCP-compliant storage design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Psychrometric Properties (The "Big Six")

Every air–vapour mixture in food industry HVAC can be fully characterised by **six interdependent properties**, conventionally read on the standard **psychrometric (Carrier) chart**:

1. **Dry-Bulb Temperature (DBT or *t*)** — The temperature measured by an ordinary mercury/bimetallic thermometer whose bulb is exposed to a stream of air not exposed to radiation. It is the *true* air temperature. **Units: °C or K.**

2. **Wet-Bulb Temperature (WBT or *tw*)** — The steady-state temperature reached when air flows over a water-wetted thermometer bulb. Heat lost by evaporation equals the sensible heat gained by the air. **WBT < DBT** unless air is saturated (100% RH), in which case WBT = DBT = DPT. **Units: °C.**

3. **Dew Point Temperature (DPT or *tdp*)** — The temperature at which the water vapour in the air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) and begins to condense, upon cooling at constant pressure. Beyond DPT, water would deposit on cold surfaces (frost on evaporator coils, condensate on chilled food surfaces). **Units: °C.**

4. **Humidity Ratio / Specific Humidity (*W*)** — The mass of water vapour associated with *unit mass of dry air*. **Units: kg water vapour / kg dry air.** This is the **most useful mass-basis property** for mass-balance psychrometric calculations.

5. **Relative Humidity (RH or φ)** — The ratio of the *actual* partial pressure of water vapour (*Pv*) to the *saturation* pressure of water vapour (*Pg*) at the same DBT. **Dimensionless (0 to 1, or 0% to 100%).**

6. **Specific Enthalpy (*h*)** — The total energy content of the moist air per kg of dry air, referenced to **0 °C for both dry air and water vapour**. **Units: kJ/kg dry air.**

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation of the Core Relations

#### Step 1: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures
For the air–vapour mixture behaving as an ideal gas at total pressure *P*:

$$P = P_a + P_v$$

where *Pa* = partial pressure of dry air, *Pv* = partial pressure of water vapour.

#### Step 2: Humidity Ratio (W) Derivation
Applying the ideal gas equation to each component on a molar basis, the mole fraction of vapour = *Pv/P*. Converting to mass ratio using molecular weights:

$$W = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \times \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$

> The factor **0.622** arises directly from *Mv/Ma = 18.02/28.97 ≈ 0.622* — this is the single most important dimensionless constant in psychrometry.

#### Step 3: Degree of Saturation (*μ*)
Defined as the ratio of the actual humidity ratio to the humidity ratio at saturation at the *same DBT*:

$$\mu = \frac{W}{W_s}$$

where *Ws* = 0.622 × *Pg* / (*P* − *Pg*). This is NOT the same as RH; they differ because *Pv* is not exactly proportional to *W* unless *P* >> *Pv*.

#### Step 4: Enthalpy of Moist Air
Taking reference at 0 °C, with *h* expressed per kg **dry air**:

$$h = h_a + W \cdot h_v = c_{p,a} \cdot t + W \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \cdot t)$$

$$h = 1.005\,t + W\,(2500 + 1.88\,t) \quad \text{[kJ/kg dry air]}$$

> The first term (**1.005 t**) is the *sensible* heat of dry air. The second (**W × (2500 + 1.88 t)**) is the *latent* heat of vapour plus its sensible heat.

#### Step 5: Specific Volume (*v*)
The volume per kg of *dry* air of the moist air mixture:

$$v = \frac{R_a \, T}{P_a} = \frac{R_a \, T}{P - P_v} = \frac{R_a \, T}{P}\,(1 + 1.6078\,W) \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/kg dry air]}$$

> The factor **1.6078** comes from (1/0.622) × (*Ra/Rv*) correction for humid volume expansion.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | SI Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | Humidity Ratio | $W = 0.622 \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | kg/kg da | Valid for *P* = 101.325 kPa |
| 2 | Saturated Humidity Ratio | $W_s = 0.622 \dfrac{P_g}{P - P_g}$ | kg/kg da | At saturation, *Pv* = *Pg* |
| 3 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_g} = \dfrac{W}{W_s} \cdot \dfrac{(1+W_s)}{(1+W)}$ | % or – | At DBT |
| 4 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{W}{W_s}$ | – | Always $\mu < \phi$ for *W* < *Ws* |
| 5 | Specific Enthalpy | $h = 1.005\,t + W(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ | kJ/kg da | Reference: 0 °C |
| 6 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P}(1 + 1.6078\,W)$ | m³/kg da | $R_a$ = 0.287 kJ/kg·K |
| 7 | Dew Point | *tdp* = saturation T at *Pv* | °C | Read from steam tables |
| 8 | Wet-Bulb | Empirical, $t_w$ from chart | °C | Found by energy balance |
| 9 | Humid Heat | $c_s = 1.005 + 1.88\,W$ | kJ/kg da·K | Used in WBT derivation |
| 10 | Humid Volume | $v_H = v$ as above | m³/kg da | Density of moist air inverse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule for KTU Problems:**
> If RH is given, compute *Pv* = *φ · Pg* (with *Pg* read at **DBT**, not WBT), then plug into equation (1) to get *W*. This *W* is the **actual** humidity ratio — used everywhere downstream (enthalpy, density, mass balance).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Industry

| Application | Psychrometric Parameter Controlled | Food Quality Effect |
|-------------|------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Cold Storage (0–4 °C)** | RH = 85–95% | Prevents weight loss (shrinkage) in apples, leafy vegetables |
| **Frozen Storage (–18 °C)** | RH = 90–95% | Prevents freezer burn (sublimation) in meat, fish |
| **Spray Drying of Milk** | Inlet air DBT 180–200 °C, RH < 5% | Drives evaporation; outlet RH = 10–25% |
| **Banana Ripening** | DBT 15–20 °C, RH 90–95%, C₂H₄ 100–150 ppm | Ethylene-humidity synergy for uniform ripening |
| **Bakery Proofing** | DBT 35–40 °C, RH 75–85% | Yeast activity and crust development |
| **Dry Sausage Curing** | DBT 12–15 °C, RH 75–80% | Controlled *aw* drop to inhibit pathogens |
| **Maple Syrup Concentration** | Outlet RH < 20% at 80 °C | Achieves 67° Brix safely |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** Modern food plants use **on-line psychrometers** (capacitance RH sensors + Pt100 RTD) integrated with PLC/DCS for real-time *aw* monitoring — a direct application of the equations above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Psychrometric (Wet-Bulb) Equation

The Wet-Bulb Temperature (*tw*) is defined thermodynamically by the *steady-state* energy balance on a small water wick fed by an infinite air stream. Let us derive the relationship that allows *W* to be computed from (*t*, *tw*, *P*).

**Setup (Figure of merit):** Consider 1 kg of dry air in contact with water at temperature *tw*. In the steady state, the latent heat released by evaporating water is exactly balanced by the sensible heat lost by the (cooler-dry-bulb) air.

**Step (a) — Mass of water evaporated per kg dry air:**
If the humidity ratio changes from *W₁* (at the upstream state) to *W₂* (at the saturated state *tw* = *tw*), the water picked up is:

$$dW = W_s(t_w) - W_1$$

**Step (b) — Latent heat absorbed for evaporation:**

$$Q_{latent} = (W_s - W) \cdot h_{fg}(t_w)$$

**Step (c) — Sensible heat lost by air (from DBT *t* to WBT *tw*):**

$$Q_{sensible} = c_{p,a} \cdot (t - t_w) + W \cdot c_{p,v} \cdot (t - t_w) = (1.005 + 1.88\,W)(t - t_w)$$

> The factor (1.005 + 1.88 W) is the **humid heat** *cs*.

**Step (d) — Equating the two in steady state:**

$$(1.005 + 1.88\,W)(t - t_w) = (W_s - W)\,h_{fg}(t_w)$$

**Step (e) — Solving for the humidity ratio W:**

Rearranging and isolating *W* on the left:

$$(1.005 + 1.88\,W)(t - t_w) = W_s\,h_{fg}(t_w) - W \cdot h_{fg}(t_w)$$

$$W\left[1.88(t - t_w) + h_{fg}(t_w)\right] = W_s\,h_{fg}(t_w) - 1.005(t - t_w)$$

$$W = \dfrac{1.005(t - t_w) - W_s\,h_{fg}(t_w)}{h_{fg}(t_w) - 1.88(t - t_w)} \cdot (-1)$$

A more conventionally written form (rearranged to put *W* on top, numerator only positive when *t* > *tw*):

$$\boxed{\,W = \dfrac{(1.005)(t - t_w) + W_s\,h_{fg}(t_w)}{h_{fg}(t_w) - 1.88(t - t_w)}\,}$$

**Validation of signs:** Since WBT < DBT, (*t* − *tw*) > 0; the denominator *hfg*(*tw*) is large (~2400–2500 kJ/kg), much bigger than 1.88 (*t* − *tw*) < 100, so denominator is always positive. Numerator is also positive → *W* > 0. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Food-Industry Significance:** This equation is the *theoretical backbone* of sling-psychrometer readings. Quality-assurance technicians in food plants use a calibrated sling-psychrometer daily to verify HVAC sensor calibration in cold rooms.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Problem)

**Problem [Adapted from KTU 2019 Scheme — Module 3]:**
In a cold storage chamber, air is at DBT **30 °C** and WBT **20 °C**. Atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa**. Determine: (a) Humidity Ratio, (b) Relative Humidity, (c) Specific Enthalpy, (d) Specific Volume, (e) Dew Point Temperature.

**Given Data (from steam tables):**
- *Pg*(30 °C) = 4.246 kPa
- *Pg*(20 °C) = 2.339 kPa
- *hfg*(20 °C) = 2458.0 kJ/kg
- *W*s at 20 °C = 0.622 × 2.339 / (101.325 − 2.339) = 0.01468 kg/kg da

**(a) Humidity Ratio W:**

$$W = \dfrac{(1.005)(30 - 20) + (0.01468)(2458.0)}{2458.0 - (1.88)(30 - 20)}$$

$$W = \dfrac{10.05 + 36.08}{2458.0 - 18.8} = \dfrac{46.13}{2439.2}$$

$$W = 0.01891 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}$$

**(b) Partial pressure of vapour:**

$$P_v = \dfrac{W \cdot P}{0.622 + W} = \dfrac{0.01891 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.01891} = \dfrac{1.916}{0.6409}$$

$$P_v = 2.990 \text{ kPa}$$

**Relative Humidity:**

$$\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_g(30\,°C)} = \dfrac{2.990}{4.246} = 0.7042 = 70.42\%$$

**(c) Specific Enthalpy:**

$$h = 1.005(30) + (0.01891)\big(2500 + 1.88(30)\big)$$

$$h = 30.15 + 0.01891 \times 2556.4 = 30.15 + 48.34$$

$$h = 78.49 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**(d) Specific Volume (using *T* = 303 K):**

$$v = \dfrac{0.287 \times 303}{101.325 - 2.990} = \dfrac{86.96}{98.335}$$

$$v = 0.8843 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg dry air}$$

**(e) Dew Point Temperature:** The temperature at which *Pg* = *Pv* = **2.990 kPa**.

From steam tables: at 24 °C, *Pg* = 2.985 kPa; at 24.1 °C, *Pg* ≈ 3.00 kPa. By linear interpolation:

$$t_{dp} \approx 24.0\,°C + \dfrac{(2.990 - 2.985)}{(3.000 - 2.985)} \times 0.1 = 24.03\,°C$$

$$\boxed{t_{dp} \approx 24.0\,°C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Dew Point Quick Method:** Without tables, use Antoine equation $\log_{10} P_g = A - \dfrac{B}{C + t}$ with constants A = 8.07131, B = 1730.63, C = 233.426 (valid 1–100 °C, *Pg* in mmHg). Convert to kPa via × 0.13332.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Reading the Psychrometric Chart

| If you know … | Then you can find … | Method |
|---------------|---------------------|--------|
| DBT + WBT | All properties | WBT line is the *adiabatic saturation* line; trace to saturation curve to read *Ws* |
| DBT + RH | W, h, v, tdp | From DBT go horizontally to φ curve, then drop vertically to read *W* and *h* |
| DBT + DPT | W, RH | From DBT go vertically down (constant *W* line) to intersect DPT isotherm; read *W* and φ |
| DBT + W | All properties | Constant *W* vertical line from DBT to saturation curve |
| DBT + h | W, RH | Slope = −1/*cs*; tilt along constant-h line to saturation curve |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Psychrometric Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measured Inputs: DBT and WBT] --> B[Steam Table Lookup]
    A --> C[Barometric Pressure Sensor]
    B --> D[Compute Saturation Ws at WBT]
    D --> E[Apply Psychrometric Equation]
    A --> E
    C --> E
    E --> F[Humidity Ratio W]
    F --> G[Partial Pressure Pv]
    F --> H[Specific Enthalpy h]
    F --> I[Specific Volume v]
    G --> J[Relative Humidity phi]
    G --> K[Dew Point tdp Lookup]
    K --> L[Final Psychrometric State Vector]
    J --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    L --> M[Food Industry Decision]
    M --> M1[Cold Storage RH Check]
    M --> M2[Drying Process Design]
    M --> M3[AC Load Calculation]
    M --> M4[Water Activity Correlation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The "state vector" *L* = {W, h, v, φ, tdp, t} is the *complete* psychrometric signature of the air sample. In food industry digital twins, this vector is updated every 10–60 seconds and fed to the building-management system (BMS).

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Psychrometric Processes in Food-Industry HVAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[State 1: Outdoor Air DBT 35 RH 60 percent] --> P1[Sensible Heating via Coil]
    P1 --> P2[State 2: Heated Air DBT 45 RH 18 percent]
    P2 --> P3[Adiabatic Saturation via Water Spray]
    P3 --> P4[State 3: Cooled and Humidified DBT 28 WBT 26]
    P4 --> P5[Cooling and Dehumidification via Chilled Coil]
    P5 --> P6[State 4: Supply Air DBT 12 RH 95 percent]
    P6 --> P7[Space Load Pickup in Cold Room]
    P7 --> P8[State 5: Return Air DBT 18 RH 70 percent]
    P8 --> P9[Mixing with Outdoor Fresh Air 20 percent]
    P9 --> P0
```

**Process Identification Table (KTU Board-Favourite Question Type):**

| Process | Lines on Chart | What Changes | What Stays Constant |
|---------|----------------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sensible Heating / Cooling | Horizontal | DBT, h | W, tdp, v (approx.) |
| Humidification (water spray) | Along constant *h* (WBT line) | W ↑, t ↓ | h, tw (approx.) |
| Dehumidification | Across saturation curve | W ↓, h ↓ | Sub-cooling |
| Cooling & Dehumidification | Down-left, crossing φ = 100% | W ↓, t ↓, h ↓ | DPT may change |
| Heating & Humidification | Up-right | W ↑, t ↑, h ↑ | — |
| Mixing of two air streams | Straight line on W vs. h chart | Mass-weighted avg | W is linear average |

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Cross-Linking Psychrometry to Food Science

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Condition DBT, RH] --> B[Equilibrium Relative Humidity ERH]
    B --> C[Water Activity of Food aw]
    C --> D1[Microbial Growth Boundary]
    C --> D2[Enzymatic Browning Rate]
    C --> D3[Lipid Oxidation Kinetics]
    C --> D4[Texture Moisture Migration]
    D1 --> E[Shelf Life Prediction]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[HACCP Compliant Storage Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Food-Industry Insight:** The classical **Labuza sorption isotherm** *aw* vs. moisture content, when combined with psychrometric RH, gives the *critical equilibrium moisture content* for crisp biscuits, dried milk, or freeze-dried coffee — this dictates the **storage RH setpoint** in food warehouses.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Define the following terms with one example each from the food industry: (a) Dry Bulb Temperature, (b) Dew Point Temperature, (c) Humidity Ratio.

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

- **(a) DBT (1 mark):** The temperature of air measured by a standard thermometer shielded from radiation and moisture. *Example:* A cold room at 4 °C DBT stores apples.
- **(b) DPT (1 mark):** The temperature at which water vapour in the air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) and begins to condense on a cooling surface. *Example:* When warm humid air (DBT 30 °C, RH 70%) enters a cold room at 4 °C, moisture condenses on the evaporator coil and chilled food surfaces — the coil surface temperature is below the DPT (~24 °C).
- **(c) Humidity Ratio *W* (1 mark):** Mass of water vapour (in kg) per kg of dry air. *Example:* *W* = 0.018 kg/kg da in the cold storage air of the earlier numerical example.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Differentiate between *Relative Humidity* and *Degree of Saturation*. Why are they unequal at sub-saturated conditions?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Relative Humidity φ (1 mark):** Ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapour to saturation pressure at the **DBT**.
$$\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_g(t)}$$

- **Degree of Saturation μ (1 mark):** Ratio of actual humidity ratio to saturated humidity ratio at the same DBT.
$$\mu = \dfrac{W}{W_s}$$

- **Why unequal (1 mark):** The relationship between *W* and *Pv* is non-linear (*W* = 0.622 *Pv* / (*P* − *Pv*)). Hence the ratio of *W*-values is not the same as the ratio of *Pv*-values. Only at *P* >> *Pv* (i.e., *W* ≪ 0.622) do φ ≈ μ. Numerically, φ > μ for any *W* < *Ws* at standard atmospheric pressure.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 3, 14 marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the working principle of a **sling psychrometer** and explain how it is used to determine the relative humidity of air in a food cold storage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Air at DBT 35 °C and RH 50% enters an air washer in a fruit ripening chamber. The air leaves the washer saturated at 25 °C. Some of this air is then reheated to 32 °C before entering the ripening chamber. Calculate: (i) The humidity ratio of the inlet air, (ii) The rate of water added per kg of dry air in the washer, (iii) The RH of the reheated air at 32 °C, (iv) The enthalpy change of the air across the washer. Take *P* = 101.325 kPa. (Use: *Pg*(35 °C) = 5.628 kPa, *Pg*(25 °C) = 3.169 kPa, *hfg*(25 °C) = 2442 kJ/kg.)

---

**Model Solution for Q3A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A sling psychrometer consists of **two identical mercury/bimetallic thermometers** mounted on a common frame (1 mark). The bulb of one thermometer (the *wet bulb*) is covered with a muslin wick soaked in distilled water; the other remains dry (the *dry bulb*) (1 mark). The frame is whirled manually or by a motor at ~5 m/s relative air velocity for 60–90 seconds (1 mark).

Working principle (2 marks): As air passes over the wet bulb, water evaporates by absorbing the latent heat of vaporisation from the bulb itself, cooling the wet-bulb thermometer. In steady state, the rate of heat loss by evaporation equals the rate of sensible heat transfer from the air, fixing the **wet-bulb temperature**. The *dry-bulb* thermometer records the true air temperature since it is dry. The *wet-bulb depression* (DBT − WBT) is then used to look up a psychrometric table or chart to read **RH** (1 mark). In a food cold storage, RH must be 85–95% for fresh produce; a sling psychrometer is the cheapest, most reliable field instrument for HACCP verification (1 mark).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Humidity ratio of inlet air (State 1):** (2 marks)

$$P_{v1} = 0.50 \times 5.628 = 2.814 \text{ kPa}$$

$$W_1 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.814}{101.325 - 2.814} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.814}{98.511} = 0.01777 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 2: Humidity ratio of air leaving washer (State 2 — saturated at 25 °C):** (1 mark)

$$P_{v2} = P_g(25\,°C) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$$

$$W_2 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.169}{101.325 - 3.169} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.169}{98.156} = 0.02008 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 3: Water added per kg dry air in the washer:** (1 mark)

$$\Delta W = W_2 - W_1 = 0.02008 - 0.01777 = 0.00231 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}$$

**Step 4: RH of reheated air (State 3 — 32 °C, same *W*):** (1 mark)

$$P_g(32\,°C) \approx 4.760 \text{ kPa (interpolated)}$$

$$P_{v3} = \dfrac{W_2 \cdot P}{0.622 + W_2} = \dfrac{0.02008 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.02008} = 3.165 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\phi_3 = \dfrac{3.165}{4.760} = 66.5\%$$

**Step 5: Enthalpy change across washer:** (2 marks)

$$h_1 = 1.005(35) + 0.01777(2500 + 1.88 \times 35) = 35.175 + 0.01777 \times 2565.8 = 35.175 + 45.59 = 80.77 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005(25) + 0.02008(2500 + 1.88 \times 25) = 25.125 + 0.02008 \times 2547.0 = 25.125 + 51.14 = 76.27 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 76.27 - 80.77 = -4.50 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

> (The negative sign indicates the air loses enthalpy in the washer — primarily due to evaporation of recirculated water, idealised as an *adiabatic saturation* process; the small energy balance residual is the makeup-water energy.)

**Final Answer Box:**
- (i) *W*₁ = **0.01777 kg/kg da**
- (ii) Δ*W* = **0.00231 kg water/kg da**
- (iii) φ₃ = **66.5%**
- (iv) Δ*h* = **−4.50 kJ/kg da** (cooling)

---

**Q3B. [Internal Choice Alternative — 14 marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the term **"specific enthalpy of moist air"** and derive the expression $h = 1.005\,t + W(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ kJ/kg dry air. State clearly the reference state used.

**(b) [7 marks]** The air in a food dehydration tunnel has DBT 80 °C and RH 8%. Calculate: (i) Humidity ratio, (ii) Dew point temperature, (iii) Specific enthalpy, (iv) Specific volume of the moist air. Take *P* = 101.325 kPa. Use steam-table data: *Pg*(80 °C) = 47.39 kPa.

---

**Model Solution for Q3B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Specific enthalpy of moist air is the total energy content (sensible + latent) per unit mass of *dry air*, expressed in kJ/kg of dry air, with the reference state chosen as **0 °C for both dry air and water vapour (in saturated liquid state)**.

**Derivation (5 marks):** The moist air mixture contains 1 kg of dry air + *W* kg of water vapour. The enthalpy of this mixture (relative to 0 °C reference) is:

$$h = h_{\text{dry air}} + W \cdot h_{\text{water vapour}}$$

For dry air heated from 0 °C to *t* °C:

$$h_a = c_{p,a} \cdot t = 1.005\,t \quad \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

For water vapour at temperature *t* (with reference as saturated liquid at 0 °C), the enthalpy includes both latent heat at 0 °C (*hfg,0* = 2500 kJ/kg) and sensible heat from 0 °C to *t*:

$$h_v = h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \cdot t = 2500 + 1.88\,t \quad \text{kJ/kg vapour}$$

Multiplying by *W* (kg vapour per kg dry air) and adding:

$$h = 1.005\,t + W(2500 + 1.88\,t) \quad \text{kJ/kg dry air} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Partial pressure of vapour (1 mark):**

$$P_v = 0.08 \times 47.39 = 3.791 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Humidity ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$W = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.791}{101.325 - 3.791} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.791}{97.534} = 0.02417 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 3: Dew point temperature (1.5 marks):** Find saturation T at *Pg* = 3.791 kPa. From steam tables: at 28 °C, *Pg* = 3.778 kPa; at 29 °C, *Pg* = 4.045 kPa.

$$t_{dp} = 28 + \dfrac{(3.791 - 3.778)}{(4.045 - 3.778)} \times 1 = 28.05\,°C \approx 28.0\,°C$$

**Step 4: Specific enthalpy (1.5 marks):**

$$h = 1.005(80) + 0.02417(2500 + 1.88 \times 80)$$

$$h = 80.40 + 0.02417 \times 2650.4 = 80.40 + 64.06 = 144.46 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Step 5: Specific volume (1.5 marks):** With *T* = 353 K:

$$v = \dfrac{0.287 \times 353}{101.325 - 3.791} = \dfrac{101.31}{97.534} = 1.0387 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg dry air}$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- (i) *W* = **0.02417 kg/kg da**
- (ii) *t*dp = **28.0 °C**
- (iii) *h* = **144.46 kJ/kg da**
- (iv) *v* = **1.039 m³/kg da**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Steam-table interpolation is mandatory** (1 mark deduction if skipped). Always show 2–3 line interpolations.
> 2. **Use DBT (not WBT) for *Pg* in RH formula.** Many students mistakenly use *Pg* at WBT — full 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Partial pressure via W formula requires inversion** for many numerical answers. Use the *two* equivalent forms:
> $$P_v = \dfrac{W \cdot P}{0.622 + W} \quad \text{AND} \quad W = 0.622 \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$
> Mark allocation usually includes 1 mark for correct formula selection.
> 4. **Reference state confusion:** Enthalpy reference is 0 °C; do NOT use 0 K (gas constant *RaT* uses 0 K in gas tables, but psychrometric *h* uses 0 °C).
> 5. **Mixing of two streams:** Always plot or write the mass-weighted average for *W* and *h* on a psychrometric chart — the mixed state lies on the straight line joining the two stream states, *not* on the WBT line.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Psychrometry** = study of air + water vapour as a single homogeneous gas.
- **Six key properties** = DBT, WBT, DPT, W, RH, h (plus v and μ).
- **Magic number 0.622** appears in *every* W-formula — memorise the origin (*Mv/Ma*).
- **Humidity ratio *W*** is the master mass-basis property; all other quantities can be derived from (*t*, *W*, *P*).
- **Sling psychrometer** measures DBT + WBT → gives all properties via chart/table.
- **DPT** is read from steam tables at *Pv*; **WBT** is found from adiabatic-saturation energy balance.
- **Specific enthalpy formula** $h = 1.005\,t + W(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ — reference 0 °C.
- **Specific volume** $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P}(1 + 1.6078\,W)$ — the **1.6078** factor is the humid-volume correction.
- **Relative humidity** φ = *Pv* / *Pg(t)* — the *Pg* is evaluated at **DBT**.
- **Degree of saturation** μ = *W* / *Ws*; always μ < φ when *W* < *Ws* at standard *P*.
- **Food-industry process mapping:** Cold storage (high RH, low DBT); drying (low RH, high DBT); ripening (moderate RH, ethylene); spray drying (very low RH, very high DBT).
- **Chart processes:** horizontal = sensible; vertical = latent; tilted along WBT line ≈ adiabatic saturation; cross of saturation curve = dehumidification.
- **Top KTU question patterns:** (1) Two-property → all other properties, (2) Process identification on chart, (3) Mixing of two streams, (4) Cooling coil Bypass Factor, (5) Air washer / evaporative cooling analysis.
- **Conversion shortcuts:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg; 1 kJ/kg·K = 0.2388 Btu/lb·°F.
- **Always show all 4–5 significant figures** in KTU 14-mark numericals; partial-step marks depend on intermediate clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Comfort Air-Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Comfort Air-Conditioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Comfort Air-Conditioning (CAC)** is defined as the simultaneous control of **temperature, humidity, air movement, air purity (ventilation), and radiant heat** within an enclosed space such that at least **80 % of the occupants** experience a state of *thermal neutrality* — neither feeling too warm nor too cool — while performing light, primarily sedentary activity.

The condition is technically evaluated against the **ASHRAE Standard 55 – Thermal Environmental Conditions for Human Occupancy**, which prescribes the **operative temperature**, permissible **relative humidity band**, and **mean radiant temperature limits** that define the human comfort zone.

### The Six Cardinal Environmental Variables

| # | Factor | Primary Controlling Parameter | Unit (SI) |
|---|--------|------------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Air Temperature | Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) | °C or K |
| 2 | Humidity | Relative Humidity (RH) / WBT | % / °C |
| 3 | Air Movement | Air velocity (V) | m/s |
| 4 | Air Purity | CO₂ concentration, dust (PM) | ppm, µg/m³ |
| 5 | Radiant Heat | Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT) | °C |
| 6 | Occupant Activity | Metabolic Rate (MR) | met or W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Out of the six variables above, only the **first three (DBT, RH, air velocity)** are *controllable* by an HVAC system at the supply-air side. The remaining three (MRT, metabolic rate, clothing) are **occupant/architecture-driven** but the system must *compensate* for them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath-Tub of Body Heat"

Imagine your body as a **100-W glowing electric bulb** continually radiating heat into the surrounding air. Now, comfort is achieved when the heat this bulb *gives out* is **exactly equal** to the heat it *loses to the surroundings* — neither more, nor less.

- If the surrounding air is **hot & humid** → bulb cannot lose heat → body feels **stuffy/sweaty** (think of a poorly ventilated kitchen in Kerala monsoon).
- If the surrounding air is **cold & drafty** → bulb loses heat too fast → body feels **chilled** (think of an over-cooled multiplex).
- The **temperature, humidity and air movement** of the room act as the three "valves" the air-conditioner must tune to keep this heat balance perfect.

> [!TIP]
> A useful one-liner for viva: *"Comfort = Heat produced by body = Heat lost to surroundings."* This is the foundation of the **Heat Balance Equation** derived in Section 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factor 1 — Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)

The **DBT** is the temperature of air measured by a standard thermometer shielded from radiation and moisture. It governs the **sensible heat transfer** between the body and the environment.

- **Sensible heat loss** from the body ∝ (Tₛₖᵢₙ − Tₐᵢᵣ) — the temperature gradient between skin (~33 °C) and air.
- For Indian/Kerala tropical climate, KTU-typical comfort DBT lies in the range **24 – 27 °C** (Kerala being hot-humid, slightly higher DBT with lower RH is preferred).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Condition (KCPL/IS 1391):** **DBT = 26.7 °C (80 °F)**, **RH = 50 %**, **Air Velocity = 0.15 m/s** — used in the Indian Standard for summer comfort in offices.

## 2.2 Factor 2 — Relative Humidity (RH) & Dew Point

Humidity quantifies the moisture content of air. **Relative Humidity (φ)** is the ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapour (Pᵥ) to the saturation pressure (Pₛ) at the same DBT.

$$\phi \;=\; \frac{P_{v}}{P_{s}(T_{db})}\;\times\;100\;\text{(\%)}$$

| RH Band | Effect on Occupant |
|---------|-------------------|
| < 30 % | Dry skin, static shocks, respiratory irritation |
| 40 – 55 % | **Comfort zone** (ASHRAE) |
| 60 – 70 % | Sticky, muggy feel, microbial growth in food areas |
| > 75 % | Condensation on surfaces, sweat does not evaporate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** A common exam error is conflating *Relative Humidity* with *Specific Humidity (ω)*. Specific humidity (kg water / kg dry air) is what **changes** when air is cooled below dew point and moisture condenses. RH is only a ratio — it can *rise* even when absolute moisture drops, simply because cool air holds less vapour.

## 2.3 Factor 3 — Air Movement (Velocity)

Air movement aids **convective and evaporative** heat loss from the skin. But there is a **non-linear trade-off**:

- **Low velocity (< 0.05 m/s):** stagnant, body heat builds up locally — "stuffy"
- **Optimum (0.15 – 0.25 m/s):** perceptible but not drafty — ideal for comfort
- **Excessive (> 0.5 m/s):** causes *local discomfort*, the **draft sensation** (especially at neck/ankle)

The convective heat-transfer coefficient is empirically related to air velocity:

$$h_{c} \;\approx\; 10.45\;-\;v\;+\;10\;\sqrt{v} \quad\;[\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}]$$

where *v* is in m/s. This is the **Mitchell equation**, valid for 0 < v < 2.5 m/s.

## 2.4 Factor 4 — Air Purity & Ventilation

The human body exhales **CO₂** (≈ 0.04 L/min at rest, 0.25 L/min during moderate work). A build-up of CO₂ beyond **1000 ppm** produces drowsiness, headaches, and reduced productivity.

**ASHRAE 62.1 – Ventilation Standard** mandates:
- Offices: **8.5 L/s per person** + **0.6 L/s per m²** of floor area
- Conference rooms: **8.5 L/s per person** + **0.6 L/s per m²**
- Food-service kitchens: **3.8 L/s per m²** (grease/dust loaded)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Food-Industry Context (OEFTT612):** In food processing halls, contaminants are *not just* CO₂ — they include **spores, aerosols, product dust (e.g., flour), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs)**. Hence **HEPA filtration** + **higher fresh-air ratio (15–20 %)** is mandated, unlike offices (~10 %).

## 2.5 Factor 5 — Radiant Heat (Mean Radiant Temperature, MRT)

Heat gained or lost by the body via *radiation* depends on the **Mean Radiant Temperature** of the surrounding surfaces, not the air temperature. Two surfaces can have identical DBT but vastly different MRT (e.g., a sun-lit glass wall vs. an insulated one).

$$\text{MRT} \;=\; \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{i}\,T_{i}^{\,4}\right]^{1/4} \quad\;[\text{K}]$$

where Fᵢ is the **angle factor (view factor)** from the occupant toward surface *i*, and Tᵢ is the absolute temperature of that surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operative Temperature (Tₒₚ)** is the *weighted* mean of air temperature and MRT used in comfort standards:
> $$T_{op} \;=\; \frac{h_{c}\,T_{a} \;+\; h_{r}\,\overline{T_{r}}}{h_{c}+h_{r}}$$
> For air velocities **v ≤ 0.2 m/s**, hᵣ ≃ 4.7 W/m²K, and Tₒₚ ≈ (Tₐ + MRT)/2.

## 2.6 Factor 6 — Clothing Insulation (CLO)

Clothing traps a layer of still air next to the skin, reducing heat loss. The unit is **clo** (1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W).

| Garment | clo |
|---------|-----|
| Naked skin | 0.0 |
| Light tropical (shirt + trousers) | 0.5 – 0.6 |
| Standard business suit | 1.0 |
| Heavy winter overcoat | 3.0 – 4.0 |

For **Kerala tropical offices**, a typical clo ≈ **0.5** is assumed in comfort calculations.

## 2.7 Factor 7 — Metabolic Rate (MET)

1 met = **58.2 W/m²** = the metabolic rate of a **seated, resting person**.

| Activity | met |
|----------|-----|
| Sleeping | 0.7 |
| Seated, typing | 1.0 – 1.2 |
| Standing, light work | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Walking (3 km/h) | 2.0 |
| Heavy labour | 3.0 – 5.0 |

## 2.8 📋 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = P_{v}/P_{s}$ | % | ψ = partial / saturation pressure |
| 2 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622\;P_{v}/(P_{t}-P_{v})$ | kg/kg dry air | $P_{t}$ = total pressure |
| 3 | Sensible Heat Load | $Q_{s} = m\,c_{p}\,(T_{i}-T_{f})$ | kW | $c_{p}$ ≈ 1.005 kJ/kg·K |
| 4 | Latent Heat Load | $Q_{L} = m\,h_{fg}\,(\omega_{i}-\omega_{f})$ | kW | $h_{fg}$ ≈ 2501 kJ/kg |
| 5 | Convective Coefficient | $h_{c} = 10.45 - v + 10\sqrt{v}$ | W/m²·K | Mitchell Eqn. |
| 6 | MRT | $MRT = \bigl(\sum F_{i}T_{i}^{4}\bigr)^{1/4}$ | K | View factor weighted |
| 7 | Operative Temperature | $T_{op} = (h_{c}T_{a}+h_{r}MRT)/(h_{c}+h_{r})$ | °C | For low v, hᵣ ≈ 4.7 |
| 8 | Metabolic Rate | 1 met = 58.2 W/m² | W/m² | Resting seated person |
| 9 | Clothing Resistance | 1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W | m²·K/W | Trapped air layer |
| 10 | Heat-Balance on Body | $M - W = (C+R+E_{sk})$ | W/m² | First-law on skin |
| 11 | Effective Temp. Index | $ET \approx T_{wbt}$ for 50 % RH | °C | Houghten-Yaglou |
| 12 | Heat Stress Index | $HSI = (E_{req}/E_{max})\times 100$ | % | Belding Hatch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorise equations (5), (7), and (10) above. These three alone are the basis of 80 % of the 14-mark comfort-AC problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat-Balance Equation on Human Body

We apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the human body taken as a control volume. The body is at thermal steady state, so net energy storage = 0.

### Step 1 — Define Energy Flows

- **M** = Total metabolic heat produced (W/m²)
- **W** = External mechanical work done (W/m²) ≈ 0 for most sedentary cases
- **C** = Heat loss by **convection** to the surrounding air
- **R** = Heat loss by **radiation** to surrounding surfaces
- **Eₛₖ** = Heat loss by **evaporation** of sweat from the skin
- **Cᵣₑₛ** = Heat loss by **respiration** (convective + latent from lungs)
- **Eᵣₑₛ** = Latent heat lost with exhaled moisture

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Energy Balance

Rate of energy in = Rate of energy out

$$
M \;-\; W \;=\; (C + R + E_{sk}) \;+\; (C_{res}+E_{res})
$$

### Step 3 — Express Each Term

**Convection:**
$$
C \;=\; h_{c}\,(T_{skin}-T_{air}) \quad\;[\text{W/m}^{2}]
$$

**Radiation:**
$$
R \;=\; h_{r}\,(\overline{T_{skin}}-\overline{T_{r}})
$$
where $h_{r}=4\varepsilon\sigma\overline{T}^{3} \approx 4.7$ W/m²·K at 30 °C.

**Evaporative Skin Loss** (driven by vapour-pressure difference):
$$
E_{sk} \;=\; h_{e}\,w\,(P_{sk}-P_{a})
$$
where $h_{e}$ = evaporative coefficient (Lewis relation: $h_{e}=16.5\,h_{c}$), $w$ = skin wettedness (0 to 1), $P_{sk}, P_{a}$ = saturated vapour pressure at skin & partial pressure in air.

### Step 4 — Define Operative & Climatic Variables

Using $T_{op}$ and assuming $T_{skin}\approx 33$ °C, the *required* evaporative loss for comfort becomes:
$$
E_{req} \;=\; M-W \;-\; (h_{c}+h_{r})\,(T_{skin}-T_{op})
$$

### Step 5 — Skin-Wettedness Limitation

For a person at rest, $w$ is bounded to **0.06 (no sweating) to 1.0 (fully drenched)**. The minimum required $w$ for thermal neutrality is:
$$
w_{req} \;=\; \frac{E_{req}}{h_{e}\,(P_{sk,sat}-P_{a})}
$$

> If $w_{req} > 1$ → environment **too hot/humid** for comfort, body cannot sweat enough.
> If $w_{req} < 0.06$ → environment **too cold**, body must shiver to maintain heat.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Temperature (ET)

**ET** combines the effects of DBT, humidity, and air velocity into a single *temperature-equivalent* index. Originally developed by **Houghten & Yaglou (1923)**.

### Step 1 — Reference Air Condition

Define **standard air** at: DBT = 17.8 °C, RH = 100 %, v = still (≃ 0.05 m/s). The wet-bulb temperature of this air = DBT = 17.8 °C.

### Step 2 — Procedure (Psycho-Physiological Test)

A large group of subjects is moved between two chambers:
- Chamber A: standard (saturated) air at temperature $T_s$
- Chamber B: test air at DBT $T_{db}$, WBT $T_{wb}$, velocity $v$

They switch chambers until the *sensations of warmth are equal*.

### Step 3 — Resultant Index

The temperature $T_s$ of the standard air at which subjects feel equal warmth is the **Effective Temperature**:

$$
\boxed{\;ET \;=\; T_{s}\big|_{sensations\;equal}\;}
$$

### Step 4 — Practical Approximation

For *moderate conditions*, ET approximates the **Wet-Bulb Temperature** at 50 % RH:

$$
ET \;\approx\; T_{wb}\;(50\%\,RH)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** ET over-emphasises humidity at low temperatures and under-emphasises it at high temperatures. Hence ASHRAE replaced it with the **Standard Effective Temperature (SET)**, which uses a more rigorous two-node human thermoregulation model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Stress Index (HSI) — Belding & Hatch

The HSI quantifies the *physiological strain* of an environment.

### Step 1 — Define Maximum Evaporative Capacity

Given a saturated skin (w = 1), the maximum possible sweat evaporation rate is:

$$
E_{max} \;=\; h_{e}\,(P_{sk,sat}-P_{a}) \quad\;[\text{W/m}^{2}]
$$

### Step 2 — Define Required Evaporation

From Section 3.1, $E_{req}$ is the sweat rate needed to maintain thermal equilibrium.

### Step 3 — Form the Index

$$
\boxed{\;HSI \;=\; \frac{E_{req}}{E_{max}}\;\times\;100\;\%}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation Bands

| HSI Range | Condition |
|-----------|-----------|
| 0 | No heat stress (no sweat needed) |
| 0 – 10 | Mild, comfortable |
| 10 – 30 | Mild to moderate |
| 30 – 60 | Severe (industrial caution) |
| 60 – 100 | Very severe |
| > 100 | Heat-stroke threshold (impossible equilibrium) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comfort at a Glance

| Variable | Increases (↑) → Body feels | Decreases (↓) → Body feels | Control Lever (HVAC) |
|----------|----------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| DBT (Air Temp.) | Warmer | Cooler | Chiller / Heater coil |
| RH (Humidity) | Sticky / muggy | Dry, irritated | Cooling coil dehumidification |
| Air Velocity | Cooler (drafts) | Stagnant | Fan / duct sizing |
| MRT (Radiation) | Warmer (esp. hot wall/glass) | Cooler (cold glass) | Insulation, blinds |
| Clo (Clothing) | Warmer | Cooler | Occupant control |
| Met (Activity) | Warmer | Cooler | Occupant control |
| CO₂ / Dust | Drowsy, unhealthy | Fresh, alert | Filter + fresh-air damper |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Six-Pillar Comfort Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Human Body in CAC Space] --> B[Comfort Loop]
    B --> C1[Factor 1: DBT - 24 to 27 C]
    B --> C2[Factor 2: RH - 40 to 55 percent]
    B --> C3[Factor 3: Air Velocity 0.15 to 0.25 m per s]
    B --> C4[Factor 4: Air Purity CO2 less than 1000 ppm]
    B --> C5[Factor 5: Mean Radiant Temperature]
    B --> C6[Factor 6: Clothing - clo value]
    B --> C7[Factor 7: Metabolic Rate - met value]
    C1 --> D[Heat Balance M minus W equals C plus R plus Esk]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    D --> E{Comfort Achieved}
    E -->|Yes| F[State of Thermal Neutrality]
    E -->|No| G[Discomfort - Stuffy or Chilled]
    G --> H[AHU Recalibration]
    H --> B
    C4 --> I[Ventilation Loop]
    I --> J[Fresh Air Damper]
    I --> K[Filter Bank]
    J --> L[Indoor Air Quality OK]
    K --> L
```

```mermaid
    A[ASHRAE Comfort Zone] --> B[Summer: 23 to 26 C DBT]
    A --> C[Winter: 20 to 23.5 C DBT]
    A --> D[Common RH band: 30 to 60 percent]

    C --> F[Optimum Point: 22 C and 35 percent RH]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Heat Loss Pathways from Body

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Metabolic Heat M] --> W[External Work W]
    M --> Q[Net Heat to Lose M minus W]
    Q --> P1[Convective Loss C - to Air]
    Q --> P2[Radiative Loss R - to Surfaces]
    Q --> P3[Evaporative Loss Esk - Sweat]
    Q --> P4[Respiratory Loss Cres and Eres]
    P1 --> OUT[Comfort Reached]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P1 -.Increased by.-> V[Raise Air Velocity]
    P2 -.Increased by.-> MRT[Lower Mean Radiant Temp]

```

```mermaid
    OA[Outside Fresh Air] --> MIX
    MIX --> F[Filter Section - Purity]
    F --> CL[Cooling or Heating Coil - DBT]
    CL --> HMD[Humidifier or Dehumidifier - RH]
    HMD --> FN[Fan Section - Air Velocity]
    FN --> SA[Supply Air to Room]
    SA --> ROOM[Conditioned Space - Comfort Achieved]
    ROOM --> RA
```
