# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> *List any six factors that affect human comfort in an air-conditioned space and indicate which are controllable by the air-conditioning system.*

**Model Answer:**

The six primary factors affecting comfort AC are:
1. **Dry-bulb temperature (DBT)** of air – *controllable* by cooling/heating coil.
2. **Relative Humidity (RH)** – *controllable* via dehumidification or humidification at the cooling coil.
3. **Air velocity** – *controllable* by fan speed and duct design.
4. **Air purity (CO₂, dust, microbes)** – *controllable* via filtration and ventilation.
5. **Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT)** of surrounding surfaces – *partially controllable* by insulation and shading.
6. **Clothing insulation (clo) and Metabolic rate (met)** – *occupant-driven, not directly controllable*; the AC must compensate for them.

(Any six with correct classification = 3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
> *Define Effective Temperature (ET) and write its limitations.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Effective Temperature is an empirically derived single index that combines the effects of DBT, humidity, and air velocity into a temperature-equivalent value. It is defined as the temperature of a *standard* saturated environment (RH = 100 %, v ≃ 0.05 m/s) at which an occupant experiences the *same sensation of warmth* as in the actual environment.

**Limitations:**
1. Over-emphasises humidity at low temperatures and under-emphasises it at high temperatures.
2. Derived for sedentary, lightly-clothed subjects — not valid for industrial metabolic rates.
3. Considers only two-node physiology; does not account for MRT or radiant asymmetry.
4. Replaced in modern practice by **Standard Effective Temperature (SET)** and **PMV (Predicted Mean Vote)** models.

(Definition 1.5 marks + any 3 limitations 1.5 marks = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### 🔵 QUESTION A — 14 MARKS
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **psychrometric comfort zone** as defined by **ASHRAE Standard 55** for summer and winter conditions. Highlight the Indian Standard conditions for tropical comfort. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A small office in Kochi is maintained at DBT = **27 °C** and RH = **55 %**. The office has 12 occupants doing light office work (met = 1.2) wearing summer clothing (clo = 0.5). The MRT is approximately equal to the DBT. Estimate (i) the operative temperature, and (ii) comment qualitatively on whether the conditions are within the comfort band. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for A(a) — 7 Marks

The **ASHRAE Comfort Zone** is plotted on the psychrometric chart with DBT (°C) on the x-axis and RH (%) on the y-axis. Key features:

**Summer Zone:**
- DBT: **23 °C to 26 °C**
- Operative temperature: 23 – 26 °C
- RH band: **30 – 60 %**
- Air velocity ≤ 0.25 m/s

**Winter Zone:**
- DBT: **20 °C to 23.5 °C**
- Operative temperature: 20 – 23.5 °C
- RH band: 30 – 60 %

**Indian Standard (IS 1391 / KCPL):**
- Summer (tropical): **DBT = 26.7 °C, RH = 50 %, v = 0.15 m/s**
- Winter: DBT ≈ 22 °C, RH ≈ 35 %

**Schematic description (replaces sketch):**

```
   RH (%)
   100 |*
       |  *
       |    *  (Saturation line φ=1)
   60  |--------*---------
       |  SUMMER   WINTER   |
       |  ZONE      ZONE    |
   30  |--------*---------
       |  *      *
       |*     *
     0 +----------------------
       17   20  23  26  29  DBT (°C)
```

The comfort region lies well below saturation to allow sweat evaporation, and within the **30–60 % RH** envelope to prevent dryness or microbial growth.

(Marks: Summer zone 2, Winter zone 2, Indian standard 2, Sketch 1 = 7)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** DBT = 27 °C, RH = 55 %, MRT ≈ 27 °C, met = 1.2, clo = 0.5, v ≃ 0.15 m/s (assumed for low-velocity indoor office).

**Step 1 — Compute convective coefficient (Mitchell):**

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.15 \;+\; 10\,\sqrt{0.15}
$$

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.15 \;+\; 10\,(0.3873)
$$

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.15 \;+\; 3.873
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{c} \;\approx\; 14.17\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}}
$$

**Step 2 — Use radiative coefficient:** At indoor conditions, $h_r \approx 4.7$ W/m²K.

**Step 3 — Compute Operative Temperature:**

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{h_{c}\,T_{a} \;+\; h_{r}\,MRT}{h_{c}+h_{r}}
$$

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{(14.17)(27) \;+\; (4.7)(27)}{14.17+4.7}
$$

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{382.59 + 126.9}{18.87}
$$

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{509.49}{18.87}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{op} \;\approx\; 27.0\;^{\circ}\text{C}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ASHRAE Summer Comfort Band:**

| Parameter | Condition | ASHRAE Limit | Verdict |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|---------|
| Tₒₚ | 27.0 °C | 23 – 26 °C | **Slightly above** |
| RH | 55 % | 30 – 60 % | ✓ Within |
| Velocity | 0.15 m/s | ≤ 0.25 m/s | ✓ Within |

**Comment (qualitative):** The **operative temperature of 27 °C is marginally higher (by ~1 °C)** than the upper limit of the ASHRAE summer comfort zone. The occupants may experience a *slight sensation of warmth* but it remains within the Indian Standard tropical limit of 26.7 °C (within 0.3 °C of standard).

**Recommendation:** Lowering the DBT by **1 °C** to 26 °C, or raising the air velocity to 0.25 m/s, would push the operative temperature comfortably into the ASHRAE band. For tropical Kerala climate, the 27 °C is *acceptable*, though *not optimum* by international benchmark.

(Marks: Step 1: 2, Step 2: 1, Step 3: 2, Step 4: 1, Recommendation: 1 = 7)

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Derive the **steady-state human heat-balance equation** and explain each term. Show how the **operative temperature** is used to simplify the analysis. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A food-processing hall in Thrissur is to be maintained at DBT = **24 °C**, RH = **50 %**, and air velocity = **0.20 m/s**. Workers are performing light standing work (met = 1.5) wearing light overalls (clo = 0.7). Determine (i) the convective heat-transfer coefficient using the Mitchell equation, and (ii) the **Heat Stress Index (HSI)** using the Belding-Hatch method, assuming $P_{sk,sat}=5.628$ kPa, $P_a=1.475$ kPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law to the human body (steady state):**

$$
M - W \;=\; (C + R + E_{sk}) + (C_{res} + E_{res})
$$

**Step 2 — Identify each term:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| M | Metabolic heat rate | 58.2 W/m² (1 met) |
| W | External mechanical work | 0 (sitting) |
| C | Convective loss | $h_c(T_{sk}-T_a)$ |
| R | Radiative loss | $h_r(T_{sk}-MRT)$ |
| Eₛₖ | Evaporative loss from skin | sweat-driven |
| Cᵣₑₛ + Eᵣₑₛ | Respiratory loss | ~10 % of M |

**Step 3 — Define operative temperature:**

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{h_{c}\,T_{a} + h_{r}\,MRT}{h_{c}+h_{r}}
$$

This allows us to *lump* C and R together:

$$
C+R \;=\; (h_{c}+h_{r})\,(T_{sk}-T_{op})
$$

**Step 4 — Simplified heat balance:**

$$
\boxed{M - W \;=\; (h_{c}+h_{r})\,(T_{sk}-T_{op}) + E_{sk} + (C_{res}+E_{res})}
$$

**Step 5 — For a seated person, $T_{sk}\approx 33\;^{\circ}$C** (skin regulatory set-point).

**Step 6 — Required evaporative loss for neutrality:**

$$
E_{req} \;=\; M - W - (h_{c}+h_{r})\,(33-T_{op}) - (C_{res}+E_{res})
$$

> This single equation encapsulates all six comfort factors and is the *operational* formula used in Fanger's PMV model.

(Marks: Eqn 2, Term identification 2, Tₒₚ definition 1, Final balance 2 = 7)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** v = 0.20 m/s, met = 1.5, clo = 0.7, Tₒₚ ≈ Tₐ = 24 °C, $P_{sk,sat}=5.628$ kPa, $P_a=1.475$ kPa.

**Part (i) — Mitchell Convective Coefficient:**

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.20 \;+\; 10\,\sqrt{0.20}
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.20} \;=\; 0.4472
$$

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.20 \;+\; 10(0.4472)
$$

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.20 \;+\; 4.472
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{c} \;\approx\; 14.72\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}}
$$

**Part (ii) — HSI Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Metabolic rate:**
$$
M \;=\; 1.5 \times 58.2 \;=\; 87.3\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Required evaporative loss** (assuming W ≈ 0, $C_{res}+E_{res} \approx 0.1M$):

Using $T_{sk}=33\;^{\circ}$C, $T_{op}=24\;^{\circ}$C, and $h_r=4.7$:

$$
C+R \;=\; (14.72 + 4.7)\,(33 - 24) \;=\; 19.42 \times 9 \;=\; 174.78\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

Wait — that is **larger** than M. This means at these conditions the body would *gain* heat. Let's correct: we should use a more appropriate skin temperature for a working person, or note that the body invokes vasodilation to lower $T_{sk}$. Assume $T_{sk}=34\;^{\circ}$C (slightly warmer due to work):

$$
C+R \;=\; 19.42 \times 10 \;=\; 194.2\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

This is still problematic; it means the operative temperature 24 °C is **too cool** for the activity. We instead compute $E_{req}$ as the *excess metabolic* load:

$$
E_{req} \;=\; M - W - (h_{c}+h_{r})(T_{sk}-T_{op}) - 0.1M
$$

$$
E_{req} \;=\; 87.3 - 19.42(33-24) - 8.73
$$

$$
E_{req} \;=\; 87.3 - 174.78 - 8.73
$$

$$
E_{req} \;=\; -96.2\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Negative required evaporation** means the body is *losing* more heat than it produces → mild **cold stress**, not heat stress. Hence HSI is effectively **0** (no sweating required).

**Step 3 — Compute maximum evaporative capacity (for completeness):**

$$
h_{e} \;=\; 16.5 \times h_{c} \;=\; 16.5 \times 14.72 \;=\; 242.9\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}
$$

$$
E_{max} \;=\; h_{e}\,(P_{sk,sat}-P_{a}) \;=\; 242.9 \times (5.628 - 1.475)
$$

$$
E_{max} \;=\; 242.9 \times 4.153 \;\approx\; 1008.7\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat Stress Index:**

Since $E_{req}<0$ (no sweat needed):
$$
\boxed{HSI \;\approx\; 0\;\%}
$$

**Conclusion:** The conditions are thermally *neutral to slightly cool*; HSI of 0 % indicates **no heat stress**. For a food-processing hall this is ideal for product stability, though workers may want to add one light garment layer.

(Marks: Mitchell calc 2, M 1, $E_{req}$ 2, $E_{max}$ 1, HSI 1 = 7)

> [!WARNING]
> **🚩 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.1·M respiratory loss term** in the heat balance. This costs ~1 mark.
> 2. **Conflating air temperature with operative temperature.** When MRT ≈ Tₐ, Tₒₚ ≈ Tₐ — but students often write Tₒₚ = Tₐ without justification.
> 3. **Incorrect unit of $h_e$:** It is **W/m²·kPa**, not W/m²·K. The Lewis relation introduces kPa; many students drop the unit and lose a mark.
> 4. **Sign of $E_{req}$:** A *negative* $E_{req}$ is physically meaningful — it means the body needs *no* evaporation. Students often write a fake positive value.
> 5. **Mitchell equation validity range** (0 ≤ v ≤ 2.5 m/s). If a problem states v = 5 m/s, you must use a different correlation or note the limitation.
> 6. **Indian Standard values:** Forgetting to convert the IS 1391 conditions (26.7 °C, 50 %, 0.15 m/s) into SI units with proper units shown loses a mark.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Six + one comfort factors**: DBT, RH, air velocity, air purity, MRT, clothing (clo), metabolic rate (met).
- [x] **First three (DBT, RH, velocity) are HVAC-controllable**; the rest are occupant/architecture-driven.
- [x] **Comfort heat-balance equation**: $M - W = C + R + E_{sk} + C_{res} + E_{res}$.
- [x] **Mitchell equation** for $h_c$: $h_c = 10.45 - v + 10\sqrt{v}$ — *memorise it*.
- [x] **Operative temperature** $T_{op} = (h_c T_a + h_r MRT)/(h_c + h_r)$.
- [x] **ASHRAE 55 summer zone**: 23 – 26 °C, RH 30 – 60 %, v ≤ 0.25 m/s.
- [x] **Indian Standard (IS 1391, tropical)**: 26.7 °C DBT, 50 % RH, 0.15 m/s.
- [x] **1 met = 58.2 W/m²**; **1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W**.
- [x] **Effective Temperature (ET)** ≈ WBT at 50 % RH (approximation).
- [x] **HSI = (E_req / E_max) × 100 %** — Belding-Hatch method.
- [x] **Ventilation (ASHRAE 62.1)**: ~8.5 L/s per person + 0.6 L/s per m².
- [x] **Kerala context**: Hot-humid → prefer slightly higher DBT (27 °C) with stronger dehumidification and ≥ 0.2 m/s air movement.
- [x] **Food-industry twist (OEFTT612)**: HEPA filtration, 15–20 % fresh air, strict control of microbial-load humidity (RH ≤ 55 %).
- [x] **Negative $E_{req}$** ⇒ cold stress, not heat stress. Always comment on the *sign*.
- [x] **Always** state units of $h_c, h_r, h_e$ explicitly — examiners reward this.
- [x] **Always** mention the **standard reference atmosphere** (1 atm = 101.325 kPa) when working with vapour pressures.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Six Factors:** **"CARE – MVP"**
> **C**lothing (clo) • **A**ir purity • **R**adiant heat (MRT) • **E**vaporative (humidity) • **M**etabolic (met) • **V**elocity • **P**ressure (atmospheric, for high-altitude corrections)
> *(Reorder to DBT, RH, V, Purity, MRT, Clo, Met as per the standard presentation.)*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of refrigeration and air conditioning systems employed in the food industry, **Classification** refers to the systematic categorization of refrigeration systems based on fundamental operating principles, thermodynamic cycles, application ranges, refrigerant types, compressor mechanisms, and end-use purposes. The food industry demands a wide spectrum of temperatures (from chilled storage at ~0 °C to frozen storage at –40 °C and cryogenic levels), necessitating diverse refrigeration technologies tailored to specific product requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** Classification of refrigeration systems with emphasis on systems relevant to food processing, preservation, cold storage, and air conditioning of food processing environments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

Think of refrigeration systems the way you think of **vehicles for transportation**. Just as vehicles are classified into bicycles, motorcycles, cars, trucks, and ships — each suited to a specific purpose (short trips vs. heavy cargo vs. long ocean voyages) — refrigeration systems are classified into families based on *how they move heat* and *what they are designed to cool*.

- A **household refrigerator** (small, electric, intermittent) is like a *scooter* — efficient for small jobs.
- A **Vapour Compression System (VCRS)** used in cold storage is like a *delivery truck* — the most common and reliable workhorse of the food industry.
- A **Vapour Absorption System (VARS)** is like a *solar-powered vehicle* — uses heat (often waste heat) instead of mechanical work.
- A **Cryogenic system** is like a *spacecraft* — designed for ultra-low temperatures used in flash-freezing and liquid nitrogen applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants (Standard SI Values):**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Standard Atmospheric Pressure: **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**
> - Freezing point of pure water: **0 °C = 273.15 K**
> - Typical chilled food storage: **0 °C to 4 °C**
> - Typical frozen food storage: **–18 °C to –25 °C**
> - Cryogenic preservation: **below –40 °C (often –196 °C with LN₂)**

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters in the Food Industry

Different food products have different thermal sensitivities, shelf-life requirements, and processing needs:

| Food Category | Required Temperature Range | Typical System Used |
|---------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Fresh fruits & vegetables (chilled) | 0 °C to 13 °C | Medium-temp VCRS |
| Dairy, meat, fish (chilled) | –1 °C to 4 °C | Medium-temp VCRS |
| Frozen foods (long-term storage) | –18 °C to –25 °C | Low-temp VCRS |
| Quick-freezing (IQF) | –30 °C to –40 °C | Cascade / Two-stage VCRS |
| Cryogenic freezing | –196 °C | Liquid nitrogen / CO₂ systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Choosing the wrong class of refrigeration system leads to **food spoilage, energy wastage, and economic losses** — making classification a critical engineering decision in food industry design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification Tree

Refrigeration systems used in the food industry are classified along **six principal axes**:

#### A. Classification Based on the Thermodynamic Cycle / Process
This is the **most fundamental classification** in engineering thermodynamics.

1. **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)**
   - Uses a mechanical compressor to circulate refrigerant.
   - Most widely used in the food industry (cold storage, chilling, freezing).
   - Components: Compressor → Condenser → Expansion valve → Evaporator.

2. **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)**
   - Uses heat energy (thermal compressor) instead of mechanical work.
   - Common refrigerant-absorbent pairs: **NH₃–H₂O** (industrial) and **H₂O–LiBr** (air conditioning).
   - Preferred where waste heat or solar energy is available.

3. **Gas Refrigeration Cycle**
   - Uses the reverse Brayton / Bell-Coleman cycle.
   - Working fluid remains in the gaseous phase throughout.
   - Used in air-craft refrigeration and certain cryogenic pre-cooling.

4. **Steam Jet (Ejector) Refrigeration System**
   - Uses a steam ejector to create vacuum and produce chilled water.
   - Used in large-scale food processing plants for chilled water supply.

#### B. Classification Based on Operating Temperature
| Class | Evaporator Temperature Range | Food Industry Application |
|-------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| High-temperature (HT) | 0 °C to 10 °C | Cooling of fruits, beverages, dairy |
| Medium-temperature (MT) | –18 °C to 0 °C | Chilled storage of meat, fish, dairy |
| Low-temperature (LT) | –40 °C to –18 °C | Frozen storage, ice cream hardening |
| Cryogenic | < –40 °C | Flash freezing (LN₂, LCO₂) |

#### C. Classification Based on Compressor Type
1. **Reciprocating (Positive Displacement)** — most common in small-to-medium food plants.
2. **Rotary** — including scroll and screw compressors for medium capacities.
3. **Centrifugal (Turbo)** — for very large capacities (large cold storages).
4. **Screw compressors** — for continuous-duty industrial freezing tunnels.

#### D. Classification Based on Construction / Stage
- **Single-stage system** — one compression stage (most food industry applications).
- **Multi-stage system** — two or more compression stages (low-temp, cascade).
- **Cascade system** — two separate systems using different refrigerants (e.g., NH₃ + CO₂).

#### E. Classification Based on Refrigerant Type
- **Primary refrigerant** — circulates through the cycle (NH₃, R-22, R-134a, R-404A, CO₂).
- **Secondary refrigerant (brine)** — absorbs heat from the food and transfers it to the primary refrigerant (e.g., glycol-water, brine, propylene glycol).

#### F. Classification Based on Application in Food Industry
1. **Chilling systems** — rapid cooling of fresh produce, meat, poultry post-slaughter.
2. **Freezing systems** — slow freezing, quick freezing (IQF), cryogenic freezing.
3. **Cold storage systems** — long-term preservation warehouses.
4. **Refrigerated transport** — reefer trucks, containers, ships.
5. **Display refrigeration** — retail chilled/frozen cabinets.
6. **Process air conditioning** — controlled atmosphere (CA) storage, ripening rooms.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}$ | dimensionless | All refrigeration cycles |
| COP of ideal VCRS (Carnot refrig.) | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Upper theoretical limit |
| Refrigeration Effect (RE) | $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | VCRS analysis |
| Compressor Work | $W_{\text{comp}} = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | VCRS analysis |
| Heat Rejection at Condenser | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | VCRS analysis |
| Ton of Refrigeration (TR) | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | kW or TR | Capacity rating |
| Heat removed by 1 TR | $1 \text{ TR} = 50.4 \text{ kJ/min} = 211 \text{ kJ/min (approx.)}$ | kJ/min | Capacity rating |
| Specific Heat relation | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ | Chilling/freezing load |
| Latent heat for freezing | $Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_f$ | kJ | Freezing load calculation |
| Energy balance (VARS) | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_{\text{generator}}}$ | dimensionless | Absorption cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for the Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle:**
> - Compression: **isentropic** ($s_1 = s_2$).
> - Condensation: **isobaric** ($P_2 = P_3$).
> - Expansion: **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$).
> - Evaporation: **isobaric and isothermal** ($P_4 = P_1$, $T_1 = T_4 = T_L$).

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Industry

The classification framework directly informs:
- **Equipment selection** in cold chain design.
- **Energy auditing** — different classes have vastly different COP (e.g., VARS ≈ 0.6 vs. VCRS ≈ 3–5).
- **Refrigerant policy compliance** — phasing out CFCs/HCFCs under Montreal Protocol pushed industry from R-12 → R-22 → R-134a/R-404A/R-717 (NH₃).
- **HACCP integration** — temperature-controlled processing lines depend on correctly classified systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: COP of an Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration System

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is the fundamental performance metric used to compare refrigeration systems across all classes. Below is a rigorous derivation using the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics.

**Step 1 — Define the Refrigeration Effect (heat absorbed in evaporator)**

In an ideal VCRS, the refrigerant enters the evaporator as a low-quality two-phase mixture (state 4) and leaves as a saturated/superheated vapour (state 1). The refrigeration effect per unit mass is:

$$
\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4
$$

This is the **useful cooling** delivered per kilogram of refrigerant circulated.

**Step 2 — Define the Compressor Work Input**

The compressor raises the refrigerant from low pressure $P_1$ to high pressure $P_2$ **isentropically**. Work input per unit mass:

$$
W_{\text{comp}} = h_2 - h_1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the First Law (Energy Conservation)**

For a complete cycle operating as a control volume in steady state, by the First Law:

$$
\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_L + Q_H = W_{\text{comp}}
$$

But more directly, for the cycle:

$$
Q_H = h_2 - h_3
$$

where $Q_H$ is the heat rejected at the condenser.

**Step 4 — Apply the Second Law (Carnot Reference)**

For the *ideal* (reversible) cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at $T_L$ (low) and $T_H$ (high):

$$
\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Carnot COP**

By definition:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{comp}}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

Substituting $Q_H$ from Step 4:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L}
$$

Factor $Q_L$ from the denominator:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \left( \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1 \right)} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\;}
$$

This is the **theoretical upper limit** of COP for any refrigeration cycle operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A refrigeration system used to preserve fish operates between an evaporator temperature of –10 °C and a condenser temperature of 40 °C. Assuming an ideal (Carnot) cycle, calculate the maximum possible COP.

**Given:**
- $T_L = -10\ °\text{C} = 263.15\ \text{K}$
- $T_H = 40\ °\text{C} = 313.15\ \text{K}$

**Solution:**

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50.0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP} \approx 5.26\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every 1 kW of work input, the ideal system can remove up to **5.26 kW** of heat from the fish cold store. Real systems achieve 40–60% of this theoretical value, i.e., COP ≈ 2.1–3.2.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Classes

| Class | Driving Energy | Typical COP | Refrigerant(s) | Food Industry Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| VCRS | Mechanical work (electricity) | 3.0 – 5.0 | R-134a, R-404A, NH₃ | Cold storage, freezing |
| VARS | Heat (steam, waste heat) | 0.5 – 1.2 | NH₃–H₂O, H₂O–LiBr | Dairy, brewery plants |
| Gas refrigeration | Mechanical work | 1.5 – 2.5 | Air | Pre-cooling in transport |
| Steam jet | Steam | 0.2 – 0.5 | Water | Chilled water in plants |
| Cryogenic | Stored cryogen | N/A | LN₂, LCO₂ | IQF, flash freezing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Classify refrigeration systems based on the thermodynamic cycle. List any four types with one food-industry application each.*

**Model Answer:**
Refrigeration systems are classified based on the thermodynamic cycle into:
1. **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** — used in cold storage for meat and fish.
2. **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** — used in dairy plants where waste steam is available.
3. **Gas refrigeration cycle** — used in aircraft chilling of perishable cargo.
4. **Steam jet refrigeration system** — used for chilled water generation in beverage plants.

*(3 marks — ½ mark for each correct type + ½ mark for application)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define Coefficient of Performance (COP). State the Carnot COP expression for a refrigeration cycle.*

**Model Answer:**
**COP** is defined as the ratio of desired refrigeration effect ($Q_L$) to the work input ($W$):
$$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}$$
For the ideal (reversible/Carnot) cycle operating between low temperature reservoir $T_L$ and high temperature reservoir $T_H$:
$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$
where temperatures are in **Kelvin**.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for formula with units note)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of refrigeration systems used in the food industry based on **(i)** thermodynamic cycle, **(ii)** operating temperature range, and **(iii)** application. Give one example of a food product preserved under each temperature class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage plant for fish operates on an ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration System. The evaporator temperature is –15 °C and the condenser temperature is 35 °C. Determine: **(i)** the Carnot COP, **(ii)** the refrigeration effect per TR, and **(iii)** the compressor work per TR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
- **(i)** Thermodynamic cycle classification: VCRS, VARS, Gas cycle, Steam jet — described with refrigerant and energy source (3 marks).
- **(ii)** Operating temperature classification: HT (0–10 °C) → fruits; MT (–18 to 0 °C) → chilled meat/fish; LT (–40 to –18 °C) → frozen foods; Cryogenic (<–40 °C) → IQF. Examples given (2 marks).
- **(iii)** Application classification: chilling, freezing, cold storage, transport, display, process AC (2 marks).

**Model Solution to (b):**
Given: $T_L = -15 + 273 = 258\ \text{K}$; $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308\ \text{K}$.

**(i) Carnot COP:**
$$
\text{COP} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258}{308 - 258} = \frac{258}{50} = 5.16
$$

**(ii) Refrigeration Effect per TR:**
1 TR = 50.4 kJ/min = 3.517 kW. The refrigeration effect *per unit mass* is $h_1 - h_4$, but the *capacity* per TR is fixed:
$$
Q_L = 3.517\ \text{kW (per TR)}
$$

**(iii) Compressor work per TR:**
$$
W = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}} = \frac{3.517}{5.16} = 0.682\ \text{kW per TR}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare **VCRS** and **VARS** with respect to energy source, components, COP range, typical refrigerants, and suitability for the food industry. Use a comparison table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refrigeration system is required to chill 2,000 kg of milk from 30 °C to 4 °C per hour. Given specific heat of milk $c_p = 3.9\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, calculate: **(i)** the heat load in kW, **(ii)** the required refrigeration capacity in TR, and **(iii)** the compressor work if COP = 3.5. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
Tabular comparison with five parameters × 1.4 marks each. Key entries:

| Parameter | VCRS | VARS |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Mechanical work (electricity) | Heat (steam, waste heat) |
| Key components | Compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator | Generator, absorber, pump, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator |
| Typical COP | 3.0 – 5.0 | 0.5 – 1.2 |
| Refrigerants | R-134a, R-404A, NH₃ | NH₃–H₂O, H₂O–LiBr |
| Food industry use | Cold storage, freezing, retail | Dairy, brewery, large plants with waste heat |

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Heat load:**
$$
Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 2000 \times 3.9 \times (30 - 4) = 2000 \times 3.9 \times 26 = 202{,}800\ \text{kJ/h}
$$

Convert to kW:
$$
Q = \frac{202{,}800}{3600} = 56.33\ \text{kW}
$$

**(ii) Capacity in TR:**
$$
\text{TR} = \frac{Q}{3.517} = \frac{56.33}{3.517} \approx 16.0\ \text{TR}
$$

**(iii) Compressor work:**
$$
W = \frac{Q}{\text{COP}} = \frac{56.33}{3.5} \approx 16.1\ \text{kW}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature units:** Always convert °C to **Kelvin** before applying Carnot COP — using °C directly is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify refrigerant:** Listing VCRS without naming a refrigerant (e.g., R-404A) loses **½ mark** in classification questions.
> 3. **Confusing COP with efficiency:** COP of a heat pump ≠ COP of a refrigerator. COP of a heat pump = COP of refrigerator + 1.
> 4. **Sign convention errors:** Refrigeration effect $Q_L$ is always **positive** by convention (heat absorbed by refrigerant).
> 5. **In VARS questions:** Many students forget to include the **pump work** in the total work input — KTU examiners specifically look for this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Classification of refrigeration systems** is organized along **six axes**: cycle, temperature, compressor, stage, refrigerant, and application.
- ✅ **VCRS is the dominant system** in the food industry due to high COP (3–5), compactness, and reliability.
- ✅ **VARS is preferred** where waste heat or solar energy is available (COP < 1 but energy cost is near zero).
- ✅ **Carnot COP** = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ is the **theoretical maximum** — real systems achieve 40–60% of this.
- ✅ **Always use Kelvin** in thermodynamic COP calculations.
- ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** — standard unit in food industry cooling capacity.
- ✅ **Ammonia (NH₃, R-717)** is widely used in large food cold storages due to high COP and zero ODP.
- ✅ **Cascade systems** (e.g., NH₃ + CO₂) are used for very low temperatures (–40 °C to –80 °C).
- ✅ **Cryogenic freezing** with LN₂ is used for IQF (Individual Quick Freezing) of premium products.
- ✅ **Secondary refrigerants** (brines, glycols) are used when direct refrigerant contact with food must be avoided.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensible Heat Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sensible Heat Factor # Module 3 — Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > Sensible Heat Factor is a fundamental psychrometric ratio that governs the slope of the *process line* on the psychrometric chart. Every air-conditioning cooling/heating coil selection problem in KTU examinations hinges on this single ratio. ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition The **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** of an air-conditioning process is defined as the ratio of the *sensible heat* transferred to (or from) the air to the *total heat* transferred to (or from) the air during the conditioning process. $$\boxed{\text{SHF} \;=\; \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_\ell} \;=\; \frac{Q_s}{Q_t}}$$ Where: - $Q_s$ = Sensible Heat Load of the air (kW) - $Q_\ell$ = Latent Heat Load of the air (kW) - $Q_t$ = Total Heat Load, $Q_s + Q_\ell$ (kW) Numerically, SHF always lies between **0** (pure latent process, e.g., pure humidification) and **1** (pure sensible process, e.g., pure sensible cooling/heating). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spice & Sauce" Recipe > [!NOTE] > Imagine you are cooking a curry. The *sensible heat* is the heat that changes the **temperature** of the dish (like the dry spices you toast — they only warm up, they don't release moisture). The *latent heat* is the heat that changes the **moisture** content (like the coconut milk you add — it condenses/evaporates but stays at the same temperature while the phase change occurs). > **SHF is simply the fraction of the total cooking effort that came from the dry spices alone.** If SHF = 0.8, then 80 % of the heat exchange was "sensible" (temperature-changing) and only 20 % was "latent" (moisture-changing). ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants Used with SHF | Constant | Symbol | Value | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Specific heat of dry air | $c_{pa}$ | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** | Used for $Q_s$ calculation | | Specific heat of water vapour | $c_{pv}$ | **1.88 kJ/kg·K** | Used in moist-air enthalpy | | Latent heat of vaporisation | $h_{fg}$ | **2501 kJ/kg** | At 0 °C reference | | $h_{fg}$ at typical AC conditions | $h_{fg}$ | **~2520 – 2560 kJ/kg** | At coil surface temperatures | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Air-Conditioning Process When moist air is conditioned in a cooling coil, two simultaneous phenomena occur: 1. **Sensible Cooling:** The dry-bulb temperature (DBT) drops from $T_1$ to $T_2$. 2. **Dehumidification:** Moisture condenses on the cold coil surface; specific humidity drops from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$. The combined process on the psychrometric chart is a straight line connecting state **1** (entering air) to state **2** (leaving air). The slope of this line — measured against the **enthalpy (vertical) axis** — is exactly the **Sensible Heat Factor**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is SHF a slope?** > On the psychrometric chart, the horizontal axis is *humidity ratio* $\omega$ (latent coordinate) and the vertical axis is *enthalpy* $h$ (total heat). The ratio of the vertical drop attributable to sensible cooling to the total vertical drop gives the SHF. Hence, **the SHF line on the psychrometric chart is a straight line drawn through state 1 at an angle governed by the SHF.** ## 2.2 Two Critical Variants of SHF In real air-conditioning design (especially for food-industry cold rooms and process halls), **two** distinct SHF values exist: ### (a) Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF) The SHF of the conditioned space alone — ratio of room sensible heat to total room heat. $$\text{RSHF} \;=\; \frac{Q_{rs}}{Q_{rs} + Q_{r\ell}}$$ ### (b) Grand Sensible Heat Factor (GSHF) When **outdoor ventilation air** is mixed with return air, the total load on the coil changes. The GSHF is the SHF of the *combined* return-air-plus-outdoor-air load. $$\text{GSHF} \;=\; \frac{Q_{rs} + Q_{os}}{Q_{rs} + Q_{os} + Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell}}$$ ### (c) Effective Sensible Heat Factor (ESHF) The coil must actually handle the load **after** the ventilation air has contributed its share of latent heat. The ESHF is the SHF the **cooling coil** must deliver. $$\text{ESHF} \;=\; \frac{Q_{rs}}{Q_{rs} + (Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell} - \text{Latent handled by mixing})}$$ > [!NOTE] > **In KTU exam problems, you will almost always be asked to find:** > (i) RSHF from given room loads, > (ii) GSHF after adding ventilation/outdoor air, > (iii) ESHF that the coil must satisfy, and finally > (iv) Plot the process line on the psychrometric chart using the SHF line drawn from the room state through the GSHF and terminating at the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)**. ## 2.3 The Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) and SHF Line The **SHF line** is the locus of all possible leaving states from a cooling coil for a given entering condition and a chosen ADP. To use it: 1. Calculate ESHF for the coil. 2. From the entering air state (point 1), draw a straight line through the **GSHF ratio** (slope determined by GSHF). 3. The intersection of this line with the saturation curve ($\phi = 100\%$) is the **ADP**. The relationship is captured in the elegant triangle relation: $$\boxed{\text{SHF} \;=\; \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{(T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}})}}$$ This is the equation you will use most frequently in KTU Part-B problems. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — every KTU Part-B question on Module 3 will draw from these.** | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_\ell}$ | Basic SHF definition | | 2 | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \, c_{pa} \, (T_1 - T_2)$ | Sensible heat load (kW), $c_{pa}$ in kJ/kg·K | | 3 | $Q_\ell = \dot{m}_a \, h_{fg} \, (\omega_1 - \omega_2)$ | Latent heat load (kW) | | 4 | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{c_{pa} \, \Delta T}{c_{pa} \, \Delta T + h_{fg} \, \Delta \omega}$ | SHF from psychrometric differences | | 5 | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)}{(T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}})}$ | Triangle relation on psychrometric chart | | 6 | $\text{GSHF} = \dfrac{Q_{rs} + Q_{os}}{Q_{rs} + Q_{os} + Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell}}$ | When outdoor air is mixed | | 7 | $\text{ESHF} = \dfrac{Q_{rs}}{Q_{rs} + Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell} - Q_{Lv}}$ | Coil must satisfy this | | 8 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q_s}{1.2 \, c_{pa} \, \Delta T}$ | Required supply airflow rate (m³/s) | | 9 | $\text{BPF} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{\text{ADP}}}{T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}}}$ | Bypass Factor — coil inefficiency | | 10 | $\text{ADP} = T_1 - \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)}{\text{SHF}}$ | Back-calculating ADP from SHF | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Food Cold Rooms (Storage of fruits, vegetables, dairy):** SHF here is typically **0.75 – 0.95** because product respiration, door-opening and lighting create *dominant sensible* loads, but moisture from produce creates a small latent component. - **Process Halls (Meat, Bakery Fermentation):** SHF drops to **0.5 – 0.7** because of high moisture generation from product and personnel. - **Hospital OT / Pharma Clean Rooms:** Strict SHF control is needed to keep relative humidity between **40 – 55 %** to inhibit microbial growth — here SHF ≈ **0.65 – 0.80**. - **Banana Ripening Chambers:** SHF ≈ **0.80 – 0.85** with elevated ethylene control. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: SHF in Terms of Psychrometric Properties **Starting Point — Definition of SHF:** $$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_t} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_\ell}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the expressions for $Q_s$ and $Q_\ell$:** The sensible heat transferred to moist air when its temperature changes by $\Delta T = (T_1 - T_2)$ is: $$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \, c_{pa} \, (T_1 - T_2)$$ The latent heat transferred when moisture condenses by $\Delta \omega = (\omega_1 - \omega_2)$ is: $$Q_\ell = \dot{m}_a \, h_{fg} \, (\omega_1 - \omega_2)$$ Substituting into the SHF expression: $$\text{SHF} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \, c_{pa} \, (T_1 - T_2)}{\dot{m}_a \, c_{pa} \, (T_1 - T_2) \;+\; \dot{m}_a \, h_{fg} \, (\omega_1 - \omega_2)}$$ **Step 2 — Cancel the common mass flow rate $\dot{m}_a$:** $$\text{SHF} = \frac{c_{pa} \, (T_1 - T_2)}{c_{pa} \, (T_1 - T_2) \;+\; h_{fg} \, (\omega_1 - \omega_2)}$$ **Step 3 — Final compact form:** $$\boxed{\text{SHF} = \frac{c_{pa} \, \Delta T}{c_{pa} \, \Delta T + h_{fg} \, \Delta \omega}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This derivation shows that **SHF is independent of the mass flow rate of air** — it depends only on the *thermodynamic state changes*. This is why a single SHF value uniquely identifies a process line on the psychrometric chart. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: The SHF Triangle Relation **Geometric Setup:** On the psychrometric chart, draw: - Point **1** (entering air) at $(h_1, \omega_1)$ - Point **2** (leaving air) at $(h_2, \omega_2)$ - Point **ADP** at the intersection of the SHF line with the saturation curve at enthalpy $h_{\text{ADP}}$ The total vertical distance from point 1 to ADP (in enthalpy units) represents **total heat removed per kg of dry air**: $$h_1 - h_{\text{ADP}} = c_{pa}(T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}}) + h_{fg}(\omega_1 - \omega_{\text{ADP}})$$ The vertical distance from point 1 to point 2 represents **sensible-plus-latent removed per kg of dry air**: $$h_1 - h_2 = c_{pa}(T_1 - T_2) + h_{fg}(\omega_1 - \omega_2)$$ **Step 1 — Definition by similarity of triangles:** Because points 1, 2 and ADP are collinear on the SHF line: $$\text{SHF} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{\text{ADP}}}$$ **Step 2 — For an ideal coil where the leaving temperature equals the ADP temperature *along the SHF line itself*:** For a coil that achieves its ADP perfectly along the SHF line, the **wet-bulb temperature** of the leaving air equals the **wet-bulb temperature at ADP**. By the linearity of the wet-bulb scale on the psychrometric chart: $$\boxed{\text{SHF} = \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{(T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}})} = \frac{(\text{WBT}_1 - \text{WBT}_2)}{(\text{WBT}_1 - \text{WBT}_{\text{ADP}})}}$$ **Step 3 — Rearranging for ADP (useful in back-calculation):** $$\text{SHF} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}}}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$\text{SHF} \cdot (T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}}) = T_1 - T_2$$ $$T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\text{SHF}}$$ $$T_{\text{ADP}} = T_1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\text{SHF}}$$ $$\boxed{T_{\text{ADP}} = T_1 - \frac{\Delta T}{\text{SHF}}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Grand Sensible Heat Factor (GSHF) **Step 1 — Total room loads:** Let $Q_{rs}$ = room sensible heat, $Q_{r\ell}$ = room latent heat. **Step 2 — Outdoor air loads:** Let $Q_{os}$ = outdoor sensible heat, $Q_{o\ell}$ = outdoor latent heat. These are calculated from: $$Q_{os} = \dot{m}_{o} \, c_{pa} \, (T_o - T_R)$$ $$Q_{o\ell} = \dot{m}_{o} \, h_{fg} \, (\omega_o - \omega_R)$$ where subscript $o$ = outdoor, $R$ = room/return. **Step 3 — Total combined load on the coil:** $$Q_{t,\text{grand}} = Q_{rs} + Q_{os} + Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell}$$ **Step 4 — Total combined sensible load:** $$Q_{s,\text{grand}} = Q_{rs} + Q_{os}$$ **Step 5 — GSHF definition:** $$\boxed{\text{GSHF} = \frac{Q_{rs} + Q_{os}}{Q_{rs} + Q_{os} + Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell}}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Effective Sensible Heat Factor (ESHF) The coil must remove: - All room sensible heat: $Q_{rs}$ - All room latent heat: $Q_{r\ell}$ - Outdoor air latent heat: $Q_{o\ell}$ (since moisture in outdoor air must be condensed out) - *But* the outdoor air has been **mixed** with return air — the latent heat of this mixing is partially handled by the enthalpy of mixing itself. The effective latent heat the coil must handle is: $$Q_{\ell,\text{eff}} = Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell} - \dot{m}_o \, (h_{o} - h_{R,\text{at } T_o}) \cdot \text{(mixing correction)}$$ A simpler engineering form (used in KTU problems): $$\boxed{\text{ESHF} = \frac{Q_{rs}}{Q_{rs} + Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell} - \text{(latent already handled by OA in mixing)}}}$$ In practical KTU problems, this often reduces to: $$\text{ESHF} \approx \frac{Q_{rs}}{Q_{rs} + Q_{r\ell,\text{net}}}$$ where $Q_{r\ell,\text{net}}$ is the *net* latent load on the coil after accounting for outdoor air mixing. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration **Problem:** Air enters a cooling coil at $T_1 = 30 °C$ DBT, $\phi_1 = 60\%$ RH. It leaves at $T_2 = 20 °C$ DBT, $\phi_2 = 90\%$ RH. Find: (a) SHF of the process (b) ADP of the coil if SHF line passes through the entering state **Given (from psychrometric chart or steam tables):** - $\omega_1 = 0.0160$ kg/kg dry air (at 30 °C, 60 % RH) - $\omega_2 = 0.0130$ kg/kg dry air (at 20 °C, 90 % RH) - $\Delta T = 30 - 20 = 10 °C$ - $\Delta \omega = 0.0160 - 0.0130 = 0.0030$ kg/kg **Solution (a):** Using the formula from Derivation 1: $$\text{SHF} = \frac{c_{pa} \, \Delta T}{c_{pa} \, \Delta T + h_{fg} \, \Delta \omega}$$ Substituting $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $h_{fg} = 2520$ kJ/kg (at ~20 °C average): $$\text{SHF} = \frac{1.005 \times 10}{1.005 \times 10 + 2520 \times 0.0030}$$ $$\text{SHF} = \frac{10.05}{10.05 + 7.56}$$ $$\text{SHF} = \frac{10.05}{17.61}$$ $$\boxed{\text{SHF} \approx 0.571}$$ **Solution (b):** From the triangle relation: $$\text{ADP} = T_1 - \frac{\Delta T}{\text{SHF}} = 30 - \frac{10}{0.571}$$ $$\text{ADP} = 30 - 17.51 \approx 12.5 °C$$ This ADP is the coil surface temperature at which the SHF line intersects the saturation curve. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SHF-Based AC System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Room State Point1
DBT1 WBT1 RH1] --> B[Room Heat Load
RSH plus RLH] B --> C[Compute RSHF
RSH by total heat] O[Outdoor Air PointO
DBTo WBTo RHo] --> D[Outdoor Load
OSH plus OLH] D --> E[Compute GSHF
grand SHF] C --> E E --> F[Determine ESHF
effective coil SHF] F --> G[SHF Line Plot
on psychrometric chart] G --> H[Find ADP
intersection with sat curve] H --> I[Select Cooling Coil
ADP and BPF] I --> J[Compute Coil
Leaving State Point2] J --> K[Verify SHF matches
calculated value] K --> L[Final AC Design
airflow and tonnage] ``` ## 4.2 Process-Line Geometry on the Psychrometric Chart ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Chart[Psychrometric Chart SHF Geometry] direction LR A1[State1
Room Return Air] -->|Sensible plus Latent line| A2[State2
Coil Leaving Air] A1 -->|SHF straight line| A3[ADP
Apparatus Dew Point] A2 -->|parallel horizontal axis| A4[omega axis latent] A1 -->|vertical axis| A5[h axis total enthalpy] A3 -->|intersects 100 percent RH| A6[Saturation Curve] end ``` ## 4.3 Relationship Between RSHF, GSHF, ESHF ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Room Sensible and Latent
RSH and RLH] --> R1[RSHF] R1 --> R2[Pure room process line] OA[Outdoor Air Load
OSensible and OLatent] --> M[Mixing Plenum
OA plus RA] R --> M M --> G[GSHF
grand SHF after mixing] G --> E[ESHF
coil must satisfy] E --> COIL[Cooling Coil
selects ADP and BPF] COIL --> SA[Supply Air
to room] SA --> R ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagrams:** The SHF is the *direction* of the process line. The ADP is the *destination* of the line where it touches the saturation curve. The BPF (Bypass Factor) determines *how close the leaving state gets to the ADP along the SHF line*. All three — SHF, ADP, BPF — are interdependent and must be solved simultaneously. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Sensible Heat Factor. Why is it called the slope of the process line on the psychrometric chart?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** is defined as the ratio of the sensible heat exchanged by the air to the total heat (sensible + latent) exchanged during the conditioning process. $$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_\ell} = \frac{c_{pa} \, \Delta T}{c_{pa} \, \Delta T + h_{fg} \, \Delta \omega}$$ It is called the **slope of the process line** because, on a psychrometric chart with enthalpy on the vertical axis and humidity ratio on the horizontal axis, the total heat removed ($Q_t$) is proportional to the vertical projection of the process line. The sensible heat removed ($Q_s$) is proportional to the component of that vertical projection corresponding to the dry-bulb cooling. Hence, the *ratio* of the sensible vertical drop to the *total* vertical drop equals the SHF — geometrically, this is the slope of the line joining the entering state to the leaving state. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Distinguish between GSHF and ESHF.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | GSHF (Grand SHF) | ESHF (Effective SHF) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Ratio of total sensible heat (room + outdoor) to total heat (room + outdoor) in the system | Ratio of room sensible heat to the *effective* total heat that the cooling coil must handle | | **Accounts for** | Mixed air load before entering coil | Coil load after outdoor air mixing effects | | **Use** | Plotting the process line on the psychrometric chart through point 1 | Selecting the cooling coil rating and ADP | | **Typical value** | Always between RSHF and ESHF | Always less than GSHF (since latent of OA is partially handled) | GSHF is generally **higher** than ESHF because the coil sees a reduced latent load compared to the grand total. **(3 marks)** --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question B1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive an expression for the Sensible Heat Factor in terms of the dry-bulb temperature change $\Delta T$ and humidity ratio change $\Delta \omega$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A food processing hall has a sensible heat load of $18$ kW and a latent heat load of $6$ kW. The outdoor air conditions are $35 °C$ DBT and $28 °C$ WBT; the room is maintained at $24 °C$ DBT and $50\%$ RH. The outdoor air fraction by mass is $25\%$ of the supply air. Determine: 1. RSHF and GSHF of the system. 2. SHF that the cooling coil must handle (ESHF), assuming outdoor air latent load is fully handled by the coil. 3. The Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) of the coil if the supply air temperature is $14 °C$. **Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** *(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Key steps for answer sheet:)* Step 1: Start from $\text{SHF} = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_\ell)$ — **(1 mark)** Step 2: Substitute $Q_s = \dot{m}_a c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2)$ and $Q_\ell = \dot{m}_a h_{fg} (\omega_1 - \omega_2)$ — **(2 marks)** Step 3: Cancel $\dot{m}_a$ from numerator and denominator — **(1 mark)** Step 4: Arrive at the compact form $\text{SHF} = c_{pa} \Delta T / (c_{pa} \Delta T + h_{fg} \Delta \omega)$ — **(2 marks)** **Assumptions** **(1 mark):** - Steady-state, uniform airflow - No heat gain/loss from ductwork - $c_{pa}$ and $h_{fg}$ are taken at average air temperature --- **Solution (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Calculate RSHF (1.5 marks):** $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_\ell} = \frac{18}{18 + 6} = \frac{18}{24} = 0.75$$ **Step 2 — Find outdoor air properties (1 mark):** At $T_o = 35 °C$ DBT, WBT $= 28 °C$: - $h_o \approx 90.0$ kJ/kg dry air (from psychrometric chart / tables) - $\omega_o \approx 0.0218$ kg/kg dry air At room state, $T_R = 24 °C$ DBT, $\phi = 50\%$: - $h_R \approx 47.8$ kJ/kg dry air - $\omega_R \approx 0.0093$ kg/kg dry air **Step 3 — Outdoor air loads (1.5 marks):** Take total supply air mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \dot{m}_o + \dot{m}_R$, where $\dot{m}_o = 0.25 \dot{m}$. Outdoor sensible heat (per unit mass of supply air): $$Q_{os} = 0.25 \dot{m} \times 1.005 \times (35 - 24) = 0.25 \dot{m} \times 11.055 = 2.764 \, \dot{m}$$ Outdoor latent heat: $$Q_{o\ell} = 0.25 \dot{m} \times 2520 \times (0.0218 - 0.0093) = 0.25 \dot{m} \times 31.5 = 7.875 \, \dot{m}$$ **Step 4 — Compute GSHF (1.5 marks):** $$\text{GSHF} = \frac{Q_{rs} + Q_{os}}{Q_{rs} + Q_{os} + Q_{r\ell} + Q_{o\ell}}$$ Expressing in units of $\dot{m}$: $$\text{GSHF} = \frac{18/\dot{m} + 2.764}{18/\dot{m} + 2.764 + 6/\dot{m} + 7.875} = \frac{20.764}{27.639} \approx 0.751$$ **Step 5 — ESHF (assuming full OA latent handled by coil) (1 mark):** The room latent is $6/\dot{m} = 6.0$ per unit $\dot{m}$, plus outdoor latent $7.875$ — total coil latent = $13.875$. Room sensible = $18.0$. $$\text{ESHF} = \frac{18.0}{18.0 + 13.875} = \frac{18.0}{31.875} \approx 0.565$$ **Step 6 — ADP using triangle relation (0.5 marks):** Room return air at $T_1 = 24 °C$ leaves coil at $T_2 = 14 °C$: $$\text{ADP} = T_1 - \frac{\Delta T}{\text{SHF}} = 24 - \frac{24 - 14}{0.565} = 24 - \frac{10}{0.565}$$ $$\boxed{\text{ADP} \approx 6.3 °C}$$ --- ### Question B2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative] **(a)** With the aid of a neat psychrometric sketch, explain the procedure for locating the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) of a cooling coil given the room SHF, outdoor conditions, and ventilation rate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cold storage for mangoes is maintained at $12 °C$ DBT, $85\%$ RH. The room sensible load is $4.5$ kW and the latent load (from fruit respiration and door operation) is $1.2$ kW. The outdoor air is at $34 °C$ DBT, $60\%$ RH, and the ventilation rate is $8\%$ of supply air. Compute: 1. RSHF of the cold room. 2. GSHF of the system. 3. The required ADP of the cooling coil if the supply air leaves at $6 °C$ DBT. **Solution (a) — Sketch and Procedure (7 marks):** **Sketch description (3 marks):** A psychrometric chart must be drawn showing: - Point 1 (room/return air state) marked at $T_R$, $\phi_R$ - The SHF line drawn from point 1 through the chart - Point 2 (coil leaving air) marked at lower temperature - The ADP marked at the intersection of the SHF line with the 100 % saturation curve - Vertical lines from points 1 and 2 to the temperature axis showing $\Delta T$ - The saturation curve explicitly drawn **Procedure steps (4 marks):** 1. Calculate RSHF = $Q_{rs}/(Q_{rs} + Q_{r\ell})$ — **(0.5 mark)** 2. Calculate outdoor air sensible and latent loads based on ventilation fraction — **(1 mark)** 3. Calculate GSHF = $(Q_{rs}+Q_{os})/(Q_{rs}+Q_{os}+Q_{r\ell}+Q_{o\ell})$ — **(0.5 mark)** 4. From point 1 on the psychrometric chart, draw a line at angle $\tan^{-1}(\text{GSHF})$ with respect to the horizontal — **(1 mark)** 5. The intersection of this line with the saturation curve gives the ADP — **(1 mark)** --- **Solution (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 — RSHF (1 mark):** $$\text{RSHF} = \frac{4.5}{4.5 + 1.2} = \frac{4.5}{5.7} \approx 0.789$$ **Step 2 — Outdoor air properties (1 mark):** At $34 °C$ DBT, $60\%$ RH: - $\omega_o \approx 0.0192$ kg/kg dry air - $h_o \approx 82.5$ kJ/kg dry air At room $12 °C$ DBT, $85\%$ RH: - $\omega_R \approx 0.0074$ kg/kg dry air - $h_R \approx 30.5$ kJ/kg dry air **Step 3 — Compute $\dot{m}$ from room sensible load (1 mark):** $$Q_{rs} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{pa} \cdot (T_R - T_s)$$ Assuming supply temperature $T_s = 6 °C$: $$\dot{m} = \frac{4.5}{1.005 \times (12 - 6)} = \frac{4.5}{6.03} \approx 0.746 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 4 — Outdoor air loads (1.5 marks):** $\dot{m}_o = 0.08 \times \dot{m} = 0.0597$ kg/s $$Q_{os} = 0.0597 \times 1.005 \times (34 - 12) = 0.0597 \times 22.11 = 1.32 \text{ kW}$$ $$Q_{o\ell} = 0.0597 \times 2520 \times (0.0192 - 0.0074) = 0.0597 \times 29.69 = 1.77 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 — GSHF (1 mark):** $$\text{GSHF} = \frac{4.5 + 1.32}{4.5 + 1.32 + 1.2 + 1.77} = \frac{5.82}{8.79} \approx 0.662$$ **Step 6 — Required ADP (1.5 marks):** Since the coil must achieve the supply temperature of $6 °C$ from the room state of $12 °C$: $$\text{SHF} = \frac{(T_R - T_s)}{(T_R - T_{\text{ADP}})}$$ $$0.662 = \frac{12 - 6}{12 - T_{\text{ADP}}} = \frac{6}{12 - T_{\text{ADP}}}$$ $$12 - T_{\text{ADP}} = \frac{6}{0.662} = 9.06$$ $$\boxed{T_{\text{ADP}} \approx 2.94 °C}$$ This is below 0 °C — in a real mango cold storage, this would risk *chilling injury* and frost formation on the coil. The KTU takeaway: **always check that ADP > 0 °C for fresh-produce cold rooms.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to cancel $\dot{m}_a$** in the SHF derivation — full 2-mark penalty (Examiner's note: SHF must be expressed *per unit mass*, not per unit time). > 2. **Confusing RSHF with GSHF with ESHF** — these are *three different quantities*; using them interchangeably loses 3–4 marks per question. > 3. **Using $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg for coil surface temperature calculations** — should use $h_{fg}$ at the *ADP temperature*, which is closer to 2520–2560 kJ/kg. > 4. **Forgetting the outdoor air latent load** in GSHF — common 2-mark deduction. > 5. **Not drawing the SHF line on the psychrometric chart** in the descriptive answer — even a rough sketch showing the line from state 1 to ADP earns the diagram marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Sensible Heat Factor** - **Definition:** SHF is the ratio $Q_s / (Q_s + Q_\ell)$ — always between 0 and 1. - **Three Forms of SHF to Master:** - RSHF (room alone) — *highest* numerically - GSHF (room + outdoor air mixed) — *intermediate* - ESHF (effective load on coil) — *lowest* - **Order:** $\text{ESHF} \le \text{GSHF} \le \text{RSHF}$ (typical ordering) - **Critical formula on the psychrometric chart:** $\text{SHF} = (T_1 - T_2) / (T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}})$ - **Back-calculation for ADP:** $T_{\text{ADP}} = T_1 - \Delta T / \text{SHF}$ - **Constants to memorise:** $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K; $h_{fg} \approx 2520$ kJ/kg at AC temperatures. - **The SHF line is drawn from state 1 through the calculated SHF slope; the intersection with the 100 % saturation curve is the ADP.** - **Cold-storage rule of thumb:** For fresh fruits/vegetables, ensure ADP > 0 °C to avoid chilling injury and coil frosting. - **Process Bakery rule of thumb:** SHF ≈ 0.5–0.7 due to high moisture generation. - **The SHF is independent of air mass flow rate** — depends only on the *ratio* of state changes. - **In KTU 14-mark problems:** Always compute (i) RSHF, (ii) GSHF with outdoor air correction, (iii) ESHF for coil selection, (iv) ADP using the triangle relation, (v) airflow rate, and (vi) draw a labelled psychrometric chart. - **BPF (Bypass Factor)** = $(T_2 - T_{\text{ADP}}) / (T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}})$ — measures coil inefficiency. - **Always carry units:** $Q$ in kW, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $h_{fg}$ in kJ/kg, $c_p$ in kJ/kg·K.

topics.title: Industrial Air-Conditioning: Winter/Summer/Year-Round Air-Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Industrial Air-Conditioning: Winter/Summer/Year-Round Air-Conditioning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Industrial Air-Conditioning — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Air-Conditioning (IAC)** is the systematic control of **temperature, humidity, air purity (dust/odor), and air motion (circulation)** within an industrial or food-processing enclosure to maintain prescribed environmental conditions that ensure product quality, worker comfort, and process stability, regardless of the external ambient climatic conditions. In food industry applications, IAC is mission-critical because most biochemical, microbiological, and enzymatic reactions in food products are highly sensitive to even small deviations in temperature and humidity. --- ## 1.2 Sub-Classification of Industrial Air-Conditioning Industrial air-conditioning is broadly classified into three operational categories, distinguished by the climatic season they are designed to combat: ### (a) Winter Air-Conditioning (WAC) > [!NOTE] > **Winter Air-Conditioning (WAC)** is the conditioning of air during the **cold and dry** outdoor season. The system must **heat** the air and **add moisture (humidify)** to maintain the required indoor comfort/process conditions. ### (b) Summer Air-Conditioning (SAC) > [!NOTE] > **Summer Air-Conditioning (SAC)** is the conditioning of air during the **hot and humid** outdoor season. The system must **cool** the air and **remove moisture (dehumidify)** to maintain the required indoor conditions. ### (c) Year-Round Air-Conditioning (YAC) > [!NOTE] > **Year-Round Air-Conditioning (YAC)** is a unified, automated HVAC system capable of providing either heating + humidification **OR** cooling + dehumidification (or any combination) **throughout the entire calendar year**, switching automatically based on outdoor weather and indoor load demands. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Cabin" Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of your Car's Climate Control System on a cold winter morning in Kerala versus a hot April afternoon in Palakkad.** > - On a **winter morning** (15 °C, foggy): the AC *heats* the cabin air and the defogger *adds moisture* to the windshield — this is **Winter AC**. > - On a **summer afternoon** (38 °C, 90 % RH): the AC *cools* the cabin and *dries* the air (water drips from the condenser drain) — this is **Summer AC**. > - A **modern climate-controlled car** automatically switches between heating, cooling, humidifying, and dehumidifying depending on what is needed — this is **Year-Round AC**. > > Now simply **scale this from a 4-seater car to a 100-tonne cold storage or a banana-ripening chamber** — and you have Industrial Air-Conditioning! --- ## 1.4 Standard Reference Design Conditions (KTU Convention) For calculation consistency, the KTU/ISHRAE-recommended **indoor design conditions** for food industry applications are: | Parameter | Standard Indoor Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Dry Bulb Temperature ($DBT_i$) | **25 °C ± 1 °C** | °C | | Relative Humidity ($RH_i$) | **50 % ± 5 %** | % | | Air Velocity | < **0.25** | m/s | | Air Cleanliness | < **10** μg/m³ dust | μg/m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > These indoor values are the **target state** — the supply air must be *processed* from any outdoor state (OAC) to reach these indoor values (IAC) via the psychrometric processes of heating/cooling/humidification/dehumidification. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Winter Air-Conditioning (WAC) — Detailed Working ### 2.1.1 Operating Conditions - Outdoor: Cold + Dry → $DBT_o \approx 5–15 °C$, $RH_o \approx 30–60\%$ - Indoor: Warm + Moderately Humid → $DBT_i \approx 22–25 °C$, $RH_i \approx 40–50\%$ ### 2.1.2 Required Air-Processing Operations The air entering from outdoors must undergo **two sequential processes**: 1. **Sensible Heating**: Raise the temperature of the air without changing its moisture content (the DBT rises at constant specific humidity $W$). 2. **Humidification**: Add water vapor to the air (the $W$ rises, and DB temperature falls slightly due to adiabatic evaporation, approaching the wet-bulb line). ### 2.1.3 Psychrometric Path — Winter AC The classical two-process winter cycle on the psychrometric chart is: $$O \xrightarrow{\text{Heating Coil}} H \xrightarrow{\text{Spray/Air-Washer}} R$$ Where: - **O** = Outdoor Air Condition - **H** = Heated Air Condition (after the heating coil) - **R** = Room Condition (the desired indoor state) ### 2.1.4 Components of a Winter AC Plant | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Air Filter** | Removes dust/particulates from intake air | | **Pre-Heater** | Initial heating to prevent freezing in humidifier | | **Heating Coil (Finned)** | Sensible heating using hot water/steam/electricity | | **Spray-Type Humidifier / Air Washer** | Adiabatic humidification using water sprays | | **Re-Heater (Optional)** | Final temperature trim to match desired supply temperature | | **Blower / Fan** | Circulates air through ducts | | **Automatic Controls** | Modulate heating/humidification based on sensor feedback | ### 2.1.5 Engineering Use Case in Food Industry - Bakery proofing rooms (dough fermentation at ~30 °C, 75 % RH) - Cheese ripening chambers (12–15 °C, 85–90 % RH) - Confectionery enrobing halls - Citrus storage --- ## 2.2 Summer Air-Conditioning (SAC) — Detailed Working ### 2.2.1 Operating Conditions - Outdoor: Hot + Humid → $DBT_o \approx 35–45 °C$, $RH_o \approx 60–90\%$ (e.g., Kerala coast) - Indoor: Cool + Moderately Dry → $DBT_i \approx 22–25 °C$, $RH_i \approx 50–55\%$ ### 2.2.2 Required Air-Processing Operations The air entering from outdoors must undergo **two sequential processes**: 1. **Cooling & Dehumidification**: Pass the air over a chilled cooling coil — the air is cooled below its dew point, causing condensation. Both DBT and $W$ fall. The exit condition lies on a line connecting the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** to the entering air. 2. **Reheating (often required)**: Because overcooling occurs to dehumidify effectively, the air may need to be reheated to the desired supply temperature without adding moisture (sensible heating). ### 2.2.3 Psychrometric Path — Summer AC $$O \xrightarrow{\text{Cooling Coil}} C \xrightarrow{\text{Reheater}} S \rightarrow R$$ Where: - **O** = Outdoor Air Condition - **C** = Condition after cooling/dehumidification coil - **S** = Supply Air Condition (after reheat) - **R** = Room Condition ### 2.2.4 Components of a Summer AC Plant | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Air Filter** | Removes particulates | | **Cooling Coil (Chilled Water/DX)** | Cools and dehumidifies the air via refrigeration | | **Condensate Drain Pan** | Removes condensed moisture | | **Re-Heater (optional)** | Sensible heating after overcooling | | **Blower / Fan** | Air circulation | | **Compressor-Condenser-Expansion Valve Train** | The vapor-compression refrigeration cycle supplying chilled fluid | | **Controls** | Thermostat, humidistat | ### 2.2.5 Engineering Use Case in Food Industry - Meat/fish processing halls - Dairy chilling rooms - Beverage carbonation/bottling rooms - Pharmaceutical-cum-food ingredient storage --- ## 2.3 Year-Round Air-Conditioning (YAC) — Detailed Working ### 2.3.1 Operating Philosophy YAC is a **dual-mode** system capable of switching between WAC and SAC modes automatically. The mode selection is determined by a year-round **enthalpy-based outdoor air sensor** or a temperature/humidity comparator. ### 2.3.2 Modes of Operation in a YAC Plant | Mode | Outdoor Condition | Required Action | |---|---|---| | **Winter Mode** | Cold + Dry | Heating + Humidification | | **Spring/Autumn Mode** | Mild | Possibly just sensible heating/cooling | | **Summer Mode** | Hot + Humid | Cooling + Dehumidification | | **Monsoon Mode** | Warm + Very Humid | Heavy Dehumidification + Sensible Cooling | | **Dry-Summer Mode (e.g., Delhi May)** | Hot + Dry | Evaporative Cooling + Humidification | ### 2.3.3 Components of a Year-Round AC Plant The YAC system integrates **all** components of WAC and SAC into a unified air-handling unit (AHU): 1. **Pre-filter + Secondary filter** 2. **Pre-heater** (steam/hot-water coil) 3. **Cooling coil** (chilled water from chiller or direct expansion) 4. **Re-heater** (steam/hot-water coil, downstream of cooling coil) 5. **Spray-type humidifier / Air washer** 6. **Face & bypass dampers** around the cooling coil (for modulation) 7. **Mixed-air dampers** (return air + fresh air) 8. **Supply + Return fan with VFD** 9. **Microprocessor-based controller** managing all dampers, valves, humidifier ### 2.3.4 The "Bypass Factor" Modulation Strategy > [!IMPORTANT] > A YAC system often uses **face-and-bypass dampers** around the cooling coil. A fraction of warm return air **bypasses** the coil and mixes with the cooled air, achieving precise temperature control without continuous compressor cycling. The fraction that passes through the coil undergoes full cooling, while the bypassed fraction remains at the pre-coil state. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All quantities below are per unit mass of dry air (basis: $m_a$ in kg of dry air/s). | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sensible Heat (Heating or Cooling) | $Q_s = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·°C | | 2 | Latent Heat (Humidification/Dehumid.) | $Q_L = m_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | kW | $h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg | | 3 | Total Heat | $Q_t = Q_s + Q_L = m_a \cdot \Delta h$ | kW | $\Delta h$ = enthalpy change | | 4 | Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) | $SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L}$ | — | Dimensionless; lies on chart | | 5 | Bypass Factor (BPF) of Coil | $BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ | — | $T_1$ = entering, $T_2$ = leaving, $T_{ADP}$ = coil surface temp | | 6 | Contact Factor (CF) of Spray | $CF = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{WB1}}$ | — | For adiabatic saturator | | 7 | Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) | $T_{ADP} \approx T_{WB}$ of entering air | °C | For dehumidifying coil | | 8 | Humidification Effectiveness | $\eta_{hum} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_s - W_1}$ | — | $W_s$ = saturation at $T_1$ | | 9 | By-Passed Air Fraction (modulating damper) | $x_{bp} = \dfrac{T_{supply} - T_{cooled}}{T_{precoil} - T_{cooled}}$ | — | Determines damper position | | 10 | Mixed Air Temperature | $T_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{OA} T_{OA} + \dot{m}_{RA} T_{RA}}{\dot{m}_{OA} + \dot{m}_{RA}}$ | °C | Mass-weighted mixing | | 11 | Mass of dry air from RSHF | $m_a = \dfrac{RSH}{1.005(T_i - T_s)}$ | kg/s | RSH = Room Sensible Heat | | 12 | Apparatus Dew Point for SHF | $ADP = T_i - \dfrac{RSH}{m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (1-BPF)}$ | °C | Used in coil selection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tip for KTU Exams:** Always carry out the **psychrometric analysis on the chart FIRST** to extract $\Delta T$, $\Delta W$, $\Delta h$, and SHF, and *then* plug into the formula. Examiners award **2 marks** for an accurate chart sketch. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Industry | Food Industry Application | Required $DBT_i$ | Required $RH_i$ | AC Type | |---|---|---|---| | Banana Ripening | 15–20 °C | 90–95 % | WAC / YAC (winter heavy humid) | | Apple Cold Storage | 0–4 °C | 90–95 % | YAC (winter mode) | | Bakery Proofing | 30–35 °C | 75–80 % | WAC | | Ice Cream Hardening | -25 to -40 °C | — | SAC (extreme) | | Mushroom Growing | 12–16 °C | 80–90 % | YAC (winter) | | Meat Processing | 7–10 °C | 80–85 % | YAC | | Chocolate Tempering | 18–22 °C | 50–55 % | YAC (strict, narrow band) | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Bypass Factor of a Cooling/Heating Coil ### Physical Setup Consider air entering a finned cooling coil at temperature $T_1$ (with humidity ratio $W_1$). The coil surface is maintained at $T_{ADP}$. A fraction of the air contacts the cold surface fully, while the rest bypasses it. ### Step 1 — Define the Bypass Factor By definition: $$BPF = \frac{\text{Amount bypassed}}{\text{Total amount}} = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$ where: - $T_1$ = dry-bulb temperature entering the coil (°C) - $T_2$ = dry-bulb temperature leaving the coil (°C) - $T_{ADP}$ = apparatus dew point (°C) — the surface temperature of the coil ### Step 2 — Verify Limiting Cases - **Case A — Perfect Coil ($BPF = 0$)**: Air leaves at coil surface temperature → $T_2 = T_{ADP}$. The numerator $T_2 - T_{ADP} = 0$, so $BPF = 0$. ✓ - **Case B — No Coil Effect ($BPF = 1$)**: Air passes through without contacting surface → $T_2 = T_1$. Numerator equals denominator → $BPF = 1$. ✓ ### Step 3 — Derive from Energy Balance Assume adiabatic coil. The mixed leaving air is a mass-weighted average: $$\dot{m}_{total} T_2 = \dot{m}_{bypass} T_1 + \dot{m}_{contact} T_{ADP}$$ Dividing by $\dot{m}_{total}$ and letting $x = \dot{m}_{bypass}/\dot{m}_{total}$: $$T_2 = x T_1 + (1-x) T_{ADP}$$ Rearrange to isolate $x$: $$T_2 - T_{ADP} = x(T_1 - T_{ADP}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$ Since by definition $x = BPF$, the derivation is complete. ∎ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A larger $BPF$ means a less effective coil (smaller contact area, lower surface temperature drop, higher air velocity). For typical industrial chilled water coils, $BPF$ ranges from **0.05 to 0.30**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Apparatus Dew Point from Room Sensible Heat (RSH) and Bypass Factor ### Given Data - Room Sensible Heat: $RSH$ (kW) - Room temperature: $T_i$ (°C) - Mass flow rate of dry air: $m_a$ (kg/s) - Coil Bypass Factor: $BPF$ (known from manufacturer) ### Step 1 — Express the Leaving Coil Temperature The temperature of air leaving the coil in terms of ADP: $$T_2 = T_{ADP} + BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP})$$ ### Step 2 — Relate RSH to Air Temperature Drop Assuming the room sensible heat is absorbed by the supply air (entering at $T_s$, leaving at $T_i$): $$RSH = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_s)$$ For the room, the supply air enters at $T_2$ (after coil), so $T_s = T_2$: $$RSH = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_2)$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute $T_2$ $$RSH = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot \big[T_i - \big\{T_{ADP} + BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP})\big\}\big]$$ $$RSH = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot \big[(T_i - T_{ADP}) - BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP})\big]$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for ADP $$\boxed{T_{ADP} = T_i - \frac{RSH}{m_a \cdot c_p} - BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP} - ??? )}$$ Re-derive cleanly by isolating $T_{ADP}$ from: $$T_2 = T_{ADP}(1 - BPF) + BPF \cdot T_1$$ $$T_2 - BPF \cdot T_1 = T_{ADP}(1 - BPF)$$ $$T_{ADP} = \frac{T_2 - BPF \cdot T_1}{1 - BPF}$$ But $T_2 = T_i - \dfrac{RSH}{m_a c_p}$, therefore: $$\boxed{T_{ADP} = \frac{\left(T_i - \dfrac{RSH}{m_a c_p}\right) - BPF \cdot T_1}{1 - BPF}}$$ This is the standard coil-selection equation used by KTU exam problems. ∎ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Summer AC (KTU Style, 14-Mark Format) ### Problem Statement A food processing hall has the following data: - Outdoor air: $DBT_o = 40 °C$, $W_o = 0.024$ kg/kg d.a. (hot & humid Kerala coast) - Desired indoor: $DBT_i = 25 °C$, $RH_i = 50\%$ - Room sensible heat: $RSH = 12$ kW - Room latent heat: $RLH = 4$ kW - Coil bypass factor: $BPF = 0.20$ - Air supplied at room temp ($T_s = T_i$) **Required:** (a) Apparatus Dew Point and supply mass flow rate. (b) Total refrigeration load in TR. ### Step 1 — Total Heat from RSH and RLH $$Q_t = RSH + RLH = 12 + 4 = 16 \text{ kW}$$ ### Step 2 — Sensible Heat Factor $$SHF = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75$$ ### Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate of Dry Air Using the sensible heat equation (air supplied at room temp, so $\Delta T$ between supply and room is zero in this scenario — we instead use the total enthalpy difference): The outside air enthalpy at $40 °C$, $W = 0.024$: $$h_o = 1.005 \cdot 40 + 0.024 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 40)$$ $$h_o = 40.20 + 0.024 \cdot (2501 + 75.2) = 40.20 + 0.024 \cdot 2576.2$$ $$h_o = 40.20 + 61.83 = 102.03 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$ The desired indoor air enthalpy at $25 °C$, $50\% RH$: $$W_i = 0.0098 \text{ kg/kg d.a. (from psychrometric tables)}$$ $$h_i = 1.005 \cdot 25 + 0.0098 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 25) = 25.125 + 0.0098 \cdot 2548 = 25.125 + 24.97$$ $$h_i = 50.10 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$ ### Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate of Dry Air $$m_a = \frac{Q_t}{h_o - h_i} = \frac{16}{102.03 - 50.10} = \frac{16}{51.93}$$ $$\boxed{m_a = 0.308 \text{ kg/s of dry air}}$$ ### Step 5 — Apparatus Dew Point For the leaving coil state at $T_2$, the room-load method gives: $$T_2 = T_i - \frac{RSH}{m_a c_p} = 25 - \frac{12}{0.308 \times 1.005} = 25 - \frac{12}{0.3095} = 25 - 38.77$$ $$T_2 = -13.77 °C$$ This is below the coil ADP — therefore the coil must operate **below** this, requiring sub-zero chilled water. Recalculating with proper assumption (room supplies air at $T_s = 14 °C$, the cooling coil provides 11 °C of temperature drop): $$T_2 = 14 °C, \quad T_1 = 40 °C$$ $$T_{ADP} = \frac{T_2 - BPF \cdot T_1}{1 - BPF} = \frac{14 - 0.20 \cdot 40}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{14 - 8}{0.80} = \frac{6}{0.80}$$ $$\boxed{T_{ADP} = 7.5 °C}$$ ### Step 6 — Total Refrigeration Load in TR $$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW (heat extraction rate)}$$ $$\text{Load in TR} = \frac{Q_t}{3.517} = \frac{16}{3.517}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Load} = 4.55 \text{ TR}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always carry 4 significant figures in intermediate steps and round only at the final answer. Marks are awarded for **method clarity** — write every equation you use. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — WAC vs SAC vs YAC | Parameter | Winter AC (WAC) | Summer AC (SAC) | Year-Round AC (YAC) | |---|---|---|---| | Outdoor state | Cold, Dry | Hot, Humid | Variable | | Primary process | Heating | Cooling | Both, as needed | | Secondary process | Humidification | Dehumidification | Both, as needed | | Main equipment | Heating coil | Cooling coil | Heating + Cooling coils | | Humidity device | Air washer / Steam injector | Cooling coil (condensation) | Both | | Re-heater needed? | Usually NO (rare) | **YES** (commonly) | **YES** (in summer mode) | | Energy source | Boiler / Heat pump / Electric heater | Vapor compression refrigeration | Both | | COP concern | High COP (heat pumps > 4) | COP ~ 2.5–3.5 | Mode-dependent | | Typical capacity | Heating coil: 30–100 kW | Cooling coil: 50–500 kW | Combined | | Common applications | Proofing, ripening | Meat/dairy halls | Cold storage, ripening, processing | | Seasonal usage | 3–4 months/year | 4–5 months/year | 12 months/year | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Summer Air-Conditioning Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeOA[OUTDOOR AIR

Return air mixes with fresh air at the mixing damper.
