22C 45%RH Bakery Proofing] nodeRM --> nodeRET[RETURN AIR] nodeRET --> nodeMD[MIXING DAMPER] nodeMD --> nodeF2 nodeBOIL[BOILER] -.Hot Water Steam.-> nodeHC nodeBOIL -.Hot Water.-> nodePH nodeCT2[CONTROLLER
Thermostat and Humidistat] -.Modulate.-> nodeHC nodeCT2 -.Modulate.-> nodeSP nodeCT2 -.Modulate.-> nodeMD ``` --- ## 4.3 Year-Round AC — Modular Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INTAKE["INTAKE SECTION"] nodeOA2[OUTDOOR AIR] nodeMD2[RETURN AIR] nodeMIX2[MIXING DAMPER] end subgraph FILTER_BANK["FILTER SECTION"] nodePF[PRE FILTER] nodeSF[SECONDARY FILTER] end subgraph PROCESSING["AIR PROCESSING SECTION"] nodePH2[PRE HEATER] nodeCC2[COOLING COIL] nodeRH2[REHEATER] nodeFBP[FACE BYPASS DAMPERS] nodeHUM2[SPRAY HUMIDIFIER] end subgraph SUPPLY["SUPPLY SECTION"] nodeBL2[SUPPLY FAN] nodeDK2[SUPPLY DUCT] nodeRM2[CONDITIONED ROOM] end nodeOA2 --> nodeMIX2 nodeMD2 --> nodeMIX2 nodeMIX2 --> nodePF --> nodeSF nodeSF --> nodeFBP nodeFBP --> nodeCC2 nodeCC2 --> nodeRH2 nodeRH2 --> nodeHUM2 nodeHUM2 --> nodeBL2 nodeBL2 --> nodeDK2 nodeDK2 --> nodeRM2 nodeRM2 --> nodeMD2 nodePH2 -.Aux.-> nodeFBP nodeCTRL2[CENTRAL CONTROLLER
Enthalpy Based] -.Modulates.-> nodeMIX2 nodeCTRL2 -.Modulates.-> nodeFBP nodeCTRL2 -.Modulates.-> nodeCC2 nodeCTRL2 -.Modulates.-> nodeRH2 nodeCTRL2 -.Modulates.-> nodeHUM2 ``` --- ## 4.4 Psychrometric Process Flow — Winter & Summer Cycles Combined ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph WINTER["WINTER AC PATH"] O1[O Outdoor Cold Dry] -->|Sensible Heating| H1[H Heated] H1 -->|Adiabatic Humidification| R1[R Room 22C 45%RH] end subgraph SUMMER["SUMMER AC PATH"] O2[O Outdoor Hot Humid] -->|Cooling Dehumid| C2[C Cooled Coil] C2 -->|Sensible Reheat| S2[S Supply 14C] S2 -->|Room Load Pickup| R2[R Room 25C 50%RH] end subgraph YEAR["YEAR ROUND AC"] WINTER SUMMER end ``` --- ## 4.5 Schematic — Face-and-Bypass Damper Assembly (Critical for YAC) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeIN[RETURN AIR
25C] --> nodeFBD[FACE AND BYPASS DAMPER ASSEMBLY] nodeFBD --> nodeCOIL[COOLING COIL
Chilled Water 7C] nodeCOIL --> nodeOUTMIX[MIXING CHAMBER
Post Coil] nodeFBD -.Bypass Path.-> nodeOUTMIX nodeOUTMIX --> nodeSPLY[SUPPLY FAN] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **How it works:** When the cooling demand is *high*, the face damper opens (forcing air through the coil) and bypass closes. When demand is *low*, the face damper closes and bypass opens (mixing cooled and warm air for precise modulation). This eliminates compressor short-cycling and is the heart of YAC energy efficiency. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Winter Air Conditioning. List any four essential components of a winter air-conditioning system with their functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Winter Air Conditioning (WAC) is the process of conditioning outdoor air during the cold and dry season to maintain prescribed indoor conditions of temperature and relative humidity by **heating and humidification** of the supply air. **Components (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Filter** — Removes dust and contaminants from intake air. 2. **Pre-Heater** — Initial heating to prevent freezing in downstream humidifier. 3. **Heating Coil** — Sensible heating of air to raise DBT. 4. **Spray Humidifier / Air Washer** — Adiabatic humidification to raise moisture content. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is meant by Year-Round Air Conditioning? Why is it preferred over separate WAC and SAC plants?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Year-Round Air Conditioning (YAC) is a single integrated HVAC plant capable of automatically switching between heating + humidification (winter mode) and cooling + dehumidification (summer mode) depending on the outdoor ambient conditions, throughout all 12 months of the year. **Why Preferred (1.5 marks):** 1. **Space saving** — single AHU replaces two plants. 2. **Lower capital cost** than installing two separate systems. 3. **Automated control** — eliminates manual seasonal changeover. 4. **Energy-efficient** via face-bypass dampers and enthalpy-based modulation. 5. **Consistent indoor conditions** — no transitional season disruption. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **a) Explain with the help of a psychrometric chart the various air-conditioning processes involved in a Winter Air-Conditioning system. (7 marks)** **b) A bakery proofing room requires 1500 m³/h of conditioned air at 30 °C DBT and 75 % RH. Outdoor air is at 15 °C DBT and 40 % RH. Assuming adiabatic humidification, determine: (i) the heat required in the heating coil, (ii) the mass of water added in the humidifier, and (iii) the sensible heat supplied by the re-heater (if any). (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Winter AC Processes (7 marks):** The Winter AC cycle on the psychrometric chart consists of three sequential processes: **Process 1 — Sensible Heating (O → H):** The outdoor air at point O (cold, dry) is passed over a heating coil. The DBT rises from $T_o$ to $T_h$ at constant specific humidity $W_o$. No moisture change occurs. This is represented as a horizontal line on the psychrometric chart moving rightward. **Process 2 — Adiabatic Humidification (H → R):** The heated air passes through a spray chamber where water at the heated DBT is sprayed. The air absorbs moisture evaporatively. The process follows a constant wet-bulb temperature line (approximately). The DBT drops slightly from $T_h$ to $T_r$, while the humidity ratio rises from $W_o$ to $W_r$ until the desired room condition is reached. **Process 3 — Room Load Pickup (R → return):** Once supplied to the room, the air absorbs sensible and latent heat from occupants, equipment, and the product load, leaving at the return air state. > [!NOTE] > **Key formulae on the chart:** > - Sensible heat in coil: $Q_s = m_a c_p (T_h - T_o)$ > - Latent heat in humidifier: $Q_L = m_a h_{fg} (W_r - W_o)$ > - No re-heater is normally required in pure WAC. **Diagram description for answer sheet:** Draw the psychrometric chart with O, H, R points marked, $W$ lines, DBT lines, and show the heating line horizontal, humidification line along constant WBT. **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given Data:** - Supply air: 1500 m³/h at $T_s = 30 °C$, $RH_s = 75\%$ - Outdoor air: $T_o = 15 °C$, $RH_o = 40\%$ - Adiabatic humidification **Step 1 — Specific Volumes and Humidity Ratios (from psychrometric tables):** At $T_o = 15 °C$, $RH_o = 40\%$: $$W_o = 0.0042 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}, \quad v_o = 0.822 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg d.a.}$$ At $T_s = 30 °C$, $RH_s = 75\%$: $$W_s = 0.0213 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}, \quad v_s = 0.879 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg d.a.}$$ **Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate of Dry Air:** $$m_a = \frac{\dot{V}}{v_s} = \frac{1500}{3600 \times 0.879} = \frac{0.4167}{0.879}$$ $$\boxed{m_a = 0.474 \text{ kg/s of dry air}}$$ **Step 3 — Heat Required in Heating Coil:** From $O(15 °C) \to H$ (heated state before humidifier). During adiabatic humidification, the air cools along the constant WBT line. The WBT of outdoor air at $15 °C$, $40\%$ is approximately $\approx 10 °C$. The humidification process is adiabatic (follows constant WBT $\approx 10 °C$). The heated air at point H has $W_o = 0.0042$ and WBT $\approx 10 °C$. The supply air at $30 °C$, $75\%$ has WBT $\approx 26 °C$ — but adiabatic humidification would end at WBT = WBT of spray water. For simplicity, KTU convention: heated air temperature before humidifier $T_h$ can be found from: $$T_h = T_s + \Delta T_{wetbulb}$$ For the humidifier to add moisture by adiabatic means, the supply WBT ($\approx 26 °C$) must equal the spray water temperature. The leaving DBT from humidifier equals $T_s = 30 °C$ (room/supply). Wait — this means spray water is at $26 °C$. Re-interpreting the cycle: The air is heated from $15 °C$ to a higher temperature, then humidified adiabatically down to $30 °C$. The temperature rise in heating coil: The maximum temperature to which outdoor air can be heated while still being able to be adiabatically cooled by humidification to $30 °C$ is found from the condition that the WBT of the heated air equals the WBT of supply ($26 °C$). For typical spray water near $30 °C$, WBT of spray water $\approx 26 °C$. So heated state H is on constant WBT = $26 °C$ line. $$T_h \approx 50 °C \text{ (approximately, from chart)}$$ Then heating coil load: $$Q_s = m_a c_p (T_h - T_o) = 0.474 \times 1.005 \times (50 - 15)$$ $$Q_s = 0.474 \times 1.005 \times 35 = 16.67 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Mass of Water Added in Humidifier:** $$\dot{m}_w = m_a (W_s - W_o) = 0.474 \times (0.0213 - 0.0042)$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_w = 0.00811 \text{ kg/s} = 29.2 \text{ kg/h of water}}$$ **Step 5 — Re-heater Load:** In pure winter AC (no overcooling), no re-heater is required. Hence: $$\boxed{Q_{reheater} = 0 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **a) With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working of a Year-Round Air-Conditioning system. List all major components and explain the role of the face-and-bypass damper. (7 marks)** **b) A year-round AC plant serves a pharmaceutical-food storage room. The outdoor air is at 35 °C DBT and 60 % RH. The desired indoor condition is 22 °C DBT and 50 % RH. The room sensible heat is 8 kW, room latent heat is 3 kW. Coil bypass factor is 0.15. Determine: (i) Mass flow rate of supply air, (ii) Apparatus dew point, (iii) Total refrigeration load in TR. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — YAC Working (7 marks):** A Year-Round Air-Conditioning system is an integrated AHU that can operate in both heating-humidification mode (winter) and cooling-dehumidification mode (summer). The block diagram consists of: 1. **Mixed-air chamber** — mixes return air with fresh outdoor air. 2. **Filter section** — pre-filter + secondary filter for air cleaning. 3. **Pre-heater** — raises temperature above 5 °C before humidification (winter mode) to prevent chiller freeze-up. 4. **Cooling coil** — with face-and-bypass dampers for modulation (summer mode). 5. **Re-heater** — downstream of cooling coil for temperature trim. 6. **Spray humidifier** — adds moisture in winter mode. 7. **Supply fan + ducts** — distribute conditioned air. 8. **Return air path** — recirculates room air to mixing chamber. 9. **Central controller** — micro-processor based, senses outdoor enthalpy, switches modes. **Face-and-Bypass Damper (3 marks alone — important KTU topic):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Function:** It is a modulating damper assembly around the cooling coil. As outdoor conditions vary through the day, the cooling load fluctuates. By opening/closing the bypass portion, the *effective* cooling of air is reduced or increased *without* varying the chilled water temperature or compressor capacity. This provides precise temperature control and saves energy by preventing compressor cycling. **Working:** When full cooling is needed, face damper fully opens, bypass closes — all air through coil. When partial cooling is needed, bypass opens, face damper partially closes — mixing warm bypass air with cooled face air to achieve desired leaving temperature. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** - $T_o = 35 °C$, $RH_o = 60\%$ → $W_o = 0.0192$ kg/kg d.a. (from tables) - $T_i = 22 °C$, $RH_i = 50\%$ → $W_i = 0.0082$ kg/kg d.a. - $RSH = 8$ kW, $RLH = 3$ kW - $BPF = 0.15$ **Step 1 — Total Load:** $$Q_t = RSH + RLH = 8 + 3 = 11 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Enthalpies (from psychrometric tables):** $$h_o = 1.005(35) + 0.0192[2501 + 1.88(35)] = 35.175 + 0.0192(2566.8) = 35.175 + 49.28$$ $$h_o = 84.46 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$ $$h_i = 1.005(22) + 0.0082[2501 + 1.88(22)] = 22.11 + 0.0082(2542.4) = 22.11 + 20.85$$ $$h_i = 42.96 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$ **Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate:** $$\boxed{m_a = \frac{Q_t}{h_o - h_i} = \frac{11}{84.46 - 42.96} = \frac{11}{41.50} = 0.265 \text{ kg/s}}$$ **Step 4 — Apparatus Dew Point:** Supply air leaves coil at $T_2$. Assume supply temperature $T_s = T_i - RSH/(m_a c_p)$: $$T_s = 22 - \frac{8}{0.265 \times 1.005} = 22 - \frac{8}{0.266} = 22 - 30.07$$ This negative temperature indicates the supply temperature is too low — recalculating with the assumption that supply air enters at $T_s = 14 °C$ (typical 8 °C below room): Using coil leaving temperature $T_2 = 14 °C$ and coil entering temperature $T_1 = 35 °C$: $$T_{ADP} = \frac{T_2 - BPF \cdot T_1}{1 - BPF} = \frac{14 - 0.15 \times 35}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{14 - 5.25}{0.85} = \frac{8.75}{0.85}$$ $$\boxed{T_{ADP} = 10.29 °C}$$ **Step 5 — Refrigeration Load in TR:** $$\text{Load} = \frac{Q_t}{3.517} = \frac{11}{3.517}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Load} = 3.13 \text{ TR}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout > > **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Mixing up SHF and BPF** — SHF is a *property of the room load*; BPF is a *property of the cooling coil*. They are NOT the same. (Loss: 2 marks) > 2. **Forgetting to add re-heater in summer AC** — When the cooling coil overcools to dehumidify, reheat is needed; omitting the re-heater in the schematic loses 2 marks. > 3. **Wrong sign convention in humidification** — Humidification increases $W$ (moisture), so write $\Delta W = W_{final} - W_{initial}$, not the reverse. (Loss: 1 mark) > 4. **Not converting kg/h to kg/s or kW to TR** — Final numerical answers must be in KTU-expected units. (Loss: 1 mark) > 5. **Missing psychrometric chart sketch** — Even in numerical problems, a small sketch with points O, H, C, R labeled earns **2 easy marks**. > 6. **Forgetting BPF in ADP calculation** — $T_{ADP} \ne T_{WB}$ of entering air when $BPF > 0$. The correct formula must be used. (Loss: 2 marks) > 7. **Year-Round AC schematic without face-and-bypass damper** — Examiner specifically checks for this — it's the defining feature of YAC. (Loss: 3 marks) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision — Read before entering exam hall.** ### ✅ Core Definitions - **WAC** = Heating + Humidification (cold-dry season) - **SAC** = Cooling + Dehumidification (hot-humid season) - **YAC** = Automated dual-mode (12-month operation) ### ✅ Key Processes on Psychrometric Chart | Process | Direction | Constant Property | |---|---|---| | Sensible Heating | Horizontal → right | $W$ constant, DBT ↑ | | Sensible Cooling | Horizontal → left | $W$ constant, DBT ↓ | | Humidification | Along constant WBT | DBT ↓, $W$ ↑ | | Dehumidification | Cooling coil line | DBT ↓, $W$ ↓ | | Reheating | Horizontal → right | $W$ constant | ### ✅ Five Must-Know Formulas 1. $Q_s = m_a c_p \Delta T$ 2. $Q_L = m_a h_{fg} \Delta W$ 3. $SHF = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_L)$ 4. $BPF = (T_2 - T_{ADP}) / (T_1 - T_{ADP})$ 5. $T_{ADP} = (T_2 - BPF \cdot T_1) / (1 - BPF)$ ### ✅ Critical Conversion - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/h** ### ✅ Critical Equipment Checklist - WAC needs: Filter + Heating Coil + Humidifier - SAC needs: Filter + Cooling Coil + Reheater - YAC needs: ALL of above + Face-Bypass Damper + Central Controller ### ✅ Food Industry Mapping (Memorize 2-3) - Banana ripening → YAC winter mode (15–20 °C, 90 % RH) - Bakery proofing → WAC (30 °C, 75 % RH) - Meat processing → YAC cold + humid (7 °C, 85 % RH) - Chocolate tempering → YAC narrow band (20 °C, 55 % RH) ### ✅ Examiner Traps to Avoid - Don't confuse $h_{fg}$ (= 2501 kJ/kg) with $h_g$ (total enthalpy of vapor). - Always state the **basis** (per kg dry air). - Mention **ADP** explicitly in any cooling coil problem. - Show the **return air mixing** in the schematic for full marks. ### ✅ Highest-Yield KTU Question Pattern > "Explain the psychrometric process for Summer/Winter AC with a sketch of the plant layout and determine the refrigeration/heating load in kW (or TR)." This single question pattern covers **all 14 marks** if answered with: (1) labeled schematic, (2) psychrometric chart with marked points, (3) all five formulae with numerical substitution, (4) correct final units, (5) a concluding sentence on the application's food-industry relevance.

topics.title: Unitary Air-Conditioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unitary Air-Conditioning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Unitary Air-Conditioning Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unitary Air-Conditioning System (UACS):** A factory-assembled, self-contained refrigeration system in which the compressor, condenser, expansion device (throttling/flow control), and evaporator are either integrated within a single cabinet (window type) or split between an indoor and an outdoor unit, but the system is delivered as **one pre-tested package** that performs air cooling, dehumidification, filtration, and circulation of conditioned air for a localized space or a single zone.

In strict ASHRAE terminology, a **unitary** equipment is one in which the evaporator or cooling coil, compressor, and condenser are combined into a single factory-made unit. This is in contrast to a **central plant** where these components are field-erected and piped together.

### 1.2 Classification Hierarchy

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies unitary equipment primarily by capacity range and architecture:

| Architecture | Typical Capacity Range | Food Industry Application |
|--------------|-----------------------|---------------------------|
| **Window Unit** | 0.5 – 2 TR | Office pantry, small QC lab |
| **Split Unit (Wall/Ceiling/Floor)** | 1.0 – 5 TR | Cold-room anteroom, packaging hall |
| **Mini-Split (Inverter / VRF)** | 0.8 – 6 TR (per indoor) | Server room, lab instrument rooms |
| **Packaged Unit (Roof-Top / Floor-Mounted)** | 5 – 100+ TR | Process halls, ripening rooms |
| **Portable / Spot Coolers** | 0.5 – 1.5 TR | Temporary zones, mobile QC |

> [!NOTE]
> **1 TR (Tonne of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW** of cooling capacity, equivalent to **12,000 BTU/hr**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a unitary AC exactly like a **toaster oven** versus a **full kitchen stove with separate burners and oven**:

- A **unitary AC** is the **toaster oven** — everything is built into one box; you just plug it in, and it cools one room.
- A **chiller plant** is the **kitchen stove** — you buy the burner, oven, ventilation, and a chef (the controls) separately and assemble them on site.

In a food factory, the small ante-room next to a cold storage often uses a window unit (toaster oven logic), whereas the entire processing floor is cooled by a chiller + AHU system (stove logic).

### 1.4 Why Unitary Systems Matter in Food Industry

1. **Zonal Cooling** — Different areas (chill storage, ripening chamber, packaging) need different temperatures simultaneously.
2. **Plug-and-Play Installation** — No civil works, no chilled-water piping, no condenser-water network.
3. **Redundancy** — Multiple independent units provide N+1 backup for critical cooling (cold-chain integrity).
4. **Local Dehumidification Control** — Critical for moisture-sensitive products (milk powder, dry-fruit processing).
5. **FSSAI / FSMA Compliance** — Maintain ambient T ≤ 25 °C in processing halls (typically achieved by unitary units).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 3 specifically emphasizes **selection criteria, application, and component arrangement** of unitary ACs in food processing environments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of a Unitary System

Every unitary AC — regardless of size — contains the **same four functional blocks** of any vapour-compression refrigeration cycle:

1. **Compressor** (Hermetic / Semi-Hermetic / Scroll / Inverter-Driven) — raises refrigerant pressure/temperature.
2. **Condenser** (Air-cooled, with propeller/fan + coil) — rejects heat to ambient.
3. **Expansion Device** (Capillary tube / TXV / EEV) — drops refrigerant pressure/temperature.
4. **Evaporator** (Direct-Expansion coil + blower) — absorbs heat from return air.

> [!NOTE]
> The **key distinguishing feature** of a unitary system is that **point 4** (evaporator) is inside the conditioned space (or its supply duct) while **points 1, 2, 3** may be inside or outside.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic (Why + How)

**Step 1 — Air Circulation:**
The return air from the room is drawn by a **squirrel-cage blower / centrifugal fan** across the filter and the DX cooling coil.

- *Why?* To bring warm, humid room air into contact with the cold coil surface.
- *How?* Fan motor typically rated **0.25 – 1.5 kW** depending on unit size.

**Step 2 — Filtration:**
Air passes through **washable synthetic / pleated filters** (G3 / G4 class per EN 779, equivalent to MERV 5–8).

- *Why?* Food industry requires particulate control to avoid product contamination.
- *How?* Filtration pressure drop: **50 – 150 Pa**.

**Step 3 — Cooling & Dehumidification:**
As air crosses the **direct-expansion (DX) evaporator coil** at temperatures between **5 – 10 °C**, two simultaneous processes occur:

- **Sensible cooling** — dry-bulb temperature drops.
- **Latent cooling (dehumidification)** — moisture condenses on coil fins when surface temperature < **Dew Point** of incoming air.

**Step 4 — Reheat (Optional, in some units):**
Some packaged units include a **hot-gas reheat coil** after the evaporator. This re-warms the air to control humidity without lowering temperature — used for ripening rooms requiring **85% RH @ 18 °C**.

**Step 5 — Air Distribution:**
The conditioned air (typically **13 – 16 °C supply**) is discharged through grilles/diffusers with face velocity **2.5 – 5 m/s**.

**Step 6 — Refrigerant Cycle:**
Liquid refrigerant from condenser → through TXV/Cap tube → low-pressure spray in evaporator → absorbs latent heat of evaporation → vapor → sucked by compressor → compressed → hot vapor → condenser → heat rejected → liquid repeats.

### 2.3 Types of Unitary Systems — Engineering Detail

#### A. Window Air-Conditioner
- Single cabinet; compressor, condenser, evaporator, controls in one box.
- Sliding into a wall/wooden frame.
- **Capacity:** 0.5 – 2 TR.
- Side louvers vent condenser heat to outside; room side louvers deliver cool air.
- *Food-industry use:* Pantry cooling, small labs.

#### B. Split Air-Conditioner
- **Indoor unit (IDU):** evaporator + blower (wall/ceiling/floor mounted).
- **Outdoor unit (ODU):** compressor + condenser + fan.
- Connected by **refrigerant copper tubing** (typically **6.35 mm liquid + 9.52 mm suction** for 1 TR; up to **22 mm** for larger units) plus control wiring.
- Maximum line length: **15–30 m** (regular split); **up to 100 m** (VRF).
- *Food-industry use:* Cold-room ante-rooms, packaging lines.

#### C. Inverter-Driven Split / Mini-VRF
- **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** on compressor motor.
- Modulates capacity from **25 % to 110 %** of rated value by varying compressor RPM (typically **1,800 – 7,800 rpm**).
- Saves **30 – 50 %** electricity vs. fixed-speed units.
- COP improves from ~**2.8 → 4.5**.

#### D. Packaged / Roof-Top Unit (RTU)
- All components in **one weatherproof cabinet**; sits on roof or beside the building.
- **Capacity:** 5 – 150 TR.
- Uses **scroll or screw compressors**; belt-drive or direct-drive backward-curved fans.
- Ducted supply / return.
- **Food-industry use:** Bulk ripening chambers, large processing halls, dairy plants.

#### E. Self-Contained / Heat Pump Units
- Reversing valve allows summer cooling / winter heating mode.
- COP_heating = Qh/W typically **3.0 – 4.5** (air-source heat pump).
- Useful in **winter in Kerala** when ambient dips to **18 – 20 °C** for food plants requiring 22 °C.

### 2.4 Sensible Heat Factor & Apparatus Dew Point — Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF):** Ratio of sensible heat load to total heat load of the conditioned space.
> SHF = Qs / (Qs + Ql)
> Where **Qs** = sensible heat (kW) and **Ql** = latent heat (kW).

In food plants:
- High internal gains from motors, lighting, ovens → SHF = **0.75 – 0.85**.
- High latent loads (open cooking, steam, fresh produce respiration) → SHF = **0.5 – 0.65**.

**Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** = the saturation temperature of the air leaving the evaporator coil. Most unitary systems are selected with **ADP = 4 – 7 °C** to avoid coil frosting.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Cooling Capacity (Tr) | Qe = m_r × (h1 − h4) | kW | h1 at evaporator exit, h4 at evaporator inlet |
| 2 | Heat Rejection | Qc = m_r × (h2 − h3) | kW | Condenser load |
| 3 | Compressor Work | Wc = m_r × (h2 − h1) | kW | Isentropic ideal work |
| 4 | COP (Cooling) | COP_R = Qe / Wc | — | Dimensionless, ideal 2.5 – 4.5 |
| 5 | COP (Heating, Heat Pump) | COP_HP = Qh / Wc | — | Qh = Qe + Wc |
| 6 | EER | EER = (Qe in BTU/hr) / (W in W) | BTU/W·hr | 1 TR ≈ 12,000 BTU/hr |
| 7 | SEER | SEER = Total cooling (BTU) / Total kWh | — | Seasonal; for inverter units |
| 8 | Sensible Heat Factor | SHF = Qs / (Qs + Ql) | — | 0 < SHF ≤ 1 |
| 9 | Bypass Factor | BPF = (T2 − Tadp) / (T1 − Tadp) | — | T1 return, T2 supply, Tadp coil surface |
| 10 | Mass Flow of Air | ma = Qs / (1.005 × (T1 − T2)) | kg/s | Sensible load formula |
| 11 | Mass Flow of Refrigerant | m_r = Qe / (h1 − h4) | kg/s | Steady-state |
| 12 | Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER) | EER = 3.517 × COP | — | Since 1 W = 3.412 BTU/hr, and 1 TR = 3.517 kW |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Exam Hint:** KTU frequently asks to convert between **COP**, **EER**, and **SEER**. Always remember:
> EER = 3.517 × COP (numerically equal for the same cooling in BTU/W·hr or TR/W).
> SEER is *averaged over the entire season* and therefore always numerically **smaller** than peak EER.

### 2.6 Selection Criteria for Food Industry Unitary ACs

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly demands understanding of:

1. **Capacity** (TR) = Total heat load × 1.15 safety factor.
2. **Air Quantity** = Qs / (1.005 × ΔT); typically **80 – 120 CFM per TR** for unitary.
3. **Condenser Type** — Air-cooled (standard in food plants due to no water scarcity).
4. **Refrigerant** — **R-32 / R-410A** (current), phasing out **R-22** (ODP = 0.055).
5. **Humidity Control** — For ripening/dry storage, select reheat or desiccant option.
6. **Hygiene Standards** — SS-304 drain trays, smooth coils (no fins on the food-side), easy-clean filters.
7. **Ambient Conditions** — Verify **T_condenser ≤ 50 °C** for adequate heat rejection (Kerala summer ambient peaks at **38 – 40 °C**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Refrigeration Effect per kg of Refrigerant

**Starting Premise:** A unit mass of refrigerant (1 kg) circulates through the four components of the vapour-compression cycle.

**State Points (Standard Notation):**
- **State 1:** Saturated/superheated vapor at compressor inlet (low pressure, low temperature).
- **State 2:** Superheated vapor at compressor exit (high pressure, high temperature).
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid at condenser exit.
- **State 4:** Liquid-vapor mixture at evaporator inlet (after expansion).

**Refrigeration Effect per kg (RE/kg):**

The refrigeration effect is the heat absorbed by **1 kg** of refrigerant in the evaporator:

$$
RE_{per\,kg} = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}
$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = enthalpy at compressor inlet (after evaporator)
- $h_4$ = enthalpy at evaporator inlet (after expansion device)

**Derivation of Mass Flow Rate (m_r):**

Total cooling capacity Qe is supplied by mass flow m_r carrying the specific enthalpy drop h1 − h4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_e \,[\text{kW}] &= \frac{m_r \,[\text{kg/s}] \times (h_1 - h_4) \,[\text{kJ/kg}]}{1} \\
m_r &= \frac{Q_e}{h_1 - h_4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step numerical example** — *Use the following R-134a data:*

| State | T (°C) | P (bar) | h (kJ/kg) | x (Quality) |
|-------|--------|---------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | −10 | 2.0 | 392.0 | Superheated vapor |
| 2 | 60 | 8.0 | 430.0 | Superheated vapor |
| 3 | 35 | 8.0 | 240.0 | Saturated liquid |
| 4 | −10 | 2.0 | 215.0 | Two-phase mixture |

**Step 1 — Calculate the Refrigeration Effect:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE_{per\,kg} &= h_1 - h_4 \\
&= 392.0 - 215.0 \\
&= 177.0 \;\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Compressor Work per kg:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\
&= 430.0 - 392.0 \\
&= 38.0 \;\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the COP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_R &= \frac{RE_{per\,kg}}{w_c} \\
&= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\
&= \frac{177.0}{38.0} \\
&= 4.658
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to EER:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EER &= 3.517 \times COP_R \\
&= 3.517 \times 4.658 \\
&= 16.38 \;\text{BTU/W·hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Heat Rejection Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= m_r \times (h_2 - h_3) \\
Q_c/Q_e &= \frac{(h_2 - h_3)}{(h_1 - h_4)} \\
&= \frac{430.0 - 240.0}{392.0 - 215.0} \\
&= \frac{190}{177} \\
&= 1.073
\end{aligned}
$$

This means for every **1 kW** of cooling, the condenser must reject **1.073 kW**. The extra **0.073 kW** is the electrical work input — confirming the **First Law**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Mass Flow of Air for a Given Sensible Load

**Given:** A ripening chamber requires a sensible cooling load Qs = **8 kW**. The room return air is **28 °C**, and the supply air must be **15 °C**.

**Step 1 — Identify the sensible heat equation:**

$$
Q_s = \dot{m}_a \times c_{p,a} \times \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
8.0 = \dot{m}_a \times 1.005 \times (28 - 15)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for mass flow rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a &= \frac{8.0}{1.005 \times 13} \\
&= \frac{8.0}{13.065} \\
&= 0.6124 \;\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow (m³/s) at standard air density (1.2 kg/m³):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} \\
&= \frac{0.6124}{1.2} \\
&= 0.510 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 0.510 \times 2118.88 \\
&\approx 1081 \;\text{CFM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine unit capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TR} &= \frac{Q_e \,[\text{kW}]}{3.517} \\
&= \frac{8.0}{3.517} \\
&= 2.275 \;\text{TR} \;\text{(sensible only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: Add latent load → total TR will be **higher** (typically 1.15 – 1.25 × sensible TR).

### 3.3 Bypass Factor & Coil Selection — Analytical Example

**Problem Statement:** Return air enters the coil at **T1 = 30 °C, RH = 60 %**. The coil ADP is **Tadp = 6 °C**. The supply air leaves at **T2 = 17 °C**. Verify whether the coil design is correct.

**Step 1 — Calculate Bypass Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BPF &= \frac{T_2 - T_{adp}}{T_1 - T_{adp}} \\
&= \frac{17 - 6}{30 - 6} \\
&= \frac{11}{24} \\
&= 0.458
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret:** BPF = 0.458 is in the **acceptable range of 0.15 – 0.50** for food-industry unitary systems. Lower BPF = better coil (more rows, higher face velocity), but more pressure drop.

**Step 3 — Calculate effective contact factor (η):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{coil} &= 1 - BPF \\
&= 1 - 0.458 \\
&= 0.542 \;\text{(or 54.2 %)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means only **54.2 %** of the air approaches the coil surface temperature; the rest bypasses the coil.

### 3.4 Inverter Capacity Modulation — Analytical Matrix

A **1.5 TR (5.275 kW)** inverter split unit operates at partial loads as follows:

| Load % | Compressor Hz | Q_cooling (kW) | Power Input (kW) | COP |
|--------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|-----|
| 100 | 90 Hz | 5.275 | 1.51 | 3.49 |
| 75 | 67 Hz | 3.95 | 1.05 | 3.76 |
| 50 | 45 Hz | 2.64 | 0.66 | 4.00 |
| 25 | 23 Hz | 1.32 | 0.36 | 3.67 |

**Mathematical Derivation of SEER:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEER &= \frac{\sum_i (Q_{cooling,i} \times t_i)}{\sum_i (W_{input,i} \times t_i)} \\
SEER &= \frac{(5.275 \times 200) + (3.95 \times 600) + (2.64 \times 1500) + (1.32 \times 1300)}{(1.51 \times 200) + (1.05 \times 600) + (0.66 \times 1500) + (0.36 \times 1300)} \\
SEER &= \frac{1055 + 2370 + 3960 + 1716}{302 + 630 + 990 + 468} \\
SEER &= \frac{9101}{2390} \\
SEER &= 3.808 \;\text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In BTU/W·hr: SEER = 3.808 × 3.517 = **13.39 BTU/W·hr** — meets BEE 5-star rating for Indian conditions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
