<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) and explain its significance in selecting a unitary air-conditioning system for a food processing hall.

**Model Answer:**
The Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) is defined as the ratio of the sensible heat load to the total heat load of a conditioned space:

$$
SHF = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}
$$

Where Qs = sensible heat (kW) and Ql = latent heat (kW).

**Significance for Food Hall Selection:**
1. **Coil Selection** — Low SHF (0.50 – 0.65, high latent) → require **larger coil face area** to achieve lower ADP for adequate dehumidification.
2. **Supply Air Quantity** — Lower SHF means supply air has higher ΔW (humidity ratio) drop → more air quantity required.
3. **Reheat Required** — When SHF < 0.75 in food halls with high fresh-air load, hot-gas reheat or desiccant dehumidification is needed.
4. **Bypass Factor Check** — Coil rows are selected so that BPF × ΔT across coil equals the required sensible drop.

**[3 Marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for application]**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** Differentiate between window type and split type unitary air-conditioners with two specific advantages of each for food industry use.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Window AC | Split AC |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Architecture** | All components in one cabinet | Indoor unit (evaporator) separated from outdoor unit (compressor + condenser) |
| **Installation** | Wall-slot or window-frame mounting | Indoor wall/ceiling + outdoor wall-mount/ground |
| **Noise Level** | Higher (~50 – 55 dB indoor) | Lower (~32 – 38 dB indoor) since compressor is outside |
| **Capacity Range** | 0.5 – 2 TR | 1 – 5 TR |

**Food Industry Advantages:**
- **Window AC** — Cheap (₹30,000 – ₹45,000 per TR), no refrigerant piping, ideal for **pantry / store rooms** with low hygiene risk.
- **Split AC** — Quiet operation suits **QC laboratories** where sound interferes with acoustic instruments; also allows **fresh-air intake** without compromising room aesthetics.

**[3 Marks]**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Part B Module 3]** A ripening chamber in a banana processing unit requires air-conditioning at **22 °C DB, 80 % RH**. The chamber dimensions are **12 m × 8 m × 4 m**. Internal heat loads are: lights **2.0 kW**, motors **1.5 kW**, occupancy (8 people) sensible **0.7 kW**, latent **0.4 kW**, banana respiration heat **1.2 kW (sensible) + 1.6 kW (latent)**. Wall heat gain **1.8 kW**. Infiltration latent load **0.6 kW**.

**(a)** Calculate the total cooling load (sensible + latent) in **TR**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Select a suitable unitary AC system, estimate the air quantity in **CMH and CFM**, and determine the **COP** if the compressor consumes **2.8 kW**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Total Sensible Load (Qs):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= Q_{lights} + Q_{motors} + Q_{occupancy,sens} + Q_{respiration,sens} + Q_{walls} \\
&= 2.0 + 1.5 + 0.7 + 1.2 + 1.8 \\
&= 7.2 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Latent Load (Ql):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_l &= Q_{occupancy,lat} + Q_{respiration,lat} + Q_{infiltration,lat} \\
&= 0.4 + 1.6 + 0.6 \\
&= 2.6 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Cooling Load (Qe):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_e &= Q_s + Q_l \\
&= 7.2 + 2.6 \\
&= 9.8 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to TR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_e [TR] &= \frac{9.8}{3.517} \\
&= 2.786 \;\text{TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sensible Heat Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SHF &= \frac{7.2}{9.8} \\
&= 0.735
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** SHF ≈ 0.735 is typical for a ripening chamber; a 4-row DX coil with hot-gas reheat is appropriate.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Air Quantity from Sensible Load:**

Assume supply air temperature **T2 = 14 °C** and room return air **T1 = 24 °C** (slightly higher due to stratification in chamber).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_1 - T_2 = 24 - 14 = 10 \;°C \\
\dot{m}_a &= \frac{Q_s}{1.005 \times \Delta T} \\
&= \frac{7.2}{1.005 \times 10} \\
&= 0.7164 \;\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volumetric flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} = \frac{0.7164}{1.2} \\
&= 0.597 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 0.597 \times 3600 \\
&= 2149.2 \;\text{m}^3/\text{hr (CMH)} \\
&= \frac{2149.2 \times 35.31}{60} \\
&\approx 1265 \;\text{CFM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Unit Selection:**

Based on 2.79 TR load with 15 % safety factor → **3 TR (10.55 kW) capacity** → select a **3 TR ducted split AC with hot-gas reheat** for humidity control.

**Step 4 — COP Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_R &= \frac{Q_e}{W_c} \\
&= \frac{9.8}{2.8} \\
&= 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — EER:**

$$
EER = 3.517 \times 3.50 = 12.31 \;\text{BTU/W·hr}
$$

This corresponds to a **3-star BEE rating**.

**[14 Marks: 7 + 7 split appropriately]**

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Part B Module 3]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **window-type unitary air-conditioner**. State its **advantages** and **disadvantages** and explain its **suitability for small food-industry applications** (e.g., store room, pantry).

**(a)** Describe the construction and working with a labelled block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the advantages, disadvantages, and two specific food-industry applications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Construction:**
A window AC consists of a single **insulated metal cabinet** divided by a partition into two sections:

- **Front (Room) Section:** Contains evaporator coil, blower, expansion device, filter, control panel.
- **Rear (Outdoor) Section:** Contains compressor, condenser coil, condenser fan (propeller type).

A **sliding shutter / louver** on the rear side vents condenser air to atmosphere.

**Working (Cycle of Operation):**

**Step 1:** Room air at **28 °C, 65 % RH** is drawn through the **return air grill** by the **centrifugal blower**.

**Step 2:** Air passes through the **synthetic filter** (G3 class) where dust/lint is captured.

**Step 3:** Air flows over the **DX evaporator coil** (ADP ≈ 6 °C). Sensible and latent heat are removed; condensate drains through the **drain pipe** to outside.

**Step 4:** Cooled air at **15 °C, 95 % RH** is discharged back into the room via the **supply louvers**.

**Step 5:** Simultaneously, **refrigerant vapor** from the evaporator is compressed by the **hermetic compressor** to high-pressure, high-temperature vapor (~**60 °C, 18 bar** for R-32).

**Step 6:** Hot vapor flows through the **condenser coil**; the **propeller fan** blows ambient air across it, condensing the refrigerant to **saturated liquid**.

**Step 7:** Liquid refrigerant passes through the **capillary tube** (expansion device), drops to low pressure, enters the evaporator, and the cycle repeats.

**Block Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RA["Room Air 28C 65 percent RH"] --> RG["Return Grille"]
    RG --> F["Filter G3"]
    F --> EC["Evaporator Coil ADP 6C"]
    EC --> BL["Centrifugal Blower"]
    BL --> SG["Supply Louver 15C"]
    SG --> RA

    EC -. "Low P Vapor".-> CMP["Hermetic Compressor"]
    CMP -. "High P Vapor".-> CC["Condenser Coil"]
    CC --> CF["Propeller Fan"]
    CF --> AMB["Ambient Air 35C"]
    CC -. "Liquid".-> CAP["Capillary Tube"]
    CAP -. "Low P Liquid Vapor".-> EC

    style CMP fill:#FFB6C1
    style CC fill:#FFD700
    style EC fill:#98FB98
```

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Advantages (4 marks):**

1. **Compact & Self-contained** — No field piping; install in **30 minutes**.
2. **Low Capital Cost** — ₹30,000 – ₹45,000 per TR (cheapest AC type).
3. **Easy Maintenance** — Single unit; service technician access is straightforward.
4. **Plug-and-Play** — Only electrical connection needed; ideal for **rural food plants** with intermittent power (with stabilizer).

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**

1. **Noisy** — Compressor inside the same cabinet as blower → **50 – 55 dB(A)** indoor noise.
2. **Wall Cutting Required** — Permanent slot in wall; not aesthetically clean.
3. **Limited Capacity** — Max ~2 TR; cannot cool large processing halls.

**Food Industry Suitability (2 marks):**

1. **Pantry / Break-Room Cooling** — Where ambient is high but food products are NOT stored; a 1.5 TR window AC maintains 24 °C for staff comfort.
2. **Spice / Dry-Goods Store Room** — Where T < 28 °C and RH < 60 % is required for shelf-life; capacity 1 TR sufficient for ~30 m².
3. **QC Sample Storage Room** — Where reference samples are kept at **22 – 25 °C**; window AC provides economical, reliable temperature control.

**[14 Marks: 7 + 7]**

---

### 5.5 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.15 safety factor** in unit selection. KTU penalizes if you specify *exact* TR without margin.
> 2. **Mixing EER and COP** units. EER is in **BTU/W·hr**; COP is dimensionless. Converting incorrectly loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring latent load from infiltration** when fresh air enters the conditioned space (e.g., ripening chamber doors).
> 4. **Using refrigerant R-22** in current-year solutions — KTU expects **R-32 / R-410A** since R-22 is phased out under the Montreal Protocol.
> 5. **Forgetting the condensate drain** in schematic drawings of unitary systems.
> 6. **Not stating the SHF** after calculating sensible and latent loads — a 1-mark item that students routinely miss.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unitary Air-Conditioning Systems — Key Takeaways:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Factory-assembled AC with all four refrigeration components either in one cabinet (window) or split between IDU + ODU.
- ✅ **Classification:** Window, Split, Mini-Split / VRF, Packaged (RTU), Heat Pump variants.
- ✅ **Capacity Range:** 0.5 TR (small window) → 100+ TR (large RTU); 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr.
- ✅ **Four Components:** Compressor, Condenser (air-cooled), Expansion Device (TXV / Capillary), Evaporator (DX coil).
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
   - COP_R = Qe / Wc
   - EER = 3.517 × COP_R
   - SHF = Qs / (Qs + Ql)
   - BPF = (T2 − Tadp) / (T1 − Tadp)
   - m_r = Qe / (h1 − h4)
- ✅ **Food Industry Use Cases:** Ripening chambers (18–22 °C, 80–85 % RH), packaging halls, QC labs, store rooms.
- ✅ **Selection Criteria:** Total load + 15 % safety, CFM/HR = 400–500 CFM/TR, air-cooled condenser, refrigerant R-32/R-410A.
- ✅ **Inverter Advantage:** SEER improvement of 30–50 % vs. fixed-speed units.
- ✅ **Common Exam Traps:** Unit confusion (kW vs. TR), missing latent load, outdated refrigerant, missing safety factor.
- ✅ **Typical SHF in Food Plants:** 0.65 – 0.80 (depending on moisture load from cooking, respiration, steam).
- ✅ **Typical Coil ADP:** 5 – 7 °C for unitary DX systems.
- ✅ **Typical Air Quantity:** 80 – 120 CFM per TR; lower for sensible-dominant, higher for latent-dominant applications.
- ✅ **Kerala-Specific Note:** Condenser design must handle **38 – 40 °C ambient**; derate capacity by ~5 % per °C above 35 °C.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Central Air-Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Central Air-Conditioning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Central Air-Conditioning System** is a large-capacity, centrally-located refrigeration and air-distribution plant in which air is **conditioned (cleaned, cooled, dehumidified/heated, humidified) at a single central station** and then distributed through a network of ducts to multiple conditioned spaces (zones) simultaneously. In the context of the **Food Industry**, Central AC is the engineering backbone of: - **Cold Storage Warehouses** (bulk storage of fruits, vegetables, meat, dairy, seafood) - **Food Processing Plants** (clean rooms, ripening chambers, blast freezers) - **Packaging Halls** (humidity-controlled zones) - **Modular Atmosphere (MA) / Controlled Atmosphere (CA) storage** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building's Lungs" > [!NOTE] > Think of a Central AC system as the **lungs of a building**: > - The **Central Plant (Chiller + Cooling Tower)** is the *heart* that pumps cooled "blood" (chilled water) out. > - The **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** is the *lung* where the air "breathes" — it mixes, filters, cools, and dehumidifies. > - The **Ductwork** acts as the *arteries* carrying this fresh, cool air to every "organ" (room/zone). > - The **Return Air** is the *veins* bringing stale air back to the lung for reconditioning. > > Just as our lungs never stop working, a Central AC operates 24×7 in food plants because the **biochemical clock of food** (respiration, ripening, microbial growth) never pauses. ## 1.3 Core Design Parameters (Bold Constants to Memorize) | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | Food-Industry Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Tons of Refrigeration** | **TR** | **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr** | Sizing chillers | | **Dry Bulb Temperature** | **DBT** | **°C** | Process air control | | **Wet Bulb Temperature** | **WBT** | **°C** | Humidity & cooling coil analysis | | **Relative Humidity** | **RH** | **%** | Critical for drying, ripening | | **Sensible Heat Factor** | **SHF** | Ratio (0 to 1) | Coil selection | | **Bypass Factor** | **BF** | Ratio (0 to 1) | Coil effectiveness | | **Air Density (Std.)** | **ρ** | **1.2 kg/m³** | CFM ↔ mass flow | | **Specific Heat of Air** | **cp** | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** | Sensible load | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Central AC Systems (KTU Taxonomy) Central AC systems are broadly classified into four families based on the **medium used to carry cooling energy** to the conditioned space: ### A) All-Air Systems (Most common in food plants) - **Single Duct System**: One supply duct carrying either cold or warm air. Simple, low cost. Used in bulk cold stores. - **Dual Duct System**: Two parallel ducts (hot deck + cold deck) feeding a mixing box at each zone. High flexibility. Used in multi-product processing halls. - **Multi-Zone System**: Single AHU with multiple dampers feeding multiple zones. Suitable for medium-sized food factories. ### B) Air-Water Systems - Combination of central AHU + chilled water fan-coil units (FCUs) at each zone. - Used in office blocks attached to food plants, QC labs. ### C) Water Systems - Only chilled water is circulated; zone-level air handling is done locally. - Used in hotels/hospitals attached to food industry complexes. ### D) Unitary / Packaged Systems - Self-contained window/split units. - Used as a *supplementary* system in small food outlets. ## 2.2 Central Plant — The "Heart" Components > [!NOTE] > **Central Plant Architecture for a Food Industry AC:** > 1. **Chiller** (Vapour Compression / Absorption) — generates chilled water at **6–8 °C**. > 2. **Chilled Water Pumps (CHWP)** — primary & secondary loops. > 3. **Cooling Tower** — rejects condenser heat to atmosphere. > 4. **Condenser Water Pumps (CWP)**. > 5. **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** — performs air conditioning. > 6. **Ductwork + Diffusers** — air distribution. > 7. **BMS / SCADA** — automation (essential in 24×7 food plants). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this table as your single-point reference during KTU ESE preparation. **No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used** — instead, `\vert` is used for absolute values to preserve markdown integrity. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sensible Heat Load** | $Q_s \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 1.08 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta T$ | kW or BTU/hr | Dry cooling load | | 2 | **Latent Heat Load** | $Q_L \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W \;=\; 0.68 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta W$ | kW or BTU/hr | Moisture removal | | 3 | **Total Heat Load** | $Q_t \;=\; Q_s + Q_L$ | kW | Sizing chiller | | 4 | **Sensible Heat Factor** | $\text{SHF} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_t}$ | ratio (0–1) | Coil selection on Psychrometric chart | | 5 | **Bypass Factor of Coil** | $\text{BF} \;=\; \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}$ | ratio (0–1) | Cooling coil effectiveness | | 6 | **Contact Factor** | $\text{CF} \;=\; 1 - \text{BF}$ | ratio (0–1) | Inverse of bypass | | 7 | **Apparatus Dew Point** | $\text{ADP} \;=\; T_1 - \text{BF} \cdot (T_1 - T_3)$ | °C | Coil surface temp proxy | | 8 | **Tons of Refrigeration** | $1 \text{ TR} \;=\; 3.517 \text{ kW} \;=\; 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | — | Capacity sizing | | 9 | **CFM from Load** | $\text{CFM} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_s}{1.08 \cdot \Delta T}$ | ft³/min | Duct design | | 10 | **Mass Flow of Air** | $\dot{m} \;=\; \rho \cdot \dot{V} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{CFM} \cdot \rho}{60}$ | kg/s | Psychrometrics | | 11 | **Mixed Air Temperature** | $T_m \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_o \cdot T_o + \dot{m}_r \cdot T_r}{\dot{m}_o + \dot{m}_r}$ | °C | Economizer cycle | | 12 | **Chiller COP** | $\text{COP} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | — | Efficiency check | | 13 | **EER (Food Industry)** | $\text{EER} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Cooling BTU/hr}}{\text{Power W}} \times \dfrac{1}{3.412}$ | — | Star rating | | 14 | **Heat of Respiration (Fruits)** | $Q_{resp} \;=\; m \cdot q_{resp}$ (m in tonnes, q in W/tonne) | W | Cold store load | | 15 | **Duct Heat Gain** | $Q_{duct} \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{amb-supply}$ | W | Duct insulation design | > **Where:** > - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of air (kg/s) > - $\Delta T$ = $T_{room} - T_{supply}$ > - $\Delta W$ = $W_{room} - W_{supply}$ (humidity ratio difference, kg/kg dry air) > - $T_1$ = Air entering coil (DBT, °C) > - $T_2$ = Air leaving coil (DBT, °C) > - $T_3$ = Apparatus Dew Point (ADP, °C) > - $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water ≈ **2501 kJ/kg** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Sector - **Blast Freezers** in seafood plants use **All-Air Dual Duct** systems to maintain -35 °C with high CFM. - **Banana Ripening Chambers** require **SHF ≈ 0.5** to balance heat and ethylene (C₂H₄) removal — coil selection is critical. - **Dairy Cold Stores** use chilled water (7 °C) to avoid freezing products on coils — **All-Water** preferred. - **Mushroom Growing Rooms** demand **RH 90–95%** — high latent load → **DX systems with reheat** common. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1: From First-Principles — Sensible Heat Equation in CFM Form ### Given: - Air mass flow: $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) - Specific heat: $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K - Temperature change: $\Delta T$ (K or °C) - Density: $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ (at NTP) - Volume flow: $\dot{V}$ in m³/s ### Derivation: **Step 1 — Start with the fundamental sensible heat equation:** $$Q_s \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$ This is the *first-principles* definition: heat added = mass × specific heat × temperature rise. **Step 2 — Convert mass flow to volumetric flow:** $$\dot{m} \;=\; \rho \cdot \dot{V}$$ This is by the definition of density ($\rho = m/V$). **Step 3 — Substitute into the heat equation:** $$Q_s \;=\; (\rho \cdot \dot{V}) \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$ **Step 4 — Substitute standard values $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K:** $$Q_s \;=\; (1.2)(1.005) \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T \;\approx\; 1.206 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T \;\; \text{[kW]}$$ **Step 5 — Convert $\dot{V}$ from m³/s to CFM (ft³/min):** $$1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \;=\; \dfrac{(3.281)^3 \text{ ft}^3}{1 \text{ s}} \times 60 \;=\; 2118.88 \text{ CFM}$$ **Step 6 — Multiply through to obtain the imperial form used in industry:** $$Q_s \;=\; 1.206 \cdot \dot{V}_{m^3/s} \cdot \Delta T \cdot 3412.14 \;=\; 1.08 \cdot \text{CFM} \cdot \Delta T \;\; [\text{BTU/hr}]$$ > **Why the factor 1.08?** It is a *lumped constant* combining density (1.2), specific heat (1.005), unit conversions (m³/s → CFM, kW → BTU/hr). KTU examiners expect you to know that this **1.08 factor is empirical and valid only at standard air conditions (ρ = 1.2 kg/m³)**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: Bypass Factor from Energy Balance ### Concept: When air passes over a cooling coil, a fraction of air **bypasses** the coil surface (never touches the cold tubes). The **Bypass Factor (BF)** quantifies this. ### Derivation: **Step 1 — Define the three reference temperatures:** - $T_1$ = DBT of air **entering** the coil - $T_2$ = DBT of air **leaving** the coil - $T_3$ = Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) — temperature of coil surface **Step 2 — Apply energy balance on the bypassed fraction:** If a fraction **BF** of air *bypasses* the coil unchanged, and fraction **(1 − BF)** is cooled to $T_3$: $$T_2 \;=\; \text{BF} \cdot T_1 + (1 - \text{BF}) \cdot T_3$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange:** $$T_2 \;=\; T_1 \cdot \text{BF} + T_3 - T_3 \cdot \text{BF} \;\Rightarrow\; T_2 - T_3 \;=\; \text{BF} \cdot (T_1 - T_3)$$ **Step 4 — Solve for BF:** $$\boxed{\;\text{BF} \;=\; \dfrac{T_2 - T_3}{T_1 - T_3}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Meaning:** BF = 0 → perfect contact (ideal coil, all air reaches ADP). BF = 1 → no contact (coil failed). A *good* cooling coil has **BF = 0.1 to 0.3**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem (Model ESE 14-mark style):** A banana ripening chamber of **100 m² floor area** has the following loads: - Wall/gain heat: **8 kW** - Product respiration: **2 kW** (10 tonnes of bananas at 0.2 W/kg) - Occupancy + lights: **1 kW** - Infiltration: **3 kW** - Latent load (moisture from ripening): **4 kW** - **Room conditions**: 18 °C DBT, 95% RH - **Supply conditions**: 12 °C DBT, 80% RH - $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg **Find:** (a) Total cooling load in TR, (b) Mass flow of supply air, (c) Sensible Heat Factor. ### Solution: **Part (a) — Total Load:** $$Q_s \;=\; 8 + 2 + 1 + 3 \;=\; 14 \text{ kW (sensible)}$$ $$Q_L \;=\; 4 \text{ kW (latent)}$$ $$Q_t \;=\; Q_s + Q_L \;=\; 14 + 4 \;=\; 18 \text{ kW}$$ Converting to TR (using **1 TR = 3.517 kW**): $$Q_t \;=\; \dfrac{18}{3.517} \;\approx\; 5.12 \text{ TR}$$ **Part (b) — Mass Flow of Air:** From psychrometric chart (or tables): - At 18 °C, 95% RH → $W_1 \approx 0.0128$ kg/kg dry air - At 12 °C, 80% RH → $W_2 \approx 0.0068$ kg/kg dry air - $\Delta T = 18 - 12 = 6$ K - $\Delta W = 0.0128 - 0.0068 = 0.0060$ kg/kg For sensible load: $$\dot{m} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_s}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} \;=\; \dfrac{14}{1.005 \cdot 6} \;=\; 2.323 \text{ kg/s}$$ > *Verify using latent formula*: $\dot{m} = Q_L / (h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W) = 4 / (2501 \cdot 0.006) = 0.267$ kg/s — discrepancy indicates inconsistency in the assumed $\Delta W$; the *sensible* value governs for coil sizing. **Part (c) — Sensible Heat Factor:** $$\text{SHF} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_t} \;=\; \dfrac{14}{18} \;\approx\; 0.778$$ > **Interpretation:** Since SHF > 0.7, a **dry coil (sensible-dominated)** is appropriate — typical for ripening chambers. --- ## 3.4 KTU Examiner's Analytical Matrix (Mark-Allocation Logic) | Step in Solution | KTU Marks Allocated | Common Errors | |---|---|---| | Identifying & listing all loads | 2 | Forgetting infiltration/respiration | | Correct numerical values substitution | 3 | Unit mismatch (BTU ↔ kW) | | Application of $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | 4 | Forgetting 1.2 kg/m³ density | | TR conversion using 1 TR = 3.517 kW | 2 | Using 3.5 or 3.516 | | Psychrometric reasoning & SHF | 3 | Mixing up SHF with BF | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Central AC Plant (Food Industry) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OUTDOOR["OUTDOOR SECTION"] CT1["COOLING TOWER"] CD1["CONDENSER"] ODA1["OUTSIDE AIR INLET"] end subgraph PLANT["CENTRAL PLANT ROOM"] CH1["CHILLER UNIT
VAPOUR COMPRESSION"] P1["CHILLED WATER PUMP"] AHU1["AIR HANDLING UNIT
AHU"] end subgraph INDOOR["CONDITIONED SPACES"] Z1["ZONE 1
COLD STORE 4 C"] Z2["ZONE 2
RIPENING 18 C"] Z3["ZONE 3
PACKING HALL 24 C"] end ODA1 -->|FRESH AIR| AHU1 RA1["RETURN AIR FROM ZONES"] --> AHU1 AHU1 -->|COOL SUPPLY DUCT| Z1 AHU1 -->|COOL SUPPLY DUCT| Z2 AHU1 -->|COOL SUPPLY DUCT| Z3 CH1 -->|CHILLED WATER 7 C| P1 P1 --> AHU1 AHU1 -->|RETURN WATER 12 C| CH1 CH1 -->|HOT REFRIGERANT| CD1 CD1 -->|HEAT REJECTION| CT1 CT1 -->|COOL WATER| CD1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Node ID safety check:** All identifiers (`CT1`, `CH1`, `P1`, `AHU1`, `Z1`, etc.) are purely alphanumeric. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting. --- ## 4.2 Schematic — Inside an AHU (Air Handling Unit) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["RETURN AIR
FROM ZONES"] --> F1["FILTER
G4 + F7"] F1 --> MX1["MIXING CHAMBER
RA + OA"] OA1["OUTSIDE AIR
ECONOMISER DAMPER"] --> MX1 MX1 --> C1["COOLING COIL
CHW 7C IN"] C1 --> H1["HEATING COIL
HOT WATER"] H1 --> FB1["FAN SECTION
CENTRIFUGAL BLOWER"] FB1 --> SA1["SUPPLY AIR
TO DUCTS"] H2["HUMIDIFIER
STEAM INJECTION"] -.->|IF NEEDED| C1 WW1["DRAIN PAN
CONDENSATE"] --> C1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Process Flow — Psychrometric Path in Central AC ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["STATE 1
OUTSIDE AIR
38 C, 70 percent RH"] -->|FILTER| P2["STATE 2
AFTER MIXING
WITH RETURN AIR"] P2 -->|COOLING COIL| P3["STATE 3
AFTER COIL
ADP REACHED"] P3 -->|REHEAT OPTIONAL| P4["STATE 4
SUPPLY AIR
FINAL DBT"] P4 -->|TO ROOM| P5["STATE 5
ROOM CONDITIONS
24 C, 50 percent RH"] P5 -->|HEAT GAIN| P1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Define Central Air-Conditioning System. List any two advantages over unitary systems.** **Model Answer:** A Central Air-Conditioning System is one in which the air is conditioned at a single, centrally located plant and then distributed to multiple conditioned zones through ductwork. **Advantages:** 1. **Lower Operating Cost per TR** at large capacities due to better part-load efficiency. 2. **Zonal Control** — multiple zones can be maintained at different conditions simultaneously (e.g., cold store vs. packing hall). 3. **Easier Maintenance** — equipment is centralized in a plant room. 4. **Lower Noise** in conditioned spaces (compressor/AHU is remote). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Define Bypass Factor (BF) of a cooling coil. What is its ideal and practical range?** **Model Answer:** **Bypass Factor** is the ratio of the difference between the leaving air temperature and apparatus dew point (ADP) to the difference between the entering air temperature and ADP. $$\text{BF} \;=\; \dfrac{T_2 - T_3}{T_1 - T_3}$$ - **Ideal BF** = 0 (perfect contact, all air reaches ADP) - **Practical BF** = 0.1 to 0.3 for a well-designed coil - **Failed/Plugged coil** → BF → 1 --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 3 Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q.A (a)** Explain the classification of Central Air-Conditioning Systems with neat diagrams. List the merits of All-Air systems for food processing plants. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Central AC systems are classified based on the **medium used to carry cooling energy**: **1. All-Air Systems** — Cold air is the only medium circulated. - *Single Duct*: One supply duct; cold or warm air. - *Dual Duct*: Hot and cold decks; mixed at zone. - *Multi-Zone*: AHU with multiple dampers for zones. **2. Air-Water Systems** — Chilled water + primary air; FCUs at zones. **3. All-Water Systems** — Only chilled water; no ductwork. **4. Unitary Systems** — Self-contained window/split units. **Merits of All-Air Systems in Food Plants:** - ✔ Allows **simultaneous humidity control** (essential for drying, ripening). - ✔ Can integrate with **economizer cycles** for energy savings. - ✔ Better **filtration** for clean rooms (aseptic packaging). - ✔ High **air change rates** (20–60 ACH) possible — vital for cold stores. - ✔ **Single point of dehumidification** — easier to maintain. --- **Q.A (b)** A cold storage has a sensible load of 40 kW and a latent load of 5 kW. The air enters the coil at 30 °C DBT, 22 °C WBT, and leaves at 14 °C DBT. The ADP is 10 °C. Find: **(i)** Bypass Factor, **(ii)** SHF, **(iii)** Required chilled water flow rate if inlet water temp = 8 °C and outlet = 14 °C. Take $c_{pw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Bypass Factor:** $$\text{BF} \;=\; \dfrac{T_2 - T_3}{T_1 - T_3} \;=\; \dfrac{14 - 10}{30 - 10} \;=\; \dfrac{4}{20} \;=\; 0.20$$ **(ii) Sensible Heat Factor:** $$Q_t \;=\; Q_s + Q_L \;=\; 40 + 5 \;=\; 45 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{SHF} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_t} \;=\; \dfrac{40}{45} \;\approx\; 0.889$$ **(iii) Chilled Water Flow:** Total heat absorbed by chilled water = 45 kW $\Delta T_{water} = 14 - 8 = 6$ °C $$\dot{m}_w \;=\; \dfrac{Q_t}{c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T} \;=\; \dfrac{45}{4.18 \cdot 6} \;\approx\; 1.794 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\dot{V}_w \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_w}{1000} \;\approx\; 1.794 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \;\approx\; 6.46 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q.B (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Central Air-Conditioning plant used in a food industry. Discuss the role of (i) Cooling Tower, (ii) Chiller, (iii) AHU. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 block diagram for the schematic.)* **Working Principle:** 1. **Chiller** compresses refrigerant (e.g., R-134a, R-404A for food plants due to low GWP in modern systems), condenses it, expands it, and produces chilled water at **6–8 °C** in the evaporator. 2. The chilled water is pumped to the **AHU's cooling coil**. 3. In the **AHU**: outside air + return air are mixed → filtered → passed over the cooling coil → supplied via ducts to zones. 4. The refrigerant's heat is rejected in the **condenser** to the **cooling tower**, which evaporates water to atmosphere. **Role of Key Components:** - **(i) Cooling Tower**: Sensible + latent heat rejection device; cools condenser water to ~30–35 °C using ambient air + evaporation. Critical in summer. - **(ii) Chiller**: The "refrigeration heart" — produces chilled water using VCC or absorption cycle. Sized in **TR**. - **(iii) AHU**: The "conditioning lung" — performs **filtering, cooling, dehumidifying, heating (optional) and circulation** of air. --- **Q.B (b)** A food processing hall requires 100,000 CFM of supply air. The room is at 25 °C DBT, 60% RH, and supply is at 15 °C DBT, 90% RH. Determine the **(i)** Sensible heat removed by the air, **(ii)** Apparatus Dew Point if BF = 0.2, **(iii)** Total cooling load in kW and TR. Use $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg and standard air density. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - CFM = 100,000 ft³/min - Room: $T_1 = 25$ °C, RH = 60% → $W_1 \approx 0.0119$ kg/kg - Supply: $T_2 = 15$ °C, RH = 90% → $W_2 \approx 0.0100$ kg/kg - $\Delta T = 10$ K, $\Delta W \approx 0.0019$ kg/kg **Conversion of CFM to mass flow:** $$\dot{V} \;=\; \dfrac{100{,}000}{2118.88} \;\approx\; 47.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\dot{m} \;=\; \rho \cdot \dot{V} \;=\; 1.2 \cdot 47.2 \;\approx\; 56.64 \text{ kg/s}$$ **(i) Sensible Heat:** $$Q_s \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 56.64 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 10 \;\approx\; 569.2 \text{ kW}$$ **(ii) ADP (using BF):** $$\text{ADP} \;=\; T_2 - \text{BF} \cdot (T_2 - T_3) \;\Rightarrow\; T_3 \;=\; T_2 - \dfrac{T_2 - \text{ADP}}{\text{BF}}$$ Re-arranging the standard BF formula: $$T_3 \;=\; \dfrac{T_1 - (T_2/\text{BF}) + T_2 \cdot (1/\text{BF} - 1)}{\text{?}}$$ Easier: use $\text{BF} = (T_2 - T_3)/(T_1 - T_3)$: $$0.2 \;=\; \dfrac{15 - T_3}{25 - T_3} \;\Rightarrow\; 0.2(25 - T_3) \;=\; 15 - T_3$$ $$5 - 0.2 T_3 \;=\; 15 - T_3 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.8 T_3 \;=\; 10 \;\Rightarrow\; T_3 \;=\; 12.5 \text{ °C (ADP)}$$ **(iii) Total Load:** $$Q_L \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W \;=\; 56.64 \cdot 2501 \cdot 0.0019 \;\approx\; 269.2 \text{ kW}$$ $$Q_t \;=\; Q_s + Q_L \;=\; 569.2 + 269.2 \;=\; 838.4 \text{ kW}$$ Converting to TR: $$Q_t \;=\; \dfrac{838.4}{3.517} \;\approx\; 238.4 \text{ TR}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert CFM to kg/s** — direct substitution of CFM into SI formula is a **3-mark penalty**. > 2. **Using 1 TR = 3.5 kW** (rounded) instead of **3.517 kW** — loses 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing SHF with BF** — SHF uses *heat loads*; BF uses *temperatures*. Read the question carefully. > 4. **Not including latent load in chiller sizing** — chiller must handle **total** load, not just sensible. > 5. **Mixing °C and K** — for $\Delta T$, they are numerically equal, so this is okay; but for absolute temperatures, K must be used. > 6. **Wrong sign in ADP derivation** — algebraic slips in BF equation cost full marks in 7-mark sub-questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Central AC** = single central plant + multiple zones via ductwork. - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr** — memorize this. - **1.08 factor** in imperial sensible heat equation is valid *only* at standard air ($\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K). - **BF** = measure of coil ineffectiveness → ideally **0**, practically **0.1–0.3**. - **SHF** = $Q_s / Q_t$ → determines coil selection on psychrometric chart. - **CF (Contact Factor)** = 1 − BF. - **ADP** is the *effective* coil surface temperature; air cannot be cooled below ADP. - **All-Air systems** dominate food industry due to high ACH and humidity control needs. - **All-Water systems** used where freezing-on-coil risk exists (dairy, fish). - **Duct heat gain** must be accounted in **insulation thickness** selection (3–5% of cooling load). - **Economizer cycle** (free cooling) can save **30–50% energy** in winter for cold stores. - **Heat of respiration** of fresh produce (fruits, vegetables) is **non-negligible** — must be added to cold-store load. - **CFM formula**: $\text{CFM} = Q_s / (1.08 \cdot \Delta T)$ — used in *reverse* to find duct size from a known load.

topics.title: Applications of Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning in Different Food Products: Fruits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications of Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning in Different Food Products: Fruits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Refrigeration in the fruit industry** is the controlled application of low temperatures to freshly harvested fruits to slow down metabolic activity (especially respiration and ethylene-induced ripening), suppress microbial spoilage, retard enzymatic degradation, and thereby extend the post-harvest shelf life while preserving sensory and nutritional quality.

**Air-conditioning in fruit storage** refers to the precise regulation of **temperature (T)**, **relative humidity (RH)**, **air velocity**, and (in advanced systems) **gas composition (O₂, CO₂, N₂)** within a storage enclosure to maintain fruit in a dormant or minimally active physiological state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fruits are **living, respiring biological systems**. They continue to consume O₂, produce CO₂, generate **respiratory heat (W)**, and emit **ethylene (C₂H₄)** even after harvest. Refrigeration does not *stop* these processes — it only *slows* them by a factor governed by the **Q₁₀ rule of thumb**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a fresh apple on your kitchen counter. Within a week, it turns brown, soft, and mealy. Now imagine placing that same apple in a refrigerator at **4 °C** with **90 % RH** — it stays crisp for 4–6 weeks. What changed?

The **refrigerator** is essentially a *metabolic brake*. Just as a person breathes faster when exercising and slower while sleeping, fruits "breathe" (respire) slower when cold. Cold temperatures also slow down the **ethylene "ripening alarm clock"** — the natural plant hormone that triggers softening, color change, and sugar conversion.

The **air-conditioner** adds another dimension: it controls *moisture*. A fruit is roughly **85 % water** by mass; if the surrounding air is too dry, the fruit loses moisture, shrivels, and loses weight (called *transpiration loss*). If too humid, fungal rot explodes. The A/C system is the *microclimate guardian* keeping that moisture balance perfect.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values Used Throughout Fruit Refrigeration:**
> - **Universal Gas Constant, R** = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - **Latent heat of vaporization of water (at 0 °C), h_fg** = **2501 kJ·kg⁻¹**
> - **Specific heat of fruits, c_p** ≈ **3.5 – 4.0 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹** (close to water due to high moisture content)
> - **Freezing point of most fruits** = **−0.5 °C to −2.5 °C**
> - **Respiratory heat of "low-respiring" fruits** (apples, citrus) = **5 – 10 mW·kg⁻¹**
> - **Respiratory heat of "high-respiring" fruits** (strawberries, bananas) = **20 – 50 mW·kg⁻¹ at 5 °C**
> - **Standard cold storage temperature band** = **0 °C to 4 °C**
> - **Standard cold storage RH band** = **85 % – 95 %**

### 1.4 Classification of Fruits — Foundation Concept

Fruits are divided into two physiological classes that dictate refrigeration strategy:

| Class | Behavior | Examples | Refrigeration Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Climacteric** | Sharp respiratory peak + ethylene burst at ripening | Banana, Mango, Apple, Tomato, Pear | Harvested mature-green; cooled to *arrest* ripening until marketing |
| **Non-climacteric** | No respiratory peak; gradual senescence | Citrus, Grape, Strawberry, Pineapple, Cherry | Harvested *ripe*; refrigeration must be *immediate* to retain quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "climacteric" with "more perishable". A non-climacteric strawberry is **far more perishable** than a climacteric apple because strawberries have a **much higher respiration rate** and no post-harvest ripening to "buy time".

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Fruit Refrigeration Engineering

#### **Pillar 1 — Pre-cooling (Field Heat Removal)**

Pre-cooling is the **rapid removal of "field heat"** — the sensible heat carried by the fruit from the orchard to the pack-house. Field heat typically raises fruit temperature to **25–35 °C**; if not removed within **2–4 hours** of harvest, shelf life is reduced by **30–50 %**.

**Pre-cooling methods (KTU high-yield):**

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Time | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Room cooling** | Still cold air | 24 – 48 h | Low-respiring fruits (apples, citrus) | Slow, non-uniform |
| **Forced-air (pressure) cooling** | High-velocity cold air (1–2 m·s⁻¹) | 4 – 6 h | Apples, pears, kiwifruit | Capital cost |
| **Hydrocooling** | Ice-cold water shower (0–2 °C) | 10 – 30 min | Mango, peach, sweet corn | Water-borne contamination; not for waxed fruits |
| **Vacuum cooling** | Flash evaporation at low pressure | 20 – 30 min | Leafy vegetables, mushrooms | High capital; only ~1 % moisture loss per °C |
| **Ice cooling (package icing)** | Crushed ice in carton | Hours | Broccoli, fish; rarely fruits | Limited application |
| **Cryogenic cooling** | Liquid N₂ or solid CO₂ spray | Minutes | Berries, specialty cut fruits | Very high cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU must-know:** Hydrocooling is faster than forced-air because **water has ~25× the volumetric heat capacity of air** and convective heat transfer is far superior in liquid media.

---

#### **Pillar 2 — Cold Storage (Long-Term Holding)**

Cold storage holds the pre-cooled fruit at a **commodity-specific temperature** and **RH** to minimize respiration, transpiration, and decay.

**Standard Cold Storage Conditions for Major Fruits (Board-Exam Favourite Table):**

| Fruit | Temp (°C) | RH (%) | Approx. Storage Life | Chilling-Sensitive? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apple | 0 – 4 | 90 – 95 | 2 – 8 months | No |
| Banana (green) | 13 – 15 | 90 – 95 | 1 – 4 weeks | **Yes** (< 12 °C causes chilling injury) |
| Mango | 10 – 13 | 85 – 90 | 2 – 4 weeks | **Yes** (< 10 °C) |
| Orange / Citrus | 3 – 9 | 85 – 90 | 1 – 3 months | No (some varieties) |
| Grape | 0 – 1 | 90 – 95 | 1 – 4 months | No |
| Strawberry | 0 | 90 – 95 | 5 – 7 days | No |
| Pear | −1 to 0 | 90 – 95 | 2 – 5 months | No |
| Pineapple | 7 – 13 | 85 – 90 | 2 – 4 weeks | **Yes** (< 7 °C) |
| Papaya | 7 – 13 | 85 – 90 | 1 – 3 weeks | **Yes** (< 7 °C) |
| Avocado | 4 – 13 | 85 – 90 | 2 – 4 weeks | **Yes** (< 4 °C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chilling Injury (CI):** A reversible physiological disorder occurring when susceptible fruits are held *below* their critical temperature (but *above* their freezing point). Symptoms: pitting, skin browning, failure to ripen, off-flavors. Common in: banana, mango, pineapple, avocado, papaya, citrus (some), tomato.

---

#### **Pillar 3 — Controlled Atmosphere (CA) & Modified Atmosphere (MA) Storage**

**CA storage** actively controls **O₂** and **CO₂** concentrations inside the room:
- **O₂ reduced** from 21 % → **1 – 5 %**
- **CO₂ elevated** from 0.03 % → **1 – 5 %**
- **N₂** = balance (inert filler)

**Mechanism of CA benefits:**
1. Low O₂ → suppresses aerobic respiration (which produces CO₂ + heat + H₂O)
2. High CO₂ → competitive inhibitor of ethylene action at receptor sites
3. Combined effect → shelf life of apples extended from 4 months to **8 – 12 months**

**MA storage** is a *passive* version: the fruit is sealed in a polymeric film package; the fruit itself consumes O₂ and releases CO₂ until equilibrium is reached.

**Commercial Gas Mixes (KTU high-yield):**
- Apples: 1.5 – 2.5 % O₂ + 1 – 3 % CO₂
- Pear: 2 – 3 % O₂ + 0.5 – 1.5 % CO₂
- Strawberry: 5 – 10 % O₂ + 15 – 20 % CO₂ (high CO₂ tolerated)
- Banana: 2 – 5 % O₂ + 2 – 5 % CO₂

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Refrigeration: Respiration & Ethylene Kinetics

The **rate of deterioration** of a fruit is proportional to its **rate of respiration**. The Arrhenius-type relationship is:

$$
r = r_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

where **r** is the respiration rate, **r₀** is the pre-exponential factor, **E_a** is the activation energy (~ 50 – 80 kJ·mol⁻¹ for fruit enzymes), **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**, and **T** is absolute temperature in K.

For engineering estimates, the **Q₁₀ rule** is used:

$$
Q_{10} = \frac{r_{(T+10)}}{r_T} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** For every **10 °C drop** in storage temperature, the respiration rate **halves to one-third**.

**Ethylene (C₂H₄) production rates** (at 20 °C, μL·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹):
- Apple: 10 – 30
- Banana: 1 – 5 (climacteric peak: 100+)
- Citrus: < 0.1
- Strawberry: < 0.1

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Ethylene is *autocatalytic* in climacteric fruits — a single ripe apple can ripen an entire cold room. This is why storage rooms must segregate **ethylene producers** (apples, bananas) from **ethylene-sensitive** commodities (kiwifruit, cucumber, cut flowers).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{field} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Field heat (sensible) to remove in pre-cooling | kJ |
| 2 | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot \dot{q}_{resp}$ | Respiratory heat load over storage time | kJ |
| 3 | $\dot{q}_{resp} = 10.7 \cdot m_{CO_2}$ | Heat released per unit CO₂ produced (approximate) | kJ per g CO₂ |
| 4 | $Q_{trans} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Transpiration (latent) heat from moisture loss | kJ |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_w = K \cdot A \cdot (p_{w,surf} - p_{w,air})$ | Water loss rate (Fick's law analogy) | kg·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $t_{cool} = \frac{m \cdot c_p}{h_c \cdot A} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_f - T_\infty}\right)$ | Newton's law of cooling time | seconds |
| 7 | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$ | Temperature coefficient of respiration | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{total} = Q_{field} + Q_{resp} + Q_{trans} + Q_{conduction}$ | Total refrigeration load | kJ or kW |
| 9 | $h_c = 10.45 - v + 10 \sqrt{v}$ | Convective HTC for air over fruit (ASHRAE) | W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\%WL = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | Percentage weight loss (quality metric) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder on Units:** In KTU board problems, mass is in **kg**, time in **hours**, and load in **W or kW**. Always convert 1 h = 3600 s when using the convective formula. **h_c** for still air is **5–10 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**; for forced-air (1 m·s⁻¹) it is **15–25 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cooling Time for a Single Fruit (Lumped Capacitance Model)

**Assumption:** Fruit is small (d < 7 cm) and Biot number **Bi = h_c·L/k_f < 0.1**, so internal conduction is much faster than surface convection → fruit has a uniform temperature that decays exponentially.

**Starting point — Newton's law of cooling:**

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} = -\frac{h_c \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p}(T - T_\infty)
$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**

$$
\frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{h_c \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p}\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from initial T_i to final T_f over time 0 to t:**

$$
\int_{T_i}^{T_f} \frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{h_c \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} \int_0^t dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left integral as ln of the ratio:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{T_f - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}\right) = -\frac{h_c \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p}\, t
$$

**Step 4 — Solve explicitly for t (the famous "half-cooling time" form):**

$$
t = \frac{m \cdot c_p}{h_c \cdot A} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_f - T_\infty}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Define the time constant τ = m·c_p / (h_c·A):**

$$
t = \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_f - T_\infty}\right)
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** The "7/8 cooling time" — the time to reach T_f where (T_f − T_∞)/(T_i − T_∞) = 1/8 — is **3τ**, and is the standard design metric in forced-air cooling because it gives **87.5 % of total field heat removal**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A batch of **5000 kg of mangoes** is to be pre-cooled from **30 °C to 13 °C** using forced-air cooling at **0 °C** with h_c = 20 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹. Average fruit: **diameter = 8 cm**, **c_p = 3.8 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**, **density ρ = 1000 kg·m⁻³**, respiratory heat = **40 mW·kg⁻¹** at 30 °C. Neglect transpiration. Calculate:
**(a)** The 7/8 cooling time
**(b)** The sensible heat to be removed (field heat)
**(c)** The respiratory heat generated during cooling
**(d)** The peak refrigeration load (assuming 7/8 cooling achieved in 5 h)

---

#### **Part (a) — 7/8 Cooling Time**

**Step 1 — Volume and surface area of one fruit (sphere):**

$$
V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.04)^3 = 2.681 \times 10^{-4}\, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
A = 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi (0.04)^2 = 2.011 \times 10^{-2}\, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of one fruit:**

$$
m_1 = \rho V = 1000 \times 2.681 \times 10^{-4} = 0.2681\, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Time constant τ:**

$$
\tau = \frac{m_1 \cdot c_p}{h_c \cdot A} = \frac{0.2681 \times 3800}{20 \times 0.02011}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{1018.78}{0.4022} = 2533\, \text{s} \approx 42.2\, \text{min}
$$

**Step 4 — For 7/8 cooling time, t = 3τ:**

$$
t_{7/8} = 3 \times 2533 = 7599\, \text{s} \approx 2.11\, \text{h}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Sensible Field Heat Q_field**

$$
Q_{field} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 5000 \times 3.8 \times (30 - 13)
$$

$$
Q_{field} = 5000 \times 3.8 \times 17 = 323{,}000\, \text{kJ}
$$

---

#### **Part (c) — Respiratory Heat During Cooling**

Average temperature during cooling ≈ (30 + 13)/2 = 21.5 °C. Using the **Q₁₀ = 2** approximation, respiration rate at 21.5 °C relative to 30 °C:

$$
\frac{r_{21.5}}{r_{30}} = 2^{(21.5 - 30)/10} = 2^{-0.85} = 0.555
$$

So $\dot{q}_{resp,\,21.5} = 0.555 \times 40 = 22.2\, \text{mW·kg}^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Total respiratory power:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{resp} = 5000 \times 0.0222 = 111\, \text{W} = 0.111\, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat generated in 2.11 h (t₇/₈):**

$$
Q_{resp} = 0.111 \times 2.11 \times 3600 = 843\, \text{kJ}
$$

(About **0.26 % of Q_field** — often neglected in practice for short pre-cooling, but critical for **long cold storage**.)

---

#### **Part (d) — Peak Refrigeration Load**

Total heat to be removed in **5 h** (assumed to achieve 7/8 cooling):

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{field} + Q_{resp} = 323{,}000 + 843 = 323{,}843\, \text{kJ}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{peak} = \frac{323{,}843}{5 \times 3600} = 17.99\, \text{kW} \approx 5.1\, \text{TR}
$$

*(1 TR = 3.517 kW)*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Refrigeration Strategy

| Fruit Property | Low Respiration | High Respiration |
|---|---|---|
| **Climacteric** | Apple, Pear → CA storage at 0 °C, 1–3 % CO₂ | Banana, Mango → Pre-cool + ripening room (ethylene trigger) |
| **Non-climacteric** | Citrus → Cold + wax coating to suppress transpiration | Strawberry, Grape → Rapid hydro-cool + MAP, near 0 °C |
| **Chilling-Sensitive** | Avocado, Papaya → 10–13 °C (above CI threshold) | Pineapple → 7–13 °C, MA packaging |
| **Chilling-Tolerant** | Apple, Pear, Grape → −1 to 0 °C optimal | Strawberry → 0 °C with high CO₂ MA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Fruit Cold Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Orchard Harvest 30C] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Pre-Cooling Stage]
    C --> C1[Forced Air Cooling]
    C --> C2[Hydrocooling]
    C --> C3[Vacuum Cooling]
    C1 --> D[Cold Storage Room]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Regular Cold Store 0 to 4C]
    D --> D2[CA Store 1 to 5 percent O2]
    D --> D3[MA Packaging Room]
    D1 --> E[Refrigerated Transport 5C]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Distribution Center]
    F --> G[Retail Display 5 to 10C]
    G --> H[Consumer Refrigerator 4C]
    D1 -.heat load.-> I[Refrigeration Unit 5 to 50 TR]
    D2 -.heat load.-> I
    I -.rejection.-> J[Ambient Air or Water Condenser]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Respiration & Refrigeration Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fruit Tissue] -->|O2 uptake| B[Aerobic Respiration]
    A -->|C2H4 emission| C[Ethylene Accumulation]
    B -->|CO2 release| D[Storage Room Atmosphere]
    C --> D
    D -->|Triggers ripening in climacteric| A
    B -->|Heat generation| E[Respiratory Heat Load]
    E --> F[Refrigeration System]
    F -->|Removes heat| G[Low T slows respiration]
    G -->|Reduces CO2| A
    F -->|Reduces C2H4| C
    D -->|CA reduces O2| H[CA Scrubber N2 flushing]
    H --> D
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — CA Storage System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Room[CA Cold Room]
        F1[Fruit Pallets]
        F2[Fruit Pallets]
        F3[Fruit Pallets]
    end
    Room -->|Sample line| S[Gas Sampling Port]
    S --> A[O2 CO2 Analyzer]
    A -->|Feedback| CC[Central Controller]
    CC -->|Open N2 valve| N2[N2 Generator PSA]
    CC -->|Open CO2 scrubber| SC[CO2 Scrubber Activated Carbon]
    CC -->|Open air inlet| AI[Fresh Air Inlet]
    N2 --> Room
    SC --> Room
    AI --> Room
    CC -->|Display| OP[Operator HMI]
    Room -. temperature .-> TC[Thermocouples]
    TC -. signal .-> CC
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Fruit Selection of Cooling Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Fruit Batch] --> Q1{High Water Loss Tolerance}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Climacteric}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[High RH Storage 95 percent waxed]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Pre Cool at 10 to 13 C]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5[Pre Cool at 0 to 4 C]
    Q4 --> Q6{Chilling Sensitive}
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Use MA packaging]
    Q6 -->|No| Q8[Use CA or regular cold store]
    Q3 --> End[Final Cold Chain]
    Q7 --> End
    Q8 --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Distinguish between climacteric and non-climacteric fruits. Give two examples of each and state how this classification affects their cold storage strategy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Climacteric fruits exhibit a sharp rise in respiration rate (CO₂ output) and a burst of ethylene (C₂H₄) production at the onset of ripening, called the *climacteric peak*. Non-climacteric fruits do not show this peak; they ripen gradually on the plant.
- **Examples (1 mark):** Climacteric — banana, mango, apple, tomato. Non-climacteric — citrus, grape, strawberry, pineapple.
- **Storage strategy (1 mark):** Climacteric fruits are harvested mature-green and stored at low temperature to *arrest* the climacteric until marketing; they may later be ripened in a controlled-ethylene ripening room. Non-climacteric fruits are harvested ripe and must be *immediately* cooled to retard senescence, as they have no post-harvest ripening to "buy time."

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supple]**
*Define "chilling injury" in fruits. Name four fruits susceptible to it and state the minimum safe storage temperature for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Chilling injury is a reversible physiological disorder that occurs when a susceptible fruit is held at temperatures **above its freezing point but below a critical threshold** (typically below 10–15 °C for tropical fruits), causing cellular dysfunction.
- **Symptoms (1 mark):** Pitting, skin browning, failure to ripen, off-flavors, increased decay upon return to warm temperatures.
- **Fruits and minimum safe temperature (1 mark):**
  - Banana: 12 – 13 °C
  - Mango: 10 – 13 °C
  - Avocado: 4 – 13 °C (variety dependent)
  - Pineapple: 7 – 10 °C
  - Papaya: 7 – 10 °C

---

### **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – July 2024, End-Sem Model]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **principle, construction, and working of a Controlled Atmosphere (CA) cold storage system** for apples. State typical gas composition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage room holds **20,000 kg of apples** at 0 °C. The apples have a respiration rate of **8 mW·kg⁻¹** at 0 °C. The room walls transmit a conductive heat gain of **1.8 kW**, and the air-handling fan motor dissipates **0.6 kW** into the room. Door-opening and miscellaneous loads amount to **0.4 kW**. Calculate the **total steady-state refrigeration load in kW and in tonnes of refrigeration (TR)**. Take 1 TR = 3.517 kW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A CA cold store for apples is a sealed, well-insulated cold room equipped with:
1. **Vapor-compression refrigeration unit** (direct expansion or brine coil) to maintain 0 – 1 °C.
2. **Gas-tight construction** — the room is lined with gas-impermeable panels (typically galvanized steel with butyl-rubber-sealed joints) and fitted with a pressure-relief valve (water-scaled or diaphragm) to allow for the small pressure swings caused by O₂ scrubbing.
3. **N₂ generator (PSA – Pressure Swing Adsorption)** to flush O₂ down to 1.5 – 2.5 %.
4. **CO₂ scrubber** (water-scrubber, activated-carbon adsorption, or ethanolamine) to keep CO₂ at 1 – 3 %.
5. **O₂/CO₂ gas analyzer** (paramagnetic for O₂, IR for CO₂) with feedback to controller.
6. **Humidifier** to maintain RH 90 – 95 %.
7. **Temperature and RH sensors** distributed throughout the room.

**Working principle:** The sealed room is first cooled to 0 °C, then the atmosphere is purged with N₂ to drop O₂ from 21 % to ~2 %. Apples, being climacteric, continue to respire: they consume O₂ and release CO₂. The CO₂ scrubber removes the excess CO₂; the O₂ analyzer signals the N₂ generator (or air inlet) to maintain target O₂. Under 2 % O₂ + 2 % CO₂ at 0 °C, apple respiration drops to ~25 % of normal-air cold storage, extending shelf life from 4 months to 8 – 12 months.

**Typical gas composition for apples:** **O₂ = 1.5 – 2.5 %; CO₂ = 1 – 3 %; N₂ = balance; T = 0 – 1 °C; RH = 90 – 95 %.**

**Mark split (a):** Construction details — 3 marks; Working — 2 marks; Gas composition & reasoning — 2 marks.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Respiratory heat load:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{resp} = m \cdot \dot{q}_{resp} = 20{,}000 \times 0.008 = 160\, \text{W} = 0.160\, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Conduction through walls:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = 1.8\, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Fan motor dissipation:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{fan} = 0.6\, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Door and miscellaneous:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{misc} = 0.4\, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Total steady-state load:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = 0.160 + 1.8 + 0.6 + 0.4 = 2.96\, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to TR:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \frac{2.96}{3.517} = 0.842\, \text{TR}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply safety factor of 1.15 (KTU convention):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{design} = 0.842 \times 1.15 = 0.968\, \text{TR} \approx 1.0\, \text{TR}
$$

**Mark split (b):** Each load component — 1 mark each (4 marks); Summation — 1 mark; TR conversion & design — 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End-Sem]

**(a)** Compare **hydrocooling, forced-air cooling, and vacuum cooling** as pre-cooling methods for fruits. State the principle, one best-suited fruit, typical cooling time, and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** **3000 kg of strawberries** are cooled from 25 °C to 2 °C using forced-air cooling at an air temperature of 0 °C. The convective heat transfer coefficient is **h_c = 18 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**. The average strawberry is a sphere of diameter 2.5 cm, density 1000 kg·m⁻³, and c_p = 3.9 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹. Calculate:
  (i) The **time constant τ**
  (ii) The **7/8 cooling time**
  (iii) The **total field heat removed** in kJ
  (iv) The **peak refrigeration load** in kW if 7/8 cooling is achieved in **20 minutes**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Method | Principle | Best Fruit | Cooling Time | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrocooling** | Direct contact with chilled water (0–2 °C) flowing over fruit | Mango, peach, sweet corn | 10 – 30 min | Risk of microbial cross-contamination; cannot be used for waxed fruits |
| **Forced-air cooling** | High-velocity cold air (1–2 m·s⁻¹) forced through stacked produce | Apple, pear, kiwifruit, strawberry | 4 – 6 h | Higher fan power; surface desiccation if RH not controlled |
| **Vacuum cooling** | Flash evaporation of surface water at low pressure (4–6 mbar) | Leafy veg, mushrooms; limited for dense fruits | 20 – 30 min | High capital; causes ~1 % moisture loss per °C; not suited for dense, low-surface-area fruits |

**Mark split (a):** Table or equivalent comparison — 5 marks; Synthesis & recommendation — 2 marks.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Geometry of one strawberry:**

$$
r = 0.0125\, \text{m}
$$

$$
V = \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.0125)^3 = 8.181 \times 10^{-6}\, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
A = 4\pi (0.0125)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of one strawberry:**

$$
m_1 = \rho V = 1000 \times 8.181 \times 10^{-6} = 0.008181\, \text{kg}
$$

**(i) Time constant τ:**

$$
\tau = \frac{m_1 \cdot c_p}{h_c \cdot A} = \frac{0.008181 \times 3900}{18 \times 0.001963}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{31.91}{0.03534} = 902.9\, \text{s} \approx 15.05\, \text{min}
$$

**(ii) 7/8 cooling time, t = 3τ:**

$$
t_{7/8} = 3 \times 902.9 = 2708.8\, \text{s} \approx 45.2\, \text{min}
$$

**(iii) Total field heat:**

$$
Q_{field} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 3000 \times 3.9 \times (25 - 2)
$$

$$
Q_{field} = 3000 \times 3.9 \times 23 = 269{,}100\, \text{kJ}
$$

**(iv) Peak load if 7/8 cooling in 20 min (note: 20 min < 45 min calculated, so this corresponds to a fractional cooling — we use the stated 20 min):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{peak} = \frac{Q_{field}}{t} = \frac{269{,}100}{20 \times 60} = 224.25\, \text{kW}
$$

Converting to TR:

$$
\dot{Q}_{peak} = \frac{224.25}{3.517} = 63.8\, \text{TR}
$$

> **Comment:** The numerical 20 min is much shorter than the natural t₇/₈, implying either higher h_c (e.g., aggressive 2 m·s⁻¹ airflow) or that 20 min corresponds to a smaller fraction of the temperature drop. The student should note this in their answer.

**Mark split (b):** Geometry — 1 mark; τ — 2 marks; t₇/₈ — 1 mark; Q_field — 1.5 marks; Peak load — 1.5 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** of 1.10 – 1.20 on total load (loses 1 mark in (b) type).
> 2. **Mixing °C and K** in Q₁₀/Arrhenius equations — temperatures in exponentials *must* be in Kelvin.
> 3. **Ignoring respiratory heat** in long-term cold-storage problems (it is negligible in short pre-cooling but **dominant** in cold-storage steady-state).
> 4. **Confusing "cooling time" and "half cooling time"** — the half-cooling time corresponds to ln(2) = 0.693, not 1.0.
> 5. **Using fruit density as 900 kg·m⁻³ (apple)** but **1000 kg·m⁻³ (berry/mango)** — read the problem carefully.
> 6. **Not stating gas composition units as % (v/v)** for CA problems — examiners are strict.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Fruits are *living*; refrigeration slows — does not stop — life processes.**
- **Two-class system:** Climacteric (banana, mango, apple) vs Non-climacteric (citrus, grape, strawberry). Determines harvest maturity and storage strategy.
- **Respiratory heat** is the key engineering load in *long-term* cold storage; *field heat* dominates *pre-cooling* loads.
- **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 – 3** is the engineer's rule of thumb for temperature-respiration kinetics.
- **Pre-cooling methods (in order of speed):** Cryogenic > Vacuum > Hydro > Forced-air > Room.
- **Hydrocooling is fastest among practical methods** because water has ~25× the volumetric heat capacity of air.
- **Chilling injury** is *not* freezing — it occurs *above* freezing point in tropical fruits stored below their critical temperature.
- **CA storage** = low O₂ (1.5 – 5 %) + high CO₂ (1 – 5 %) + low T (0 – 1 °C) for apples/pears → 2× to 3× extension in shelf life.
- **MA packaging** uses fruit's own respiration to modify internal atmosphere; passive and cheap.
- **Ethylene** is autocatalytic in climacteric fruits — segregate producers (apple, banana) from sensitive commodities (kiwifruit, cut flowers).
- **Key numbers to memorize:** c_p ≈ 3.8 – 4.0 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹; ρ ≈ 1000 kg·m⁻³; h_c (still air) ≈ 5 – 10; h_c (forced air) ≈ 15 – 25; 1 TR = 3.517 kW.
- **Lumped capacitance model** valid for d < 7 cm and Bi < 0.1 — most small fruits qualify.
- **7/8 cooling time = 3τ** is the standard design metric for forced-air pre-cooling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vegetables (Post-Harvest Refrigeration Context):** Edible plant tissues (roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits, tubers, and bulbs) that are harvested at a defined physiological maturity and require carefully controlled refrigeration parameters (temperature **0 °C to 13 °C**, relative humidity **85–98 %**, and controlled atmosphere composition) to suppress respiration, transpiration, microbial decay, and enzymatic senescence during post-harvest handling and storage.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, vegetables are treated as **living, respiring, and transpiring biological commodities** whose post-harvest life is governed by:

- **Respiration Rate ($R_{CO_2}$)** measured in $\text{mL CO}_2 / (\text{kg·h})$
- **Transpiration Rate ($T_r$)** measured in $\text{g water} / (\text{kg·h})$
- **Heat of Respiration ($Q_r$)** measured in $\text{mW/kg}$ or $\text{W/tonne}$
- **Chilling Sensitivity Index (CSI)** — empirical classification parameter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Sleeping Athlete"**
> Imagine a vegetable as a marathon runner who has just finished a race (harvest). The runner is still **breathing hard (respiring)**, **sweating (transpiring)**, and **slowly losing energy reserves**. If left in the open sun, the runner collapses quickly (wilting, senescence). If placed in a **cool, humid recovery room (cold storage)** with **regulated oxygen (CA storage)**, the runner's metabolism slows down — extending usable life from 2–3 days to **weeks or months**.

The cold chain does **not stop life** in vegetables — it **slows life down** to a controllable pace.

## 1.3 Classification of Vegetables (Syllabus Foundation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Vegetables are classified by **edible plant part** AND by **chilling sensitivity**. Both classifications are critical for refrigeration design.

| Class | Examples | Typical Storage Temp (°C) | RH (%) | Approx. Shelf Life |
|-------|----------|--------------------------|--------|--------------------|
| **Leafy Vegetables** | Lettuce, Spinach, Cabbage | 0 | 95–100 | 1–3 weeks |
| **Root & Tuber** | Carrot, Beet, Potato (cured) | 0–4 | 95–98 | 2–9 months |
| **Bulb Vegetables** | Onion, Garlic | 0 | 65–70 | 6–9 months |
| **Solanaceous Fruits** | Tomato, Brinjal, Capsicum | 7–13 | 90–95 | 1–3 weeks |
| **Cucurbits** | Cucumber, Gourd, Pumpkin | 10–13 | 85–90 | 2–6 months |
| **Legumes (Pods)** | Peas, Beans | 0–7 | 95 | 1–3 weeks |
| **Cruciferous** | Cauliflower, Broccoli | 0 | 95–100 | 2–4 weeks |
| **Stem Vegetables** | Celery, Asparagus | 0–2 | 95–100 | 2–4 weeks |

## 1.4 Why Refrigeration is Mandatory for Vegetables

- **High Respiration Rate:** Vegetables respire actively post-harvest, generating **metabolic heat**.
- **High Moisture Content (80–95 %):** Promotes microbial growth and wilting.
- **Ethylene Sensitivity:** Many vegetables are damaged by trace ethylene ($\ge 0.1\,\text{ppm}$).
- **Chilling Injury Risk:** Some tropical vegetables are *more damaged by cold than by warmth*.

## 1.5 Standard Refrigeration Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key constants and metrics used throughout this module:
> - **Specific heat of fresh vegetables ($C_p$):** $\approx 3.5\text{–}4.0\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - **Latent heat of freezing ($L_f$):** $\approx 250\text{–}290\,\text{kJ/kg}$
> - **Initial freezing point:** $-0.5\,°C$ to $-2.5\,°C$ (varies with sugar/salt content)
> - **Respiratory quotient (RQ):** $R_q = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{\dot{V}_{O_2}} \approx 1.0$ for aerobic respiration
> - **Respiration heat release:** $\approx 10.5\,\text{kJ per L of O}_2$ consumed

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Post-Harvest Physiology of Vegetables

### 2.1.1 Respiration — The Central Phenomenon

Vegetables, after harvest, remain **metabolically active living tissues**. They consume **O₂**, release **CO₂**, and generate **heat** through aerobic respiration:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \longrightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Energy (Heat)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This exothermic reaction is the **primary biological heat load** that the refrigeration system must overcome, *in addition to* the conduction/convection loads.

**Key Respiration Parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Units |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-------|
| Respiration Rate | $R_{CO_2}$ | 3 – 80 | $\text{mL CO}_2 / (\text{kg·h})$ |
| Heat of Respiration | $Q_r$ | 5 – 150 | $\text{mW/kg}$ |
| Q₁₀ Coefficient | $Q_{10}$ | 2 – 3 | Dimensionless |
| Activation Energy | $E_a$ | 40 – 80 | $\text{kJ/mol}$ |

### 2.1.2 The Q₁₀ Rule (Temperature Sensitivity)

> [!NOTE]
> For every **10 °C rise** in temperature, the respiration rate **doubles or triples**.
> For every **10 °C fall**, respiration rate is **halved or thirded**.

Mathematically:

$$
R_2 = R_1 \cdot (Q_{10})^{\Delta T / 10}
$$

Where $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$.

**Engineering Implication:** A vegetable stored at 25 °C respires **8–27× faster** than at 0 °C. **Rapid pre-cooling is non-negotiable.**

### 2.1.3 Transpiration

Water loss from vegetable surfaces leads to **wilting, shrivelling, and turgor loss**. The transpiration rate is governed by:

$$
T_r = k_t \cdot (P_{sat}(T_{prod}) - P_{air}) \cdot \dfrac{A}{m}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $T_r$ | Transpiration rate (g/kg·h) |
| $k_t$ | Mass transfer coefficient (m/h) |
| $P_{sat}$ | Saturation vapour pressure at product temperature |
| $P_{air}$ | Partial vapour pressure of air |
| $A$ | Surface area (m²) |
| $m$ | Mass (kg) |

> [!WARNING]
> A **5 % weight loss** due to transpiration renders most vegetables **unmarketable**. Hence, **high RH (95–98 %)** is critical in cold storage.

### 2.1.4 Chilling Injury (CI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chilling Injury:** Irreversible physiological damage caused by storing chill-sensitive vegetables at temperatures **above their freezing point but below a critical threshold (≈ 10–13 °C)**.

| Sensitive Vegetable | Safe Min Temp | CI Threshold |
|---------------------|---------------|--------------|
| Tomato (ripe) | 7–10 °C | < 7 °C |
| Cucumber | 10 °C | < 10 °C |
| Capsicum | 7 °C | < 7 °C |
| Brinjal | 10 °C | < 10 °C |
| Pumpkin | 13 °C | < 13 °C |
| Sweet Potato | 13 °C | < 13 °C |

**Symptoms:** Surface pitting, water-soaked lesions, off-flavours, failure to ripen, internal browning, accelerated decay.

### 2.1.5 Freezing Injury

If tissue temperature drops below the **initial freezing point** ($T_f$, typically $-0.5$ to $-2.5\,°C$ for most vegetables), ice crystals form **intra-cellularly**, rupturing cell walls. The result is **structural collapse** upon thawing — known as *freeze-burn* or *blanching injury*.

## 2.2 Refrigeration Requirements of Vegetables

### 2.2.1 Optimum Storage Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four pillars of vegetable cold storage design:
> 1. **Temperature** — suppress respiration and microbial activity.
> 2. **Relative Humidity (RH)** — minimize transpiration.
> 3. **Atmosphere Composition** — retard senescence via CA/MAP.
> 4. **Ethylene Control** — prevent autocatalytic spoilage.

### 2.2.2 Controlled Atmosphere (CA) & Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)

| Parameter | Normal Air | Typical CA for Vegetables |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------------|
| O₂ | 21 % | 2–5 % |
| CO₂ | 0.03 % | 3–10 % |
| N₂ | 78 % | Balance |

> [!NOTE]
> Reducing O₂ from 21 % → 3 % and elevating CO₂ to 5 % can **double the storage life** of many leafy and root vegetables.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_r$ | Total respiratory heat load | W |
| 2 | $R_2 = R_1 \cdot Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10}$ | Respiration rate change with temperature | $\text{mL CO}_2/(\text{kg·h})$ |
| 3 | $Q_r = 10.5 \cdot \dot{V}_{O_2}$ | Heat released per L of O₂ consumed | $\text{kJ/L O}_2$ |
| 4 | $q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible cooling load | $\text{kJ}$ or W |
| 5 | $q_l = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat of freezing load | $\text{kJ}$ or W |
| 6 | $Q_{prod} = q_s + q_l + Q_{resp}$ | Total product cooling load | W |
| 7 | $Q_{total} = Q_{prod} + Q_{trans} + Q_{equip}$ | Total refrigeration load | W |
| 8 | $T_f = -1.0 \cdot \sum m_i x_i$ | Freezing point depression (Raoult's) | °C |
| 9 | $RH = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{sat}(T)} \times 100$ | Relative humidity | % |
| 10 | $T_r = k_t \cdot \Delta P_v \cdot \dfrac{A}{m}$ | Transpiration rate | $\text{g/(kg·h)}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Designing pre-coolers, reefer containers, and ripening chambers.
- **Industrial Cold Storage:** Sizing compressors for tropical produce export (e.g., Kerala vegetable export industry).
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Shelf-life extension for retail vegetables.
- **Energy-Efficient Design:** Optimizing $Q_{10}$-based temperature selection to reduce compressor load.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Refrigeration Load for a Vegetable Cold Storage

### Step 1: Identify Heat Load Components

The total refrigeration load $Q_{total}$ comprises **four distinct contributions**:

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{prod} + Q_{trans} + Q_{equip} + Q_{infil}
$$

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| $Q_{prod}$ | Heat to be removed from the vegetable product (sensible + latent + respiration) |
| $Q_{trans}$ | Heat transmitted through walls/ceilings/floors of cold room |
| $Q_{equip}$ | Heat from lights, motors, workers |
| $Q_{infil}$ | Heat from door openings and air infiltration |

### Step 2: Product Heat Load — Three Sub-components

$$
Q_{prod} = Q_s + Q_l + Q_r
$$

**Step 2a: Sensible Heat (Pre-cooling above freezing point)**

$$
Q_s = m \cdot C_{p,\,above} \cdot (T_i - T_f)
$$

**Step 2b: Latent Heat of Freezing**

$$
Q_l = m \cdot L_f
$$

**Step 2c: Sensible Heat (Post-freezing cooling)**

$$
Q_{s,\,post} = m \cdot C_{p,\,below} \cdot (T_f - T_{final})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For vegetables stored at temperatures **above their freezing point** (typical case), $Q_l$ and $Q_{s,\,post}$ are **zero**. Only $Q_s$ and $Q_r$ apply.

### Step 3: Respiration Heat Load

From stoichiometry: 1 mole of glucose (180 g) releases 2815 kJ. Using volumetric conversion at STP:

$$
Q_r = 10.5 \cdot \dot{V}_{O_2} \quad [\text{kJ per L O}_2]
$$

Or using respiration rate $R$ in $\text{mL CO}_2/(\text{kg·h})$ and mass $m$:

$$
Q_r = \dfrac{m \cdot R \cdot 10.5}{22{,}400} \cdot 3600 \quad [\text{kJ/h}]
$$

Where $22{,}400\,\text{mL}$ is the molar volume of gas at STP.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Numerical Problem (Model KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A cold storage contains **5,000 kg of carrots**. The carrots enter at **20 °C** and must be cooled to **1 °C** for storage. Respiration rate at 1 °C is $R = 4\,\text{mL CO}_2/(\text{kg·h})$. Calculate the **peak refrigeration load** assuming cooling must be completed in **4 hours**.

### Given Data

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| $m$ | 5,000 kg |
| $T_i$ | 20 °C |
| $T_{final}$ | 1 °C |
| $C_p$ (carrot, above freezing) | 3.85 kJ/(kg·K) |
| $R$ | 4 mL CO₂/(kg·h) |
| Cooling time | 4 h |

### Step A: Sensible Heat Load $Q_s$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_i - T_{final}) \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}000 \times 3.85 \times (20 - 1) \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}000 \times 3.85 \times 19 \\[6pt]
&= 365{,}750\,\text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B: Respiration Heat Load $Q_r$

Total respiration in 4 hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total CO}_2 &= m \cdot R \cdot t \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}000 \times 4 \times 4 \\[6pt]
&= 80{,}000\,\text{mL CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to L O₂ equivalent (assuming RQ = 1.0):

$$
\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{1000} = 80\,\text{L O}_2
$$

Respiration heat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_r &= 10.5 \times 80 \\[6pt]
&= 840\,\text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C: Total Refrigeration Energy

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= Q_s + Q_r \\[6pt]
&= 365{,}750 + 840 \\[6pt]
&= 366{,}590\,\text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D: Convert to Power (W or TR)

Time = 4 h = 14,400 s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{366{,}590}{14{,}400} \\[6pt]
&= 25.46\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

In tonnes of refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{TR} &= \dfrac{25.46}{3.517} \\[6pt]
&= 7.24\,\text{TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Peak refrigeration load $\approx$ **7.24 TR** or **25.46 kW** for 5 tonnes of carrots cooled in 4 hours.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Vegetable vs. Storage Parameters

| Vegetable | Storage T (°C) | RH (%) | Respiration $R$ (mL/kg·h at T) | Freezing Pt (°C) | Approx. Shelf Life |
|-----------|----------------|--------|--------------------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Cabbage | 0 | 95–100 | 6 (at 0 °C) | -0.9 | 5–6 mo |
| Carrot | 0 | 95–100 | 4 (at 0 °C) | -1.4 | 4–6 mo |
| Onion (dry) | 0 | 65–70 | 3 (at 0 °C) | -0.8 | 6–9 mo |
| Potato (cured) | 4 | 90–95 | 3 (at 4 °C) | -0.6 | 5–10 mo |
| Tomato (mature green) | 13 | 90–95 | 12 (at 13 °C) | -0.6 | 2–4 wk |
| Cucumber | 10 | 85–90 | 8 (at 10 °C) | -0.5 | 1–2 wk |
| Capsicum | 7 | 95 | 10 (at 7 °C) | -0.7 | 2–3 wk |
| Spinach | 0 | 95–100 | 22 (at 0 °C) | -0.3 | 1–2 wk |
| Cauliflower | 0 | 95–100 | 13 (at 0 °C) | -0.8 | 2–4 wk |
| Brinjal | 10 | 90–95 | 11 (at 10 °C) | -0.8 | 1–2 wk |

## 3.4 Derivation: Q₁₀-Based Storage Life Prediction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q₁₀ Relationship:**
> The shelf life $S$ of a vegetable is inversely proportional to its respiration rate.

$$
S \propto \dfrac{1}{R}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{S_1}{S_2} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = (Q_{10})^{\Delta T / 10}
$$

### Worked Example

> A spinach sample has shelf life **7 days** at **5 °C**. Estimate shelf life at **0 °C**, taking $Q_{10} = 2.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{S_{0°C}}{S_{5°C}} &= (Q_{10})^{(5-0)/10} \\[6pt]
&= (2.5)^{0.5} \\[6pt]
&= 1.581
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{0°C} = 7 \times 1.581 \approx 11.07\,\text{days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Dropping temperature from 5 °C to 0 °C extends spinach shelf life by **~58 %**.

> 3. **Brinjal (Eggplant)** — Safe minimum storage temperature: **10 °C** (below this, internal browning occurs).

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> *"What is the Q₁₀ coefficient in vegetable respiration? State its typical range."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Q₁₀ coefficient** quantifies the **temperature sensitivity of respiration rate** in vegetables. It is defined as the **factor by which the respiration rate increases for every 10 °C rise** in temperature.
>
> **Mathematical form:** $R_2 = R_1 \cdot (Q_{10})^{\Delta T / 10}$
>
> **Typical range for vegetables:** $Q_{10} = 2.0$ to $3.0$.
>
> **Engineering significance:** A higher Q₁₀ means the vegetable is more sensitive to temperature abuse — emphasizing the importance of **rapid pre-cooling** and **strict cold-chain maintenance**.
