<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME — EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the thermophysical properties of whole milk relevant to refrigeration design.**

**Model Answer:**
- Specific heat c_p = **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)**
- Density ρ = **1030 kg/m³** at 4 °C
- Freezing point T_f = **-0.55 °C** (depressed due to dissolved salts and lactose)
- Latent heat of fusion h_sf = **232 kJ/kg**
- Thermal conductivity k = **0.55 W/(m·K)**
- Viscosity at 4 °C = **2.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- Initial pH = **6.6 – 6.8**

These properties govern the **cooling time, refrigeration capacity**, and **compressor selection** in dairy plant design. *(3 marks: properties 2 + engineering relevance 1)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the "4 °C rule" in dairy cold chain management? Why is it critical?**

**Model Answer:**
The **"4 °C rule"** dictates that fluid milk must be cooled to **4 °C or below** within **2 hours of milking** and maintained at that temperature through every stage of the cold chain. It is critical because:
1. It minimizes the **microbial doubling time** (extended from 20 min to > 6 h).
2. It preserves **sensory and nutritional quality** (prevents lipolysis and proteolysis).
3. It is the **legal benchmark** under FSSAI and Codex for pasteurized milk.
4. It extends shelf life by **3 – 4 weeks** versus unrefrigerated storage.
*(3 marks: rule 1 + reasons 2)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse specific heat of whole milk (3.93) with cream (3.78) or butter (2.30).** Choosing the wrong value leads to **3-mark deduction**.
> - **Always state the assumed operation hours** when converting kJ/day to kW. Default = **16 h/day** for plant refrigeration.
> - **Regeneration credit** must be calculated explicitly in HTST problems — examiners allot **2 marks** for it.
> - **Units in final answer** (kW, TR, or kJ/h) must be consistent. **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12660 kJ/h**.

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A dairy plant processes 15 000 kg/day of whole milk, received at 32 °C. The milk is cooled to 4 °C in a plate heat exchanger using chilled water at 1 °C, then HTST pasteurized at 72 °C, and finally re-cooled to 4 °C. A regenerative section recovers 85 % of the sensible heat from the hot milk to the cold incoming milk. Compute:**
**(a) The refrigeration load for pre-cooling and the final cooling sections. (7 marks)**
**(b) The net refrigeration capacity of the plant in TR and compressor kW (assume COP = 2.8). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Given:**
- m = 15 000 kg/day
- c_p (whole milk) = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K)
- T_in = 32 °C, T_cool = 4 °C, T_pasteurize = 72 °C
- Regeneration efficiency η_r = 0.85
- COP = 2.8
- Operating time = 16 h/day

### Part (a) — Sensible Heat Calculations

**Pre-cooling load (32 °C → 4 °C):**

$$
Q_{pre} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 15000 \times 3.93 \times (32 - 4)
$$

$$
Q_{pre} = 15000 \times 3.93 \times 28 = 1{,}650{,}600 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Convert to kW:**

$$
Q_{pre,kW} = \frac{1{,}650{,}600}{16 \times 3600} = 28.65 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final cooling load (72 °C → 4 °C):**

$$
Q_{final} = 15000 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 4) = 15000 \times 3.93 \times 68
$$

$$
Q_{final} = 4{,}008{,}600 \text{ kJ/day} = \frac{4{,}008{,}600}{57{,}600} = 69.59 \text{ kW}
$$

**Heat recovered by regeneration:**

$$
Q_{regen} = 0.85 \times 69.59 = 59.15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Net final cooling load:**

$$
Q_{final,net} = 69.59 - 59.15 = 10.44 \text{ kW}
$$

### Part (b) — Total Refrigeration Capacity

**Total net load:**

$$
Q_{net} = Q_{pre} + Q_{final,net} = 28.65 + 10.44 = 39.09 \text{ kW}
$$

**Convert to TR:**

$$
Q_{TR} = \frac{39.09}{3.517} = 11.11 \text{ TR}
$$

**Compressor power:**

$$
W_{comp} = \frac{Q_{net}}{\text{COP}} = \frac{39.09}{2.8} = 13.96 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** Q_net = **39.09 kW ≈ 11.11 TR**; Compressor power = **13.96 kW**

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A continuous scraped-surface ice cream freezer processes 2 000 kg/h of mix. The mix enters at 5 °C, leaves at -5 °C (semi-frozen with 50 % water frozen). Calculate:**
**(a) The total refrigeration load including both sensible and latent heat contributions. (7 marks)**
**(b) The refrigerant mass flow rate if R-717 (NH₃) is used, with evaporator temperature -30 °C and condenser temperature 25 °C. Take h_fg(NH₃) at -30 °C = 1167 kJ/kg. Assume isentropic compression with k = 1.31 for NH₃. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Given:**
- Mass flow rate ṁ = 2 000 kg/h = 0.5556 kg/s
- c_p (ice cream mix) = 3.30 kJ/(kg·K)
- T_in = 5 °C, T_out = -5 °C
- Fraction of water frozen = 0.50
- Water content of mix ≈ 65 % → mass of water frozen per kg = 0.65 × 0.5 = 0.325 kg
- h_fg (milk-water system) ≈ 232 kJ/kg (use water's latent heat with correction)

### Part (a) — Total Heat Removal

**Sensible heat (5 °C → -5 °C):**

$$
Q_{sensible} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_f) = 0.5556 \times 3.30 \times (5 - (-5))
$$

$$
Q_{sensible} = 0.5556 \times 3.30 \times 10 = 18.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Latent heat (50 % water in mix frozen):**

Mass of water frozen per second:

$$
\dot{m}_{water,frozen} = 0.5556 \times 0.65 \times 0.50 = 0.1806 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{latent} = 0.1806 \times 232 = 41.90 \text{ kW}
$$

**Total refrigeration load:**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} = 18.33 + 41.90 = 60.23 \text{ kW}
$$

### Part (b) — Refrigerant Mass Flow

**Mass flow of NH₃:**

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = \frac{Q_{total}}{h_{fg,evap}} = \frac{60.23}{1167} = 0.0516 \text{ kg/s} = 185.76 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Compressor work (theoretical):**

Pressure at -30 °C (NH₃ saturation): P₁ ≈ 1.194 MPa
Pressure at 25 °C (NH₃ saturation): P₂ ≈ 1.515 MPa
Pressure ratio: r_p = 1.515 / 1.194 = **1.269**

For isentropic compression with k = 1.31:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} = 243.15 \times (1.269)^{0.2366}
$$

$$
T_2 = 243.15 \times 1.0604 = 257.8 \text{ K} = -15.35 \text{ °C}
$$

**Compressor power (theoretical):**

$$
W_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m}_{NH_3} \cdot k \cdot R_{specific} \cdot T_1}{k-1} \left[(r_p)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right]
$$

For NH₃, R_specific = 488 J/(kg·K):

$$
W_{comp} = 0.0516 \times \frac{1.31 \times 488}{0.31} \times (1.0604 - 1) = 0.0516 \times 2063 \times 0.0604
$$

$$
W_{comp} = 6.43 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** Q_total = **60.23 kW (17.13 TR)**; ṁ(NH₃) = **185.76 kg/h**; W_comp = **6.43 kW**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Re-read 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Dairy products** are water-rich, perishable biological systems requiring an unbroken cold chain from farm to fork.
- The **"4 °C rule"** is the cornerstone of fluid milk storage; **ice cream requires -25 °C** hardened storage.
- **Whole milk c_p = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K)**; **cream (30 % fat) c_p = 3.78 kJ/(kg·K)**; **butter c_p = 2.30 kJ/(kg·K)**; **ice cream mix c_p = 3.30 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- **Freezing point of milk = -0.55 °C** (colligative depression by lactose and salts).
- **Latent heat of milk ≈ 232 kJ/kg** (lower than pure water's 333.5 kJ/kg due to dissolved solids).
- **HTST pasteurization** = 72 °C / 15 s; **LTLT** = 63 °C / 30 min; **UHT** = 135 – 150 °C / 4 – 15 s.
- **Regenerative PHE** recovers **80 – 95 %** of final cooling sensible heat in pasteurization, drastically reducing refrigeration load.
- **Sensible cooling load formula:** Q_s = m·c_p·ΔT (kJ or kW).
- **Latent load in freezing:** Q_L = m·h_sf.
- **Lumped cooling curve:** T(t) = T_c + (T_i - T_c)·exp(-(U·A / m·c_p)·t).
- **Plank-Evers equation** governs block-freezing time for butter, paneer, and ice cream.
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12 660 kJ/h** — memorize the conversion.
- **Compressor power = Q_e / COP** — typical dairy plant COP = 2.5 – 3.5.
- **Refrigerants used:** R-134a (small chillers), R-717/NH₃ (large industrial), R-404A (ice cream), CO₂ (R-744) emerging in transcritical systems.
- **Ice cream freezer** uses a **scraped-surface heat exchanger (SSHE)** with air injection for **overrun (50 – 100 %)**.
- **Cold room RH control:** 85 – 90 % for cheese ripening; 80 – 85 % for cheese storage; 90 – 95 % for unwrapped butter.
- **Insulation standard:** **PUF (polyurethane foam)** of **80 – 100 mm** for cold rooms, **k_insul ≈ 0.022 W/(m·K)**.
- **Heat gains from people** = 250 – 300 W per person (use only if workers enter the cold room; the *ktu board* does not usually include this for milk silos).
- **Don't forget** to convert kJ/day → kW using plant operation hours (typically 16 h/day, sometimes 20 h).
- **Always state** your assumptions explicitly — **FSSAI** storage temperatures, **Codex** standards, or **NMPPB** guidelines.
- **Cheese ripening** is the *only* dairy operation where **heating** is sometimes part of cold-chain design (controlled warm-up to 10 – 13 °C for enzymatic activity).
- **Ghee** does NOT require continuous refrigeration after production; **butter** does.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-mile advice for the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam):**
> - Always write down **Given Data** and **Assumptions** in a separate box at the top of any 14-mark answer.
> - Show the **unit conversion step** explicitly (kg/day → kg/s or kJ/day → kW).
> - End every answer with a **one-line engineering interpretation** — e.g., *"This 11.11 TR plant is suitable for a mid-size dairy handling 15 000 L/day of milk."*
> - Practise at least **3 numerical problems** on milk cooling and **1 on ice cream** — these are the **most repeated** KTU question themes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling and Distribution of Food through Refrigerated and Air-Conditioned Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Handling and Distribution of Food through Refrigerated and Air-Conditioned Transport # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Refrigerated Transport (also called "Reefer Transport")** is a specialized mode of food logistics in which perishable food products are conveyed from the point of production/processing to the point of consumption under continuously controlled low-temperature conditions, thereby preserving quality, inhibiting microbial spoilage, and extending shelf life. The system integrates three engineering subsystems: **(a) a thermally insulated enclosure, (b) a mechanical vapour-compression refrigeration plant, and (c) a controlled air-circulation network** to maintain uniform product temperature throughout the cargo space. **Air-Conditioned Transport** refers to a milder form of temperature-controlled transport used for **short-duration, short-distance movement** of foods where only ambient-temperature stabilization (typically **18–22 °C**, ~**65–72 °F**) or chilling (**4–8 °C**) is required, rather than deep-freezing. It is commonly employed for bakery, confectionery, chocolate, and fresh-cut produce. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** A student must clearly distinguish between **Refrigerated Transport** (active cooling for perishable/frozen foods) and **Air-Conditioned Transport** (passive/active thermal conditioning for non-frozen sensitive goods). Marks are awarded for explicit differentiation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View) Imagine you are carrying a scoop of vanilla ice cream home on a hot Kerala summer afternoon. What happens? - If you wrap it in a cotton cloth (insulation) and walk fast (less heat gain) → it lasts longer but still melts. - If you put it in a **thermos flask with an active cooling gel pack** (insulated container + refrigeration effect) → it stays frozen for hours. - If you put it in a **mini-fridge on wheels with a small compressor running** (full refrigerated transport) → it stays perfectly frozen even for a 12-hour highway journey. That "mini-fridge on wheels" is exactly what a **Reefer Truck** is. The cotton cloth is just **insulation**, and the gel-pack is the **air-conditioned transport** idea. > [!NOTE] > **Key Realisation:** Cold Chain = Factory Cold Store → **Reefer Truck** → Distribution Hub → Retail Display Cabinet → Consumer Refrigerator. **Break any link** and the food spoils. This is the central philosophy of Module 4. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants | Parameter | Typical Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Truck body wall U-value (PUF insulated) | **0.30 – 0.40** | W/(m²·K) | | Truck body wall U-value (EPS insulated) | **0.45 – 0.55** | W/(m²·K) | | Specific heat of fresh meat | **3.0 – 3.5** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Specific heat of fresh fish | **3.5 – 3.9** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Specific heat of fruits/vegetables | **3.5 – 4.0** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Latent heat of freezing water (ice) | **335** | kJ/kg | | Respiration heat (apples at 0 °C) | **0.010 – 0.015** | W/kg | | Respiration heat (bananas at 13 °C) | **0.10 – 0.12** | W/kg | | Recommended truck interior RH | **85 – 95** | % | | Air velocity inside cargo space | **1.0 – 2.5** | m/s | | Standard "T" floor groove depth | **3 – 5** | mm | ## 1.4 Classification of Refrigerated Transport Modes 1. **Road (Highway) – Reefer Trucks / Reefer Vans:** Most flexible; door-to-door service. 2. **Rail – Refrigerated Wagons / Containers:** Bulk long-distance haulage. 3. **Sea – Reefer Ships & Reefer Containers (ISO 40-ft/20-ft):** International intercontinental trade. 4. **Air – Air-freighter with insulated LD-3 containers:** High-value perishables (saffron, caviar, vaccines, fresh tuna). # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Refrigerated Transport System The engineering of refrigerated transport rests on **five sequential functional layers**: 1. **Pre-Cooling Layer:** The vehicle body and the food product are pre-cooled to the target carriage temperature *before* loading. **Why?** Because if you load warm product into a cold truck, the refrigeration unit has to first extract the *field heat* of the product, which dramatically **increases pull-down time** and may **overload the compressor**, causing failure mid-transit. 2. **Insulation Layer:** The walls, floor, ceiling, and doors are constructed with **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** or **Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)** of thickness **50–100 mm** to minimise conductive heat ingress. **Why?** Because the truck body is essentially a **mobile cold store**, and any conductive heat gain must be minimised to reduce compressor work. 3. **Refrigeration Unit Layer:** A compact, engine-driven or diesel-driven, or fully electric **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** is mounted on the front wall of the truck. **Why?** Because VCRS is the most thermodynamically efficient way to remove **kW-scale heat loads** in a mobile package. 4. **Air Circulation Layer:** An **evaporator blower fan** forces cold air through the top of the cargo space; the air descends through the product load and returns along the **"T-bar floor"** (grooved aluminium floor channels) to the evaporator. **Why?** Because uniform air circulation prevents **hot pockets** and ensures every product experiences the same temperature. 5. **Monitoring & Control Layer:** A **microprocessor-based digital controller** with thermistor probes continuously logs temperature, defrost cycles, and door-opening events. Modern units transmit data via **GSM/GPS telemetry** to a central cold-chain dashboard. **Why?** Because **regulatory compliance** (FSSAI, HACCP, ATP Agreement) demands continuous temperature records. ## 2.2 Modes of Heat Ingress into a Refrigerated Vehicle The total refrigeration load ($Q_{total}$) on the truck refrigeration unit is the **sum of six contributing heat streams**: $$ Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6 $$ Where: - $Q_1$ → Heat conduction through walls, floor, ceiling, doors - $Q_2$ → Sensible + latent heat of the product (pull-down + respiration) - $Q_3$ → Heat from air infiltration during door openings - $Q_4$ → Heat from fan motors, evaporator defrost, lights - $Q_5$ → Heat from solar radiation (roof, sidewalls) - $Q_6$ → Heat from crew/personnel entering the cargo space ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Load Component | Formula | Units | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wall transmission | $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | All surfaces (walls, floor, roof, doors) | | 2 | Product sensible cooling | $Q_{2s} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Pulling temperature of pre-cooled food | | 3 | Product latent (freezing) | $Q_{2L} = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | W | Phase change of water in food | | 4 | Respiration heat (fresh produce) | $Q_{2R} = m \cdot q_{resp}$ | W | Fruits & vegetables during transit | | 5 | Air infiltration load | $Q_3 = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T + \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | W | Door-openings + breathing losses | | 6 | Fan & equipment heat | $Q_4 = P_{fan} + P_{light} + P_{defrost}$ | W | Internal heat sources | | 7 | Solar radiation load | $Q_5 = A_{roof} \cdot I_{solar} \cdot \alpha$ | W | Roof & sidewalls in tropical climate | | 8 | Personnel heat | $Q_6 = n \cdot q_{person}$ | W | Crew inside during loading | | 9 | Heat of respiration temperature rise | $\dot{T} = \dfrac{Q_{resp}}{m \cdot c_p}$ | K/h | Used to estimate time-temperature abuse | | 10 | Air-changes per hour (door-open) | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_d}{V \cdot t}$ | 1/h | Door-opening frequency calculation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard engineering metric for insulated truck body heat gain:** For a typical 9-tonne payload reefer truck running at $\Delta T = 30$ K, the wall transmission load $Q_1$ alone lies in the range **800–1500 W**. Always size the refrigeration compressor with a **15–20 % safety margin**. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice - **Economic Angle:** Cold-chain failure accounts for **~30–40 %** of post-harvest losses in developing economies. Properly designed refrigerated transport can cut this loss dramatically. - **Regulatory Angle:** In the European Union, the **ATP (Agreement on the International Carriage of Perishable Foodstuffs)** strictly regulates the insulation and refrigeration performance of road vehicles. - **Sustainability Angle:** A well-insulated truck with proper door-seals reduces refrigerant charge leakage and **carbon footprint**, aligning with **F-Gas Regulations** and KTU's sustainability-mandated graduate attributes. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Heat-Load Calculation for a Reefer Truck ### Given Problem Statement A refrigerated truck is to transport **5,000 kg of fresh fish** from Kochi to Delhi. The following data is supplied: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Payload mass ($m$) | 5,000 kg | | Product entering temperature ($T_1$) | +5 °C | | Product required carriage temperature ($T_2$) | 0 °C | | Outside ambient temperature ($T_{amb}$) | +35 °C | | Truck interior dimensions | 5.0 m (L) × 2.2 m (W) × 2.0 m (H) | | Wall U-value (PUF 80 mm) | 0.40 W/(m²·K) | | Specific heat of fish ($c_p$) | 3.6 kJ/(kg·K) | | Door openings per trip | 4 | | Volume of air entering per door opening | 1.5 m³ | | Total trip duration | 24 h | | Fan motor power | 250 W | | Internal lighting power | 100 W (operating 1 h) | | Roof area exposed to sun | 11.0 m² | | Solar intensity on roof (Kerala noon) | 900 W/m² | | Roof absorptivity ($\alpha$) | 0.4 | | Latent heat of freezing water ($h_{fg}$) | 335 kJ/kg | | Fraction of water frozen in fish | 0 % (already above freezing) | ### Required Compute the **total steady-state heat load** on the truck refrigeration unit. --- ### Step 1 — Wall Transmission Load ($Q_1$) **Surface area calculation:** - Floor + Ceiling = $2 \times (5.0 \times 2.2) = 22.0 \text{ m}^2$ - Two side walls = $2 \times (5.0 \times 2.0) = 20.0 \text{ m}^2$ - Front + Rear walls = $2 \times (2.2 \times 2.0) = 8.8 \text{ m}^2$ - **Total $A = 50.8$ m²** $$ \Delta T = T_{amb} - T_{interior} = 35 - 0 = 35 \text{ K} $$ $$ Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.40 \times 50.8 \times 35 $$ $$ Q_1 = 711.2 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** Heat flows from hot outside air → through insulated walls → into cold cargo. Lower U-value (better insulation) and lower $\Delta T$ both reduce this load. --- ### Step 2 — Product Sensible Cooling Load ($Q_{2s}$) The fish must be cooled from +5 °C to 0 °C (sensible cooling above freezing point — no latent heat since no phase change). $$ Q_{2s} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t} $$ $$ Q_{2s} = \dfrac{5000 \times 3.6 \times (5 - 0)}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{2s} = \dfrac{90000}{86400} $$ $$ Q_{2s} = 1.042 \text{ kW} = 1042 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** Energy required = mass × specific heat × temperature drop. Dividing by trip time gives the *average* load — but in practice, this load is highest in the **first 4–6 hours** (pull-down phase), so compressor sizing is based on **peak-hour load**, not 24-h average. --- ### Step 3 — Respiration Heat Load ($Q_{2R}$) For fresh fish, respiration is negligible (fish are not living tissues). However, if the load were **fresh fruits** (e.g., mangoes, bananas), respiration heat would apply. For the fish load: $Q_{2R} = 0$ W. > **Logic:** Only **living plant tissues** (fruits, vegetables) respire. Animal tissues and frozen foods have zero respiration heat. --- ### Step 4 — Air Infiltration Load ($Q_3$) Air enters each time the door is opened. Total air exchange = $4 \times 1.5 = 6.0$ m³ per trip. **Sensible component:** $$ Q_{3s} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T}{t} $$ Assuming $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $c_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$: $$ Q_{3s} = \dfrac{6.0 \times 1.2 \times 1005 \times 35}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{3s} = \dfrac{253260}{86400} $$ $$ Q_{3s} = 2.93 \text{ W} \approx 3 \text{ W} $$ **Latent component (moisture carried by warm air):** Warm air at 35 °C carries moisture; cold air at 0 °C holds less, so moisture **condenses inside** the truck. Using humidity ratio difference $\Delta W \approx 0.012$ kg/kg: $$ Q_{3L} = \dfrac{6.0 \times 1.2 \times 2501000 \times 0.012}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{3L} = \dfrac{216086.4}{86400} $$ $$ Q_{3L} = 2.50 \text{ W} $$ $$ Q_3 = Q_{3s} + Q_{3L} \approx 5.4 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** In a single 24-hour long-haul trip, air infiltration is **negligible** because door openings are few. But in **last-mile distribution** with 50+ stops, infiltration can become the **dominant load**. --- ### Step 5 — Fan + Light + Defrost Heat ($Q_4$) Assuming defrost runs for 30 minutes total in 24 hours: $$ Q_{4,fan} = 250 \text{ W (continuous)} $$ $$ Q_{4,light} = \dfrac{100 \times 1}{24} = 4.17 \text{ W} $$ $$ Q_{4,defrost} = 500 \text{ W (heater)} \times \dfrac{0.5}{24} = 10.4 \text{ W} $$ $$ Q_4 = 250 + 4.17 + 10.4 = 264.6 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** Fan motor heat is the **largest internal heat source** and is often underestimated by students in KTU exams. The fan's electrical input ultimately becomes heat inside the cargo space. --- ### Step 6 — Solar Radiation Load ($Q_5$) $$ Q_5 = A_{roof} \cdot I_{solar} \cdot \alpha = 11.0 \times 900 \times 0.4 $$ $$ Q_5 = 3960 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** This is a **massive** load. A white-painted roof with $\alpha = 0.4$ is assumed; a black tarpaulin cover could push this to **8000+ W**. This is why KTU examiners often include a solar load term. --- ### Step 7 — Personnel Heat ($Q_6$) Assuming **1 worker** spends 30 minutes inside during loading/unloading: $$ Q_6 = n \cdot q_{person} \cdot \dfrac{t_{occ}}{t_{trip}} = 1 \times 275 \times \dfrac{0.5}{24} $$ $$ Q_6 = 5.7 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** Negligible over a 24-h period, but important in **short-trip milk or bakery distribution**. --- ### Step 8 — Total Heat Load $$ Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_{2s} + Q_{2R} + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6 $$ $$ Q_{total} = 711.2 + 1042 + 0 + 5.4 + 264.6 + 3960 + 5.7 $$ $$ Q_{total} = 5988.9 \text{ W} \approx 6.0 \text{ kW} $$ **Applying 20% safety margin (KTU Examiner Standard):** $$ Q_{design} = 1.20 \times 5988.9 = 7186.7 \text{ W} \approx 7.2 \text{ kW} $$ > **Result Interpretation:** A refrigeration compressor of at least **7.5 kW cooling capacity** is required. In practice, the engineer selects the next standard size, typically an **8–10 kW** reefer unit (e.g., Carrier, Thermo King, Daikin, or Indian make Subros unit). --- ### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix (Contributions of Each Load) | Load Component | Value (W) | % of Total | |---|---|---| | $Q_1$ Wall transmission | 711 | 11.9 % | | $Q_{2s}$ Product sensible cooling | 1042 | 17.4 % | | $Q_{2R}$ Respiration | 0 | 0 % | | $Q_3$ Air infiltration | 5.4 | 0.1 % | | $Q_4$ Fan + light + defrost | 264.6 | 4.4 % | | $Q_5$ Solar radiation | 3960 | **66.1 %** | | $Q_6$ Personnel | 5.7 | 0.1 % | | **Total** | **5988.9** | **100 %** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** Solar radiation dominates **66 %** of the load. **Design Implication:** A reflective white roof coating and a roof-mounted sunshade are **not optional accessories** — they are essential energy-efficiency measures. This is a frequently-asked KTU question. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Refrigerated Transport System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[External Heat Sources
Ambient Air, Solar Radiation, Door Openings] --> B1[Insulated Enclosure
PUF Walls, T-Floor, Door Seals] B1 --> C[Refrigerated Cargo Space
0 C to -25 C depending on product] C --> D[Evaporator Unit
Fin-and-Tube Heat Exchanger] D --> E[Blower Fan
Circulates Cold Air at 1-2.5 m/s] E --> C D --> F[Expansion Valve
Throttles Liquid Refrigerant] F --> D F --> G[Compressor
VCRS - Driven by Truck Engine or Diesel] G --> H[Condenser
Air-cooled, Side or Roof Mounted] H --> I[Receiver and Liquid Line
Stores High-Pressure Liquid] I --> F J[Temperature Sensors
Thermistor Probes x 3] --> K[Microprocessor Controller
Setpoint, Defrost, Alarms] K --> G K --> L[Data Logger
GSM GPS Telemetry Unit] L --> M[Central Cold-Chain Dashboard
Cloud Server] C --> N[Product Load
Fresh, Chilled or Frozen Food] N --> C B1 --> O[Personnel Access
Loading, Unloading, Inspection] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Air Circulation Pattern Inside the Cargo Space ("Top-Down Throw") ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Refrigeration Unit
Front Wall Mounted] -->|Forces Cold Air| B[Ceiling Plenum Chamber] B -->|Distributes Air| C[Top of Product Load] C -->|Air Descends| D[Through Product Stacks
Bulkhead Divider Used] D -->|Warmed Air Returns| E[T-Bar Floor Grooves
3-5 mm deep channels] E -->|Back to Evaporator| A F[Side Wall Insulation
PUF 50-100 mm] --- B G[Roof Insulation
White Reflective Paint] --- B H[Floor Insulation
PUF with Aluminium Tread Plate] --- E ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stowage / Load Distribution Rules ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pre-Loading Stage] --> B[Pre-cool Truck Body to Target Temp] B --> C[Pre-cool Product to Target Temp] C --> D[Inspect Hygiene, Cleanliness, Door Seals] D --> E[Load Frozen Items First, then Chilled] E --> F[Maintain Air Channels Between Pallets] F --> G[Do Not Block T-Floor Grooves] G --> H[Secure Load with Straps to Prevent Shift] H --> I[Close Door, Start Refrigeration Unit] I --> J[Log Temperature, Depart] J --> K[In-Transit: Monitor Every 30 min] K --> L[At Destination: Verify Temp Before Unloading] L --> M[Clean, Sanitize, Pre-cool for Next Trip] ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. Define "Reefer Transport." List any four modes of refrigerated transport used in the food industry. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **Model Answer:** **Reefer Transport** is the conveyance of perishable food products under continuously controlled low-temperature conditions using a thermally insulated vehicle fitted with a mechanical refrigeration unit that maintains the cargo space at a predefined setpoint temperature, typically ranging from **+5 °C** (chilled) to **−25 °C** (frozen). **Four Modes of Refrigerated Transport:** 1. **Road Transport** – Reefer trucks and vans for door-to-door distribution; most flexible mode. 2. **Rail Transport** – Insulated refrigerator wagons (e.g., Indian Railways' "Reefer Parcel Vans") for long-distance inland haulage of bulk perishables. 3. **Sea Transport** – Reefer ships and ISO-standard 20-ft / 40-ft reefer containers for international trade (Kerala's seafood export industry relies on this mode). 4. **Air Transport** – Insulated LD-3 air-freight containers used for high-value perishables such as fresh tuna, saffron, and chilled caviar. > [!NOTE] > Marks distribution: **1 mark** for definition + **2 marks** for four modes (0.5 each). --- ### Question 2. Explain the concept of "Pre-cooling" of a refrigerated truck before loading. Why is it essential? **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **Model Answer:** **Pre-cooling** refers to the practice of running the refrigeration unit of an empty (or pre-loaded) truck for **1–3 hours before the cargo is placed inside**, so that the wall, floor, ceiling, and the residual air mass reach the target carriage temperature. **Why Pre-cooling is Essential:** 1. **Reduces Peak Pull-Down Load** — Cold walls absorb much less field heat from incoming product, so the compressor is not overloaded. 2. **Prevents Condensation** — Warm walls would cause moisture condensation on product surfaces, accelerating microbial growth. 3. **Saves Energy & Refrigerant** — A shorter pull-down phase means less compressor running time, lower fuel consumption, and reduced wear. 4. **Ensures Uniform Temperature** — Pre-cooled walls eliminate thermal gradients that could otherwise "cook" the outer layers of palletised product. > [!NOTE] > Marks distribution: **1.5 marks** for definition + **1.5 marks** for four essential reasons. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a refrigerated truck system**, clearly identifying the functions of the **insulated body, refrigeration unit, evaporator blower, T-bar floor, and temperature controller.** [7 Marks] **(b)** A **6000 kg** consignment of fresh apples (initial temperature **+15 °C**, target temperature **0 °C**) is to be transported in a reefer truck. The truck wall area is **55 m²** with U-value **0.45 W/(m²·K)**. Outside temperature is **38 °C**. Respiration heat of apples at 0 °C is **0.015 W/kg**, and the trip lasts **30 hours**. Compute: (i) wall transmission load, (ii) product sensible cooling load, (iii) respiration load, (iv) total refrigeration load with **20 % safety margin**. Take $c_p$ of apples = **3.85 kJ/(kg·K)**. [7 Marks] --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Functional Architecture (7 Marks) The refrigerated truck system consists of **five integrated subsystems**: 1. **Insulated Body** — Constructed with an outer skin of GRP/aluminium, PUF/EPS insulation core (50–100 mm), and an inner food-grade stainless-steel or GRP liner. Provides thermal barrier. 2. **Refrigeration Unit** — A self-contained VCRS, typically **engine-driven via belt/PTO** (Power Take-Off) or **diesel-driven standalone**, mounted on the front wall or roof. Includes compressor, condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator. 3. **Evaporator Blower Fan** — Located inside the cargo space behind the evaporator coil; forces cold air into the ceiling plenum at **1.0–2.5 m/s**, ensuring forced-convection heat removal from every product surface. 4. **T-Bar Floor** — Aluminium floor with **longitudinal grooves (3–5 mm deep)** acting as return-air ducts. Cold air is delivered at the top, descends through the load, and returns along the grooves to the evaporator inlet — completing a closed air loop. 5. **Temperature Controller** — Microprocessor-based unit with **multiple thermistor probes** (return air, supply air, product probe). Logs data, triggers **automatic hot-gas defrost**, and activates **alarms** if temperature deviates from setpoint. (Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid diagram for visual clarity.) **Marks split:** Diagram – 2 marks; Five subsystems explained – 5 × 1 mark each = 5 marks. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Heat-Load Calculation (7 Marks) **Given:** - $m = 6000$ kg; $T_1 = +15$ °C; $T_2 = 0$ °C - $A = 55$ m²; $U = 0.45$ W/(m²·K); $T_{amb} = 38$ °C - $q_{resp} = 0.015$ W/kg; $t = 30$ h; $c_p = 3.85$ kJ/(kg·K) **(i) Wall Transmission Load:** $$ Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.45 \times 55 \times (38 - 0) $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.45 \times 55 \times 38 = 940.5 \text{ W} $$ **(ii) Product Sensible Cooling Load:** $$ Q_{2s} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t} = \dfrac{6000 \times 3.85 \times 15}{30 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{2s} = \dfrac{346500}{108000} = 3.21 \text{ kW} = 3210 \text{ W} $$ **(iii) Respiration Load:** $$ Q_{2R} = m \cdot q_{resp} = 6000 \times 0.015 $$ $$ Q_{2R} = 90 \text{ W} $$ **(iv) Total Refrigeration Load:** $$ Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_{2s} + Q_{2R} = 940.5 + 3210 + 90 = 4240.5 \text{ W} $$ **With 20 % safety margin:** $$ Q_{design} = 1.20 \times 4240.5 = 5088.6 \text{ W} \approx 5.1 \text{ kW} $$ **Recommended Compressor Capacity:** **5.5 kW to 6.0 kW** (next standard size). --- ### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Compare **Refrigerated Transport** and **Air-Conditioned Transport** for food products. Give two examples of foods suitable for each mode, and state the typical temperature range maintained in each. [7 Marks] **(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **"Top-Down Air-Circulation"** principle used in refrigerated trucks. List any **four stowage rules** that must be followed when loading product into a reefer truck. [7 Marks] --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks) | Feature | Refrigerated Transport | Air-Conditioned Transport | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Long-duration, low-temperature preservation | Short-duration, ambient or mildly chilled conditions | | Temperature Range | **−25 °C to +5 °C** (chilled or frozen) | **+4 °C to +22 °C** (chilled or ambient) | | Insulation Thickness | **50–100 mm PUF** | **25–50 mm** (lighter insulation) | | Refrigeration Capacity | High (3–15 kW) | Low (1–3 kW) | | Trip Duration | **8 hours to 30 days** | **1 to 6 hours** | | Cost | High (refrigerated unit + insulated body) | Moderate | | Examples of Suitable Foods | Frozen seafood, ice cream, frozen meat, frozen peas | Fresh bread, chocolates, fresh-cut salad, dairy (short-haul) | | Example Products (Kerala) | Frozen shrimp (Kochi → Mumbai), frozen tuna | Banana chips, halwa, cake, ghee (within Kerala distribution) | --- #### Part (b) — Top-Down Air-Circulation Principle (7 Marks) **Principle:** Cold air is **denser** than warm air. The refrigeration unit's **evaporator blower** forces chilled air into a **ceiling plenum**. This cold air descends vertically through the product load by **natural buoyancy-assisted forced convection**, absorbs heat from the products, becomes warmer (less dense), and is channelled back to the evaporator via the **T-bar floor grooves** at the bottom of the cargo space. **Diagram (simplified):** ``` ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ Ceiling Plenum │ ← Cold air from evaporator │═════════════════════════│ │ ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓ │ │ [PRODUCT LOAD] │ ← Air descends, picks up heat │ ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓ │ │═════════════════════════│ │ T-Floor Return Duct │ ← Warm air returns to evaporator └─────────────────────────┘ ↓ [EVAPORATOR UNIT] → re-cools → cycle repeats ``` **Four Stowage Rules:** 1. **Maintain air gaps** of at least **10 cm** between pallet stacks and side walls/ceiling. 2. **Do NOT block the T-floor grooves** with cargo, packaging, or debris. 3. **Use bulkhead dividers** between products of *different* target temperatures (e.g., chilled meat must not share space with frozen peas). 4. **Secure the load** with straps/lashings to prevent shifting during transit, which would block airflow and cause bruising of fresh produce. 5. **Stagger-box stowage** (interlocking pattern) maximises airflow contact with product surfaces. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks in Refrigerated Transport Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the 20 % safety margin** on the final answer — KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for missing this. > 2. **Ignoring the solar radiation load** $Q_5 = A \cdot I \cdot \alpha$ — a **very common omission**. Always include it for any Indian/Kerala climate question. > 3. **Confusing sensible and latent heat** — fish/meat above 0 °C has only sensible cooling, but if chilled meat is *frozen mid-trip* by accident, latent heat of **335 kJ/kg** applies. > 4. **Mixing up mass-specific heat values** — exam answer keys use $c_p = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K) for meat and $c_p = 3.85$ kJ/(kg·K) for produce. Do not use $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) (that's for water only). > 5. **Wrong units in final answer** — Express final refrigeration capacity in **kW**, not W, when the load exceeds 1000 W. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Refrigerated transport** maintains **−25 °C to +5 °C**; **air-conditioned transport** maintains **+4 °C to +22 °C**. - ✅ The **five subsystems** are: insulated body, refrigeration unit, evaporator blower, T-floor return duct, and temperature controller. - ✅ **Total Heat Load** $Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6$ (always apply **20 % safety margin**). - ✅ **Solar radiation** is often the **largest** contributor in tropical climates like Kerala — never ignore it. - ✅ **Pre-cooling** the truck body before loading is mandatory; it reduces pull-down load and prevents condensation. - ✅ **T-bar floor** channels the return air to the evaporator — must not be blocked by cargo. - ✅ **Top-down air circulation** ensures uniform temperature and rapid heat removal. - ✅ **ATP Agreement** (Europe) and **FSSAI regulations** govern temperature compliance during transit. - ✅ **Respiration heat** applies only to **fresh fruits and vegetables** — not to meat, fish, or frozen foods. - ✅ **Common refrigerants** used in reefer units: **R-404A, R-452A, R-134a, and emerging low-GWP R-744 (CO₂) and R-290 (propane)**. - ✅ **Standard insulation**: **PUF (Polyurethane Foam)** preferred for trucks (low thermal conductivity **~0.022 W/m·K**); EPS used for cheaper/light-duty vehicles. --- > **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — Handling and Distribution of Food through Refrigerated and Air-Conditioned Transport** > *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, OEFTT612 Syllabus, and NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework.*

topics.title: Food Freezing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Food Freezing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Food Freezing** is the thermal process of reducing the temperature of a food product **below its freezing point** such that a significant portion of the free water (typically >80%) is converted into ice crystals, thereby arresting microbial growth, retarding enzymatic activity, and significantly extending the shelf life of perishable food products. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (OEFTT612), food freezing is treated as a **unit operation in food preservation** that combines principles of **heat transfer, thermodynamics, and food chemistry**. The freezing point of most foods is not exactly 0 °C; it is depressed due to dissolved sugars, salts, and other solutes. Hence the formal freezing point is referred to as the **initial freezing point (T_f)** of the food. ### Key Definitions - **Freezing Point (T_f):** The temperature at which the first crystal of ice begins to form in the food matrix. - **Latent Heat of Freezing (L_f):** The energy (in kJ/kg) that must be removed from the food to convert liquid water into ice at constant temperature. Pure water has **L_f = 334 kJ/kg**. - **Platen Temperature (T_a):** The temperature of the cooling/refrigerant medium in contact with the food surface. - **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g):** The temperature below which the unfrozen water in food exists in a glassy (amorphous) state, critical for frozen food quality. - **Freezing Time (t_f):** The total time required to reduce the thermal center of the food from its initial temperature T_i to a specified final storage temperature. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "Pausing a Movie Reel"** > Imagine a movie playing on a screen — that is your fresh food: enzymes are reacting, microbes are multiplying, water is migrating, lipids are oxidizing. To stop the movie, you don't destroy the reel; you simply **pause it**. Food freezing is the "pause button" for biological and chemical reactions. > - The **freezer is the pause button**. > - **Ice crystals** are like the still frame — they lock the water in place so microbes (the actors) cannot move. > - **Fast freezing** is like snapping a high-speed photo (small, uniform crystals → less cellular damage). > - **Slow freezing** is like taking a blurry photo (large, irregular crystals → punctured cell walls, drip loss on thawing). > [!TIP] > A simple rule of thumb used in industry: **the faster the freezing, the smaller the ice crystals, and the better the texture & nutritional retention on thawing.** ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Bolded for High Recall) - Latent heat of fusion of pure water: **L = 334 kJ/kg** - Specific heat of water (above freezing): **C_pw = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)** - Specific heat of ice: **C_pi = 2.09 kJ/(kg·K)** - Latent heat of pure ice: **144 Btu/lb ≈ 334 kJ/kg** - Typical initial freezing point range for foods: **−0.5 °C to −2.5 °C** - Typical final storage temperature for frozen food: **−18 °C (0 °F)** — the legal commercial standard in most countries. - Recommended thermal center temperature for blast-frozen food: **−18 °C**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Stages of Food Freezing The freezing curve (T-t curve) of a food product exhibits **three distinct thermal stages**. Understanding these stages is the foundation of every freezing-time calculation that KTU examiners test. ### Stage 1: Sensible Heat Removal (Pre-cooling Zone) - Food is cooled from initial temperature T_i down to the initial freezing point T_f. - Energy removed = m × C_p × (T_i − T_f) - The slope of the cooling curve is **steep** because air/water does not undergo phase change. ### Stage 2: Latent Heat Removal (Phase Change Zone — the Plateau) - The largest fraction of total refrigeration load is removed here. - Approximately **80% of the free water** must be converted to ice. - The curve becomes **flat** because energy leaves as latent heat (L = 334 kJ/kg), not as temperature drop. - This zone typically spans **−1 °C to −5 °C** for most foods — the **"Zone of Maximum Ice Formation"**. ### Stage 3: Sub-cooling (Tempering the Ice) - Remaining sensible heat is removed from the ice + bound water matrix. - The temperature drops from T_f to the final storage temperature T_s (usually −18 °C). - The slope becomes steep again, but the energy is small (~10–15% of total load). > [!IMPORTANT] > The bulk of the **refrigeration load** (and hence the **electricity bill** of any cold storage) is consumed in **Stage 2** (latent heat removal). This is why **freezing time** formulas focus on the latent heat component. ## 2.2 Ice Crystal Formation — The Quality Determinant | Freezing Rate | Crystal Size | Location | Quality Impact | |---|---|---|---| | Slow (< 0.5 cm/h) | Large (> 100 μm) | Extra-cellular | Cell wall rupture, high drip loss | | Quick (0.5–3 cm/h) | Medium (50–100 μm) | Mixed | Acceptable for meat/fish | | Rapid (3–10 cm/h) | Small (10–50 μm) | Intra-cellular | Superior texture & juiciness | | Cryogenic (> 10 cm/h) | Micro (< 10 μm) | Uniform intra | Best quality, but expensive | > [!NOTE] > **Why does fast freezing form small crystals?** A high rate of heat removal creates a steep temperature gradient. Ice nucleates at many points simultaneously, exhausting the available water before any single crystal can grow large. ## 2.3 Classification of Freezing Methods (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping) 1. **Air Blast Freezing** — Most common. Cold air at −30 to −40 °C blown over food. Uses finned evaporators and axial fans. 2. **Plate (Contact) Freezing** — Food pressed between refrigerated metal plates. High surface heat transfer coefficient (h ≈ 100–300 W/m²K). 3. **Immersion Freezing** — Food submerged in cold brine (NaCl, CaCl₂) or glycol solutions. Very fast. 4. **Spray Freezing** — Cold liquid sprayed onto food (e.g., crust freezing of tuna). 5. **Cryogenic Freezing** — Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂ at −196 °C) or Liquid CO₂ (−78 °C). Ultra-rapid; used for premium products. 6. **Fluidized Bed Freezing** — Small particles (peas, berries) frozen in a vibrated air stream. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Master this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions in KTU ESE on this topic.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $t = \dfrac{\rho \, L}{\theta_a - \theta_f} \left( \dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k} \right)$ | **Plank's Equation (basic)** | s | | 2 | $t = \dfrac{\rho \left( L + C_p (T_i - T_f) \right)}{\theta_a - \theta_f} \left( \dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k} \right)$ | **Modified Plank's Equation** (with sensible heat) | s | | 3 | $E_{total} = m [C_p (T_i - T_f) + L + C_{pi} (T_f - T_s)]$ | Total energy to freeze mass m | kJ | | 4 | $Q_{latent} = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat load | kJ | | 5 | $Q_{sensible,above} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_i - T_f)$ | Sensible heat above freezing | kJ | | 6 | $Q_{sensible,below} = m \cdot C_{pi} \cdot (T_f - T_s)$ | Sensible heat below freezing | kJ | | 7 | $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h \cdot D}{k}$ | Biot number check (>0.1 → Plank valid) | dimensionless | | 8 | $T_f = 0 - 1.86 \cdot m_{sol}$ | Freezing point depression (molal) | °C | | 9 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{m \cdot L + m \cdot C_p (T_i - T_f)}{t_f}$ | Required refrigeration capacity | kW | ### Geometry Constants (P, R) for Plank's Equation | Geometry | P | R | |---|---|---| | Infinite Slab | 1/2 | 1/8 | | Infinite Cylinder | 1/4 | 1/16 | | Sphere | 1/6 | 1/24 | ### Nomenclature Block - $t$ = freezing time (s) - $\rho$ = density of food (kg/m³) - $L$ = latent heat of freezing (J/kg) - $\theta_a$ = refrigerant/medium temperature (°C) - $\theta_f$ = initial freezing point of food (°C) - $D$ = characteristic thickness/diameter (m) - $h$ = surface heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - $k$ = thermal conductivity of frozen food (W/m·K) - $C_p$ = specific heat above freezing (J/kg·K) - $T_i, T_s$ = initial & final storage temperatures (°C) - $E$ = total energy removed (kJ) - $m$ = mass (kg) - $\dot{Q}$ = rate of heat removal / refrigeration capacity (kW) ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility These equations are used by: - **Cold storage designers** to size compressors, condensers, and evaporators. - **Food process engineers** to optimize blast-freezer air velocities (−6 to −10 m/s typical) for throughput. - **Logistics planners** to estimate plate-freezer load cycles in seafood export industries (Kerala's shrimp and tuna sectors are major users). - **Quality control labs** to predict drip loss and texture retention based on freezing rate. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Plank's Equation for an Infinite Slab **Plank's equation (1913)** is derived from the unsteady-state heat conduction with a moving phase-change front. The assumptions are: 1. The food is an **infinite slab** of thickness 2D (or semi-infinite body of thickness D from one side). 2. Initial temperature is uniform = T_i. 3. Surface temperature is suddenly dropped to θ_a. 4. Latent heat L is the dominant energy term. 5. Thermal conductivity k is constant (frozen zone). 6. Surface heat transfer coefficient h is constant. ### Step 1: Heat Conduction Through the Frozen Layer At any instant, the heat flux through the frozen layer of thickness x is governed by Fourier's law: $$ q'' = \dfrac{k \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_a)}{x} $$ ### Step 2: Convective Heat Transfer at the Surface The convective heat transfer from refrigerant to food surface is: $$ q'' = h \cdot (\theta_s - \theta_a) $$ ### Step 3: Energy Balance at the Moving Front Equating the two heat fluxes (the rate of heat leaving the surface = rate of conduction through the frozen crust): $$ h \cdot (\theta_s - \theta_a) = \dfrac{k \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_a)}{x} $$ Solving for the unknown surface temperature θ_s: $$ \theta_s = \theta_a + \dfrac{k \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_a)}{h \cdot x} $$ ### Step 4: Latent Heat Released at the Front The rate at which ice is forming at the receding front (per unit area) is governed by: $$ \rho \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} = h \cdot (\theta_s - \theta_a) $$ ### Step 5: Substitution Substituting the expression for θ_s into the above: $$ \rho \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{k \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_a)}{x + \dfrac{k}{h}} $$ ### Step 6: Separation of Variables and Integration Separating variables: $$ \left( x + \dfrac{k}{h} \right) dx = \dfrac{k \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_a)}{\rho \cdot L} \, dt $$ Integrating from x = 0 to x = D, and t = 0 to t = t: $$ \int_{0}^{D} \left( x + \dfrac{k}{h} \right) dx = \dfrac{k \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_a)}{\rho \cdot L} \int_{0}^{t} dt $$ ### Step 7: Final Integration Evaluating the LHS: $$ \left[ \dfrac{x^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{k \cdot x}{h} \right]_{0}^{D} = \dfrac{D^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{k \cdot D}{h} $$ Equating: $$ \dfrac{D^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{k \cdot D}{h} = \dfrac{k \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_a) \cdot t}{\rho \cdot L} $$ ### Step 8: Solving for t Rearranging: $$ t = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{\theta_f - \theta_a} \cdot \left( \dfrac{D^{2}}{2 k} + \dfrac{D}{h} \right) $$ ### Step 9: Compact Form with P, R Introducing the geometry constants $P = 1/2$ and $R = 1/8$ for the **infinite slab**, and noting that the half-thickness is the characteristic dimension: $$ t = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{\theta_a - \theta_f} \cdot \left( \dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k} \right) \quad \text{(s)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The term $\dfrac{P \cdot D}{h}$ is the surface (convective) resistance, and $\dfrac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k}$ is the conductive resistance of the frozen layer.** This is why **h** (air velocity in a blast freezer) and **D** (package thickness) are the two most powerful design levers. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Modified Plank's Equation (Including Sensible Heat) ### Step 1: Identify the Missing Energy Term The classical Plank's equation ignores sensible heat above and below the freezing plateau. For high-moisture foods this is a 10–20% error. To correct it, the latent heat L is replaced by an **effective latent heat** $L_{eff}$ that includes the sensible heat of pre-cooling. ### Step 2: Energy Above Freezing Point The energy to cool from T_i to T_f is: $$ E_{above} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_i - T_f) $$ ### Step 3: Effective Latent Heat The total energy to be removed before the thermal center reaches T_f is the sum of sensible + latent heat, divided by mass: $$ L_{eff} = L + C_p (T_i - T_f) $$ ### Step 4: Substitution into Plank's Equation Replacing L with L_eff in the original derivation: $$ t_{mod} = \dfrac{\rho \left( L + C_p (T_i - T_f) \right)}{\theta_a - \theta_f} \cdot \left( \dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k} \right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** For most practical applications (T_i = +20 °C, T_f = −2 °C, L = 250 kJ/kg, C_p = 3.5 kJ/(kg·K)), the sensible heat term adds ~30% to the effective latent heat. **Always use the modified form in KTU problems unless the question explicitly states to ignore sensible heat.** ## 3.3 Worked Example — Sample KTU-style Numerical **Problem:** A slab of fish (2D = 6 cm thick) is to be frozen in a blast freezer. - $D$ = 0.03 m - $\rho$ = 1050 kg/m³ - $L$ = 235 kJ/kg = 235,000 J/kg - $C_p$ = 3.6 kJ/(kg·K) = 3600 J/(kg·K) - $T_i$ = +5 °C, $T_f$ = −2 °C - $\theta_a$ = −30 °C (refrigerant air) - $h$ = 50 W/(m²·K) - $k$ = 1.5 W/(m·K) - Geometry: infinite slab (P = 1/2, R = 1/8) ### Solution **Step 1: Calculate effective latent heat** $$ L_{eff} = 235{,}000 + 3600 \times (5 - (-2)) = 235{,}000 + 25{,}200 = 260{,}200 \text{ J/kg} $$ **Step 2: Calculate the geometry term** $$ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.03}{50} + \dfrac{0.125 \times (0.03)^{2}}{1.5} $$ $$ = \dfrac{0.015}{50} + \dfrac{0.125 \times 0.0009}{1.5} $$ $$ = 3 \times 10^{-4} + 7.5 \times 10^{-5} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$ **Step 3: Calculate freezing time** $$ t = \dfrac{1050 \times 260{,}200}{-2 - (-30)} \times 3.75 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ t = \dfrac{2.7321 \times 10^{8}}{28} \times 3.75 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ t = 9.7575 \times 10^{6} \times 3.75 \times 10^{-4} = 3659 \text{ s} $$ $$ t \approx 61 \text{ minutes} $$ **Step 4: Sanity Check (Biot Number)** $$ \text{Bi} = \dfrac{h \cdot D}{k} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.03}{1.5} = 1.0 $$ Since Bi > 0.1, the **internal conduction resistance is significant**, validating the use of Plank's equation. ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Freezing Process — Time-Temperature Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB A["START: Food at Ti = +20 to +30 C"] --> B["STAGE 1: Pre-cooling
Sensible Heat Removal
m.Cp.deltaT"] B --> C["Food reaches initial
Freezing Point Tf approx -1 to -2 C"] C --> D["STAGE 2: Phase Change
PLATEAU Region
Latent Heat L = 334 kJ per kg
80 percent of water becomes ice"] D --> E["Zone of Maximum Ice Formation
-1 C to -5 C
Largest energy demand"] E --> F["STAGE 3: Sub-cooling
Sensible Heat of Ice
m.Cp_ice.deltaT"] F --> G["END: Final Storage at -18 C
Commercial Frozen Standard"] G --> H["Stored in Cold Warehouse
or Distribution Channel"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Typical Air-Blast Freezing System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph REFRIG["Refrigeration Cycle"] comp["Compressor
Reciprocating or Screw"] cond["Condenser
Air-cooled or Water-cooled"] receiver["Liquid Receiver"] valve["Expansion Valve
TXV"] end evap["Evaporator Coil
Finned Tube at -35 to -40 C"] fan["Axial Fan
Velocity 6 to 10 m per s"] food["Food on Trays
Inside Cabinet"] cold["Cold Air Stream
h approx 30 to 80 W per m2K"] back["Return Warm Air"] comp --> cond --> receiver --> valve --> evap evap --> cold fan --> cold cold --> food food --> back back --> evap ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram of Freezing Methods ```mermaid flowchart TB central["FOOD FREEZING METHOD SELECTION"] --> method1["AIR BLAST
h = 30 to 80"] central --> method2["PLATE CONTACT
h = 100 to 300"] central --> method3["IMMERSION BRINE
h = 500 to 1000"] central --> method4["CRYOGENIC LN2
h = 1000 to 5000"] method1 --> app1["Fish, Meat, Fruits, Vegetables"] method2 --> app2["Fish Fillets, Patties, Cartons"] method3 --> app3["Shrimp, Crab, Tuna in Brine"] method4 --> app4["Premium Berries, Sushi, Ice Cream"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The surface heat transfer coefficient **h** increases by **2 orders of magnitude** from air-blast to cryogenic. This is the fundamental reason cryogenic freezing is the fastest and produces the smallest ice crystals. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define the term "initial freezing point" of food. Why is it always lower than 0 °C?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **initial freezing point (T_f)** of a food is the temperature at which the first ice crystal begins to form in the food matrix when it is being cooled. It is always lower than 0 °C (typically between **−0.5 °C and −2.5 °C**) because food is not pure water — it contains dissolved substances such as **sugars, salts, amino acids, and organic acids**. According to **Raoult's Law of Freezing Point Depression**, dissolved solutes lower the chemical potential of water, requiring the temperature to drop below 0 °C before the free water can overcome the colligative barrier and form ice. (3 marks) --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between slow freezing and quick freezing with respect to ice crystal size and food quality.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Slow Freezing | Quick Freezing | |---|---|---| | Freezing rate | < 0.5 cm/h | > 3 cm/h | | Crystal size | Large (> 100 μm) | Small (< 50 μm) | | Crystal location | Extra-cellular | Intra-cellular | | Cell wall damage | High | Minimal | | Drip loss on thawing | High | Low | | Texture & juiciness | Poor | Excellent | In slow freezing, water migrates from inside the cell to the extra-cellular space forming large crystals that rupture cell walls, causing high drip loss. In quick freezing, intra-cellular nucleation produces numerous small crystals that preserve the cellular structure. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Most students **forget the unit conversion** from kJ to J when using Plank's equation. ρ must be in kg/m³, L in **J/kg** (NOT kJ/kg), h in W/m²K, k in W/mK, D in **metres**, and t comes out in **seconds**. > - Many students **assume P = 1/2, R = 1/8 for every geometry**. Read the question — slab, cylinder, and sphere have different P, R. > - Students often **ignore the modified Plank's equation** even when T_i is given. If T_i is provided, examiners expect the modified version. > - Always state the **assumptions** of Plank's equation (1 mark is reserved for this in KTU valuation). --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Slab / Plate Freezer Numerical [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** A 5 cm thick slab of beef is to be frozen in a plate freezer. Given: - Half-thickness D = 0.025 m - ρ = 1050 kg/m³ - L = 235 kJ/kg - C_p = 3.5 kJ/(kg·K) - T_i = +10 °C, T_f = −2 °C, θ_a = −30 °C - h = 60 W/m²K, k = 1.4 W/mK - Geometry: infinite slab (P = 1/2, R = 1/8) **Calculate the freezing time using the modified Plank's equation. Also calculate the Biot number and comment on the validity of the equation.** (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1: Effective latent heat** $$ L_{eff} = L + C_p (T_i - T_f) $$ $$ = 235{,}000 + 3500 \times (10 - (-2)) $$ $$ = 235{,}000 + 3500 \times 12 = 235{,}000 + 42{,}000 = 277{,}000 \text{ J/kg} $$ **Step 2: Geometry term** $$ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.025}{60} + \dfrac{0.125 \times (0.025)^{2}}{1.4} $$ $$ = \dfrac{0.0125}{60} + \dfrac{0.125 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{1.4} $$ $$ = 2.083 \times 10^{-4} + 5.58 \times 10^{-5} = 2.641 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$ **Step 3: Freezing time** $$ t = \dfrac{1050 \times 277{,}000}{-2 - (-30)} \times 2.641 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ t = \dfrac{2.9085 \times 10^{8}}{28} \times 2.641 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ t = 1.0388 \times 10^{7} \times 2.641 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2743 \text{ s} \approx 45.7 \text{ min} $$ **Step 4: Biot number** $$ \text{Bi} = \dfrac{h \cdot D}{k} = \dfrac{60 \times 0.025}{1.4} = 1.07 $$ Since Bi >> 0.1, internal conduction resistance dominates, and **Plank's equation is valid**. (7 marks) --- **(b)** List and explain the **six common methods of food freezing** with a suitable food product for each. Compare them on the basis of heat transfer coefficient and freezing time. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (b) 1. **Air-Blast Freezing:** Cold air (−30 to −40 °C) is blown over food. h ≈ 30–80 W/m²K. Used for fish, meat, poultry, fruits, vegetables. (1 mark) 2. **Plate (Contact) Freezing:** Food placed between hollow refrigerated plates. h ≈ 100–300 W/m²K. Used for fish fillets, meat patties, packaged products. (1 mark) 3. **Immersion Freezing:** Food dipped in cold brine (NaCl/CaCl₂) or propylene glycol. h ≈ 500–1000 W/m²K. Used for shrimp, crab. (1 mark) 4. **Spray Freezing:** Refrigerant or brine is sprayed over food. Used for crust-freezing of whole tuna. (1 mark) 5. **Cryogenic Freezing:** Uses Liquid Nitrogen (−196 °C) or Liquid CO₂ (−78 °C). h ≈ 1000–5000 W/m²K. Used for premium berries, sushi, ice cream. (1 mark) 6. **Fluidized Bed Freezing:** Small food particles suspended in a vibrated cold air stream. h ≈ 200–500 W/m²K. Used for peas, diced vegetables, berries. (1 mark) **Comparison Table:** As h increases, freezing time decreases exponentially. Cryogenic is fastest but most expensive. Air-blast is most economical but slowest. (1 mark) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Theoretical & Energy Load [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** Explain the **three stages of food freezing** with the help of a temperature-time curve. Why is the second stage called the "plateau region" and the "zone of maximum ice formation"? (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a) The cooling curve of a food product during freezing has three distinct stages: **Stage 1 — Sensible Heat Removal (Pre-cooling):** The food is cooled from its initial temperature T_i (e.g., +25 °C) down to its initial freezing point T_f (≈ −2 °C). Energy removed = m·C_p·(T_i − T_f). The temperature falls rapidly; the slope of the curve is steep. (2 marks) **Stage 2 — Phase Change (Plateau / Zone of Maximum Ice Formation):** At T_f, the free water begins to crystallize into ice. The temperature remains **almost constant** for an extended period because the energy being removed is **latent heat of fusion (L = 334 kJ/kg)**, not sensible heat. This is why the curve is **flat (plateau)**. Approximately **80% of the water in food** converts to ice in this zone, hence called the "zone of maximum ice formation." It typically spans **−1 °C to −5 °C** and represents the largest portion of total refrigeration load. (3 marks) **Stage 3 — Sub-cooling:** The temperature of the ice + unfrozen water matrix drops from T_f to the final storage temperature (usually −18 °C). The slope becomes steep again, and the energy removed is m·C_pi·(T_f − T_s). (2 marks) --- **(b)** A cold storage unit is required to freeze **500 kg of fish** per day. The fish enters at **+5 °C** and is to be stored at **−18 °C**. The initial freezing point is **−2 °C**, latent heat is **235 kJ/kg**, C_p above freezing is **3.5 kJ/(kg·K)**, and C_p below freezing is **1.8 kJ/(kg·K)**. Assuming the freezing is completed in 8 hours, calculate: 1. Total energy to be removed per batch. 2. Required refrigeration capacity in kW. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** m = 500 kg, T_i = +5 °C, T_f = −2 °C, T_s = −18 °C, L = 235 kJ/kg, C_p (above) = 3.5 kJ/(kg·K), C_p (below) = 1.8 kJ/(kg·K), t = 8 h = 28,800 s. **Step 1: Energy above freezing (Stage 1)** $$ E_1 = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_i - T_f) $$ $$ = 500 \times 3.5 \times (5 - (-2)) = 500 \times 3.5 \times 7 = 12{,}250 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 2: Latent heat (Stage 2)** $$ E_2 = m \cdot L = 500 \times 235 = 117{,}500 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 3: Energy below freezing (Stage 3)** $$ E_3 = m \cdot C_{pi} \cdot (T_f - T_s) $$ $$ = 500 \times 1.8 \times (-2 - (-18)) = 500 \times 1.8 \times 16 = 14{,}400 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 4: Total energy** $$ E_{total} = E_1 + E_2 + E_3 = 12{,}250 + 117{,}500 + 14{,}400 = 144{,}150 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 5: Required refrigeration capacity** $$ \dot{Q} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{t} = \dfrac{144{,}150}{28{,}800} \approx 5.005 \text{ kW} $$ **Result:** Total energy = **144,150 kJ per batch**; Required capacity ≈ **5.0 kW** (≈ 1.42 TR). (7 marks) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-revision checklist before the KTU exam:** - ⭐ **Definition:** Freezing is the removal of heat to convert >80% of free water to ice. - ⭐ **Initial freezing point** is depressed (below 0 °C) due to dissolved solutes (Raoult's Law). - ⭐ **Three stages:** Sensible cooling → Latent (plateau) → Sub-cooling. - ⭐ **Latent heat of water = 334 kJ/kg** is the biggest energy sink. - ⭐ **Plank's equation** is the master equation — geometry constants: Slab (1/2, 1/8), Cylinder (1/4, 1/16), Sphere (1/6, 1/24). - ⭐ **Modified Plank's** replaces L with $L + C_p(T_i - T_f)$. - ⭐ **Freezing rate ∝ 1/crystal size ∝ 1/cell damage ∝ quality.** - ⭐ **Biot number > 0.1** is required for Plank's equation validity. - ⭐ **Cryogenic** has the highest h, fastest freezing, smallest crystals, highest cost. - ⭐ **Air-blast** has h ≈ 30–80; **Plate** h ≈ 100–300; **Immersion** h ≈ 500–1000; **Cryogenic** h ≈ 1000–5000 W/m²K. - ⭐ **Commercial standard storage temperature = −18 °C.** - ⭐ **Biot number** $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{hD}{k}$ — always calculate and check. - ⭐ **Unit conversion trap:** Plank's equation needs SI units (D in m, L in J/kg, t in seconds). - ⭐ **Drip loss on thawing** is a direct measure of freezing quality — less drip means better (faster) freezing. - ⭐ **Glass transition temperature (T_g)** is critical for freeze-dried and amorphous foods; storage must be below T_g to prevent caking and structural collapse. - ⭐ **Sharp freezing** means rapid freezing across the plateau zone (commercial air blast usually achieves this).

topics.title: Frozen Food Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Frozen Food Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Frozen Food Storage** is a specialized low-temperature preservation technique in which food products are stored at sub-zero temperatures (typically between **−18 °C and −25 °C**) in insulated, mechanically refrigerated enclosures to arrest microbial growth, suppress enzymatic activity, and retard chemical deterioration, thereby extending the shelf life of perishable food commodities from a few days to several months or even years.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition Boundaries**
> The term "frozen food storage" in the KTU 2024 scheme for OEFTT612 specifically refers to the **post-freezing holding phase** of the cold chain — i.e., the warehousing stage *after* individual quick freezing (IQF), air-blast freezing, or cryogenic freezing has already been completed. It does *not* include the freezing process itself (covered separately in Module 3).

### 1.2 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Standard Value (KTU Reference) | Functional Meaning |
|------|--------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Design Storage Temperature** | **−18 °C (0 °F)** | International Codex Alimentarius & FSSAI standard for long-term frozen food preservation |
| Design Relative Humidity | **90–95 %** | Prevents surface sublimation (freezer burn) |
| Recommended Air Velocity over Product | **0.1 – 0.5 m/s** | Maintains uniform temperature without desiccation |
| Acceptable Product Temperature Tolerance | ± 1 °C of setpoint | Per IS 13152 / Codex standards |
| Maximum Recommended Storage Duration (Meat) | 6 – 12 months | Depends on species, fat content, packaging |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Time Capsule for Food"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Think of a frozen food storage room as a **"biological pause button"** for food. When you place a strawberry inside your home freezer at −18 °C, you are essentially "stopping the clock" of the strawberry's life. The enzymes that would normally ripen and rot it cannot function because the water inside the cells is locked into ice crystals. Microbes cannot grow because their cellular water is also frozen. Chemistry slows down dramatically. When you remove the strawberry weeks or months later and thaw it, its life resumes almost exactly where it was paused — though some cellular damage from ice crystals will have occurred.
>
> This is the foundational principle behind every frozen warehouse, cold chain logistics network, and home freezer in the world.

### 1.4 Distinction: Chilled Storage vs. Frozen Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU examination pitfall is confusing *chilled* and *frozen* storage. The two differ fundamentally in their physics, equipment, and product outcomes:

- **Chilled Storage (0 °C to +8 °C):** Product is held *above* its freezing point. Most water remains liquid. Microbial activity is slowed but not halted. Examples: Fresh fruits, vegetables, dairy.
- **Frozen Storage (≤ −18 °C):** Product is held *below* its freezing point. A significant fraction of freezable water exists as ice. Microbial activity is essentially arrested. Examples: Frozen meat, seafood, ice cream, ready-to-eat meals.

### 1.5 Why −18 °C? The Scientific Rationale

The choice of **−18 °C (0 °F)** as the universal design temperature is not arbitrary. At this temperature:

- The vapor pressure of water is reduced to approximately **125 Pa**, limiting moisture migration.
- Microbial metabolic activity drops to negligible levels.
- Enzymatic reactions proceed at less than **5 %** of their rate at +4 °C.
- Lipid oxidation (rancidity in fatty foods) is dramatically suppressed.

$$\text{Rate Ratio} = \frac{r_{+4°C}}{r_{-18°C}} \approx 8 \text{ to } 20 \times$$

This means a product that would spoil in **7 days** at +4 °C can remain acceptable for **12 to 24 months** at −18 °C — provided packaging and handling protocols are maintained.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Functional Requirements of a Frozen Food Storage System

For a frozen storage facility to meet KTU / FSSAI standards, it must simultaneously satisfy five engineering and biological requirements:

1. **Thermal Containment:** Wall, floor, and ceiling assemblies must limit conductive heat ingress to a calculable maximum (typically **6 – 10 W/m²** for an insulated envelope).
2. **Temperature Uniformity:** Air distribution must maintain product temperature within **± 1 °C** of the setpoint throughout the chamber.
3. **Humidity Control:** Surface Relative Humidity of **90 – 95 %** must be sustained to avoid sublimation losses.
4. **Air Circulation:** Forced convection (via ceiling or wall-mounted evaporators with fans) must provide 0.1 – 0.5 m/s across the product surface.
5. **Sanitary Design:** Floors and walls must be smooth, non-absorbent, and washable to comply with HACCP requirements.

### 2.2 Classification of Frozen Food Storage Facilities

| Class | Capacity (tonnes) | Temperature Range | Typical User |
|-------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Walk-in Freezer (Small Commercial)** | 0.5 – 5 t | −18 °C to −25 °C | Restaurants, retail stores |
| **Cold Room / Walk-in Warehouse** | 5 – 500 t | −18 °C to −22 °C | Distributors, processors |
| **Bulk Cold Warehouse** | 500 – 10 000 t | −18 °C to −20 °C | National logistics hubs |
| **Public Cold Store** | > 10 000 t | −22 °C to −25 °C | Port / export terminals |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Frozen Storage Load Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU student must memorize for the Module 4 ESE and internal assessments. The standard temperature convention is **T = −18 °C (inside)** and an outside ambient of **+35 °C** (typical Indian summer design condition).

| # | Load Component | Formula | Unit Convention |
|---|----------------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Heat gain through walls, ceiling, floor | $Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W |
| 2 | Heat gain due to product (sensible, above freezing) | $Q_{s1} = m \cdot c_{p,\text{above}} \cdot (T_i - T_f)$ | kW |
| 3 | Heat gain due to product (latent — freezing) | $Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | kW |
| 4 | Heat gain due to product (sensible, below freezing) | $Q_{s2} = m \cdot c_{p,\text{below}} \cdot (T_f - T_s)$ | kW |
| 5 | Heat gain due to respiration (fruits/veg, pre-frozen negligible) | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_r$ | W (per day basis) |
| 6 | Heat gain due to air infiltration / door opening | $Q_{air} = V_{in} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T$ | W |
| 7 | Heat gain due to lighting | $Q_{\text{lights}} = n_{\text{lamps}} \cdot P_{\text{lamp}} \cdot F_u$ | W |
| 8 | Heat gain due to personnel (per worker) | $Q_{\text{person}} = 270 \text{ W (sensible)} + 70 \text{ W (latent)}$ | W |
| 9 | Heat gain due to fan motors (in evaporator) | $Q_{\text{fan}} = P_{\text{motor}} / \eta_{\text{motor}}$ | W |
| 10 | Total refrigeration load (steady-state) | $Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 + \dots + Q_9$ | kW |
| 11 | Storage life approximation (Q₁₀ rule) | $\theta = \theta_0 \cdot 2^{\frac{T_0 - T}{10}}$ | days/months |
| 12 | U-value for composite wall | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{x_k}{k_k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | W/m²·K |

**Standard Constants (to memorize):**

- $c_{p,\text{above}} \approx 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (food above freezing)
- $c_{p,\text{below}} \approx 1.8 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (frozen food)
- $h_{fg} \approx 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (latent heat of fusion of water in food)
- $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m³}$ (air density at 20 °C)
- $c_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (air specific heat)
- Freezing point of most foods: **−1 °C to −3 °C**

### 2.4 The Q₁₀ Rule — Predicting Storage Life

> [!NOTE]
> The **Q₁₀ rule** states that for every 10 °C reduction in storage temperature (within the biological range), the rate of deterioration is halved. For frozen storage above the glass transition temperature, this remains a useful engineering approximation.

$$\theta = \theta_0 \cdot 2^{\frac{T_0 - T}{10}}$$

**Example intuition:** If a product lasts 7 days at +4 °C, then at −18 °C (a 22 °C drop):
$$\theta = 7 \cdot 2^{\frac{22}{10}} = 7 \cdot 2^{2.2} \approx 7 \cdot 4.59 \approx 32 \text{ days}$$

This linear extrapolation in days works well, but the actual storage life at −18 °C is governed by slow reactions (lipid oxidation, protein denaturation) and is typically **months to years** — the Q₁₀ model underestimates true frozen storage duration.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Frozen food storage is the **backbone of international food trade**. Without it:

- India could not export marine products to Japan or the EU.
- Seasonal fruits (mango, litchi) could not reach global markets year-round.
- Vaccine cold chains (which also use −20 °C stages) would not exist in modern form.
- Military and space food programs would be impossible.

The engineering challenge is balancing **tight temperature control** with **energy efficiency**, since refrigeration is the single largest operating cost in any frozen warehouse.
