## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Derivation — Total Refrigeration Load of a Frozen Storage Room

We derive the total instantaneous refrigeration load by summing all heat inflows. This is the calculation KTU examiners most commonly test at 14 marks.

**Given (Illustrative KTU-style Problem Data):**

- Storage room dimensions: **10 m × 8 m × 4 m** (Length × Width × Height)
- Wall insulation: 100 mm PUF, $k = 0.024 \text{ W/m·K}$
- Wall surface area (excluding floor & ceiling): $2 \times (10 + 8) \times 4 = 144 \text{ m}²$
- Floor and ceiling: same insulation, $A_{\text{flr}} = A_{\text{ceil}} = 80 \text{ m}²$
- Inside temperature: $T_i = -18$ °C ; Outside temperature: $T_o = +35$ °C
- $\Delta T = 35 - (-18) = 53$ K
- Surface heat transfer coefficients: $h_i = 9$ W/m²·K (interior forced convection), $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K (exterior still air, 35 °C ambient)
- Product in-out: 2 000 kg/day entering at +5 °C, leaving at −18 °C (already frozen batch in/out)
- 6 fluorescent lamps, 40 W each, usage factor $F_u = 0.6$
- 2 workers, 4 hours/day in cold room
- Door openings: equivalent 10 air changes per day, $V = 320$ m³
- Fan motor in evaporator: 1.5 kW, efficiency 0.85

---

#### Step 1 — Compute Overall U-value for the Insulated Wall

The total thermal resistance is the sum of three contributions: inside convective resistance, conductive resistance of the insulation, and outside convective resistance.

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

Substituting numerical values:
$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{0.100}{0.024} + \frac{1}{12}
$$

Converting each term:
$$
\frac{1}{U} = 0.1111 + 4.1667 + 0.0833
$$

$$
\frac{1}{U} = 4.3611 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
U = 0.2293 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Heat Gain Through Walls (Q₁)

The four perimeter walls:

$$
Q_{1a} = U \cdot A_{\text{wall}} \cdot \Delta T = 0.2293 \times 144 \times 53
$$

$$
Q_{1a} = 0.2293 \times 144 \times 53 = 1\,750.0 \text{ W}
$$

Floor (assume ground at +20 °C, so $\Delta T_{\text{flr}} = 20 - (-18) = 38$ K):

$$
Q_{1b} = U \cdot A_{\text{flr}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{flr}} = 0.2293 \times 80 \times 38
$$

$$
Q_{1b} = 0.2293 \times 80 \times 38 = 697.1 \text{ W}
$$

Ceiling (assume roof at +45 °C under sun, so $\Delta T_{\text{ceil}} = 45 - (-18) = 63$ K):

$$
Q_{1c} = U \cdot A_{\text{ceil}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{ceil}} = 0.2293 \times 80 \times 63
$$

$$
Q_{1c} = 0.2293 \times 80 \times 63 = 1\,155.7 \text{ W}
$$

Total wall + floor + ceiling load:

$$
Q_1 = Q_{1a} + Q_{1b} + Q_{1c} = 1\,750.0 + 697.1 + 1\,155.7
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_1 = 3\,602.8 \text{ W} = 3.60 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Heat Gain from Product (Q₂)

The product enters at +5 °C and must leave at −18 °C. Its freezing point is approximately **−2 °C**. The calculation involves three sub-steps.

**Sub-step 3.1 — Sensible cooling from +5 °C to −2 °C:**

$$
Q_{2a} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,\text{above}} \cdot (5 - (-2)) = \frac{2\,000}{24 \times 3\,600} \times 3.5 \times 7
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{2\,000}{86\,400} = 0.02315 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{2a} = 0.02315 \times 3.5 \times 7 = 0.567 \text{ kW}
$$

**Sub-step 3.2 — Latent heat removal (freezing) at −2 °C:**

$$
Q_{2b} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} = 0.02315 \times 335
$$

$$
Q_{2b} = 7.755 \text{ kW}
$$

**Sub-step 3.3 — Sensible cooling of frozen product from −2 °C to −18 °C:**

$$
Q_{2c} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,\text{below}} \cdot (-2 - (-18)) = 0.02315 \times 1.8 \times 16
$$

$$
Q_{2c} = 0.667 \text{ kW}
$$

**Total product load:**

$$
Q_2 = Q_{2a} + Q_{2b} + Q_{2c} = 0.567 + 7.755 + 0.667
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_2 = 8.989 \text{ kW} \approx 9.0 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Heat Gain from Air Infiltration / Door Opening (Q₃)

We assume 10 complete air changes per day through door operations. The air inside is at −18 °C; the air entering is at +35 °C with 60 % RH.

Mass flow of infiltrating air per day:

$$
m_{\text{air}} = V \cdot N \cdot \rho_a = 320 \times 10 \times 1.2 = 3\,840 \text{ kg/day}
$$

Convert to mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \frac{3\,840}{86\,400} = 0.04444 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Using specific heat of air and the 53 K temperature difference:

$$
Q_3 = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T = 0.04444 \times 1.005 \times 53
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_3 = 2.367 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The latent component of air infiltration (moisture condensation) is added by using the enthalpy difference $\Delta h$ instead of $c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T$. For a precise KTU 14-mark answer, this distinction should be acknowledged.

---

#### Step 5 — Heat Gain from Lighting (Q₄)

$$
Q_4 = n_{\text{lamps}} \cdot P_{\text{lamp}} \cdot F_u = 6 \times 40 \times 0.6
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_4 = 144 \text{ W} = 0.144 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

#### Step 6 — Heat Gain from Personnel (Q₅)

Two workers, 4 hours/day, with sensible heat of 270 W and latent heat of 70 W per person (moderate work, cold environment):

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{person}} = 2 \times (270 + 70) = 680 \text{ W (instantaneous)}
$$

Average over 24 hours:

$$
Q_5 = 680 \times \frac{4}{24} = 113.3 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_5 = 0.113 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

#### Step 7 — Heat Gain from Fan Motors (Q₆)

Evaporator fan motor electrical input becomes heat inside the room:

$$
Q_6 = \frac{P_{\text{motor}}}{\eta_{\text{motor}}} = \frac{1\,500}{0.85} = 1\,765 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_6 = 1.765 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

#### Step 8 — Total Refrigeration Load (Q_total)

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6
$$

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 3.60 + 9.00 + 2.37 + 0.144 + 0.113 + 1.765
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = 16.99 \text{ kW} \approx 17.0 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Component-Wise Contribution

| Component | Load (kW) | % of Total | Engineering Lever to Reduce It |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| Walls / Floor / Ceiling | 3.60 | 21.2 % | Thicker insulation, lower $k$ |
| Product | 9.00 | 53.0 % | Pre-cool product before entry |
| Air infiltration | 2.37 | 13.9 % | Air curtains, strip curtains, reduced door openings |
| Fan motors | 1.77 | 10.4 % | EC fans, variable-speed drives |
| Personnel | 0.11 | 0.6 % | Limit access time, training |
| Lighting | 0.14 | 0.8 % | LED retrofit, occupancy sensors |
| **TOTAL** | **16.99 kW** | **100 %** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The product load (53 %) and the air infiltration load (14 %) together account for **two-thirds** of the total refrigeration demand. In practice, **pre-cooling the product in a separate blast tunnel** before it enters the long-term storage room is the single most effective design strategy to reduce installed compressor capacity and operating cost.

### 4.2 Schematic — Stacking and Air Circulation Pattern

The diagram below shows the **canonical air circulation pattern** in a properly loaded frozen storage room. Cold air descends from the ceiling-mounted evaporator, sweeps horizontally across the product, and returns warmed to the evaporator.

```mermaid

```

### 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Matrix

| Subsystem | Interacts With | Type of Interaction | Risk if Mismanaged |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Insulated envelope | External environment | Conductive heat ingress | Excessive compressor cycling, ice formation at wall joints |
| Evaporator fan | Product, air | Convective heat removal | Temperature stratification, uneven freezing |
| Defrost controller | Evaporator coil | Periodic heating cycle | Loss of storage capacity during defrost |
| Humidity sensor | Product surface | Mass transfer of water vapor | Freezer burn if RH < 85 % |
| PLC / SCADA | All subsystems | Control signals | System runaway, food spoilage if sensors fail |
| Cold chain transport | Dispatch bay | Thermal continuity | Temperature abuse between storage and retail |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024]**
**Define frozen food storage. What is the standard recommended storage temperature, and why is this specific value chosen over lower temperatures such as −30 °C or −40 °C?**

**Model Answer:**

Frozen food storage is the practice of holding food products at sub-zero temperatures (typically **−18 °C**) in mechanically refrigerated, insulated enclosures after the freezing process has been completed, to preserve quality and extend shelf life.

The standard design temperature is **−18 °C** because:

1. It is low enough to convert the majority of freezable water into ice, arresting microbial and enzymatic activity.
2. It is the internationally harmonized standard under **Codex Alimentarius (CAC/RCP 8-1976)** and Indian **FSSAI / IS 13152** norms.
3. Going lower (e.g., −30 °C) provides diminishing returns on shelf life (less than 50 % extension) while dramatically increasing capital and operating cost — approximately **1.6× to 2.0× the energy consumption** per °C of additional cooling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse "storage temperature" with "freezing temperature." The freezing process may use −35 °C or −40 °C (blast freezers), but the *storage* phase stabilizes at **−18 °C** unless the product demands ultra-low storage (e.g., certain vaccines, premium fish).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023]**
**What is "freezer burn"? Explain its cause and the two most effective engineering controls to prevent it in a frozen food storage facility.**

**Model Answer:**

**Freezer burn** is the localized dehydration and oxidative damage that occurs on the surface of frozen food when water sublimates directly from the solid (ice) phase to vapor, and the resulting dry patches undergo oxidation.

**Cause:** Temperature fluctuation in the storage room causes sublimation of surface ice. The water vapor migrates to colder surfaces (or escapes through the packaging), leaving a desiccated, discolored, and texture-degraded region.

**Engineering Controls:**

1. **Maintain RH at 90 – 95 %** inside the chamber using proper humidification and minimal door openings.
2. **Use moisture-impermeable packaging** (e.g., vacuum-sealed multilayer laminates with aluminum foil, or LDPE bags with thickness > 70 µm).
3. **Limit temperature fluctuations to ± 1 °C** through adequate refrigeration capacity and proper defrost cycle design.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**A frozen food storage room has the following design data:**

- Room size: 12 m × 10 m × 5 m (L × W × H)
- Wall insulation: 120 mm expanded polystyrene (EPS), $k = 0.035$ W/m·K
- Inside temperature: −20 °C, Outside design temperature: +38 °C
- Surface coefficients: $h_i = 10$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 14$ W/m²·K
- Daily product throughput: 3 000 kg entering at +4 °C, leaving at −20 °C, freezing point −2 °C
- 4 workers, 6 hours/day; 8 lamps of 50 W each, usage factor 0.5
- 1.8 kW evaporator fan motor, 85 % efficiency
- Door openings equivalent to 8 air changes per day

**(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the total refrigeration load for the storage room, listing each component separately.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the engineering strategies that could reduce this load by at least 20 %, identifying which strategies are most cost-effective.**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — U-value Calculation:**

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{0.120}{0.035} + \frac{1}{14}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{U} = 0.100 + 3.4286 + 0.0714 = 3.6000
$$

$$
U = 0.2778 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

**Step 2 — Surface Areas:**

$$
A_{\text{wall}} = 2 \times (12 + 10) \times 5 = 220 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{flr}} = A_{\text{ceil}} = 12 \times 10 = 120 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Heat Gain Through Walls, Floor, Ceiling:**

Assuming floor on ground at +25 °C and ceiling roof at +50 °C under sun:

$$
Q_1 = 0.2778 \times [220 \times 58 + 120 \times 45 + 120 \times 70]
$$

$$
Q_1 = 0.2778 \times [12\,760 + 5\,400 + 8\,400] = 0.2778 \times 26\,560
$$

$$
Q_1 = 7\,378 \text{ W} = 7.38 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat Gain from Product:**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{3\,000}{86\,400} = 0.03472 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{2a} = 0.03472 \times 3.5 \times 6 = 0.729 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
Q_{2b} = 0.03472 \times 335 = 11.63 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
Q_{2c} = 0.03472 \times 1.8 \times 18 = 1.125 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
Q_2 = 0.729 + 11.63 + 1.125 = 13.48 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Air Infiltration:**

$$
m_{\text{air}} = 600 \times 8 \times 1.2 = 5\,760 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 0.0667 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_3 = 0.0667 \times 1.005 \times 58 = 3.89 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Lighting and Personnel:**

$$
Q_4 = 8 \times 50 \times 0.5 = 200 \text{ W} = 0.20 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
Q_5 = 4 \times 340 \times \frac{6}{24} = 340 \text{ W} = 0.34 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Fan Motor:**

$$
Q_6 = \frac{1\,800}{0.85} = 2\,118 \text{ W} = 2.12 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Total Load:**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 7.38 + 13.48 + 3.89 + 0.20 + 0.34 + 2.12
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = 27.41 \text{ kW} \approx 27.4 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

To reduce the load by 20 %, target savings = $0.20 \times 27.4 = 5.5$ kW.

**Strategy 1 — Pre-cool Product (Highest Impact):**
The product load (13.48 kW) is the largest single contributor. Installing a **separate blast pre-cooling room** at −5 °C, with a separate refrigeration circuit, will cut the main storage room's product load to nearly zero. Savings: ~10 kW. Cost: Moderate capital, large operating savings.

**Strategy 2 — Improve Insulation:**
Switching from 120 mm EPS to 150 mm PUF ($k = 0.024$ W/m·K) reduces $Q_1$ by approximately 50 %. Savings: ~3.7 kW. Cost: Moderate.

**Strategy 3 — Air Curtain at Door:**
Reducing infiltration air changes from 8 to 4 per day saves ~50 % of $Q_3$. Savings: ~1.9 kW. Cost: Low (one-time).

**Strategy 4 — LED Lighting + EC Fans:**
Replacing fluorescent lamps with LEDs (saving 75 % lighting load) and replacing the AC fan with an EC fan (saving 60 % fan power). Combined savings: ~1.4 kW. Cost: Low with rapid payback.

**Cost-Effectiveness Ranking (Best → Worst):**
1. LED + EC fan retrofit (payback < 18 months)
2. Air curtain at door (payback 1 – 2 years)
3. PUF insulation upgrade (payback 3 – 5 years)
4. Blast pre-cooler (payback 4 – 7 years, but enables major capacity expansion)

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the cold chain concept as applied to frozen foods, with a labelled block diagram showing all stages from farm/fishery to consumer.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the constructional features of a modern frozen food cold storage room, including insulation, vapour barriers, floor heating (if any), and door design.**

**Model Solution Outline for Part (a):**

The **cold chain** is a continuous, temperature-controlled supply chain that preserves the quality and safety of frozen food from the point of origin to the point of consumption. Any break in the chain is called a **temperature abuse event** and causes irreversible quality loss.

**Stages of the Frozen Food Cold Chain:**

1. **Primary Production** (farm / fishery / slaughterhouse): Initial cooling and pre-freezing.
2. **Initial Processing & Quick Freezing** (IQF / blast freezer): Rapid freezing to retain texture.
3. **Frozen Storage at Origin** (−18 °C warehouse).
4. **Cold Chain Transport** (reefer trucks, containers at −18 °C).
5. **Regional Distribution Warehouse** (−18 °C hub).
6. **Retail Outlet** (display freezer at −18 °C or below).
7. **Consumer's Home Freezer** (−15 °C to −18 °C).
8. **Preparation & Consumption** (thawing and cooking).

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Farm Fishery Slaughterhouse +4C] --> S2[Blast Freezer IQF -35C]
    S2 --> S3[Origin Frozen Warehouse -18C]
    S3 --> S4[Reefer Truck Transport -18C]
    S4 --> S5[Regional Hub Warehouse -18C]
    S5 --> S6[Retail Display Freezer -18C]
    S6 --> S7[Consumer Home Freezer -15 to -18C]
    S7 --> S8[Thawing and Cooking]
    S8 --> S9[End Consumer]
    style S1 fill:#ffe6cc
    style S2 fill:#cce6ff
    style S3 fill:#cce6ff
    style S4 fill:#cce6ff
    style S5 fill:#cce6ff
    style S6 fill:#cce6ff
    style S7 fill:#cce6ff
```

**Model Solution Outline for Part (b):**

**Constructional Features of a Modern Frozen Food Cold Storage Room:**

1. **Insulation Materials:** Polyurethane Foam (PUF) with $k = 0.020 - 0.024$ W/m·K is the industry standard for −18 °C rooms. Thickness: 100 – 150 mm for walls, 150 – 200 mm for ceiling, and 100 – 150 mm for floor. For very low temperature rooms (−30 °C and below), extruded polystyrene (XPS) or PUF-cork composites are used.

2. **Vapour Barrier:** A continuous **0.2 mm thick polyethylene or aluminium foil sheet** is installed on the *warm* side of the insulation. This prevents water vapour migration from the warm ambient into the cold insulation, where it would condense, freeze, and destroy the insulation's R-value. Without a proper vapour barrier, ice formation inside the wall can cause structural damage and microbial growth (Aspergillus, Penicillium).

3. **Floor Construction:** The floor is a critical element. Three common designs exist:
   - **Floor-on-ground with under-floor heating:** A network of electrical or hot-glycol heating cables beneath the floor prevents the ground from freezing, which would otherwise cause **frost heave** and structural damage to the building.
   - **Suspended floor with ventilated plenum:** Used in very low temperature rooms; air is circulated beneath the floor to keep the underlying soil from freezing.
   - **Insulated floor on a heated slab:** Common in India for −18 °C rooms; combines rigid foam insulation with embedded heating elements.

4. **Wall and Ceiling Joints:** All insulation joints are staggered, sealed with expanding foam, and covered with metal flashing (galvanized iron or aluminium) to ensure continuity of the vapour barrier.

5. **Door Design:** Insulated swing doors or sliding doors with **100 mm PUF core**, double-sealed gaskets, **heated pressure-relief vents** (to prevent door from being impossible to open when the room is at lower pressure than the outside — a common KTU exam point), and **clear PVC strip curtains** on the interior side to reduce infiltration during brief openings.

6. **Internal Fittings:** Galvanized steel pallet racking, epoxy-coated floors with a 1 % slope toward a floor drain (for wash-down), and LED lighting with motion sensors.

7. **Refrigeration Plant:** Ceiling- or wall-mounted evaporator units with **electric or hot-gas defrost** capability, with condensate drained to a heated drain pan to prevent re-freezing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently forget to mention the **vapour barrier** and the **floor heating system**. In KTU valuation, omission of either typically costs 1.5 to 2 marks each. Also, never recommend a single-layer insulation panel; always mention both insulation *and* its protective facing.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Frozen Food Storage: High-Density Revision Summary**

- **Standard Design Temperature:** **−18 °C** (Codex / FSSAI / IS 13152), with RH **90 – 95 %**.
- **Five Functional Requirements:** Thermal containment, temperature uniformity, humidity control, air circulation, sanitary design.
- **Q₁₀ Rule:** Shelf life approximately **doubles** for every 10 °C reduction in storage temperature.
- **Freezer Burn:** Caused by sublimation due to T fluctuation or low RH. Prevented by (a) tight T control ± 1 °C, (b) RH 90 – 95 %, (c) vapour-impermeable packaging.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ (walls)
  - $Q = m \cdot (c_{p,\text{above}} \cdot \Delta T_1 + h_{fg} + c_{p,\text{below}} \cdot \Delta T_2)$ (product — 3-stage cooling)
  - $Q = V \cdot n \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T$ (air infiltration)
  - $1/U = 1/h_i + \Sigma x_k/k_k + 1/h_o$ (composite wall)
- **Standard Constants:** $c_{p,\text{above}} = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{p,\text{below}} = 1.8$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 335$ kJ/kg, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.
- **Thermal Conductivity (k) for Common Insulations:** PUF = 0.024, EPS = 0.035, XPS = 0.029, Cork = 0.040, Mineral wool = 0.040 (all in W/m·K).
- **Load Component Ranking (Typical):** Product (50 %) > Walls (20 %) > Air infiltration (15 %) > Fan motors (10 %) > Personnel & lighting (5 %).
- **Vapour Barrier:** Always on the **warm side** of the insulation. Typical material: **0.2 mm polyethylene** or **aluminium foil laminate**.
- **Floor Heating:** Required to prevent **frost heave** in frozen storage rooms where the ground can otherwise freeze.
- **Pressure-Relief Vent:** Mandatory on insulated doors to prevent pressure differential lock-up.
- **Defrost Cycle:** Required on evaporator coils; **hot-gas defrost** is more energy-efficient than electric defrost in large facilities.
- **Cold Chain Integrity:** Every break (above −15 °C) counts as a **temperature abuse event** and is non-recoverable in terms of microbial quality.
- **HACCP Compliance:** All surfaces must be smooth, non-absorbent, washable, and non-toxic. Floors must slope to drains. Lighting must be shatterproof.
- **Common KTU Mistakes:**
  - Confusing storage temperature (−18 °C) with blast-freezing temperature (−35 °C).
  - Forgetting to convert daily mass flow to **kg/s** before using the load formula.
  - Omitting the **vapour barrier** in constructional descriptions.
  - Using Celsius in temperature difference instead of Kelvin (numerically identical, but students sometimes write wrong units).
  - Applying chilled-storage humidity (85 %) to frozen storage — frozen storage needs **90 – 95 %** to prevent sublimation.
